EDCS-10101848.translation: Tertia Claudia Rivis willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-10101848.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tertia Claudia Rivis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101850.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Nepotiano decurioni municipii Varvarinorum qui vixit annos LI dies XXII Aurelia Veri[na ------\n
EDCS-10101850.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Nepotianus, decurion of the municipality of Varvarini, who lived 51 years and 22 days, Aurelia Verina... (rest of the inscription is lost).
EDCS-10101850.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Nepotianus, a decurion from Varvarini, who died at the age of 51 years and 22 days, with a dedication from Aurelia Verina, likely dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-10101851.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titiae Flaviae Crescentillae sanctitatis unicae sinceritatis incomparabilis sapientiae singularis suis desideranda grata omnibus amabilis notis cuius nomen mutavit affectus ex Crescentilla enim vocata est mamma quae sola sine inimico vixit cuius casum nemo non flevit Victoria filia matri carissimae posuit quae vixit annos LXXI\n
EDCS-10101851.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Titia Flavia Crescentilla, of unique sanctity, incomparable sincerity, singular wisdom, desired by her own, pleasing to all, amiable to strangers, whose name was changed by affection, for she was called Crescentilla from mamma, who lived without an enemy, whose death no one did not mourn. Victoria, her dearest daughter, placed this for her most beloved mother, who lived 71 years.
EDCS-10101851.summary: This is an elaborate funerary inscription for Titia Flavia Crescentilla, praising her virtues and recording her death at age 71, with the monument erected by her daughter Victoria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101853.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Proculus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101853.translation: To Father Liber. Proculus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10101853.summary: This is a votive inscription by Proculus dedicating a vow to the god Liber Pater, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101854.text_edition: \n Publio Atilio Aebutiano praefecto praetorio clarissimo viro patrono optimo ordo Asseriatium \n
EDCS-10101854.translation: To Publius Atilius Aebutianus, most distinguished prefect of the praetorian guard, an excellent patron, the council of Asseriatum.
EDCS-10101854.summary: This is a dedication by the council of Asseriatum to Publius Atilius Aebutianus, a high-ranking official and patron, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101856.text_edition: \n Lucius Caninius Titi filius Claudia Fronto IIvir flamen divi Claudii IIvir quinquennalis\n
EDCS-10101856.translation: Lucius Caninius Fronto, son of Titus, of the Claudia tribe, duovir, flamen of the deified Claudius, quinquennial duovir.
EDCS-10101856.summary: This inscription lists the public offices held by Lucius Caninius Fronto, including duovir and flamen of the deified Claudius, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-10101859.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Daeico Aureli Fortunionis TV \n
EDCS-10101859.translation: To the departed spirits of Daeico Aurelius Fortunio. TV.
EDCS-10101859.summary: This funerary inscription honours Daeico Aurelius Fortunio, likely a freedman given the name structure, and is dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101860.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of a person named Caninius, but the rest of the text is missing and its purpose is unknown.

EDCS-10101861.translation: To Lucius Clodius, son of Quintus, in his fourth [year/decade/etc.].
EDCS-10101861.summary: This inscription names Lucius Clodius, son of Quintus, and possibly refers to his age or a period of time in his life, likely a funerary or dedication text.

EDCS-10101863.summary: This inscription records the full name of Papirius Secundus, son of Caius, likely a personal monument or part of a larger record.

EDCS-10101864.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agathe Saecundino sic filio annorum XV SV PO \n
EDCS-10101864.translation: To the departed spirits of Agathe, daughter of Saecundinus, aged fifteen years. SV PO.
EDCS-10101864.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Agathe, daughter of Saecundinus, who died at fifteen years old, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101865.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Epicatiae coniugi Geminus maritus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10101865.translation: To his well-deserving wife Epicatia, Geminus erected this monument.
EDCS-10101865.summary: This funerary inscription records Geminus dedicating a monument to his wife, Epicatia, indicating a loving sentiment and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101867.text_edition: \n Titia Marci filia Rufina TAB votum solvit libens m(erito)\n
EDCS-10101867.translation: Titia Rufina, daughter of Marcus, paid her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10101867.summary: This inscription records Titia Rufina, daughter of Marcus, fulfilling a vow, likely a votive dedication made in gratitude.

EDCS-10101868.text_edition: \n Caesaris Augusti inter Sidrinos et Asseriates Quintus Aebutius Liberalis |(centurio) legionis XI definit\n
EDCS-10101868.translation: Between the Sidrini and Asseriates, Quintus Aebutius Liberalis, a centurion of the eleventh legion, passed away during the reign of Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-10101868.summary: This inscription records the death of Quintus Aebutius Liberalis, a centurion of the eleventh legion, during the reign of Augustus, potentially marking a military or territorial boundary.

EDCS-10101870.translation: To the departed spirits of Au[relius/gustus/etc.].
EDCS-10101870.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription beginning with 'Dis Manibus', indicating a funerary context, but the full name of the deceased is lost.

EDCS-10101872.text_edition: \n Valetudini Statae sacrum Lurnio Calpurnia votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-10101872.translation: Sacred to Valetudo Stata. Lurnius Calpurnia paid her vow willingly.
EDCS-10101872.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Valetudo Stata by Lurnius Calpurnia, who willingly fulfilled a vow, likely related to health.

EDCS-10101874.text_edition: \n Baebilius Maxsimus sic sibi et suis\n
EDCS-10101874.translation: Baebilius Maxsimus, for himself and his family.
EDCS-10101874.summary: This inscription records a dedication or claim made by Baebilius Maxsimus for himself and his household.

EDCS-10101875.text_edition: \n Quintus Gellius Hermeros sibi Quinto G(ellio) Maximo? fil(io)\n
EDCS-10101875.translation: Quintus Gellius Hermeros for himself and for his son, Quintus Gellius Maximus (?).
EDCS-10101875.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Quintus Gellius Hermeros for himself and his son, likely named Quintus Gellius Maximus, possibly on a tomb or other monument.

EDCS-10101876.text_edition: \n Quinto Hostilio annorum VIII Ius Lupus \n
EDCS-10101876.translation: To Quintus Hostilius, aged 8, Ius Lupus.
EDCS-10101876.summary: This inscription records a dedication or memorial for Quintus Hostilius, an 8-year-old boy, set up by Ius Lupus.

EDCS-10101877.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a daughter named Procula, likely on a tomb or memorial.

EDCS-10101878.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a daughter named Quinta, the daughter of Caius, likely on a tomb or memorial.

EDCS-10101880.text_edition: \n IA ex testamento fratris posuit rio Celso\n
EDCS-10101880.translation: Set up according to the will of his brother, for Celsus.
EDCS-10101880.summary: This inscription records a dedication to someone named Celsus, set up by a brother in accordance with a will.

EDCS-10101883.text_edition: \n Iovi Kalpurnia Peculiaris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101883.translation: Kalpurnia Peculiaris willingly fulfilled her vow to Father Liber as deserved.
EDCS-10101883.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Kalpurnia Peculiaris to the god Liber Pater.

EDCS-10101884.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Quintus Hostilius Barcinus voto fecit\n
EDCS-10101884.translation: Quintus Hostilius Barcinus made this in fulfillment of a vow to Father Liber.
EDCS-10101884.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Quintus Hostilius Barcinus to the god Liber Pater.

EDCS-10101885.text_edition: \n ------] finis inter Ansienses et Corinienses secundum conventionem utriusque partis derectus mensuris actis iussu Auli Duceni Gemini legati Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-10101885.translation: [...boundary] established between the Ansienses and the Corinienses according to the agreement of both parties, with measurements taken, by order of Aulus Ducenius Geminius, imperial legate with praetorian power.
EDCS-10101885.summary: This inscription records the formal establishment of a boundary between two groups, the Ansienses and the Corinienses, under the authority of the imperial legate Aulus Ducenius Geminius.

EDCS-10101888.text_edition: \n area viva fecit iae Terti Calpurnio \n
EDCS-10101888.translation: Made for T(ertius?) Calpurnius, by his living wife.
EDCS-10101888.summary: This inscription records a monument or dedication made by a wife for her living husband, T(ertius?) Calpurnius.

EDCS-10101893.text_edition: \n Baebius Priscus Volcano votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101893.translation: Baebius Priscus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Vulcan.
EDCS-10101893.summary: This is a votive inscription by Baebius Priscus to the god Vulcan, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101895.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Heraclio filio dulcissimo qui vixit nnos VI menses VIII dies XXII et Heracliae quae vixit nnos XXV infelicissimis set sic sibi pientissimis ut mater filium non amplius quam unam noctem desereret ut merito filius patri et uxor marito assiduas lacrimas relinquerent Velox filio dulcissimo et coniugi incomparabilibus\n
EDCS-10101895.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Heraclius, my dearest son, who lived 6 years, 8 months, and 22 days, and for Heraclia, who lived 25 years, most unfortunate but most devoted, so that a mother would not desert her son for more than one night, and so that the son, the wife, and the husband deservedly left behind constant tears. Velox for his dearest son and incomparable wife.
EDCS-10101895.summary: This is a tomb inscription set up by Velox for his son Heraclius and his wife Heraclia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101896.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quartillae matri pientissimae quae vixit nnos plus minus LXXXV filii bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-10101896.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Quartilla, their most devoted mother, who lived more or less 85 years; her sons erected this for her, who deserved well of them.
EDCS-10101896.summary: This is a tomb inscription erected by sons for their devoted mother Quartilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101897.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Rubrius Onesimus sibi et Mariae Arabinae uxori et suis vivos sic fecit excipitur huic sepulchro ius itus ambiti ex ceteris partibus quoque versus per pedes centenos hoc sepulchrum heredes non sequetur\n
EDCS-10101897.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Rubrius Onesimus made this for himself, for his wife Maria Arabina, and for his family while alive, so that they may be received into this tomb; the right of passage and access from all other directions for one hundred feet shall not be followed by heirs to this tomb.
EDCS-10101897.summary: This is a funerary self-dedication inscription by Marcus Rubrius Onesimus for himself, his wife, and family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101898.text_edition: \n Dis Salutaribus Cnaeus Cornelius Severus ob periculum quod evaserat\n
EDCS-10101898.translation: To the Gods of Health, for the danger from which Gnaeus Cornelius Severus escaped.
EDCS-10101898.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gnaeus Cornelius Severus to the Salutares (Gods of Health), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101899.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Empress Faustina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101900.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-10101900.text_edition: \n Aurelia Gorgonia conparabit sic <l=I>oci conc<e=I>ssion<e=I>m <u=Y>t ne q<u=Y>is ali<u=Y>s post<e=I>a <velle=YIIII>t a<liu=IIY>m corp<u=Y>us pon<e=I>r<e=I> q<u=Y>od si q<u=Y>i comtempserit sic habebit de<u=Y>m i<u=Y>dicem et in<f=E>er<e=F>t TOIPSSO |(denarios) cent<u=Y>(m) milia \n
EDCS-10101900.translation: Aurelia Gorgonia will arrange for the concession of the place, so that no one else will later want to place a body there; if anyone attempts this, they will have Jupiter as judge and will pay a penalty of one hundred thousand denarii.
EDCS-10101900.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Aurelia Gorgonia concerning the concession of burial space and imposing a penalty for violations, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101901.text_edition: \n Caio Cursineio Dextro Varena Quinta coniugi bene merito\n
EDCS-10101901.translation: To Gaius Cursinius Dexter, from Varena Quinta, his deserving wife.
EDCS-10101901.summary: This is a tomb inscription from Varena Quinta to her deserving husband Gaius Cursinius Dexter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101902.text_edition: \n Cursineiae Fortunatae Ianinius Valens coniugi bene meritae\n
EDCS-10101902.translation: To Cursinia Fortunata, from Ianinius Valens, her deserving husband.
EDCS-10101902.summary: This is a tomb inscription from Ianinius Valens to his deserving wife Cursinia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101903.text_edition: \n Quinto Tullio Primigenio Quintus Tullius Ausimus libertus et heres\n
EDCS-10101903.translation: To Quintus Tullius Primigenius, from Quintus Tullius Ausimus, his freedman and heir.
EDCS-10101903.summary: This is a tomb inscription by Quintus Tullius Ausimus, freedman and heir, to Quintus Tullius Primigenius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101905.translation: Marcia, while still alive, dedicated this to her living husband.
EDCS-10101905.summary: This is a dedication by Marcia to her husband, likely on a tomb, from the period when the couple was alive.

EDCS-10101911.text_edition: \n ------] Caio M Severo equiti ex singularibus ex cohorte praetoria IX stipendiorum II vixit annos XXX Aelia Maxima mater filio infelicissimo bene merenti et sivi sic viva <f=E>ecit \n
EDCS-10101911.translation: To Gaius M. Severus, an equestrian of the Praetorian Guard, formerly a personal attendant, with 9 years of service. He lived 30 years. Aelia Maxima, his mother, erected this for her most unfortunate son, who deserved well.
EDCS-10101911.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gaius M. Severus, an equestrian soldier in the Praetorian Guard, set up by his grieving mother, Aelia Maxima.

EDCS-10101912.translation: Lucius Valerius, centurion of the century of Proclus.
EDCS-10101912.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Valerius, a military officer (centurion), and mentions his subordinate Proclus, possibly on a tombstone or military record.

EDCS-10101913.summary: This is a simple naming inscription, likely for a tombstone, identifying Quinctius Proculus, son of Maximus.

EDCS-10101915.text_edition: \n Lucio Calpurnio Maximo Muttiena Clementis liberta Maxima coniugi suo viva\n
EDCS-10101915.translation: To Lucius Calpurnius Maximus, from his wife Maxima, freedwoman of Muttiena Clemens, while she was alive.
EDCS-10101915.summary: This is a dedication made by Maxima, a freedwoman, to her husband Lucius Calpurnius Maximus, likely on a tomb they erected while alive.

EDCS-10101916.text_edition: \n Sentonae Sextus Aemilius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101916.translation: Sextus Aemilius fulfilled his vow to Sentona willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10101916.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Sextus Aemilius fulfilling a vow to the deity Sentona.

EDCS-10101918.text_edition: \n Vestae Sextus Aquilius Lucifer votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101918.translation: Sextus Aquilius Lucifer fulfilled his vow to Vesta willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10101918.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Sextus Aquilius Lucifer fulfilling a vow to the goddess Vesta.

EDCS-10101922.text_edition: \n In memoriam liberorum suorum Aquillia Luci filia Oculatina de pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-10101922.translation: In memory of her children, Aquillia, daughter of Lucius, made this at her own expense.
EDCS-10101922.summary: This is a memorial inscription set up by Aquillia, daughter of Lucius, to commemorate her deceased children, funded by herself.

EDCS-10101923.text_edition: \n Sentonae sacrum Caius Vibius Florus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101923.translation: This sacred dedication to Sentona was made by Gaius Vibius Florus, who fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10101923.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Gaius Vibius Florus dedicating something sacred to the deity Sentona.

EDCS-10101924.text_edition: \n Sentonae sacrum Tullia Fusca votum solvit\n
EDCS-10101924.translation: Tullia Fusca made this sacred dedication to Sentona, fulfilling her vow.
EDCS-10101924.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Tullia Fusca dedicating something sacred to the deity Sentona.

EDCS-10101925.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Caius Vibius Fesius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101925.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Gaius Vibius Fesius gladly fulfilled his vow according to merit.
EDCS-10101925.summary: An altar dedicated to the god Silvanus by Gaius Vibius Fesius, likely fulfilling a vow, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101926.text_edition: \n Acaica Hoia pro Benigna et Iadestino votum solvit\n
EDCS-10101926.translation: Acaica Hoia fulfilled her vow for Benigna and Iadestinus.
EDCS-10101926.summary: A dedication made by Acaica Hoia for two individuals named Benigna and Iadestinus, likely a votive offering, possibly Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101927.text_edition: \n nama Sexti filia Matto votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10101927.translation: Matto, daughter of Sextus, gladly fulfilled her vow according to merit.
EDCS-10101927.summary: A votive inscription by Matto, daughter of Sextus, who willingly fulfilled a vow, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101929.summary: A dedication or memorial to Gavillia, daughter of Lucius, possibly part of a funerary monument, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101930.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Gaius Valerius Senecus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101931.text_edition: \n NAETI ANEN ius Quinti filius Volt\n
EDCS-10101931.summary: A fragment of an inscription, likely a personal name and patronymic, possibly Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101932.summary: Part of an inscription identifying an individual as the freedman of Quintus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101934.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the names Gaius Geminius and Claudia, possibly a dedication or funerary text, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101937.text_edition: \n sorori piae Iuliae Tertullinus frater fecit quae vixit annos XXII\n
EDCS-10101937.translation: For my pious sister, Julia Tertullinus the brother made this, who lived 22 years.
EDCS-10101937.summary: A funerary monument erected by a brother, Tertullinus, for his sister Julia, who died at the age of 22, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101940.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Leucius Asclepius patri suo infelicissimo Leucio Asclepio enpiri[co! ------\n
EDCS-10101940.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Leucius Asclepius to his most unfortunate father, Leucius Asclepius, imperial [---].
EDCS-10101940.summary: A funerary dedication to an unfortunate father, Leucius Asclepius, by his son Leucius Asclepius, possibly indicating imperial status for the father, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101941.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Servilia Prima mater Martino filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-10101941.translation: To the Divine Manes. Servilia Prima for her most devoted son, Martinus.
EDCS-10101941.summary: A tombstone inscription dedicated by a mother to her son, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10101945.translation: Curtia Gemelia to Ursio, her nurse.
EDCS-10101945.summary: A funerary dedication by Curtia Gemelia to her nurse, Ursio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101946.text_edition: \n Caio Curtio Maximo Lucius Curtius Firmus fratri posuit\n
EDCS-10101946.translation: Lucius Curtius Firmus placed this for his brother, Gaius Curtius Maximus.
EDCS-10101946.summary: A funerary monument erected by Lucius Curtius Firmus for his brother, Gaius Curtius Maximus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101947.text_edition: \n Gaius Octavius Pudens solo suo hic situs est\n
EDCS-10101947.translation: Gaius Octavius Pudens lies here on his own land.
EDCS-10101947.summary: A burial inscription recording the death and burial place of Gaius Octavius Pudens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101948.text_edition: \n ------ fili]o infelicissima mater aetatis primo qui mihi flore periit percussus cornu bubus dum pabula ponit ad quem dum curro dum sic miser ante sic periit infelixs sic genetrix Diti tria funera duxsi sic lugebam natas cum mihi natus obiit quod superest matri saltem concedite Manes ut sint qui voltus post mea fata premant Marci Octavi Pulli fili Rufi\n
EDCS-10101948.translation: To her son, [infelicitous mother]... who perished in the first bloom of youth, struck by a bull's horn while putting out fodder... alas, how wretchedly he perished thus, thus his mother twice. Three funerals I led to Dis, and mourned daughters when my son died. What remains, Manes, grant at least to your mother, that there may be those who press my face after my death. Son of Marcus Octavius Pullus, Rufinus.
EDCS-10101948.summary: A deeply emotional funerary epitaph by a mother mourning the death of her son, who was killed by a bull, and lamenting previous family losses, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101949.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Catapliae uxori conlibertae fidelissimae conservatrici optimae et amantissimae \n
EDCS-10101949.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Cataplia, wife, freedwoman, most faithful, preserver, excellent, and most loving.
EDCS-10101949.summary: A funerary dedication to a wife, Cataplia, described as a faithful freedwoman and loving partner, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101951.summary: A simple funerary inscription stating that 'Mother Prim' made it, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101952.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septima Publi A fe \n
EDCS-10101952.translation: To the Divine Manes. Septima for Publius A[---].
EDCS-10101952.summary: A funerary inscription by Septima for Publius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101953.text_edition: \n Curtia Severa annorum XL hic sita Priscilla matri suae bene merenti\n
EDCS-10101953.translation: Curtia Severa, aged 40, lies here. For Priscilla, her mother, who deserved well.
EDCS-10101953.summary: A tombstone inscription for Curtia Severa, aged 40, dedicated by her mother Priscilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101962.summary: A funerary dedication to a wife, Campia, by her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10101963.text_edition: \n Servi Cai libertus Fus posuit voto soluto\n
EDCS-10101963.translation: Servus Fus, freedman of Caius, erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-10101963.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by a freedman named Fus for Caius, likely a deity or patron.

EDCS-10101964.text_edition: \n Liccaeus Vei filius |(centurio) de liburna Lucusta testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Dabali Triti heredis amico merenti annorum XXX loco publice dato\n
EDCS-10101964.translation: Liccaeus, son of Veius, a centurion from the ship Lucusta, ordered this to be made by his will, under the supervision of his friend Dabalius Tritus, his heir, for him who deserved it, at the age of 30, on public land.
EDCS-10101964.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Liccaeus, a centurion, who commissioned his own tomb to be built by his heir.

EDCS-10101970.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Super hic situs est vixit annos II menses VII\n
EDCS-10101970.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Super is buried here; he lived 2 years and 7 months.
EDCS-10101970.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial and lifespan of a child named Super.

EDCS-10101971.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Vivio sic Traeboniano sic Gallo et Imperatori Caesari Gaio Vivio sic Atinio sic Veldumniano Volusiano Auggustis\n
EDCS-10101971.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Gaius Vibius Traianus Gallus and Caesar Gaius Vibius Atinius Volusianus Volusianus Augustus.
EDCS-10101971.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman Emperors Trajanus Gallus and Volusianus, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-10101972.text_edition: \n Imperatori Gaiulo Iulio Vero Macximino sic Pio Felici et Caio Iulio Vero Macximo sic i nobilissimo Caesari Augusto Caesaris nostri filio\n
EDCS-10101972.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, the most noble Caesar, son of our Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-10101972.summary: This inscription honors the Roman Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Caesar, dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-10101974.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Claudio Constanti nobilissimo Caesari filio domini nostri Constantini Maximi Victoris sem semper Augusti\n
EDCS-10101974.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantius, most noble Caesar, son of our lord Constantine the Great Victorious, always Augustus.
EDCS-10101974.summary: This inscription dedicates a monument to Constantius Chlorus, son of Constantine the Great, dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-10101977.text_edition: \n Domino nostro nobilissimo Caesari Galerio o\n
EDCS-10101977.translation: To our lord, the most noble Caesar Galerius.
EDCS-10101977.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman Emperor Galerius Caesar, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-10101979.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Claudio Tacito Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-10101979.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-10101979.summary: This inscription honors the Roman Emperor Tacitus, dating to his brief reign in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-10101981.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Numerianus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-10101981.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Numerianus, who was proclaimed Caesar by his father Carinus in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-10101982.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator III consul \n
EDCS-10101982.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pius Felix, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, Imperator for the third time, Consul.
EDCS-10101982.summary: This inscription lists the imperial titles of Maximinus Thrax, indicating a date in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-10101983.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator III consul pater patriae et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nob<i=E>lissimus Caesar \n
EDCS-10101983.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, pious, fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, imperator for the third time, consul, father of the country, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-10101983.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Caesar Maximinus, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-10101987.translation: To the most noble Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius.
EDCS-10101987.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, likely Constantius Chlorus, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-10101988.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris Publio Licinio Valeriano et Publio Licinio Gallieno Piis Felicibus Auggustis ppontificibus mmaximis tribunicia potestate a Tilurio milia passuum \n
EDCS-10101988.translation: To our Lords Emperors Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Gallienus, pious, fortunate Augusti, chief priests, holding tribunician power, from Tilurius, milestones.
EDCS-10101988.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperors Valerian and Gallienus and possibly marks milestones along a road, dating to their joint reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10101992.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Valerio Diocletiano et Imperatori Caesari Caio Valerio Maximiano PPiis FFelicibus perpetuis princincpibus\n
EDCS-10101992.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian and Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Maximian, pious, fortunate, perpetual princes.
EDCS-10101992.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, likely from the period of their co-rule in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-10101995.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus tribunicia pontifex maximus potestas pater patriae imperator III consul II Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar\n
EDCS-10101995.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, pious, fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the country, imperator for the third time, consul for the second time, Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-10101995.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Caesar Maximinus, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-10102004.summary: This inscription records the name and titles of Emperor Claudius Gothicus, who reigned in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10102007.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XIIII consul IIII pater patriae censor XXXXIIII\n
EDCS-10102007.translation: To Emperor Caesar Tiberius Claudius Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, imperator for the fourteenth time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, censor for the forty-fourth time.
EDCS-10102007.summary: This inscription records the full imperial titles of Emperor Claudius Gothicus, likely dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10102117.translation: Freely fulfilled a vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-10102117.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to the god Hercules Augustus, likely by an individual soldier or official.

EDCS-10102118.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Aurelius Salvianus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae C II A ANNSVS \n
EDCS-10102118.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the place, Aurelius Salvianus, centurion of the XIII Gemina legion, made this offering. [X] years old.
EDCS-10102118.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Salvianus, a centurion of the XIII Gemina legion, to Jupiter and the local genius, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-10701891.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Saturna Caesio Castrensi marito suo fecit qui vixit annis XXXX\n
EDCS-10701891.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Saturnina Caesio made this for her husband Castrensis, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-10701891.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Castrensis, who lived 40 years, and was dedicated by his wife Saturnina Caesio.

EDCS-10701893.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Dometia sic vixit annos XVIII Aurelii Domitius et Tata filiae p h s s f\n
EDCS-10701893.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Dometia lived thus for 18 years. Aurelius Domitius and Tata, to their daughter, placed this (monument) out of their own funds.
EDCS-10701893.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Dometia, recording her age at death and the identity of her parents, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12000832.text_edition: \n Annaea Decimi filia Maxima Luci Saeni Luculli Iunoni ob honorem\n
EDCS-12000832.translation: Annaea Maxima, daughter of Decimus, to Lucius Saenius Lucullus, for the priesthood.
EDCS-12000832.summary: This is a dedication to the magistrate Lucius Saenius Lucullus by Annaea Maxima, likely a votive offering for an office held, possibly from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-12000833.translation: To Venus, a gift from Tertulla Catena.
EDCS-12000833.summary: This is a dedication of a gift to the goddess Venus by Tertulla Catena, likely a votive offering, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12000834.text_edition: \n Fortunae sacrum Caius Agrius Fortunatus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12000834.translation: Sacred to Fortuna. Caius Agrius Fortunatus gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-12000834.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Fortuna by Caius Agrius Fortunatus, who fulfilled a vow, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12000835.text_edition: \n Castori et Polluci sacrum Quintus Lusius Secundus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12000835.translation: Sacred to Castor and Pollux. Quintus Lusius Secundus gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-12000835.summary: This is a votive inscription to the divine twins Castor and Pollux by Quintus Lusius Secundus, who fulfilled a vow, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12000836.text_edition: \n Veneri Augustae sacrum in memoriam Septimiae Lupulae ex testamento Septimiae Ursinae matris detracta XX ex libris LXXXXV semis Publius Umbrius Saturninus heres posuit\n
EDCS-12000836.translation: Sacred to Augusta Venus, in memory of Septimia Lupula, from the will of her mother Septimia Ursina: 20 denarii deducted from 95 and a half. Publius Umbrius Saturninus, the heir, placed (this).
EDCS-12000836.summary: This is a funerary inscription and dedication to Venus, funded by an inheritance from Septimia Ursina for her deceased daughter Septimia Lupula, with the heir Publius Umbrius Saturninus erecting the monument, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12000838.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Turranio Clementi marito defuncto annorum LII Laelia Quinta Turranio Clementiano filio karo infelicissimo defuncto nnum XXXII multa peragrasti Germaniam terram secutus hic iacet erepta pietas pulchraque iuventus et sibi et suis hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-12000838.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Turranius Clemens, husband, deceased at 52 years. For Laelia Quinta Turranianus Clementianus, beloved and most unfortunate son, deceased at 32 years. You traveled much of Germany, pursuing your path; here you lie. Piety and beautiful youth were snatched away. This monument will not pass to an heir, for himself and his own.
EDCS-12000838.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for a husband and son, lamenting their deaths and emphasizing the tomb's non-hereditary status, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12000839.text_edition: \n ------ Ae]milius Messor et Aelia Vitalis parentes et Aemilius Marcus frater piissimi et posuerunt hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur sub ascia dederunt dedicaveruntque\n
EDCS-12000839.translation: Aemilius Messor and Aelia Vitalis, parents, and Aemilius Marcus, most dutiful brother, erected this monument; it will not pass to an heir. Under the ax they gave and dedicated it.
EDCS-12000839.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unnamed individual, erected by their parents and brother, specifying that the tomb will not be inherited, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12000840.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Silvino Publi filio \n
EDCS-12000840.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Ulpius Silvinus, son of Publius.
EDCS-12000840.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Silvinus, son of Publius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12000841.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Gnorius Satyrus Gnoriae Secundinae filiae annorum XIIX vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-12000841.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Gnorius Satyrus made this for Gnoria Secundina, daughter, aged 18 years, while alive; for himself and his family.
EDCS-12000841.summary: This is a funerary epitaph and dedication to the Underworld gods, made by Marcus Gnorius Satyrus for his daughter Gnoria Secundina and himself and his family, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12300218.translation: To the veterans who served in the Ravenna fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had completed twenty or more years of service and were settled in Pannonia, and to their children and descendants, Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, acclaimed imperator for the sixth time, father of his country, consul-elect for the third time, has granted citizenship and the right to marry, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they later married, provided each man had only one wife. This was decreed on the Nones of April, during the consulship of Caesar, son of Augustus, Domitian, and Gnaeus Pedius Cascus. This record, concerning the centurion Platorius, son of Venetus, of the Maezeii, has been transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the Capitoline Hill, outside the temple, near the altar of the Gens Julia, on the left side of the podium, tablet 1, page 2, location 44.
EDCS-12300218.summary: This inscription, dated to 80 AD, is an official grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Vespasian to veterans of the Ravenna fleet settled in Pannonia.

EDCS-12300242.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300242.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII imperator XXII censor perpetuus consul XVI pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohorte III Alpinorum et in VIII Voluntariorum civium Romanorum qui peregrinae condicionis probati erant et sunt in D<a=E>lmatia sub Quinto Pomponio Rufo qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt item dimisso honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Idus Iulias Marco Lollio Paullino Valerio Asiatico Saturnino Caio Antio Iulio Quadrato consulibus cohortis III Alpinorum cui praeest Caius Vibius Maximus pediti Veneto Diti filio Daverso et Madenae Plarentis filiae uxori eius Deramistae et Gaio filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII imperator XXII consul XVI censor perpetuus pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohorte III Alpinorum et in VIII Voluntariorum civium Romanorum qui peregrinae condicionis probati erant et sunt in D<a=E>lmatia sub Quinto Pomponio Rufo qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt item dimisso honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Idus Iulias Marco Lollio Paullino Valerio Asiatico Saturnino Caio Antio Iulio Quadrato consulibus cohortis III Alpinorum cui praeest Caius Vibius Maximus pediti Veneto Diti filio Daverso et Madenae Plarentis filiae uxori eius Deramistae et Gaio filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Auli Volumni Expectati Quinti Orfi Cupiti Cnaei Egnati Vitalis Sexti Manli Cinnami Luci Pulli Sperati Publi Atini Amermni Luci Pulli Verecundi \n
EDCS-12300242.translation: To the foot soldiers and cavalrymen who serve in the III Alpinorum Cohort and the VIII Voluntariorum Cohort of Roman citizens, who were proven to be of foreign origin and are stationed in Dalmatia under Quintus Pomponius Rufus, who have completed twenty or more years of service, and also those honorably discharged after completing their service, Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the twelfth time, acclaimed imperator for the twenty-second time, consul for the sixteenth time, perpetual censor, father of his country, has granted citizenship and the right to marry, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they later married, provided each man had only one wife. This was decreed on the third day before the Ides of July, during the consulship of Marcus Lollius Paullinus Valerius Asiaticus Saturninus and Gaius Antius Iulius Quadratus. This record, concerning the foot soldier Ditus, son of Vetus, from Venetia, of the III Alpinorum Cohort commanded by Gaius Vibius Maximus, and his wife Madena, daughter of Plarentis, and their son Gaius, has been transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the statue of Minerva.

EDCS-13300216.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Nonio Euticho sic Oristilla sic soror\n
EDCS-13300216.translation: To the spirits of the departed: to Lucius Nonius Eutychus, and Oristilla, and his sister.
EDCS-13300216.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Lucius Nonius Eutychus, Oristilla, and a sister, likely set up by family members.

EDCS-13302687.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Graecus qui et Fortunatus Marci libertus libens posuit\n
EDCS-13302687.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Protector. Graecus, also called Fortunatus, freedman of Marcus, set this up willingly.
EDCS-13302687.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, set up by a freedman named Graecus, who also went by Fortunatus.

EDCS-14800164.text_edition: \n Veneri Augustae sacrum in memoriam Vibiae Proculae ex testamento Flaviae Tertullinae matris de libris C Flavii Severus et Pudentilla posuerunt\n
EDCS-14800164.translation: Sacred to Venus Augusta, in memory of Vibia Procula, from the will of her mother Flavia Tertullina, from the books. Flavius Severus and Pudentilla set this up.
EDCS-14800164.summary: This is a sacred dedication to Venus Augusta, erected in memory of Vibia Procula by her mother's will, and set up by Flavius Severus and Pudentilla.

EDCS-14800165.text_edition: \n Divo Augusto sacrum Publius Dolabella consul Caesaris Augusti legatus pro praetore\n
EDCS-14800165.translation: Sacred to the deified Augustus. Publius Dolabella, consul, legate with praetorian authority of Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-14800165.summary: This is a sacred dedication to the deified Augustus, erected by Publius Dolabella, a legate in imperial service.

EDCS-14800166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Gemino iuveni infelicissimo defuncto Roma VC F AC Iulia et Gemella sorores fratri pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-14800166.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Geminus, a most unfortunate youth, now deceased. Iulia and Gemella, sisters, set this up for their most devoted brother.
EDCS-14800166.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by two sisters, Iulia and Gemella, to their deceased brother, Geminus.

EDCS-14800167.text_edition: \n Publius Quintilius Fortunatus Publius Quintilius Valens filius et Quinta filia fecerunt\n
EDCS-14800167.translation: Publius Quintilius Fortunatus. Publius Quintilius Valens, his son, and Quinta, his daughter, made this.
EDCS-14800167.summary: This is a dedication for Publius Quintilius Fortunatus, made by his son and daughter.

EDCS-14800168.text_edition: \n Stenius Fortis Lupulae collibertae bene merenti\n
EDCS-14800168.translation: To Stenius Fortis, for his fellow freedwoman Lupula, who deserved well.
EDCS-14800168.summary: This is a dedication by Stenius Fortis to his fellow freedwoman, Lupula, for her merit.

EDCS-14800169.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sempronio Valentioni Valeria Victorina \n
EDCS-14800169.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Sempronius Valens. Valeria Victorina [set this up].
EDCS-14800169.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Sempronius Valens by Valeria Victorina.

EDCS-14800170.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agrio Dicaeo patri bene merenti et Agrio Maximo fratri infelicissimo defuncto annorum XIIII Vinicia Plotia\n
EDCS-14800170.translation: To the Divine Spirits, for Agrio Diceo, father, well-deserving, and for Agrio Maximo, brother, most unhappy, who died at the age of 14 years. Vinicia Plotia.
EDCS-14800170.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Vinicia Plotia to her father and brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14800171.text_edition: \n Lucius Ancharenus Quinti filius Sergia Laranda miles legionis VII nnorum XL stipendiorum XXI hic situs est Quintus Ancharenus haec tan posuit tibi dona frater quia longe a patria te cecidisse dolet optaram vivo potius dare vina non obito care ferenda tuo si quicquam sentire potes mpore nostro damus haec o sit tibi terra levis frater fratri posuit\n
EDCS-14800171.translation: Lucius Ancharenus, son of Quintus, of the Sergia tribe, from Laranda, a soldier of the 7th legion, aged 40 years, with 21 years of service, lies here. Quintus Ancharenus placed these offerings for you, brother, because he grieves that you died far from home; I wish I could have given you wine while alive, not at death. Your grief is unbearable; if you can feel anything, we give these. May the earth lie lightly upon you, brother for brother, he placed this.
EDCS-14800171.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Quintus Ancharenus for his brother, Lucius Ancharenus, a soldier who died far from home, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14800172.text_edition: \n Lucius Cusius Luci libertus Ascanius annorum XX hic situs est patronus posuit bene merito\n
EDCS-14800172.translation: Lucius Cusius Ascanius, freedman of Lucius, aged 20 years, lies here. His patron placed [this] for the well-deserving.
EDCS-14800172.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Cusius Ascanius, a freedman, set up by his patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15100101.text_edition: \n conservatrici potenti Calpurnia Luci Pisonis auguris filia Cnaei Pisonis neptis donum dedit\n
EDCS-15100101.translation: For the powerful conservator, Calpurnia, daughter of Lucius Piso, the augur, granddaughter of Gnaeus Piso, gave this offering.
EDCS-15100101.summary: This is a dedication to a powerful conservator (possibly a deity or a personified concept) by Calpurnia, daughter of Lucius Piso and granddaughter of Gnaeus Piso, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15700109.text_edition: \n Veneri Ansoticae sacrum Baebia Cai filia Maximilla ex testamento Luci Corneli Bassi fecit\n
EDCS-15700109.translation: Sacred to Venus Ansotice. Baebia Maximmilla, daughter of Caius, made this according to the will of Lucius Cornelius Bassus.
EDCS-15700109.summary: This is a religious dedication to Venus Ansotice, commissioned by Baebia Maximmilla in accordance with the will of Lucius Cornelius Bassus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900045.text_edition: \n Titus Appuleius Titi Luci libertus Antigonius Anzoticae votum solvit\n
EDCS-15900045.translation: Titus Appuleius Antigonus, freedman of Titus, has paid his vow to Anzotica.
EDCS-15900045.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to the deity Anzotica by Titus Appuleius Antigonus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16000504.text_edition: \n Liberai sic Iulia Maximina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16000504.translation: For Liberitas, Julia Maximina paid her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-16000504.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to the deity Liberitas (Liberty) by Julia Maximina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100255.text_edition: \n Caius Utius Spuri filius testamento fieri iussit sibi et Publio Utio fratri suo et Clodiae Faustae concubinae suae multa peragratus ego terraque marique debitum reddidi in patria nunc situs hic iaceo stat lapis et nomen vestigia nulla\n
EDCS-16100255.translation: Caius Utius, son of Spurius, ordered by his will that this be done for himself, for Publius Utis, his brother, and for Clodia Fasta, his concubine. Having traveled much through both land and sea, I have now paid the debt owed and lie here in my homeland. This stone stands, and my name, but no trace of me.
EDCS-16100255.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Caius Utius in his will for himself, his brother, and his concubine, reflecting his travels and final resting place, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100256.text_edition: \n Dianae et Silvano et Mercurio Augustis sacrum Ianuarius Cai Honorati servus posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100256.translation: Sacred to the August Gods Diana, Silvanus, and Mercury. Ianuarius, slave of Caius Honoratus, placed this willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-16100256.summary: This is a religious dedication to Diana, Silvanus, and Mercury by Ianuarius, a slave of Caius Honoratus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200948.translation: For Julius P., a wool merchant. Eutyches.
EDCS-16200948.summary: This is a dedication to Julius P., a wool merchant, by Eutyches, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16800443.text_edition: \n Augustae Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius vir clarissimus praeses provinciae Dalmatiae clementiae eius semper dicatissimus\n
EDCS-16800443.translation: Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, most illustrious man and governor of the province of Dalmatia, always most devoted to her clemency.
EDCS-16800443.summary: This is a dedication to an Augusta (likely an empress) by a provincial governor, Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, probably from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16900021.text_edition: \n Quintus Marcius Quinti filius Cornelia Bassus domo Benevento miles legionis VII annorum stipendiorum XVII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit ex arbitratu Maximi\n
EDCS-16900021.translation: Quintus Marcius Bassus, son of Quintus, of the Cornelia tribe, from Benevento, a soldier of the Seventh Legion with 17 years of service, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by his will, according to the judgment of Maximu.
EDCS-16900021.summary: This is a soldier's tomb inscription, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE, recording the details of Quintus Marcius Bassus and his burial arrangements.

EDCS-16900065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Messoriano filio infelicissimo ex collegio Veneris defunctus Aterno qui vixit annos XXXV Aelius Anianus et Aurelia Prima obsequentissimo titulum posuerunt\n
EDCS-16900065.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelius Anianus and Aurelia Prima set up this monument to their most unfortunate son Aternus, who died at age 35, from the collegium of Venus, to their most obedient son.
EDCS-16900065.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aternus, set up by his parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17000067.text_edition: \n Iovi Fulminatori Titus Aurelius Quintus posuit\n
EDCS-17000067.translation: Titus Aurelius Quintus dedicated this to Jupiter the Thunderer.
EDCS-17000067.summary: This is a simple dedication to Jupiter Fulminator by Titus Aurelius Quintus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20401672.text_edition: \n Sexto Servilio Sexti filio Titiano infelici defuncto nnum XVIII mensium III dierum IIII Aelia Maxima infelicissima mater sibi et filio piissimo posuit\n
EDCS-20401672.translation: For Sextus Servilius Titianus, unfortunate, who died at 18 months, 3 days, and 4 hours. Aelia Maxima, most unfortunate mother, set this up for herself and her most devoted son.
EDCS-20401672.summary: This is a poignant funerary epitaph for a very young child, Sextus Servilius Titianus, set up by his mother Aelia Maxima, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20401678.text_edition: \n Stenio Hermeti marito bene merenti Charite coniunx\n
EDCS-20401678.translation: Charite, his wife, to her husband Stenius Hermetus, who deserved well.
EDCS-20401678.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a wife, Charite, to her deserving husband, Stenius Hermetus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20601174.text_edition: \n Caius Licinius Cai filius Fabia domo Sinope veteranus legionis VII annorum L stipendiorum XXVII hic situs est heredes posuerunt\n
EDCS-20601174.translation: Caius Licinius Primus, son of Caius, of the Fabia tribe, from Sinope, a veteran of the Seventh Legion with 50 years of age and 27 years of service, is buried here; his heirs set this up.
EDCS-20601174.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a veteran of the Seventh Legion, Caius Licinius Primus, detailing his origins, age, and service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20601175.text_edition: \n Lucius Luc Cai filius Primus veteranus legionis XV Apollinaris hic situs est testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-20601175.translation: Lucius Luc Primus, son of Lucius, a veteran of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-20601175.summary: This is a soldier's tomb inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording the burial arrangements of veteran Lucius Luc Primus of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion.

EDCS-20601176.text_edition: \n elius Ma P domo R veteranus legionis VII stipendiorum XXVIII annos LXII filio et Patrono V posuit matri\n
EDCS-20601176.translation: Elisu Ma P, from R, a veteran of the Seventh Legion with 28 years of service and 62 years of age, set this up for his son and Patron, and for his mother.
EDCS-20601176.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, set up by a veteran of the Seventh Legion for his son, Patron, and mother.

EDCS-20601177.text_edition: \n AGA eques cohortis I Belgarum armorum custos tur(ma) Maximi annorum? stipendiorum XXIII \n
EDCS-20601177.translation: A knight of the First Cohort of Belgae, armor bearer of the turma of Maximu, with 23 years of service.
EDCS-20601177.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording the career details of a cavalryman (eques) in the First Cohort of Belgae, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20601178.text_edition: \n Tures Alburi filius eques cohortis I Bracaraugustanorum nnorum XXV stipendiorum VII hic situs est heres posuit\n
EDCS-20601178.translation: Tures, son of Alburius, a cavalryman of the 1st Cohort of Bracaraugustani, aged 25, with 7 years of service, lies here; his heir erected this.
EDCS-20601178.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tures, a cavalryman, erected by his heir, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-20601179.text_edition: \n Veranus Caturonis filius eques cohortis I Bracaraugustanorum turma Tironis nnnorum XXXIIX stipendiorum XVIII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit heredes posuerunt Tiro decurio et Crispus Caturonis\n
EDCS-20601179.translation: Veranus, son of Caturonus, a cavalryman of the 1st Cohort of Bracaraugustani, in the troop of Tiro, aged 38, with 18 years of service, lies here; he ordered this to be made in his will. His heirs erected this: Tiro, a decurion, and Crispus, son of Caturonus.
EDCS-20601179.summary: A funerary epitaph for Veranus, a cavalryman, detailing his age, service, and erection by his heirs, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-23801439.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that seems to record a dedication or funerary request for a person named Stratonice.

EDCS-24200355.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Maximae Publius Bennius Priscianus miles cohortis VIII voluntariorum tibicem sic consularis hospitae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-24200355.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Bennius Priscianus, a soldier of the 8th Volunteer Cohort, erected this for Valeria Maxima, a consular musician, his respected guest.
EDCS-24200355.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Maxima, a musician, erected by Publius Bennius Priscianus, a soldier, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200356.text_edition: \n Sexto Alvio sic Procusio annorum L Aufidia Maxima coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-24200356.translation: To Sextus Alvius Procus, aged 50. Aufidia Maxima erected this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-24200356.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextus Alvius Procus, erected by his wife Aufidia Maxima, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200357.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lusidenius Glicerus sic delicato suo bene merenti posuit Lusidenius Glicerus sic fecit Secundae filiae suae\n
EDCS-24200357.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lusidenius Glicerus erected this for his beloved one, his well-deserving servant. Lusidenius Glicerus made this for his daughter Secunda.
EDCS-24200357.summary: A funerary epitaph with a dedication to a beloved person and a second dedication to a daughter named Secunda, erected by Lusidenius Glicerus, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200358.translation: Aemilia Aprulla for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-24200358.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed husband, erected by his wife Aemilia Aprulla, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fabiae Vrsae coniugi incomparabili quae vixit nnos XXXVII Basileus maritus posuit\n
EDCS-24200359.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Fabia Ursa, his incomparable wife, who lived 37 years. Her husband Basileus erected this.
EDCS-24200359.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fabia Ursa, an "incomparable" wife, erected by her husband Basileus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200360.text_edition: \n Lucio Statio T IIIIIIviro Lucius Statius Luci libertus \n
EDCS-24200360.translation: To Lucius Statius, duovir. Lucius, freedman of Lucius, [erected this].
EDCS-24200360.summary: A dedication to Lucius Statius, a duovir, likely erected by his freedman, dating to the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-24200362.translation: Quintus Cominius Martialis erected this for his wife.
EDCS-24200362.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed wife, erected by Quintus Cominius Martialis, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-24200364.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus infelicissimo infanti Valerio Maximiano qui vixit annis II mensibus VI diebus XXVII VLVS Valerius Veer filio \n
EDCS-24200364.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Valerius Maximianus, an unluckiest infant, who lived 2 years, 6 months, and 27 days. Valerius Veer placed [this] for his son.
EDCS-24200364.summary: A funerary epitaph for an infant named Valerius Maximianus, dedicated by his father Valerius Veer.

EDCS-24200370.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Pomponio Barbario Respectus amico \n
EDCS-24200370.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Pomponius Barbarus Respectus, a friend.
EDCS-24200370.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Pomponius Barbarus Respectus, described as a friend.

EDCS-24200371.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Titio Priscino aedili IIviro annorum XXXIII mensium VII dierum VIII Laetilia Fructa mater filio pientissimo fecit\n
EDCS-24200371.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Gaius Titius Priscinus, formerly aedile and duumvir, aged 33 years, 7 months, and 8 days. Laetilia Fructa, his mother, made [this] for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-24200371.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Titius Priscinus, a former magistrate, dedicated by his devoted mother.

EDCS-24400333.text_edition: \n Caius Fulvius Cai filius Voltinia Philippis miles legionis XI nnum XXX stipendiorum X\n
EDCS-24400333.translation: Gaius Fulvius Philippis, son of Gaius, of the Voltinian tribe, a soldier of the 11th Legion, aged 30 years, with 10 years of service.
EDCS-24400333.summary: A military diploma or epitaph recording the details of a soldier named Gaius Fulvius Philippis of the 11th Legion.

EDCS-24400345.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci filius Voltinia domo Philippis miles legionis VII nnorum XXXV stipendiorum XV hic situs est testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-24400345.translation: Lucius Valerius Philippis, son of Lucius, of the Voltinian tribe, a soldier of the 7th Legion, aged 35 years, with 15 years of service, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-24400345.summary: An epitaph for Lucius Valerius Philippis, a soldier of the 7th Legion, who specified its construction in his will.

EDCS-24400840.translation: For Sarmentius Geminus, to the holy god Sarapis.
EDCS-24400840.summary: A dedication to the god Sarapis by Sarmentius Geminus.

EDCS-24400841.text_edition: \n Isidi Serapi Libero Liberae voto suscepto pro salute Scapulae filii sui Publius Quinctius Paris solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-24400841.translation: Publius Quinctius Paris willingly and deservedly fulfilled a vow undertaken for the health of his son Scapula, to Isis, Serapis, Liber, and Libera.
EDCS-24400841.summary: A votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow for the health of a son, dedicated to several deities by Publius Quinctius Paris.

EDCS-24400842.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Magnio Victori Lucius Cluentius Cornelianus conservo \n
EDCS-24400842.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Magnus Victor. Lucius Cluentius Cornelianus [placed this] for a fellow slave.
EDCS-24400842.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Magnus Victor, dedicated by a fellow slave named Lucius Cluentius Cornelianus.

EDCS-24600956.text_edition: \n Aureliae Messiae infelicissimae quae vixit nnos XXXV Signius Lupus maritus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-24600956.translation: For Aurelia Messia, most unfortunate, who lived 35 years. Signius Lupus, her husband, placed [this] for her who deserved well.
EDCS-24600956.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Messia, dedicated by her husband Signius Lupus.

EDCS-24900088.text_edition: \n Quintus Vibius Luci filius Dianae votum solvit eisdem aram de suo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-24900088.translation: Quintus Vibius, son of Lucius, fulfilled a vow to Diana and arranged for an altar to be made from his own resources.
EDCS-24900088.summary: A votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow and the construction of an altar to Diana by Quintus Vibius.

EDCS-24900089.text_edition: \n Publius Annaeus Quinti libertus Epicadus aedem Leiberi Patrus faciundam coiravit lubens merito\n
EDCS-24900089.translation: Publius Annaeus Epicadus, freedman of Quintus, willingly and deservedly undertook the construction of the temple of Liber Pater.
EDCS-24900089.summary: This is a dedication of a temple to Liber Pater by a freedman, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24900091.text_edition: \n Quintus Safinius Quinti filius Sextus Marius Luci libertus magistri Naronae Quintus Marcius Quinti filius Publius Annaeus Quinti libertus Epicadus quaestores turrim faciendam coiraverunt\n
EDCS-24900091.translation: Quintus Safinius Sextus, son of Quintus, Lucius Marius, freedman of Marius, the magistrates of Narona, Quintus Marcius, son of Quintus, and Publius Annaeus Epicadus, freedman of Quintus, undertook the construction of the tower.
EDCS-24900091.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tower by local magistrates in Narona, probably dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24900124.text_edition: \n Turus Patalius Granp Opiavi filius Venetus Lastimeis Hospolis filius praetores murum locaverunt longum pedes CXI altum pedes XX eisdem probaverunt\n
EDCS-24900124.translation: Taurus Patalius, son of Granpus, from Opiavia, a Venetian, and Lastimeis Hospolis, son of Lastimeis, the praetors, contracted for a wall 111 feet long and 20 feet high, and approved it for themselves.
EDCS-24900124.summary: This inscription details a contract for the construction and approval of a wall by two praetors, likely dating to the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-25002168.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Divi filio Sicilia recepta Caius Papius Celsus Marcus Papius Kanus fratres\n
EDCS-25002168.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the Divine [Augustus], upon the recovery of Sicily, Caius Papius Celsus and Marcus Papius Kanus, brothers.
EDCS-25002168.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caesar after the recovery of Sicily, made by two brothers, Caius Papius Celsus and Marcus Papius Kanus, likely dating to the late Republic or early Principate.

EDCS-26001142.translation: Lucius Caicilius [built/commissioned] the wall of the tower.
EDCS-26001142.summary: This is a very brief inscription recording Lucius Caicilius's involvement with a wall and tower, likely a dedication or ownership marker from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600639.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus bene merenti Festio sic patri qui vixit annos CX Conse sic filia posuit\n
EDCS-26600639.translation: To the spirits of the departed, his well-deserving father who lived 110 years, thus his daughter erected [this monument].
EDCS-26600639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a father who lived to 110 years old, erected by his daughter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600640.text_edition: \n [] Cai liberta Fortunata annorum hic sita est XIIX si pietas prodest <c=Q>uiquam vixisse modeste vos precor o Manes sit mihi terra levis libertas cui olim fuerat promissa est ante ditis sub fatum venit in arbitrium vivite felices quibus est Fortuna superstes spenque sic meam oppressit fatus in Hiluricum sic \n
EDCS-26600640.translation: [] Fortunata, freedwoman of Caius, aged 18, is buried here. If kindness benefits anyone, I beg you, O shades, that living modestly might have been of use. May the earth lie lightly on me. Freedom, which was once promised to me, was granted before fate came under the judgment of the underworld. Live happily, you for whom Fortune remains. Thus fate oppressed my hope in Illyricum.
EDCS-26600640.summary: This is a poignant funerary epitaph of a young freedwoman named Fortunata, lamenting her unfulfilled promise of freedom and praying for a light earth, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600641.text_edition: \n Lusia Gemellina filia Lusio Gemellino patri pio fecit\n
EDCS-26600641.translation: Lusia Gemellina made [this] for her devoted father, Lusias Gemellinus.
EDCS-26600641.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a daughter, Lusia Gemellina, for her pious father, Lusias Gemellinus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600642.text_edition: \n Haline nnorum XV Sextus Lusius Leonas coniug[i ------\n
EDCS-26600642.translation: Halina, aged 15, [and] Sextus Lusius Leonas for his wife [---]
EDCS-26600642.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph recording the death of a young woman named Halina and mentioning Sextus Lusius Leonas, likely her husband or relative, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600643.text_edition: \n Iulia Rufina mater infeli<c=G>issimae filiae Viviae Valeriae quae vixit annos duos menses quattuor dies novem \n
EDCS-26600643.translation: Iulia Rufina, mother of her most unhappy daughter Vivia Valeria, who lived two years, four months, and nine days.
EDCS-26600643.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a very young child, Vivia Valeria, who died after living just over two years, erected by her grieving mother, Iulia Rufina, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600644.text_edition: \n ] Marciae Maritimae Nicephorus Aprilis Augusti liberti servus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600644.translation: To Marcia Maritima, Nicephorus, freedman of Aprilis Augusti, to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-26600644.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a freedman to his deserving wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-26600645.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a woman named Domatia Prima, likely Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-26600646.text_edition: \n Lucius Pomponius Spuri filius Longinus ex testamento situs\n
EDCS-26600646.translation: Lucius Pomponius Longinus, son of Spurius, buried from his will.
EDCS-26600646.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial arrangements made in the will of Lucius Pomponius Longinus, likely Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-26600647.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Primus Phileroti filio bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600647.translation: Dedicated to the Divine Shades. Primus to his well-deserving son, Phileros.
EDCS-26600647.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Primus to his deserving son, Phileros, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600648.text_edition: \n Satrius Secundus Primitivae coniugi pientissimae quae vixit nnos XVII menses VII dies X\n
EDCS-26600648.translation: Satrius Secundus to his most devoted wife, Primitiva, who lived 17 years, 7 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-26600648.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Primitiva, dedicated by her devoted husband Satrius Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600649.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Saturio coniugi Titiae Titi filiae\n
EDCS-26600649.translation: Dedicated to the Divine Shades. To Saturius, husband of Titia, daughter of Titus.
EDCS-26600649.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Saturius, the husband of Titia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600650.text_edition: \n Caius Amerinus Publi libertus Latinus situs\n
EDCS-26600650.translation: Caius Amerinus Latinus, freedman of Publius, buried.
EDCS-26600650.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Amerinus Latinus, a freedman, likely Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-26600652.text_edition: \n Augusto sacrum Caius Iulius Macrini libertus Martialis IIIIIIvir magister Mercurialis ob honorem idem ludos scaenicos per triduum dedit et cantharum argenteum pondo |Sunciarum septem\n
EDCS-26600652.translation: Sacred to Augustus. Caius Iulius Martialis, freedman of Macrinus, a IIIIvir and magister Mercurialis, for the honor, gave stage games for three days and a silver goblet weighing seven ounces.
EDCS-26600652.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Augustus by Caius Iulius Martialis, a local magistrate, who funded games and donated a silver goblet, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-26600654.text_edition: \n Divo Augusto sacrum Quintus Sextilius Corinthus Caius Stertinius Synegdemus Lucius Vibius Amaranthus Lucius Aquillius Aptus Lucius Titusidius Chryseros Caius Valerius Herma IIIIIIviri magistri Mercuriales ob honorem\n
EDCS-26600654.translation: Sacred to the Divine Augustus. Quintus Sextilius Corinthus, Caius Stertinius Synegdemus, Lucius Vibius Amaranthus, Lucius Aquillius Aptus, Lucius Titusidius Chryseros, Caius Valerius Herma, the IIIIviri and magistri Mercuriales, for the honor.
EDCS-26600654.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Divine Augustus by six local magistrates, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-26600656.text_edition: \n Dianae Nemorensi sacrum Tiberius Claudius Claudianus praefectus cohortis I Bracaraugustanorum ex voto suscepto de suo\n
EDCS-26600656.translation: Sacred to Diana of Nemi. Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, prefect of the I Cohort of Bracaraugustani, according to a vow undertaken at his own expense.
EDCS-26600656.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana Nemorensis by Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, a military officer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600657.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Aeterno Titus Flavius Magnus ex voto\n
EDCS-26600657.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Eternal, Titus Flavius Magnus, according to his vow.
EDCS-26600657.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter, offered by Titus Flavius Magnus.

EDCS-26600658.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum Severi et Antonini Auggustorum et Getae nobilissimi Caesaris et Iuliae Augustae matris Auggustorum et castrorum Gaius Statius Tacitianus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit libens merito Pompeiano et Avito consulibus\n
EDCS-26600658.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti, and of Geta, the most noble Caesar, and of Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperors and of the camps, Gaius Statius Tacitianus, a military assistant of a consular governor of the 14th Legion Gemina, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly, with Pompeianus and Avitus as consuls.
EDCS-26600658.summary: This is a votive inscription for the safety of the imperial family, dedicated to Jupiter by a military assistant of the 14th Legion Gemina, dated by the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus.

EDCS-26600659.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Patron of Cohorts.
EDCS-26600659.summary: A brief dedication to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Patron of Cohorts.

EDCS-26600660.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Iovi Optimo Maximo aeterno sacrum Claudius Marcus factus ex optione beneficiarius Senecionis consularis \n
EDCS-26600660.translation: Sacred to the holy god Jupiter, Best and Greatest, eternal. Claudius Marcus, having been appointed by choice as a military assistant to Senecio, the consular governor, dedicated this.
EDCS-26600660.summary: A dedication to the god Jupiter, Best and Greatest, eternal, made by Claudius Marcus, a military assistant.

EDCS-26600661.text_edition: \n Quintus Pliseniius Severianus |(centurio) legionis XI Claudiae templum Liberi Patris vetustate corruptum porticibus adiectis restituit\n
EDCS-26600661.translation: Quintus Plisenius Severianus, a centurion of the 11th Claudian Legion, restored the temple of Liber Pater, damaged by age, by adding porticoes.
EDCS-26600661.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Liber Pater by Quintus Plisenius Severianus, a centurion of the 11th Legion.

EDCS-26600662.text_edition: \n Templum Liberi Patris et Liberae vetustate dilabsum sic restituit cohors I Belgarum adiectis porticibus curam agente Flavio Victore |(centurione) legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis Severo et Pompeiano II consulibus\n
EDCS-26600662.translation: The cohort of the 1st Belgae restored the temple of Liber Pater and Libera, which had fallen into disrepair due to age, by adding porticoes, under the care of Flavius Victor, a centurion of the 1st Legion Adiutrix, Pious and Faithful, with Severus and Pompeianus II as consuls.
EDCS-26600662.summary: This inscription details the restoration of a temple to Liber Pater and Libera by the 1st Cohort of Belgae, under the supervision of a centurion of the 1st Legion Adiutrix, dated by the consulship of Severus and Pompeianus II.

EDCS-26600665.text_edition: \n Marcus Lusius Trofimas Marci libertus IIIIIIvir magister Mercurialis ob honorem\n
EDCS-26600665.translation: Marcus Lusius Trofimas, freedman of Marcus, an officer of the IIIIvirate and a master of the guild of Mercuriales, for the sake of his office.
EDCS-26600665.summary: This records Marcus Lusius Trofimas, a freedman and local official, in connection with his magistracy.

EDCS-26600666.text_edition: \n A(ulus) Obultronius Grat(us) Publius nnus Stamitus Lucius Battius Lucius Safinius Quartus Caius Licinius Telamo seviri magistri Mercuriales aere accepto libentes a in solo privato aedem aidificaverunt de sua pecunia faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600666.translation: Aulus Obultronius Gratus, Publius... Stamitus, Lucius Battius, Lucius Safinius Quartus, Gaius Licinius Telamo, six men who were masters of the guild of Mercuriales, having received payment, willingly built a shrine on private land and took care that it be built with their own money.
EDCS-26600666.summary: This inscription records the construction of a shrine to Mercury on private land by six local officials and guild masters, funded by themselves.

EDCS-26600667.translation: Titus Haterius Hilarus, for the sake of his office.
EDCS-26600667.summary: This records Titus Haterius Hilarus in relation to his holding an office or magistracy.

EDCS-26600669.text_edition: \n Thermas rei publicae hiemales rogante populo in ruinam conlapsas Marcus Aurelius Valerius vir perfectissimus ducenarius ex protectoribus lateris divini de frugalitate sua item atque suorum saxis et caementis denuo aedificavit et lavantes rei publicae tradidit epulum quoque civibus suis ea die praebuit Messala et Grato consulibus dedicante Marco Aurelio Tiberiano viro perfectissimo praeside provinciae Delmatiae sic\n
EDCS-26600669.translation: Marcus Aurelius Valerius, a man of the highest rank, a ducenarius from the divine imperial bodyguards, rebuilt the public winter baths, which had collapsed into ruin at the people's request, from his own resources and those of his family, with stone and mortar, and handed them over to the public for bathing, and also provided a banquet for his fellow citizens on that day, with Messala and Gratus as consuls, dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Tiberianus, a man of the highest rank, governor of the province of Dalmatia, as follows:
EDCS-26600669.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of public winter baths by Marcus Aurelius Valerius, a high-ranking official, and his subsequent dedication of them, dated by the consulship of Messala and Gratus.

EDCS-26600670.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemilio Forti beneficiario cohortis VIII voluntariorum Lucius Aemilius Hermes IIIIIIvir et Aemilia Saturnina patrono bene merenti sib<i=E> et suis et posterisque aeorum sic \n
EDCS-26600670.translation: To the divine shades. Lucius Aemilius Hermes, a member of the college of six, and Aemilia Saturnina erected this for their well-deserving patron Aemilius Fortis, a beneficiary of the Eighth Cohort of Volunteers, and for themselves, their family, and their descendants.
EDCS-26600670.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Aemilius Fortis by Lucius Aemilius Hermes and Aemilia Saturnina, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600673.text_edition: \n Marcus Heredius Marci filius Palatina veteranus legionis VII hic situs est fecit Faustus libertus\n
EDCS-26600673.translation: Marcus Heredius, son of Marcus, of the Palatine tribe, a veteran of the Seventh Legion, lies here. Faustus, his freedman, made this.
EDCS-26600673.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Heredius, a veteran of the Seventh Legion, erected by his freedman Faustus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600674.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Magius Cnaei filius Palatina veteranus situs\n
EDCS-26600674.translation: Gnaeus Magius, son of Gnaeus, of the Palatine tribe, a veteran, lies here.
EDCS-26600674.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gnaeus Magius, a veteran, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600675.text_edition: \n patrono iterum itenque! sacerdoti et Gaio Statio Verecundo e Quintus Umbrius \n
EDCS-26600675.translation: Quintus Umbrius [erected this] for his patron, again and repeatedly a priest, and for Gaius Stasius Verecundus.
