EDCS-26600675.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication by Quintus Umbrius to his patron, who held priestly and repeat priestly offices, and to Gaius Stasius Verecundus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600676.text_edition: \n Publio Annaeo seviro magistro Mercuriali Lucio Lacutulano Luci liberto P Lucio Lacutulano patri Lacutulanae Maximae Annaeae Cai filiae Secundae Lacutulana Luci filia viva sibi et suis\n
EDCS-26600676.translation: Publius Annaeus, a sevirus and Mercurialis magistratus, and Lucius Lacutulanus, freedman of Lucius, for their father Lucius Lacutulanus; and Lacutulana Maxima, daughter of Annaeus Secundus, and Lacutulana, daughter of Lucius, while alive, [erected this] for themselves and their families.
EDCS-26600676.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Publius Annaeus and Lucius Lacutulanus for their father, and by Lacutulana Maxima and Lacutulana for themselves and their families, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600677.translation: To Lucius Calius Fuscus, colleague of the guild of carpenters.
EDCS-26600677.summary: This is likely a dedication or memorial for Lucius Calius Fuscus, a member of the carpenters' guild, possibly dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600678.text_edition: \n Caius Catellius Cai libertus Stabilis Caius Catellius Cai libertus Licinus IIIIIIviri testamento fieri iusserunt\n
EDCS-26600678.translation: Gaius Catellius Stabilis, freedman of Gaius, and Gaius Catellius Licinus, freedman of Gaius, four times members of the college of six, ordered this to be made in their will.
EDCS-26600678.summary: This funerary epitaph states that Gaius Catellius Stabilis and Gaius Catellius Licinus, both freedmen and members of the college of six, commissioned the monument in their wills, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-26600679.text_edition: \n o Luci liberto Hyllo ? IIIIII Augustali Maximae coniugi eius Turbo aedilis IIIIvir testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-26600679.translation: Turbo, an aedile and four times Augustalis, ordered this to be made in his will for Lucius Hyllo, his freedman, and for Maxima, his wife.
EDCS-26600679.summary: This inscription records an order made in the will of Turbo, an aedile and Augustalis official, to erect a monument for his freedman Lucius Hyllo and his wife Maxima, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27400524.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Proculo Kapitonis defuncto annorum XXVI Aurelia Severina marito et Aelia Proculina patri pientissimo pro pietate posuerunt\n
EDCS-27400524.translation: To the divine shades. Aurelia Severina erected this for her most dutiful husband Aelius Proculus Kapiton, who died at the age of twenty-six, and Aelia Proculina for her most dutiful father.
EDCS-27400524.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelius Proculus Kapiton, erected by his wife Aurelia Severina and daughter Aelia Proculina, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27400525.translation: [---] Flavia Gentilis, daughter of Flavia Aspr[---]
EDCS-27400525.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, possibly part of a family record or funerary monument, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27400526.text_edition: \n Titus Aurelius Marcus decurio municipii defunctus Salona annorum L Au<r=I>(elii) Albanus LIus Lu<p=I>us patri pientissimo posuerunt \n
EDCS-27400526.translation: To Titus Aurelius Marcus, a town councillor of the municipality, who died aged 50. Aurelii Albanus and Lucius erected this for their most devoted father.
EDCS-27400526.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by sons for their deceased father, Titus Aurelius Marcus, a decurion. The approximate date is inferable from the names and context.

EDCS-27400527.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Quintiliano decurioni municipii Malvesatii IIvirali defuncto nnorum LXV Publii Aelii Maximus et Silvanus et Tattaia patri pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-27400527.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Maximus, Silvanus, and Tattaia erected this for their most devoted father, Publius Aelius Quintilianus, a town councillor and former duovir of the municipality of Malvesati, who died aged 65.
EDCS-27400527.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Aelius Quintilianus, a former duovir and decurion, and was set up by his sons and daughter. He died aged 65.

EDCS-27400544.text_edition: \n Lucius Gaviarius augur IIvir quinquennalis decurionum decreto murum reficiendum coeravit idemque probavit\n
EDCS-27400544.translation: Lucius Gaviarius, as augur and quinquennial duovir, by decree of the councillors, undertook the repair of the wall and approved it himself.
EDCS-27400544.summary: This inscription records Lucius Gaviarius, an augur and quinquennial duovir, who financed and approved the repair of a wall by decree of the town councillors.

EDCS-27400546.text_edition: \n Flavia Cai filia libens posuit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400546.translation: Flavia, daughter of Caius, willingly erected this; the site was granted by decree of the councillors.
EDCS-27400546.summary: This is a dedication by Flavia, daughter of Caius, for a site granted by decree of the town councillors. The purpose is unspecified.

EDCS-27400547.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Flaviae Eutiae pie<n=AI>tissimae quae vixit annos plus minus XXX Epidius Filippus sic maritus posuit \n
EDCS-27400547.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavia Eutia, most devoted, who lived about 30 years. Epidius Filippus, her husband, erected this. Dedicated under the axe.
EDCS-27400547.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Flavia Eutia, who lived about 30 years, and was set up by her husband, Epidius Filippus.

EDCS-27400548.text_edition: \n Tito Aurelio Sextiano equiti Romano decurioni municipii S Titus Aurelius Lupercus filio pientissimo in memoriam posuit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400548.translation: Titus Aurelius Lupercus erected this for his most devoted son, Titus Aurelius Sextianus, a Roman knight and town councillor of the municipality of S[---]. The site was granted by decree of the councillors.
EDCS-27400548.summary: This is a memorial inscription set up by a father for his deceased son, Titus Aurelius Sextianus, a Roman knight and decurion, with the site granted by decree of the town councillors.

EDCS-27400549.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Dexter et Amavilis! vivi sibi posuerunt et Masimillae! filiae quae vixit annos XXV\n
EDCS-27400549.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Dexter and Amavilis erected this for themselves while alive, and for their daughter Masimilla, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-27400549.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Dexter and Amavilis for themselves and their deceased daughter, Masimilla, who died at 25.

EDCS-27400550.text_edition: \n Sexto Cipio Cai filio Sergia Firminiano Gaius Cipius Aper pater titulum poni iussit et epulo dedicari Gaius Cipius Aper consobrinus heres posuit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400550.translation: Gaius Cipius Aper, the father, ordered this tablet to be set up and the feast to be dedicated. Gaius Cipius Aper, the cousin and heir, erected it. The site was granted by decree of the councillors.
EDCS-27400550.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument and dedication of a feast for Sextus Cipius, son of Caius, by his father and cousin/heir, with the site granted by decree of the councillors.

EDCS-27400551.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27400551.text_edition: \n Caio Clodio Cai filio Sergia Vitellino IIviro iure dicundo iudici ex V decuriis equo publico huic defuncto ordo decurionum locum sepulturae inpensam funeris decrevit\n
EDCS-27400551.translation: For Caius Clodius, son of Caius, who died while serving as duovir with jurisdiction and judge from the five decuries on public horse. The council of decurions decreed a burial place and funeral expenses for him.
EDCS-27400551.summary: This inscription records the posthumous grant of a burial place and funeral expenses by the town council for Caius Clodius, who had served as duovir and judge.

EDCS-27400552.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caesonia Nardis Caesonio Hermeti pientissimo coniugi viva fecit nnorum XLV et suis omnibus sub ascia dedicavit\n
EDCS-27400552.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caesonia Nardis made this for her most devoted husband, Caesonius Hermetes, aged 45, and for all her family, and dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-27400552.summary: This funerary inscription was dedicated by Caesonia Nardis for her husband, Caesonius Hermetes, aged 45, and their entire family.

EDCS-27400553.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Clodiae Euphrosynae nnorum XXIIII Clodius Euphrosynus et Clodia Frequentilla parentes vivi fecerunt et Clodio Eucarpo magistro\n
EDCS-27400553.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Clodia Euphrosyna, aged 24. Clodius Euphrosynus and Clodia Frequentilla, her parents, erected this while alive, and Clodius Eucarpus, magistrate.
EDCS-27400553.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Clodia Euphrosyna, erected by her parents, and mentions a magistrate, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-27400554.text_edition: \n Sextus Publius Gracilis annorum LX Quintus Publius Gracilis filius annorum XX Marcus Aemilius Celer annorum XXV hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-27400554.translation: Sextus Publius Gracilis, aged 60. Quintus Publius Gracilis, his son, aged 20. Marcus Aemilius Celer, aged 25. Here they lie.
EDCS-27400554.summary: This epitaph records the burial of three individuals: Sextus Publius Gracilis, his son Quintus Publius Gracilis, and Marcus Aemilius Celer, with their ages, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400555.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Statiliae Ianuariae coniugi dulcissimae Publius Lurius Stephanus amoris mei S pientis\n
EDCS-27400555.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. To Statilia Ianuaria, my sweetest wife. Publius Lurius Stephanus, mindful of my love.
EDCS-27400555.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Statilia Ianuaria by her grieving husband, Publius Lurius Stephanus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400556.text_edition: \n Quintus Valerius Luci filius Quadratus nnorum LX hic situs est\n
EDCS-27400556.translation: Quintus Valerius Quadratus, son of Lucius, aged 60. Here he lies.
EDCS-27400556.summary: This simple epitaph records the burial of Quintus Valerius Quadratus and his age, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400557.text_edition: \n Quintus Manlius Quinti filius Sergia Rufus decurio iudex ex quinque decuriis equo publico testamento fieri iussit et epulo dedicari in hoc opus Statia Sexti filia Fida mater adiecit HS XXXVtriginta quinque milia et summae operi et epulo relictae XX fisco intulit HS XIIItredecim miliaCC solo publico\n
EDCS-27400557.translation: Quintus Valerius Quadratus, son of Lucius, aged 60. Here he lies.
EDCS-27400557.summary: This epitaph records that Quintus Manlius Rufus, son of Quintus, a decurion and judge, who was a member of the equestrian order, ordered this monument to be built by his will and to be dedicated with a feast, and Statia Fida, mother, daughter of Sextus, added 35,000 sesterces for the total cost of the monument and feast, and also left 13,200 sesterces for the treasury for the feast and on public land.

EDCS-27400559.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caesia.
EDCS-27400559.summary: This is a very short funerary epitaph to Caesia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400560.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iuliae Tertullae vixit annum I menses VIII Hordionius Cyranus pater\n
EDCS-27400560.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. For Julia Tertulla, who lived 1 year and 8 months. Hordionius Cyranus, her father.
EDCS-27400560.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Julia Tertulla at a young age and was erected by her father, Hordionius Cyranus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400561.text_edition: \n Publia Luci filia Saecunda sic annorum XXVI hic sita est\n
EDCS-27400561.translation: Publia Saecunda, daughter of Lucius, aged 26. Here she lies.
EDCS-27400561.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Publia Saecunda and her age, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400562.text_edition: \n Publio Lurio Urso vixit annos XX menses II \n
EDCS-27400562.translation: To Publius Lurius Ursus, who lived 20 years and 2 months.
EDCS-27400562.summary: This epitaph records the death of Publius Lurius Ursus at a young age, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400563.translation: To Luria, daughter of Publius, M.
EDCS-27400563.summary: This fragmented funerary epitaph records the name Luria, daughter of Publius, with an incomplete patronymic or cognomen, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400564.text_edition: \n Naeus Minucius Lentinus nnorum L hic situs est\n
EDCS-27400564.translation: Naeus Minucius Lentinus, aged [?], lies here.
EDCS-27400564.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Naeus Minucius Lentinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum mater Hordionia filio Hordionio carissimo qui vixit annos XXVII\n
EDCS-27400565.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Hordionia, his mother, to her dearest son Hordionius, who lived 27 years.
EDCS-27400565.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a mother to her son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400567.text_edition: \n Statio filio Sergia Valenti patri nnorum LXIII et Egnatiae Luci filiae Bucculae matri nnorum XXXXVII Valentinus et Crassus parentibus suis fecerunt\n
EDCS-27400567.translation: Valentinus and Crassus made this for their parents: for Sergia Valenti, father, 63 years old, and for Egnatiae Luci, daughter Buccula, mother, 47 years old.
EDCS-27400567.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Valentinus and Crassus for their parents, Sergia Valenti and Egnatia Buccula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400568.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Statiae Paulinae annorum VII\n
EDCS-27400568.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. For Statia Paulina, aged 7 years.
EDCS-27400568.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Statia Paulina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400571.text_edition: \n Diocas Diocaenu sic Cilix nnorum XXXV Lup bene merenti\n
EDCS-27400571.translation: For Diocas Diocaenu, a Cilician, aged 35, Lup, deserving of goodwill.
EDCS-27400571.summary: This funerary inscription is for Diocas Diocaenu, a Cilician, erected by Lup, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400572.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Caesius Licentinus vixit annos XXXX\n
EDCS-27400572.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Caesius Licentinus lived 40 years.
EDCS-27400572.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Caesius Licentinus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400573.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Caesius Niger qui vinxit et vixit L\n
EDCS-27400573.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Caesius Niger, who lived and existed 50 years.
EDCS-27400573.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Marcus Caesius Niger, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400575.text_edition: \n Marcus Plaetorius Marci filius Sergia hic ille situs est vixit annos XXV\n
EDCS-27400575.translation: Marcus Plaetorius, son of Marcus, of the Sergia tribe, lies here; he lived 25 years.
EDCS-27400575.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Marcus Plaetorius, a young man of the Sergia tribe, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400576.text_edition: \n Tifatiae Amatae Secunda filia matri nnum XXXXX\n
EDCS-27400576.translation: For Tifatiae Amata Secunda, daughter, to her mother, 50 years.
EDCS-27400576.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Tifatiae Amata Secunda to her mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400577.text_edition: \n Valerio Fortunato et Valerio Felici et Valeriae Crescentillae Valeria Finita parentibus bene merentibus\n
EDCS-27400577.translation: Valeria Finita for her well-deserving parents: for Valerius Fortunatus and Valerius Felix and Valeria Crescentilla.
EDCS-27400577.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Valeria Finita for her parents, Valerius Fortunatus, Valerius Felix, and Valeria Crescentilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400578.text_edition: \n Caio Egnatio Cai filio Sergia Marcello decurioni Iulia Tertulla mater Iulium Philinum et Iulium Crescentem libertos testamento ponere iussit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400578.translation: Julia Tertulla, mother, ordered by her will that her freedmen, Iulius Philinus and Iulius Crescentes, be set up. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27400578.summary: A funerary monument erected by Julia Tertulla for her freedmen, with the land granted by the local council, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-27400581.text_edition: \n Publio Cornelio Dolabellae consuli VIIviro epuloni sodali Titiensi legato pro praetore divi Augusti et Tiberi Caesaris Augusti civitates superioris [provinciae HIllyrici]\n
EDCS-27400581.translation: To Publius Cornelius Dolabella, consul, member of the VIIviri Epulones, sodalis Titiensis, legate pro praetore of the divine Augustus and Tiberius Caesar Augustus, the cities of the upper province of Illyricum.
EDCS-27400581.summary: An honorific inscription dedicated to Publius Cornelius Dolabella by the cities of Illyricum, highlighting his distinguished career, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-27400582.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Tromentina Osilliano Novia Bassilla mater et Novia Iustilla avia posuerunt et sportulis decurionibus Augustalibus et sexviris datis item pugilum spectaculo dedicaverunt huic universus ordo decurionatus honorem et locum statuae decrevit\n
EDCS-27400582.translation: Novia Bassilla, mother, and Novia Iustilla, grandmother, erected this for Publius Aelius Osillianus, son of Publius, of the Tromentina tribe. They dedicated it with distributions to the Augustal decurions and the six leaders of the city; likewise, with a boxing match. The entire order of decurions decreed this honor and a place for a statue to him.
EDCS-27400582.summary: A funerary and honorific monument dedicated to Publius Aelius Osillianus by his mother and grandmother, which included public games and a statue, likely from a Roman municipality in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-27400583.text_edition: \n Marco Pomentino Marci filio Tromentina Turboni IIviro iure dicundo et Marco Pomentino Boriae filio nnorum XVIII Pomentina Tertulla patri et fratri pientissimis fecit\n
EDCS-27400583.translation: Pomentina Tertulla made this for her most devoted father, Marcus Pomentinus Turbon, son of Marcus, IIvir for dispensing justice, and for her brother, Marcus Pomentinus, son of Boria, aged 18.
EDCS-27400583.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Pomentina Tertulla to her father, a local magistrate, and her brother, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-27400585.text_edition: \n Publius Vibius Publi filius Urbicus Publius Anulenus Bassus IIviri iure dicundo cisternam ex pecunia publica reficiendam curaverunt\n
EDCS-27400585.translation: Publius Vibius Urbicus, son of Publius, and Publius Anulenus Bassus, IIviri, oversaw the repair of the cistern at public expense.
EDCS-27400585.summary: A public works record showing two IIviri overseeing the repair of a cistern funded by the state, likely from a Roman municipality in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-27400586.text_edition: \n Exuperius Fortunius et Aelia Titia parentes pientissimi Exuperiae Fortuniae filiae carissimae memoriam fecerunt quae vixit annis XXVII menses tres dies XV si quis voluerit super hoc corpus ponere inferet rei publicae pondo argenti CXXV\n
EDCS-27400586.translation: Exuperius Fortunius and Aelia Titia, most devoted parents, made this memorial for their dearest daughter, Exuperia Fortunia, who lived 27 years, 3 months, and 15 days. If anyone wishes to place something upon this body, they shall pay 125 pounds of silver to the state.
EDCS-27400586.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating Exuperia Fortunia, with a penalty for disturbing the grave, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27400587.text_edition: \n Quintus Statius Laetus Quinto Statio Paquarius item Lucio Statio Clarif \n
EDCS-27400587.translation: To Quintus Staius Laetus, to Quintus Staius Paquarius, and also to Lucius Staius Clarif.
EDCS-27400587.summary: An incomplete inscription listing three individuals, likely a dedication or a family memorial, from the Roman period.

EDCS-27400588.text_edition: \n Conubii decus egregium lux alma parentum eximiumque bonum corporis atque animi invidia fati rapitur Vincentia florens et nunc ante patrem conditur Helionem quin potius corpus nam mens aeterna profecto pro meritis potitur sedibus Elysiis\n
EDCS-27400588.translation: An outstanding glory of marriage, a cherished light of parents, and an exceptional good of body and soul, is snatched away by the envy of fate, Vincentia, in her prime, and now is buried before her father. Helion, rather her body, for her soul undoubtedly enjoys eternal seats in the Elysian Fields according to her merits.
EDCS-27400588.summary: A poetic epitaph lamenting the death of Vincentia, praising her virtues and suggesting her soul's eternal rest, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27400589.text_edition: \n Lartidia Recepta Cai Lartidi Crispi |(centurionis) cohortis VIII voluntariorum nnorum XXVII patronus posuit hic sita est\n
EDCS-27400589.translation: Her patron erected this for Lartidia Recepta, daughter of Caius Lartidius Crispus, a centurion of the 8th cohort of volunteers, aged 27. She lies here.
EDCS-27400589.summary: A funerary inscription for Lartidia Recepta, daughter of a centurion, erected by her patron, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27400590.text_edition: \n Sabinae Hermetis Novi Persici et Noviae Donatae et Novio Hermae Sabinus per sic suis fecit <et=DE> Sabinino milite cohortis VIII voluntiariorum annorum XXII \n
EDCS-27400590.translation: Sabinus made this for his own people: Hermetis Novi Persici, Novia Donata, and Novius Hermas, and also for Sabinus, a soldier of the 8th cohort of volunteers, aged 22.
EDCS-27400590.summary: A funerary inscription erected by Sabinus for his family members and a fellow soldier, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27400591.text_edition: \n Votum militum Marco Trio Ti N\n
EDCS-27400591.translation: A vow by the soldiers of Marcus Trio, son of Titus.
EDCS-27400591.summary: This inscription records a military vow made by soldiers dedicated to Marcus Trio.

EDCS-27400592.text_edition: \n Luci filia Invitilla filio piissimo locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400592.translation: To her most devoted son, Invitilla, daughter of Lucius, was granted a place by decree of the town council.
EDCS-27400592.summary: This inscription records the allocation of a burial plot to a devoted son by a mother, following a decree of the local decurions.

EDCS-27400593.text_edition: \n lustratores us Placidus et unius Epidianus IIviri\n
EDCS-27400593.translation: Placidus and Epidianus, IIviri, were purifiers.
EDCS-27400593.summary: This inscription names two officials, Placidus and Epidianus, who held the office of IIviri and were responsible for a ritual purification.

EDCS-27400594.text_edition: \n Aeliae Cre quae CON IST defeci marit \n
EDCS-27400594.summary: This inscription is a fragment likely dedicated to Aelia Cre by her husband.

EDCS-27400595.text_edition: \n ESPRP us fecit sibi et suis enae Eglogeni V \n
EDCS-27400595.translation: ESPRP made this for himself and his family, from Eglogeni V.
EDCS-27400595.summary: This inscription records a person named ESPRP who dedicated a monument for himself and his family.

EDCS-27400596.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lusia Maxima Optatae aviae pientissimae posuit\n
EDCS-27400596.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lusia Maxima placed this for her most devoted grandmother, Optata.
EDCS-27400596.summary: This tombstone was erected by Lusia Maxima in memory of her devoted grandmother, Optata.

EDCS-27400597.text_edition: \n Publio Marcio Publi filio Tromentina Messiano Varius Festus heres ex testamento posuit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27400597.translation: Varius Festus, the heir according to the will, placed this for Publius Marcius Messianus, son of Publius, of the Tromentina tribe; a place was granted by decree of the town council.
EDCS-27400597.summary: This inscription records the burial of Publius Marcius Messianus, whose monument was erected by his heir according to his will, with the land granted by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-27400598.text_edition: \n Nymphio Cai Leppi Hilario consulari posuit\n
EDCS-27400598.translation: Nymphius placed this for Caius Leppius Hilarus, the consul.
EDCS-27400598.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Nymphius for Caius Leppius Hilarus, who held the consular office.

EDCS-27400599.text_edition: \n Tertia Ismarniensis nnorum XL hic sita est\n
EDCS-27400599.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Tertia Ismarniensis, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-27400600.text_edition: \n annorum XXV hic sita est nius posuit e suo contubernali suae bene merenti a se et te terra precor leviter super ossa residas sentiat ut pietas praemia quae meruit\n
EDCS-27400600.translation: Aged 25, lies here. [Name] placed this from his own funds for his well-deserving companion, and I beg you lightly rest upon her bones and feel the rewards she has earned through her devotion.
EDCS-27400600.summary: This tombstone, erected for a woman aged 25 by her male companion, includes a plea for her soul to rest peacefully and receive her deserved reward.

EDCS-27400601.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Epidiae infelicissimae defunctae nnorum XXX Publius Aelius Cassianus maritus coniugi pientissimae posuit\n
EDCS-27400601.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Aelius Cassianus erected this for his most dutiful wife, Aelia Epdia, most unfortunate, who died at the age of 30.
EDCS-27400601.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his deceased wife, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400602.text_edition: \n Lucio Calenio Apro Lucius Calenius Rufus pater filio nnorum V posuit\n
EDCS-27400602.translation: Lucius Calenius Rufus erected this for his son, Lucius Calenius Aper, who died at the age of 5.
EDCS-27400602.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a father for his young son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400603.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Severus se vivo sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-27400603.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Ulpius Severus made this for himself and his family while still alive.
EDCS-27400603.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating a self-funded burial plot for Marcus Ulpius Severus and his family, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400605.text_edition: \n Timotheo Lucio homini infelicissimo Fulvinia Tertia cognato inconparabili sic posuit\n
EDCS-27400605.translation: Fulvinia Tertia erected this for her incomparable kinsman, Timotheus Lucius, a most unfortunate man.
EDCS-27400605.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a relative for the deceased Timotheus Lucius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400606.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Spedia Ma<r=B>ce<l=I>lina Spedio Pau<l=I>ino annorum IX filio bene merenti titulum posuit \n
EDCS-27400606.translation: To the Divine Shades. Spedius Paulus, aged 9, a well-deserving son, is honored with this epitaph erected by Spedia Marcellina.
EDCS-27400606.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Spedius Paulus, erected by his mother, Spedia Marcellina, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400607.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Nobilis mater bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27400607.translation: To the Divine Shades. A mother erected this for her well-deserving Nobilis.
EDCS-27400607.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her deceased child, Nobilis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400608.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus <F=V>orbiae Aphrodisiae filiae sanctissimae Lucius Forbius Verinus pater \n
EDCS-27400608.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Forbius Verinus erected this for his most devoted daughter, Forbia Aphrodisia.
EDCS-27400608.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a father for his deceased daughter, Forbia Aphrodisia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500001.text_edition: \n S Fortunae Reduci Titus Flavius Sabinus beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27500001.translation: To Fortune the Returner. Titus Flavius Sabinus, a beneficiarius consularis of the I Italica legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27500001.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Fortuna Redux by a soldier of the I Italica legion, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27500002.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Atilius Gen miles legionis XIII geminae legionis I adiutricis beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27500002.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Caius Atilius Gen, a soldier of the XIII Gemina legion and a beneficiarius consularis of the I Adjutrix legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27500002.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a soldier holding dual legionary affiliations, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27500003.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii Novensium salvis civibus Novensibus Aurelius Donatus votum posuit\n
EDCS-27500003.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the municipality of Novensium, for the safety of the citizens of Novensium. Aurelius Donatus set up this vow.
EDCS-27500003.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and the Genius of Novensium by a citizen, Aurelius Donatus, for the well-being of the community, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500004.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii Novensium Caelius Saturninus beneficiarius consularis legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis Imperatore Lucio Septimio Severo Augusto bis consule\n
EDCS-27500004.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the Novensium municipality, Caelius Saturninus, a staff officer of a consular governor of the I Adjutrix legion, pious and loyal, under Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Augustus, during his second consulship.
EDCS-27500004.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and the local genius by a legionary officer, likely dating to the period of Septimius Severus' second consulship (c. 197-201 CE).

EDCS-27500005.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii Novensium Gaius Vibius Pomptina Ianuarius beneficiarius consularis legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens merito IIviris quinquennalibus Aurreliis Maximo et Anneo\n
EDCS-27500005.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the Novensium municipality, Gaius Vibius Pomptina Ianuarius, a staff officer of a consular governor of the I Adjutrix legion, gladly paid his vow as deserved, during the tenure of the duoviri quinquennales Aurelius Maximus and Annius.
EDCS-27500005.summary: This records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter and the local genius by a legionary officer, mentioning local magistrates and dating to the period when these officers held office.

EDCS-27500006.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Acutianus <b=R>eneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae Gordianae domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Aviola consulibus libens animo \n
EDCS-27500006.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Acutianus, a staff officer of a consular governor of the XIIII Gemina Gordiana legion, to our lord Gordian Augustus and [Aviola], during the consulship of [Silvanus] and Aviola, gladly and willingly.
EDCS-27500006.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Augustus by a legionary officer, mentioning Emperors Gordian and Aviola, likely dating to the consulship of Silvanus and Aviola (239 CE).

EDCS-27500008.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Medico Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate XXIV imperatori V consuli III patri patriae pecunia publica decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27500008.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 24th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, at public expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500008.summary: This text is an official dedication or acclamation for Emperor Marcus Aurelius, listing his titles and dignities, and noting it was funded by public money, likely dating to c. 187 CE.

EDCS-27500009.text_edition: \n Caius Appuleius Etruscus speculator veteranus legionis XI decurio allectus Salonae et Gaius Appuleius Etruscus filius annorum XIIII hic siti sunt Cuparia Lupula p(osuit)\n
EDCS-27500009.translation: Caius Appuleius Etruscus, a veteran speculator of the XI legion, a decurion promoted [to this rank], and Caius Appuleius Etruscus, his son aged 14, are buried here. Cuparia Lupula placed [this memorial].
EDCS-27500009.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a veteran army officer and his young son, erected by his wife or mother, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500010.text_edition: \n Scaptia Florentia veteranus legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis testamento fieri iussit hic situs est in fronte pedes X in agrum sic pedes X\n
EDCS-27500010.translation: Scaptia Florentia, a veteran of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, ordered this to be made in her will. She is buried here. In the front, 10 feet; in depth, 10 feet.
EDCS-27500010.summary: This is a tomb inscription indicating the burial plot dimensions for a female veteran of the XI Claudia legion, who arranged for her burial by will, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hermeti nnorum XXIIII Crescentilla soror bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27500011.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Hermetus, aged 24. His sister Crescentilla placed [this] for him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-27500011.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Hermetus, erected by his sister, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500013.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Sulpicius Calvio centurio legionis I Minerviae praepositus cohortis I Belgarum hoc in loco maiestate et numine eius servatus\n
EDCS-27500013.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Sulpicius Calvius, centurion of the I Minervia legion, commander of the I Belgarum cohort, protected in this place by his majesty and divine power.
EDCS-27500013.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter by a centurion in command of an auxiliary cohort, expressing gratitude for protection, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Artorius Castus |(centurio) legionis III Gallicae item |(centurio) legionis VI ferratae item |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis item |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae item primus pilus eiusdem legionis praeposito classis Misenatium praeff(ectus) legionis VI victricis duci sic leggionum alarum Britannicimiarum adversus Armoricanos procurator centenario sic provinciae Liburniae iure gladii vivus ipse sibi et suis ex testamento\n
EDCS-27500014.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Artorius Castus, a centurion of the III Gallica legion, also a centurion of the VI Ferrata legion, also a centurion of the II Adjutrix legion, also a centurion of the V Macedonica legion, also chief centurion (primus pilus) of the same legion, commander of the fleet of Misenum, prefect of the VI Victrix legion, leader of the British auxiliary legions and alae against the Armoricans, procurator of the province of Liburnia with the power of life and death, lived and erected this himself for himself and his family by his will.
EDCS-27500014.summary: This is an exceptionally detailed funerary monument for Lucius Artorius Castus, recording his extensive military career from centurion to fleet commander and procurator, erected by himself before his death, likely dating from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27500015.text_edition: \n Gellio Dextro auguri IIIIviro iure dicundo Sexto Gellio Eroni IIIIIIviro \n
EDCS-27500015.translation: To Gellius Dexter, augur, duovir iuri dicundo; to Sextus Gellius Eron, duovir.
EDCS-27500015.summary: This is a dedication or record of office for two individuals, Gellius Dexter and Sextus Gellius Eron, holding municipal magistracies and a religious office, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Vindemiae annorum XXXV Rubellius Pisticus et Rubellia Vindemia filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-27500016.translation: To the departed spirits: For Cornelia Vindemia, aged 35. Rubellius Pisticus and Rubellia Vindemia placed this for their most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-27500016.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Cornelia Vindemia at age 35, set up by her parents Rubellius Pisticus and Rubellia Vindemia.

EDCS-27500018.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Florio Crispino defuncto annorum II mensium III Publius Florius Severus filio infelici\n
EDCS-27500018.translation: To the departed spirits: For Publius Florius Crispinus, deceased at 2 years and 3 months. Publius Florius Severus placed this for his unfortunate son.
EDCS-27500018.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Florius Crispinus at the age of 2 years and 3 months, set up by his father Publius Florius Severus.

EDCS-27500019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Lucretio Marcello defuncto annorum XXVI Avidia Luculla fratri infelici\n
EDCS-27500019.translation: To the departed spirits: For Marcus Lucretius Marcellus, deceased at 26 years. Avidia Luculla placed this for her unfortunate brother.
EDCS-27500019.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Marcus Lucretius Marcellus at age 26, set up by his sister Avidia Luculla.

EDCS-27500020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lupae defunctae nnorum XV Dracontilla mater infelix bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27500020.translation: To the departed spirits: For Lupa, deceased at 15 years. Her unfortunate mother Dracontilla placed this for her deserving daughter.
EDCS-27500020.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Lupa at age 15, set up by her mother Dracontilla.

EDCS-27500021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mercurialis Silvinae conservae con sic qua vixi annos XLV ex qua habeo natos V bene merenti\n
EDCS-27500021.translation: To the departed spirits: For Mercurialis Silvina, my fellow slave with whom I lived for 45 years and from whom I have 5 children. Placed for her deserving person.
EDCS-27500021.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Mercurialis Silvina, set up by her partner (fellow slave) with whom she had lived for 45 years and had 5 children.

EDCS-27500022.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Messor Ditoni coniugi defunctae annorum XX bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27500022.translation: To the departed spirits: For Messor Ditoni, wife, deceased at 20 years. Placed for her deserving person.
EDCS-27500022.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Messor Ditoni at age 20, set up by her husband.

EDCS-27500023.text_edition: \n Murcidia Maxima viva fecit sibi et Publio Aelio Epidio marito suo et suis hoc monumentum heredem sequetur\n
EDCS-27500023.translation: Murcidia Maxima made this monument for herself while alive, and for Publius Aelius Epidius, her husband, and for her own family. It will pass to the heir.
EDCS-27500023.summary: This is a self-made funerary monument by Murcidia Maxima for herself, her husband Publius Aelius Epidius, and their family, with provisions for inheritance.

EDCS-27500024.translation: To the departed spirits: For Suricina.
EDCS-27500024.summary: This is a very brief funerary epitaph dedicated to Suricina.

EDCS-27500025.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentiae Valeriae defunctae annorum \n
EDCS-27500025.translation: To the departed spirits: For Terentia Valeria, deceased.
EDCS-27500025.summary: This fragmentary funerary epitaph records the death of Terentia Valeria.

EDCS-27500026.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Vipsanio Marcello Vipsanius Lupus consubrino sic bene merenti\n
EDCS-27500026.translation: To the departed spirits: For Lucius Vipsanius Marcellus. Vipsanius Lupus placed this for his deserving fellow cousin.
EDCS-27500026.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Lucius Vipsanius Marcellus, set up by his fellow cousin Vipsanius Lupus.

EDCS-27500028.text_edition: \n Lucio Aelio Caesare II Publio Coelio Balbino Vibullio Pio consulibus VII Idus Octobres Caius Domitius Valens IIvir iure dicundo praeeunte Caio Iulio Severo pontifice legem dixit in ea verba quae infra scripta sunt Iuppiter Optime Maxime quandoque tibi hodie hanc aram dabo dedicaboque ollis legibus ollisque regionibus dabo dedicaboque quas hic hodie palam dixero uti infimum solum huius arae est si quis hic hostia sacrum faxit quod magmentum nec protollat it circo tamen probe factum esto ceterae leges huic arae eaedem sunto quae arae Dianae sunt in Aventino monte dictae hisce legibus hisce regionibus sic uti dixi hanc tibi aram Iuppiter Optime Maxime do dico dedicoque uti sis volens propitius mihi collegisque meis decurionibus colonis incolis coloniae Martiae Iuliae Salonae coniugibus liberisque nostris\n
EDCS-27500028.translation: Under the consulship of Lucius Aelius Caesar and Publius Coelius Balbinus Vibullius Pius, on the 9th day of October.  Caius Domitius Valens, one of the two duovirs with judicial authority, with Caius Julius Severus, the pontiff, presiding, spoke the law in the following terms: Jupiter Optimus Maximus, whenever I give and dedicate this altar to you today, I will give and dedicate it according to those laws and those boundaries which I shall declare publicly here today. Just as the lowest soil of this altar is, if anyone performs a sacrifice with a victim here, and does not prolong the offering, may it nevertheless be considered properly done.  Let the remaining laws for this altar be the same as those spoken for the altar of Diana on the Aventine hill, according to these laws and these boundaries, just as I have said. To you, Jupiter Optimus Maximus, I give, declare, and dedicate this altar, so that you may be willing and favourable to me, my colleagues, the decurions, colonists, and inhabitants of the colony of Martia Julia Salona, and to our wives and children.
EDCS-27500028.summary: This inscription records a formal dedication of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Domitius Valens, a duovir, likely dating to the 2nd century CE based on the consuls.

EDCS-27500029.text_edition: \n Caesia Titi filia Secunda Boati Aesculapio \n
EDCS-27500029.translation: To Aesculapius, from Caesia Secunda, daughter of Titus, wife of Boatus.
EDCS-27500029.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication to Aesculapius by Caesia Secunda.

EDCS-27500030.text_edition: \n Fortunae Conservatrici pro salute Marci Aureli Iuli viri clarissimi auguris praesidis provinciae Dassius notarius votum solvit\n
EDCS-27500030.translation: Dassius the notary fulfilled his vow to Fortuna Conservatrix for the health of Marcus Aurelius Iulius, a most distinguished man, augur and governor of the province.
EDCS-27500030.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a notary to Fortuna Conservatrix for the well-being of the provincial governor, Marcus Aurelius Iulius.

EDCS-27500031.text_edition: \n Fortunae Domesticae sacrum Aulus Obultronius Beryllus\n
EDCS-27500031.translation: Sacred to Fortuna Domestica, from Aulus Obultronius Beryllus.
EDCS-27500031.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Fortuna Domestica by Aulus Obultronius Beryllus.

EDCS-27500032.text_edition: \n Herculi Sextus Aquillius Severus |(centurio) cohortis V donis donatus ab Imperatore Traiano bello Dacico decurio Salonis et Flanonae locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27500032.translation: To Hercules. Sextus Aquillius Severus, centurion of the 5th cohort, honored with gifts by Emperor Trajan in the Dacian War, was granted the position of decurion of Salona and Flanona by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500032.summary: This inscription honors Hercules and records the appointment of Sextus Aquillius Severus, a centurion decorated in the Dacian Wars, as a decurion in Salona and Flanona.

EDCS-27500033.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Lucius Egnatius Luci filius Cnaei nepos Cnaei pronepos Tromentina Clemens decurio augur\n
EDCS-27500033.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, from Lucius Egnatius Clemens, son of Lucius, grandson of Cnaeus, great-grandson of Cnaeus, of the Tromentina tribe, decurion and augur.
EDCS-27500033.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Egnatius Clemens, who held prominent civic and religious offices in his colony.

EDCS-27500034.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Maximus Germani votum posuit\n
EDCS-27500034.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Maximus Germani placed his vow.
EDCS-27500034.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Maximus Germani.

EDCS-27500036.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabus sacrum Tiberius Severus\n
EDCS-27500036.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the gods and goddesses, from Tiberius Severus.
EDCS-27500036.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other deities by Tiberius Severus.

EDCS-27500037.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et divo Claudio Caesari Augusto Germanico tribunicia potestate XIIII Publius Anteius Publi Antei Syntrophi libertus Herma IIIIIIvir et Augustalis porticum voto soluto libens merito loco accepto decurionum decreto\n
EDCS-27500037.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the deified Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, who held tribunician power for the 14th time. Publius Anteius Herma, freedman of Publius Anteius Syntrophus, an IIIIvir and Augustalis, dedicated this portico, having fulfilled his vow with pleasure and merit, in a location accepted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500037.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a portico to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the deified Emperor Claudius by Publius Anteius Herma, a local official, with the approval of the decurions.

EDCS-27500038.text_edition: \n Iovi O<pt=E>imo Maximo caelesti patrono Gaius Caesius Corymbus et Faberia Cara votum solverunt libentes merito \n
EDCS-27500038.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the celestial patron. Gaius Caesius Corymbus and Faberia Cara willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-27500038.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Caesius Corymbus and Faberia Cara to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-27500039.text_edition: \n Messor Cai I S votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27500039.translation: Messor, son of Caius, willingly fulfilled his vow as deserved.
EDCS-27500039.summary: This is a votive inscription from Messor, son of Caius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500041.text_edition: \n Curia Prisca Matri Magnae fanum r<e=I>fecit signa posuit Larophorum cymbala tynpana sic catillum forfices aram dat libens animo \n
EDCS-27500041.translation: Prisca dedicated a sanctuary to the Great Mother, set up images, and willingly gave a Larophorum, cymbals, tympana, a small dish, scissors, and an altar.
EDCS-27500041.summary: This is a dedication of a sanctuary and various cult objects to the Great Mother by Prisca, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500042.text_edition: \n Publius Iulius Rufus aedem Matri Magnae de sua pecunia faciendum curavit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-27500042.translation: Publius Iulius Rufus arranged for the construction of a temple to the Great Mother at his own expense and dedicated it himself.
EDCS-27500042.summary: This inscription records Publius Iulius Rufus commissioning and dedicating a temple to the Great Mother at his own expense, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500043.text_edition: \n Lucius Statius Luci filius Facula quinquennalis Matri Deum aedem de pecunia sua fecit ex voto\n
EDCS-27500043.translation: Lucius Statius, son of Lucius, from Facula, a quinquennalis, built a temple to the Mother of the Gods at his own expense, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-27500043.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Statius, a quinquennalis, building a temple to the Mother of the Gods at his own expense, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500044.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto pro salute et incolumitate Pamphili dispensatoris Auggustorum nnostrorum Fortunatus arcarius\n
EDCS-27500044.translation: Fortunatus, an accountant for our Emperors, dedicated this to the Unconquered God for the health and safety of Pamphilus, his steward.
EDCS-27500044.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered God by Fortunatus for the health of Pamphilus, a steward to the emperors, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500045.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum pro salute Liberalis nostri Coetonicus servus\n
EDCS-27500045.translation: Coetonicus, a slave of Liberalis, dedicated this sacred offering to the August Nymphs for his health.
EDCS-27500045.summary: This inscription records Coetonicus, a slave, dedicating a sacred offering to the August Nymphs for his well-being, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500047.text_edition: \n Mescenia Valentina imperio Veneris Iunonem sic donum dedit\n
EDCS-27500047.translation: By the command of Venus, Mescenia Valentina gave Juno as a gift.
EDCS-27500047.summary: This inscription records Mescenia Valentina dedicating Juno as a gift under the influence of Venus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500048.text_edition: \n Prima Fulviniae Urbana Curtiae Oecumene Iuliae magistrae Veneris donum dederunt\n
EDCS-27500048.translation: Prima, Fulvinia Urbana, and Oecumene, daughter of Curtia, the attendants of Venus, gave this gift.
EDCS-27500048.summary: This inscription records a gift given by Prima, Fulvinia Urbana, and Oecumene, attendants of Venus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500050.text_edition: \n Veneri Victrici in memoriam Albuciae Cai filiae Pontiae uxoris Marcus Marius Paulus locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27500050.translation: To Venus Victrix, in memory of Albucia Pontia, wife of Marcus Marius Paulus, son of Caius; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500050.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Venus Victrix, commemorating Albucia Pontia, and notes the granting of land by the local council, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500052.text_edition: \n Constantio IIII et Maximiano IIII nobilissimis Caesaribus conssulibus praefecto Aurelio Valentiniano qui m<i=E>n<i=E>strabit! at sic Tritones Kalendis Februariis Volusius Adiutor Aurelius Asiaticus Aurelius Armentius Aurelius Vates Aurelius Antonianus Aurelius Ursilianus Aurelius Lucianus Aurelius Lapns sic Aurelius Dalmatius Aurelius Me<r=P>cu<r=P>ius Sabino et Rufino consulibus IRAN NAEC RIDV MAE IEAF (Hadrian[us ------ \n
EDCS-27500052.translation: In the consulship of Constantinus IV and Maximianus IV, and of [Aurelius?] Valentinianus, the prefect who will rule; on the Kalends of February, Volusius Adiutor, Aurelius Asiaticus, Aurelius Armentius, Aurelius Vates, Aurelius Antonianus, Aurelius Ursilianus, Aurelius Lucianus, Aurelius Lapus, Aurelius Dalmatius, Aurelius Mercurius [....] in the consulship of Sabinus and Rufinus IRAN NAEC RIDV MAE IEAF (Hadrian[us ------
EDCS-27500052.summary: This complex inscription lists multiple individuals and commemorates events under specific consulships, including the prefect Valentinianus, dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-27500053.text_edition: \n DDominis nnostris Diocletiano VIII et Maximiano VII Auggustis conssulibus Aurelio Valentiniano viro perfectissimo bis praefecto Kalendis Februariis m<i=E>n<i=E>stravimus at sic Triton<e=I>s Aurelius Mercurius Aurelius Secundinus Aurelius Fortunius Aurelius Severianus Aurelius Sarmatio Aurelius Antonius Aurelius Ursus Iulius Secundus Aurelius Uranius Aurelius Forensis DDominis nnostris Constantino Augusto V et Licinio iuniore Caesare consulibus Aurelio Xenone bis praefecto Kalendis Februariis m<i=E>n<i=E>strabimus sic at sic Tritones Aurelius Lucentius Aurelius ius Aurelius Gregorius Aurelius Firminus Aurelius Marcellinus Aurelius Dalmatius Valentinus Aurelius Vincentius Aurelius Messor PECVS ORVS Dominis nostris Constantino Augusto set Constantino no<b=R>ilissimo Caesare praefe<c=O>ito Quin<t> ilio Faintil<l=I>o at sic Tritones Kalen<d=O>is Februariis Aurelius Martinus Aurelius Donatus Varius Terentianus Aurelius Stercorius Aurelius Exuperius Aurelius Terentianus Aurelius Alexander Aurelius Heraclianus Aurelius Reditus Aurelius Maurenus Aurelius Balbinus Aurelius Euticius Aurelius Leuntius Aurelius Senator Ministri ad Tritones Aelius Valerianus Varius Sabinus Iulius Silvius Aurelius Fortunius Papirius Crescenti--- Claudius Barbianus Dirrutius Crescenti--- Aelius Dalmatius ex permissu Nocturni Novelli patroni collegii feliciter \n
EDCS-27500053.translation: To our Lords Diocletian VIII and Maximian VII, Augusti, as consuls, on the Kalends of February, we Aurelius Mercurius, Aurelius Secundinus, Aurelius Fortunius, Aurelius Severianus, Aurelius Sarmatio, Aurelius Antonius, Aurelius Ursus, Iulius Secundus, Aurelius Uranius, Aurelius Forensis, formerly prefects, presented to Aurelius Valentiniano, vir perfectissimus, formerly twice prefect.  To our Lords Constantine Augustus V and Licinius the Younger Caesar, as consuls, on the Kalends of February, we Aurelius Lucentius, Aurelius Ius, Aurelius Gregorius, Aurelius Firminus, Aurelius Marcellinus, Aurelius Dalmatius Valentinus, Aurelius Vincentius, Aurelius Messor, formerly twice prefects, presented at the Tritones. To our Lords Constantine Augustus and Constantine the Most Noble Caesar, prefect Quintilius Faintilius, at the Tritones, on the Kalends of February, we Aurelius Martinus, Aurelius Donatus, Varius Terentianus, Aurelius Stercorius, Aurelius Exuperius, Aurelius Terentianus, Aurelius Alexander, Aurelius Heraclianus, Aurelius Reditus, Aurelius Maurenus, Aurelius Balbinus, Aurelius Euticius, Aurelius Leuntius, Aurelius Senator, ministers at the Tritones. Aelius Valerianus, Varius Sabinus, Iulius Silvius, Aurelius Fortunius, Papirius Crescenti---, Claudius Barbianus, Dirrutius Crescenti---, Aelius Dalmatius, by permission of Nocturnus Novellus, patron of the college, happily.
EDCS-27500053.summary: This inscription is a complex list of officials and names, likely related to a collegium or guild, dedicating something on the Kalends of February across several consular periods.

EDCS-27500054.translation: Aurelia Renata placed [this] during her magistracy.
EDCS-27500054.summary: This inscription records a woman, Aurelia Renata, who placed a monument during her term as an official.

EDCS-27500055.text_edition: \n magister II i<n=M> memoriam Luriae Hygiae filiae donum posuit cuius dedicatione collegio epulum dedit loco dato decreto decurionum \n
EDCS-27500055.translation: As magistrate for the second time, in memory of Luria Hygeia, his daughter, he placed a gift, to which he gave a feast for the college on the day of dedication, in a place granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500055.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument or dedication made by a second-time magistrate in honor of his daughter, Luria Hygeia, including a college feast.

EDCS-27500056.translation: Species fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-27500056.summary: This is a votive inscription by an individual named Species, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-27500057.text_edition: \n Pro salute domini nostri Caius A I posuit\n
EDCS-27500057.translation: For the health of our lord, Caius A[---] placed [this].
EDCS-27500057.summary: This inscription records an offering or dedication made by Caius A[---] for the well-being of the emperor.

EDCS-27500059.text_edition: \n IANAM VI CON Flavius Primitivos sic Mius Agrius Liberalis Pomponio Lupio Obultronia Nicia\n
EDCS-27500059.translation: Flavius Primitivos and Mius Agrius Liberalis [dedicated this] to Pomponius Lupius and Obultronia Nicia, [as part of] the VI CON [service?].
EDCS-27500059.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or record of a donation by Flavius Primitivos and Mius Agrius Liberalis to Pomponius Lupius and Obultronia Nicia, possibly related to a magistracy or service.

EDCS-27500060.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Drusi filio Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XI imperatori XXIIII consuli V censori patri patriae Publio Anteio legato pro praetore\n
EDCS-27500060.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 11th time, imperator for the 24th time, consul for the 5th time, censor, father of the fatherland, by Publius Anteius, legate pro praetore.
EDCS-27500060.summary: This inscription is a dedication or record of service presented to Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, listing his numerous titles and officials involved.

EDCS-27500061.text_edition: \n Divo Nervae Lucius Vettius Luci filius Sergia Catulus IIvir iure dicundo ex pecunia honoraria IIviratus sui locus datus publice decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27500061.translation: To the deified Nerva, Lucius Vettius, son of Lucius, of the Sergia tribe, as duovir for rendering justice, from the honorary funds of his duovirate; the site was granted publicly by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500061.summary: This inscription records a public site granted for a monument dedicated to the deified Emperor Nerva by Lucius Vettius, who served as duovir.

EDCS-27500062.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXIIII consule III patre patriae vexillationes legionum II piae et III Concordiae pedes CC sub cura Publi Aeli Amyntiani |(centurionis) frumentarii legionis II Traianae\n
EDCS-27500062.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 24th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, 200 feet of the vexillations of Legio II Pia and Legio III Concordia, under the supervision of Publius Aelius Amyntianus, centurion of the frumentarii of Legio II Traiana.
EDCS-27500062.summary: This inscription records the measurement of 200 feet belonging to detachments of two legions under the command of a centurion during the reign of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-27500063.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-27500063.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Constanti victori ac triumfatori semper Augusto Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius vir clarissimus praeses provinciae Dalmatiae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-27500063.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantius, ever victorious and triumphant Augustus, Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, vir clarissimus, governor of the province of Dalmatia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-27500063.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, governor of Dalmatia, to Emperor Constantius.

EDCS-27500064.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-27500064.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Constantio victoriosissimo semper Augusto Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius vir clarissimus praeses provinciae Dalmatiae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-27500064.translation: To our Lord Flavius Constantius, most victorious, ever Augustus, Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, most distinguished, governor of the province of Dalmatia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-27500064.summary: This is a dedication by Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, governor of Dalmatia, to Emperor Constantius, likely dating to his reign (337-361 AD).

EDCS-27500066.text_edition: \n Publio Balsamio Sabiniano clarissimo pueri tribuno laticlavio filio Aureli Sabiniani viri egregii procuratoris ducis provinciae Dalmatiae tribuni protectorum Augusti N vigor Concordius Victorinianus patrono praestantissimo\n
EDCS-27500066.translation: To Publius Balsamius Sabinianus, most distinguished youth, tribune with laticlavian rank, son of Aurelius Sabinianus, excellent man, procurator, commander of the province of Dalmatia, tribune of the emperor's protectors. Concordius Victorinianus, to his most outstanding patron.
EDCS-27500066.summary: This dedicates a monument to Publius Balsamius Sabinianus, a young military officer, by Concordius Victorinianus, likely his patron, probably dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-27500067.text_edition: \n Casconiae Marcellinae clarissimae feminae matri pientissimae quae vixit nnos LVII Casconia Marcellina clarissima femina filia et heres si quis hoc sepulchrum tentaverit aperire dederit fisco SS L milia\n
EDCS-27500067.translation: To Casconia Marcellina, most distinguished lady, a most devoted mother, who lived 57 years. Casconia Marcellina, her daughter and heir, most distinguished lady. If anyone attempts to open this tomb, he shall pay 50,000 denarii to the treasury.
EDCS-27500067.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Casconia Marcellina, setting a penalty for desecration of her tomb by her daughter and heir, Casconia Marcellina, likely from the 3rd or 4th century AD.

EDCS-27500068.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Cai filio Tromentina Respecto Terentiano clarissimo iuveni Xviro stlitibus iudicandis filio et Caediciae Luci filiae Lucillae Crispinilla mater infelicissima ave vale\n
EDCS-27500068.translation: To Gaius Valerius Respectus Terentianus, son of Gaius Valerius Tromentinus, most distinguished youth, a decemvir for settling disputes, [dedicated by] his mother Caedicia Lucilla, daughter of Lucius, most unhappy. Hail and farewell.
EDCS-27500068.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Valerius Respectus Terentianus, a young man, set up by his grieving mother, dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27500069.text_edition: \n Consentio deorum Marcana Sozomene imperio fecit\n
EDCS-27500069.translation: Marcana Sozomene made this by the authority of the gods.
EDCS-27500069.summary: This is a short dedication by Marcana Sozomene, attributing the act to divine authority, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500071.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Silvestri ex <vi=IV>so posuit Septimus pro filio suo \n
EDCS-27500071.translation: To Diana Augusta, Silvestrius erected this from a vow for his son.
EDCS-27500071.summary: This is a votive offering to Diana Augusta by Silvestrius on behalf of his son, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500072.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Quintus Raonius Diodorus ex voto posuit libens merito IIII Idus Maias Aproniano et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-27500072.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Quintus Raonius Diodorus erected this willingly and deservedly in fulfillment of a vow, on the fourth day before the Ides of May, when Apronianus and Bradua were consuls.
EDCS-27500072.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Raonius Diodorus, dated by the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua, likely in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-27500073.text_edition: \n Pro salute Titi Valeriani Eufrosinus sic servus votum solvit Laribus Augustis\n
EDCS-27500073.translation: For the well-being of Titus Valerianus, Eufrosinus the slave fulfilled his vow to the August Lares.
EDCS-27500073.summary: This is a votive inscription by a slave, Eufrosinus, to the Lares Augusti for the health of Titus Valerianus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500074.text_edition: \n Nocturno sacrum Caius Cassius Valens ben<e=I>ficiarius ex imperio \n
EDCS-27500074.translation: Sacred to Nocturnus. Gaius Cassius Valens, beneficiarius, by order.
EDCS-27500074.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Nocturnus by Gaius Cassius Valens, a soldier of the beneficiarius rank, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500075.text_edition: \n Aedem Nymphis et Silvano Augusto sacrum Lucius Aprofennius Circitor voto soluto posuit\n
EDCS-27500075.translation: Lucius Aprofennius Circitor erected this temple sacred to the Nymphs and Silvanus Augustus, having fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27500075.summary: This is an inscription recording the erection of a temple dedicated to the Nymphs and Silvanus Augustus by Lucius Aprofennius Circitor in fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27500076.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Caius Popillius Expetitus ex visu\n
EDCS-27500076.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus, from a vision of Gaius Popillius Expetitus.
EDCS-27500076.summary: An altar dedicated to Silvanus Augustus by Gaius Popillius Expetitus, apparently inspired by a divine vision.

EDCS-27500077.text_edition: \n Dis Syriis Caius Albucius Cai libertus Restitutus\n
EDCS-27500077.translation: To the Syrian Gods, Gaius Albucius Restitutus, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-27500077.summary: An altar dedicated to the Syrian Gods by Gaius Albucius Restitutus, a freedman.

EDCS-27500078.text_edition: \n Marcus Coelius Senilis et Publicia Ingenua posuerunt\n
EDCS-27500078.translation: Marcus Coelius Senilis and Publicia Ingenua erected this.
EDCS-27500078.summary: A monument erected by Marcus Coelius Senilis and Publicia Ingenua.

EDCS-27500079.summary: A dedicatory inscription by Mescenia Tertia, likely wishing good fortune upon the recipient or the monument itself.

EDCS-27500080.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXIIII patre patriae cohors I |(miliaria) Delmatarum sub cura Grani Fortunati tribuni cohortis eiusdem muri pedes DCCC in his turris una\n
EDCS-27500080.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 24th year, father of the country, the I (Miliaria) cohort of Dalmatians, under the command of Granius Fortunatus, tribune of the same cohort, built 800 feet of wall, including one tower.
EDCS-27500080.summary: A record of military construction, specifically 800 feet of wall and a tower, built by the I (Miliaria) cohort of Dalmatians during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-27500081.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-27500081.text_edition: \n Flavio Constanti nobilissimo ac beatissimo Caesari collegium fabrum Veneris\n
EDCS-27500081.translation: To Flavius Constantius, most noble and most blessed Caesar, by the association of the artisans of Venus.
EDCS-27500081.summary: A dedication to Caesar Flavius Constantius by the guild of the artisans of Venus.

EDCS-27500091.text_edition: \n Aesculapio Augusto sacrum Publius Servilius IIIIIIvir Augustalis Flavialis Titialis Nervialis testamento ponendum iussit ex HS et ob dedicationem \n
EDCS-27500091.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius Augustus. Publius Servilius, a member of the six highest priesthoods, Augustalis, Flavialis, Titialis, Nervialis, ordered it to be set up in his will, from 100 sesterces, and for the dedication.
EDCS-27500091.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Aesculapius Augustus by Publius Servilius, who stipulated its erection in his will, with a financial provision.

EDCS-27500093.text_edition: \n Genio plebis sacrum Lucius Septimulenus Vitalis Quintus Lusius Acrabanus Quintus Iulius Daphnus Marcus Curtius Sporus IIIIIIviri ob honorem magistri Mercuriales\n
EDCS-27500093.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the common people. Lucius Septimulenus Vitalis, Quintus Lusius Acrabanus, Quintus Iulius Daphnus, Marcus Curtius Sporus, as duoviri, in honor of the magistracy of the Mercuriales.
EDCS-27500093.summary: A dedication to the Genius of the common people by four officials (duoviri) associated with the magistracy of the Mercuriales.

EDCS-27500094.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Maximus lapidarius ex voto aram posuit\n
EDCS-27500094.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Maximus the stonecutter placed this altar in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-27500094.summary: A votive altar dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Maximus the stonecutter.

EDCS-27500095.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Publius Mescenius Severianus votum solvit <l=I>(ibens) merito \n
EDCS-27500095.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Publius Mescenius Severianus gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-27500095.summary: A votive inscription for Jupiter Optimus Maximus, recording Publius Mescenius Severianus fulfilling his vow.

EDCS-27500096.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Munnius Imnis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27500096.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Munnius Imnis gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27500096.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter by Lucius Munnius Imnis.

EDCS-27500097.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Marcus Ulpius Kalendinus beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae Fusco II et Dextro consulibus\n
EDCS-27500097.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of our lord, Marcus Ulpius Kalendinus, an aide to the consular legate of the First Italian Legion, under consuls Fuscus II and Dexter.
EDCS-27500097.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter by Marcus Ulpius Kalendinus for the emperor's health, dated by consuls to the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27500098.text_edition: \n Marci Lusi Severi clarissimi viri Modestus libertus templum ex voto restituit\n
EDCS-27500098.translation: Modestus, freedman of Marcus Lusus Severus, a most distinguished man, restored the temple in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-27500098.summary: A freedman named Modestus restored a temple, dedicating his work to his patron Marcus Lusus Severus.

EDCS-27500099.text_edition: \n Libero Augusto sacrum Lusius Felicio Luci Lusi Severini libertus posuit\n
EDCS-27500099.translation: Sacred to Liber Augustus. Lusius Felicio, freedman of Lucius Lasius Severinus, erected this.
EDCS-27500099.summary: A dedication to Liber Augustus by Lusius Felicio, freedman of Lucius Lasius Severinus.

EDCS-27500100.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Soli Marcus Lusius Trofimas libertus posuit\n
EDCS-27500100.translation: To the Unconquered God Sol. Marcus Lusius Trofimas, freedman, erected this.
EDCS-27500100.summary: A dedication to the sun god Sol by Marcus Lusius Trofimas, a freedman.

EDCS-27500101.text_edition: \n Lucius Magius Princeps Mercurio votum solvit\n
EDCS-27500101.translation: To Mercury, Lucius Magius Princeps fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27500101.summary: A votive dedication to Mercury by Lucius Magius Princeps.

EDCS-27500102.text_edition: \n Mercurio Augusto sacrum Marcus Ulpius Augusti libertus Nedymus Caius Pollius Albanus Titus Vetulenus Titi libertus Abascantus Quintus Cornelius Augustalis Lucius Volceius Cerdo IIIIIIviri magistri Mercuriales ob honorem\n
EDCS-27500102.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. The IV viri magistrates of the Mercuriales, for their office: Marcus Ulpius freedman of Augustus Nedymus, Caius Pollius Albanus, Titus Vetulenus freedman of Titus Abascantus, Quintus Cornelius Augustalis, Lucius Volceius Cerdo.
EDCS-27500102.summary: A dedication to Mercury Augustus by the IV viri magistrates of the Mercuriales group.

EDCS-27500103.text_edition: \n Mercurio Augusto sacrum Lucius Volceius Luci libertus Silvester IIIIIIvir ob honorem\n
EDCS-27500103.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. Lucius Volceius Silvester, freedman of Lucius Volceius, a IV vir, for his office.
EDCS-27500103.summary: A dedication to Mercury Augustus by Lucius Volceius Silvester, a IV vir.

EDCS-27500104.text_edition: \n Ninfis sic Augustis sacrum I M P librarius\n
EDCS-27500104.translation: Sacred to the Nymphs, especially the August Nymphs. I M P, scribe.
EDCS-27500104.summary: A dedication to the Nymphs by a scribe identified as I M P.

EDCS-27500105.text_edition: \n Saturno Augusto sacrum Claudia Aesernina sacerdos divae Augustae testamento poni iussit\n
EDCS-27500105.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Claudia Aesernina, priestess of the divine Augusta, ordered this to be set up in her will.
EDCS-27500105.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus, ordered by will by Claudia Aesernina, priestess of the divine Augusta.

EDCS-27500106.text_edition: \n Lucius Lusius Corpio Lucius Lusius Quintio Luci liberti IIIIIIviri magistri Mercuriales ob honorem\n
EDCS-27500106.translation: Lucius Lusius Corpio and Lucius Lusius Quintio, freedman of Lucius, as vigintivirs and magistrates of the Mercuriales, [dedicate this] in honor of their office.
EDCS-27500106.summary: This is a dedication by local officials (vigintivirs and magistrates) in honor of their tenure of office, possibly related to the cult of Mercury.

EDCS-27500107.text_edition: \n Publius Mescenius Trophimus Titus Flavius Asiaticus Titus Flavius Surus Lucius Bovianius Pareus IIIIIIviri ob honorem magistri Mercuriales\n
EDCS-27500107.translation: Publius Mescenius Trophimus, Titus Flavius Asiaticus, Titus Flavius Surus, and Lucius Bovianius Pareus, as vigintivirs and magistrates of the Mercuriales, [dedicate this] in honor of their office.
EDCS-27500107.summary: This records a dedication by four vigintivirs and magistrates, likely in honor of their official duties, perhaps related to the cult of Mercury.

EDCS-27500109.summary: This inscription is a short dedication, likely on a tomb or a shrine, to a person named Constantio.

EDCS-27500110.text_edition: \n Marcus Lusius PON ob dedicationem balnei EXVOS CENT \n
EDCS-27500110.translation: Marcus Lusius [dedicates] the bathhouse by vow.
EDCS-27500110.summary: This records the dedication of a bathhouse by Marcus Lusius, likely in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-27500112.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marco Avilio Nummio |(centurioni) cohortis III [Alpinorum ------\n
EDCS-27500112.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Marcus Avilius Nummius, centurion of the III Alpinorum cohort, [and others lost].
EDCS-27500112.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Marcus Avilius Nummius, a centurion, and possibly others.

EDCS-27500114.text_edition: \n Publius Lastus Auli filius Scaeva domo Florentia eques veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis nnum LX stipendiorum XXX et Lastae Publi libertae Apiculae Publio Lastio Felici Publio Lasto Diodoro liberto testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-27500114.translation: Publius Lastus Scaeva, son of Aulus, from Florence, a veteran cavalryman of the Seventh Claudian Loyal and Faithful legion, with 60 years of service and 30 years of pay, ordered this to be made in his will for Lasta Publia Apicula, freedwoman of Publius, and for Publius Lastus Diodorus, a freedman of Publius.
EDCS-27500114.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by a veteran cavalryman, Publius Lastus Scaeva, for his freed slaves, indicating his wishes for their commemoration after his death.

EDCS-27500115.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a tombstone or ownership mark, identifying a person named Mantus.

EDCS-27500116.text_edition: \n Marcius VS miles um APLA stipendiorum VII F\n
EDCS-27500116.translation: Marcius VS, soldier of [a unit], with 7 years of service.
EDCS-27500116.summary: This is a fragment of a military inscription recording the name and service length of a soldier.

EDCS-27500118.text_edition: \n Aulo Annaeo Auli filio Aniensi Flacco flamini Augustali IIIIviro II quinquennali IIIIviro iure dicundo praefecto iure dicundo ex decreto decurionum pontifici Aninia Decimi filia uxor\n
EDCS-27500118.translation: Aninia, daughter of Decimus, wife of Aulus Annaeus Aulus son Aniensis Flaccus, who served as Augustal flamen, vigintivir, quinquennial magistrate, magistrate with judicial power, prefect with judicial power, [dedicates this] by decree of the decurions, and as pontiff.
EDCS-27500118.summary: This inscription honors Aulus Annaeus Aniensis Flaccus for his numerous public offices, with his wife Aninia dedicating it as a pontiff and by decree of the local council.

EDCS-27500120.text_edition: \n enio CI o IIIIIIviro s Acceptus filius patri votum fecit et sibi et suis\n
EDCS-27500120.translation: [... ] and Acceptus, son of CI o, made a vow to his father for himself and his family, as vigintivir.
EDCS-27500120.summary: This is a dedication by Acceptus, son of CI o, likely on behalf of his father and family, in his capacity as a vigintivir.

EDCS-27500121.text_edition: \n Lucio Aconio Agathopo IIIIIIvir convictor Concordiae\n
EDCS-27500121.translation: To Lucius Aconius Agathopus, a member of the college of six, a companion.
EDCS-27500121.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or a tombstone for Lucius Aconius Agathopus, who was a member of a specific college and a companion, possibly to a deity or a person of status.

EDCS-27500122.text_edition: \n Lucio Aemilio Eutycheti IIIIIIviro Lucius Aemilius locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27500122.translation: To Lucius Aemilius Eutyches, a member of the college of six; Lucius Aemilius was granted this place by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27500122.summary: This inscription records that the decurions granted a specific location to Lucius Aemilius Eutyches, who was a member of the college of six.

EDCS-27500123.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Annaeo Palaestrico IIIIIIviro thiasus iuventutis fecit\n
EDCS-27500123.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. The society of youth erected this for Lucius Annaeus Palaestricus, a member of the college of six.
EDCS-27500123.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a youth society for Lucius Annaeus Palaestricus, a member of the college of six.

EDCS-27500124.text_edition: \n A---ius Luci libertus Eutropus IIIIIIvir vivos sic posuit\n
EDCS-27500124.translation: A---ius Eutropus, freedman of Lucius, a member of the college of six, set this up while alive.
EDCS-27500124.summary: This inscription records that A---ius Eutropus, a freedman and a member of the college of six, erected this monument during his lifetime.

EDCS-27500125.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai libertus Martialis IIIIIIvir vivos sic fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-27500125.translation: Gaius Julius Martialis, freedman of Gaius, a member of the college of six, made this while alive for himself and his family.
EDCS-27500125.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Gaius Julius Martialis, a freedman and member of the college of six, for himself and his family.

EDCS-27500126.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Pomp Urso IIIIIIviro nnorum XLV marito bene merenti Terentia Dafine uxor cum sic nnorum XXV\n
EDCS-27500126.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Lucius Pomp[onius] Ur us, member of the college of six, aged 45, a deserving husband. Terentia Dafine, wife, aged 25.
