EDCS-27700042.text_edition: \n Aureliae Iulianae puellae innocentissimae qui sic vixit annos decem septem mensis sic undecim dies duodecim Flavius Iulius et Aurelia Emerius arcam posuerunt filiae deposita die XVII Kalendas Ianuarias\n
EDCS-27700042.translation: For Aurelia Iuliana, most innocent girl, who lived ten years, seventeen months, and twelve days. Flavius Iulius and Aurelia Emerius erected this tomb on the day before the seventeenth of the Kalends of January.
EDCS-27700042.summary: A tomb erected by Flavius Iulius and Aurelia Emerius for their daughter Aurelia Iuliana, recording her age and the date of her burial.

EDCS-27700043.translation: For Aurelia Sabina, to her most [---] husband.
EDCS-27700043.summary: A dedication to Aurelia Sabina, with a damaged epithet for her husband.

EDCS-27700044.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Saturninae coniugi incomparabili quae vixit annis XXVII Quintus Iulius Vilanesis m<a>ri<t>us posuit\n
EDCS-27700044.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Aurelia Saturnina, incomparable wife, who lived 27 years. Quintus Iulius Vilanesis, her husband, erected this.
EDCS-27700044.summary: A funerary monument erected by Quintus Iulius Vilanesis for his wife Aurelia Saturnina, noting her age at death.

EDCS-27700045.text_edition: \n Aureliae Valentinae defunctae nnorum XXXVII Crispinius Felix coniugi inconparabili sic\n
EDCS-27700045.translation: For Aurelia Valentina, deceased, aged 37 years. Crispinius Felix to his incomparable wife.
EDCS-27700045.summary: A dedication by Crispinius Felix to his wife Aurelia Valentina, recording her age at death.

EDCS-27700046.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27700046.text_edition: \n Aurelia Victorina quae emit arca ex proprio suo et vixit annus sic XLV et dies tres et post obitum ipseius sic fecit Fortunatus alonnus sic ipseius sic ex boto sic ipseius fecit si quis voluerit corpus inponere dabit in fiscum |(denariorum folles |(mille)\n
EDCS-27700046.translation: Aurelia Victorina bought this tomb from her own funds and lived 45 years and three days, and after her death Fortunatus, her son, made this as vowed. Whoever wishes to place a body here will pay a thousand denarii into the treasury.
EDCS-27700046.summary: Aurelia Victorina purchased a tomb and lived 45 years; her son Fortunatus later fulfilled a vow by completing it, establishing a fee for future burials.

EDCS-27700047.text_edition: \n Lucio Baebio I patri optimo Minatiae FirmillaeAELus FirNIAIP \n
EDCS-27700047.translation: To Lucius Baebius I., most excellent father. Minatiae Firmillae AELus FirNIAIP
EDCS-27700047.summary: A dedication to Lucius Baebius I., likely erected by his daughter and son-in-law.

EDCS-27700048.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Baebio Vindematori Baebia Secura patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700048.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Baebia Secura placed this for her well-deserving father, Lucius Baebius Vindemator.
EDCS-27700048.summary: A funerary dedication set up by a daughter for her father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700049.text_edition: \n Lucius Baebidius Vitalis et Iulia Quarta Lucio Baebidio Primitivo delicio defuncto nnorum XII et Baebidiae Amandae coniugi bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-27700049.translation: Lucius Baebidius Vitalis and Iulia Quarta placed this for their beloved Lucius Baebidius Primitivus, who died at the age of 12, and for their deserving wife Baebidia Amanda.
EDCS-27700049.summary: A funerary monument erected by a couple for their son and wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Baebilius Euryalus et Valeria Lais vivi fecerunt sibi et libertis libertabusque suis et amico Statio Crescenti aditum ad sepulcrum et unam DIO FIIIC \n
EDCS-27700050.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Baebilius Euryalus and Valeria Lais, while alive, made this for themselves, and for their freedmen and freedwomen, and for their friend Statius Crescens, granting access to the tomb and one [space].
EDCS-27700050.summary: A funerary epitaph for a couple who established their tomb for themselves and their freedpersons and a friend, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700051.text_edition: \n Basilissae infelicissimae defunctae nnorum XXX et Surae filiae defunctae nnorum II verna filius bene merentibus\n
EDCS-27700051.translation: For the most unhappy Basilissa, who died at the age of 30, and for her daughter Sura, who died at the age of 2, a well-deserving son.
EDCS-27700051.summary: A funerary inscription honoring two deceased females, likely mother and daughter, set up by a son, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700052.summary: A simple funerary dedication for Gaius Bennius Valens, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700053.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Buccioni nnrum XX Sextus S M pius posuit\n
EDCS-27700053.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sextus S. M. erected this for Buccio, aged 20, with piety.
EDCS-27700053.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Sextus S. M. for Buccio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700054.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius C S posuit P Veneriae matri pientissimae\n
EDCS-27700054.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius C. S. erected this for his most dutiful mother, P. Veneria.
EDCS-27700054.summary: A funerary dedication by a son for his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700055.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesia Romana Caesiae Luci filiae Ducatrici filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700055.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Caesia Romana, daughter of Lucius Caesius, a well-deserving daughter, the Ducatricius.
EDCS-27700055.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caesia Romana, possibly indicating a military connection with the term 'Ducatricius', likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700056.text_edition: \n Lucio Aemilio Ursino fratri eius posuit Titus Aemilius Protogenes cum Stergio liberto\n
EDCS-27700056.translation: Titus Aemilius Protogenes, together with his freedman Stergius, placed this for Lucius Aemilius Ursus, his brother.
EDCS-27700056.summary: A funerary monument erected by two individuals for the brother of one of them, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700057.text_edition: \n Quintus Agrinius Onesimus et Agriniae Charite Agriniae Fortunatae filiae\n
EDCS-27700057.translation: Quintus Agrinius Onesimus and Agrinia Charite [placed this] for Agrinia Fortunata, [their] daughter.
EDCS-27700057.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700058.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aneniae Fuscinae quae vicxit sic annos plus minus III mater carissimae posuit\n
EDCS-27700058.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Her most beloved mother erected this for Anenia Fuscina, who lived about three years.
EDCS-27700058.summary: This is a tombstone for a young child, Anenia Fuscina, who died at approximately three years of age, set up by her mother.

EDCS-27700059.text_edition: \n Marco Antonio Agathoni Iulia Serena marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700059.translation: Iulia Serena for her husband Marcus Antonius Agatho, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700059.summary: This is a dedication on a tombstone from Iulia Serena to her deserving husband, Marcus Antonius Agatho.

EDCS-27700060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Agrippae qui vixit nnos XXXVIII Arria Prima marito incomparabili posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-27700060.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arria Prima erected this for her husband Antonius Agrippa, who lived 38 years, incomparable, and for herself.
EDCS-27700060.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Arria Prima to her husband Antonius Agrippa, who died at 38, and also includes a space for herself.

EDCS-27700061.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Prima mater misera Antonio Iulio filio infelicissimo annorum XLV cum suis fratribus miseris\n
EDCS-27700061.translation: To the spirits of the departed. A miserable mother erected this for her most unfortunate son, Antonius Iulius, 45 years old, and for her miserable brothers.
EDCS-27700061.summary: This inscription is a memorial from a grieving mother for her son Antonius Iulius, who died at 45, and also for her other unfortunate sons.

EDCS-27700062.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Apiciae Selloni Agatonus Dassius coniugi pientissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700062.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Agatonus Dassius erected this for his most devoted wife Apicia Sellona, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700062.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Agatonus Dassius to his deserving and devoted wife, Apicia Sellona.

EDCS-27700063.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Apro defuncto nnorum XX Nigrina nepoti posuit\n
EDCS-27700063.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Nigrina erected this for her grandson Apro, who died at 20 years of age.
EDCS-27700063.summary: This inscription is a memorial set up by Nigrina for her grandson Apro, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-27700064.text_edition: \n Lucius Aprofennius Proculus posuit sibi et Iuliae Cai filiae Maximae uxori et Lucio Aprofennio Ci<rc=N>itori et Lucio Aprofennio Luculloni nnum XXXIII defuncto quod si longa magis nexissent stamina Parcae aequius iste lapis patris super ossibus esset \n
EDCS-27700064.translation: Lucius Aprofennius Proculus erected this for himself, and for his daughter Iulia Maxima, wife of Caius, and for Lucius Aprofennius Circonitor, and for Lucius Aprofennius Lucullonus, who died at 33 years of age; if only the Fates had spun longer threads, this stone would have been more justly placed over his father's bones.
EDCS-27700064.summary: This is a family tombstone set up by Lucius Aprofennius Proculus for himself, his deceased daughter, and his deceased son Lucius Aprofennius Lucullonus, with a lament for the brevity of life.

EDCS-27700065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Offonio Satrio defuncto annorum XV Anna alumno pientissimo\n
EDCS-27700065.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Anna erected this for Publius Offonius Satrius, who died at 15 years of age, her most devoted student.
EDCS-27700065.summary: This inscription records the death of Publius Offonius Satrius at 15, commemorated by Anna, referred to as his devoted student.

EDCS-27700066.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Arrio Aeneae defuncto annorum XXVIII Vindemia coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700066.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vindemia erected this for her husband Quintus Arrius Aeneas, who died at 28 years of age, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700066.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated by Vindemia to her deserving husband, Quintus Arrius Aeneas, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-27700067.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Arrius Messianus Secundinae bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700067.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arrius Messianus for Secundina, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700067.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Arrius Messianus to Secundina, acknowledging her merit.

EDCS-27700068.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atania Marcia Marcellae filiae defunctae nnrum VIII\n
EDCS-27700068.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Atania Marcia, daughter, deceased at 8 years of age.
EDCS-27700068.summary: A funerary dedication for a young girl named Atania Marcia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700069.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et securitati Sexto Atilio Quartino defuncto nnorum XLII et Iuliae Secundinae uxori eius Sextus Atilius Manumissus alumno quem semper vice fili dilexit vivus posuit locus concessus a Cassia Afrodete sic\n
EDCS-27700069.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed and for the security of Sextus Atilius Quartinus, deceased at 42 years, and of his wife Iulia Secundina. Sextus Atilius Manumissus, his freedman, whom he always loved as a son, erected this monument while alive; the plot was granted by Cassia Aphrodite. Thus.
EDCS-27700069.summary: A funerary monument set up by a freedman for his deceased patron and his wife, indicating a close paternal relationship, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700070.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Avidiae Secundinae defuntae annorum XXII \n
EDCS-27700070.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Avidius Secundina, deceased at 22 years of age.
EDCS-27700070.summary: A funerary dedication for a young woman named Avidius Secundina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700071.text_edition: \n Aurelius Diogenes innocens vixit nnos XXV Lucianus fratri karissimo\n
EDCS-27700071.translation: Aurelius Diogenes, innocent, lived 25 years. Lucianus to his dearest brother.
EDCS-27700071.summary: A tombstone for Aurelius Diogenes, erected by his brother Lucianus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700072.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Epicteto Marcus Paenius pro us \n
EDCS-27700072.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Paenius for Aurelius Epictetus, ...
EDCS-27700072.summary: A funerary dedication to Aurelius Epictetus, possibly set up by Marcus Paenius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700073.text_edition: \n Aurelii Glycon et Valentia virgini vivi sibi posuerunt\n
EDCS-27700073.translation: Aurelius Glycon and Valentia, while alive, set this up for themselves.
EDCS-27700073.summary: A funerary monument erected by a couple, Aurelius Glycon and Valentia, for their future burial, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Maximo puero annorum V dierum XIIII plus minus filio carissimo posuit Aurelius Maximinus pater\n
EDCS-27700075.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Titus Aurelius Maximus, a boy of 5 years and 14 days, more or less, dearest son, erected Aurelius Maximinus, father.
EDCS-27700075.summary: A funerary monument erected by a father for his young son, Titus Aurelius Maximus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700076.text_edition: \n Aurelius Paes Vindemio filio pientissimo defuncto nnorum X \n
EDCS-27700076.translation: Aurelius Paes to his most dutiful son, Vindemius, deceased at 10 years of age.
EDCS-27700076.summary: A funerary dedication for a son named Vindemius, erected by his father Aurelius Paes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700077.translation: Aurelios Zosimos, who lived here [---]. To the Spirits of the Departed.
EDCS-27700077.summary: A funerary inscription mentioning Aurelios Zosimos and addressed to the Spirits of the Departed, possibly from a Greek-speaking context within the Roman Empire.

EDCS-27700078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Cassia Aelio Victorino marito pientissimo con sic quo vixit nnos XXV bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700078.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Cassia to her most dutiful husband, Aelius Victorinus, who lived well with her for 25 years, to him deserving well, erected this.
EDCS-27700078.summary: A funerary monument erected by a wife for her deserving husband, Aelius Victorinus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Cyerema C P \n
EDCS-27700079.translation: To the divine spirits. Aurelia Cyerema, freedwoman of the people.
EDCS-27700079.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelia Cyerema, identified as a freedwoman of the people, and is likely a tombstone dedication.

EDCS-27700080.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Flaviae coniugi inconparabili sic Iulius Innocentius et sibi vivens posuit\n
EDCS-27700080.translation: To the divine spirits. To Aurelia Flavia, his incomparable wife. Julius Innocentius placed this, for himself and for her, during his lifetime.
EDCS-27700080.summary: This tombstone, set up by Julius Innocentius for his wife Aurelia Flavia, is a memorial to an 'incomparable' spouse, placed during his own lifetime.

EDCS-27700081.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Maximinae quae vixsit sic annos quinqua<g=C>inta coniu<g=C>i di<g=C>nissimae maritus posuit \n
EDCS-27700081.translation: To the divine spirits. To Aurelia Maximina, who lived fifty years, a most devoted wife. Her husband placed this.
EDCS-27700081.summary: This inscription records the death of Aurelia Maximina, who lived fifty years, and was erected by her husband as a memorial to his 'most devoted' wife.

EDCS-27700082.text_edition: \n Aurelia Privata quae vixit annos plus minus XXXXXX Aurelius Donatianus matri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700082.translation: Aurelia Privata, who lived more or less fifty years. Aurelius Donatianus placed this for his well-deserving mother.
EDCS-27700082.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Aurelius Donatianus for his 'well-deserving' mother, Aurelia Privata, who lived approximately fifty years.

EDCS-27700083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Veneriae coniugi carissimae quae vixit annos <L=I>I Tertullianus Augusti libertus maritus posuit \n
EDCS-27700083.translation: To the divine spirits. To Aurelia Veneria, dearest wife, who lived 31 years. Tertullianus, freedman of Augustus, her husband, placed this.
EDCS-27700083.summary: This tombstone honors Aurelia Veneria, who died at the age of 31, and was erected by her husband, Tertullianus, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-27700084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Ursae quae vixit annis duobus diebus V Aurelius Ursinus pater dolens <tit=ITI>ulum <f=E>ecit \n
EDCS-27700084.translation: To the divine spirits. To Aurelia Ursa, who lived two years and five days. Aurelius Ursinus, her grieving father, made this monument.
EDCS-27700084.summary: Aurelius Ursinus, a grieving father, erected this monument to his daughter Aurelia Ursa, who died at the age of two years and five days.

EDCS-27700085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Bonitae alumnae defunctae nnorum XVIIII mensium IIII Clodia Crescentina bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700085.translation: To the divine spirits of Bonita, deceased foster-child, 19 years and 4 months old. Clodia Crescentina, for her deserving ward.
EDCS-27700085.summary: This funerary inscription honors Bonita, a foster-child who died at 19 years and 4 months old, dedicated by Clodia Crescentina for her deserving ward.

EDCS-27700086.text_edition: \n Aemilia Clementilla Tertullae Annae et duobus Apris fratribus infelicissimis annum quaternum mensium trium bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700086.translation: To Aemilia Clementilla, for Tertulla Anna and her two brothers Apris, most unfortunate, who lived four years and three months. For them deserving.
EDCS-27700086.summary: This inscription appears to be a memorial for Aemilia Clementilla, Tertulla Anna, and two brothers named Apris, all described as 'most unfortunate' and who lived to be four years and three months old.

EDCS-27700087.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agriae Annuclae mensium III Cinnamis mater infelix\n
EDCS-27700087.translation: To the divine spirits of Agria Annucla, 3 months old. Her unhappy mother Cinnamis.
EDCS-27700087.summary: This funerary inscription is from an unhappy mother, Cinnamis, for her infant daughter Agria Annucla, who died at three months old.

EDCS-27700088.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexto Atilio Niconi defuncto nnorum VIII Atilia Hediste et Nicon Nicostratu filio infelicissimo locum Pacuvia Tryfera donabit sic\n
EDCS-27700088.translation: To the divine spirits of Sextus Atilius Nicon, deceased, 8 years old. Atilia Hediste and Nicon Nicostratus, for their most unfortunate son, donated the place. Pacuvia Tryfera will donate.
EDCS-27700088.summary: This tombstone, dedicated by Atilia Hediste and Nicon Nicostratus for their unfortunate 8-year-old son Sextus Atilius Nicon, records the donation of the burial plot by Pacuvia Tryfera.

EDCS-27700089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attio Magno defuncto nnorum VII mensium II Privata filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-27700089.translation: To the departed spirits. For Attius Magnus, deceased at seven years and two months. His most unhappy mother, Privata.
EDCS-27700089.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Attius Magnus, dedicated by his mother, Privata.

EDCS-27700090.text_edition: \n Albucia Felicula Acte filiae annorum XVII et sibi\n
EDCS-27700090.translation: For Alburcia Felicula, and for her daughter Acte, aged seventeen, and for herself.
EDCS-27700090.summary: A funerary epitaph for Alburcia Felicula and her daughter Acte, likely a joint memorial set up by Alburcia.

EDCS-27700091.text_edition: \n Aurelio Annio et Liciniae Iuventillae Aurelia Annia filia parentibus\n
EDCS-27700091.translation: For Aurelius Annio and Licinia Iuventilla, from their daughter Aurelia Annia.
EDCS-27700091.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Annio and Licinia Iuventilla, dedicated by their daughter Aurelia Annia.

EDCS-27700092.text_edition: \n Aurelius Castus et Aurelia Iuliane Aurelio Casto filio qui decidit mensium quattuor et dierum novem\n
EDCS-27700092.translation: For Aurelius Castus and Aurelia Iuliane, and for their son Aurelius Castus, who died at four months and nine days.
EDCS-27700092.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child named Aurelius Castus, set up by his parents Aurelius Castus and Aurelia Iuliane.

EDCS-27700093.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius C Arg Turrania Aprilis marito\n
EDCS-27700093.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius C. Argius Turranus, and for his wife Aprilis.
EDCS-27700093.summary: A funerary epitaph to the departed spirits, commemorating Lucius C. Argius Turranus and his wife Aprilis.

EDCS-27700094.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Achillaeo liberto fidelissimo Titus Aurelius Pompeius patronus posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700094.translation: To the departed spirits. For Titus Aurelius Achillaeus, his most faithful freedman. Titus Aurelius Pompeius, his patron, set this up for one who deserved well.
EDCS-27700094.summary: A funerary epitaph for a freedman, Titus Aurelius Achillaeus, erected by his patron Titus Aurelius Pompeius.

EDCS-27700095.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27700095.text_edition: \n Aurelius Vindemius qui vixit annus! sexsaginta sic arcam sibi et coniugi suae carissimae Luciae posuit si qui autem de heredibus meis se ipsum vel alios suorum suae rum posuerit hered\n
EDCS-27700095.translation: Aurelius Vindemius, who lived sixty years, set up this tomb for himself and for his dearest wife Lucia. And whoever among my heirs places himself or any of his family here...
EDCS-27700095.summary: An epitaph for Aurelius Vindemius, who set up a tomb for himself and his wife Lucia, with a clause regarding future burials.

EDCS-27700124.text_edition: \n Caio Aemilio Ingenuo aedili et IIviro iure dicundo et Aemiliae Hippolytae matri Vestibulum et ambitus monumenti a bathro clatrorum intra loricam quae spectat in meridie ad viam munitam longa pedes XXXII lata pedes LV et a bathro clatrorum a parte dextra et sinistra et a postico omnium angulorum IIII lata pedes quinos denos Vestibulum et ambitus monumenti a bathro clatrorum intra loricam quae spectat in meridie ad viam munitam longa pedes XXXII lata pedes LV et a bathro clatrorum a parte dextra et sinistra et a postico omnium angulorum IIII lata pedes quinos denos\n
EDCS-27700124.translation: For Gaius Aemilius Ingenuus, aedile and duovir for dispensing justice, and for Aemilia Hippolyta, his mother. The vestibule and perimeter of the monument from the clathrate baths within the wall, which faces south towards the paved road, are 32 feet long and 55 feet wide, and from the clathrate baths on the right and left and at the rear, the corners, each 15 feet wide...
EDCS-27700124.summary: An inscription detailing the construction and dimensions of a monumental tomb, likely commissioned by or for Gaius Aemilius Ingenuus and his mother Aemilia Hippolyta.

EDCS-27700125.text_edition: \n Caio Albio Nigro Lucio Albio Cai filio Nigro Lucio Albio Luci filio Leugae aedili IIIIviro iure dicundo Caio Albio Luci filio Lepido decurioni Salonitanorum Albia Cai filia Lepida\n
EDCS-27700125.translation: For Gaius Albius Niger, son of Lucius Albius Niger; for Lucius Albius, son of Lucius, a decurion of the Leugae; for Gaius Albius Lepidus, son of Lucius, a decurion of Salonitae; for Albia Lepida, daughter of Gaius.
EDCS-27700125.summary: A funerary monument commemorating several members of the Albius family, including decurions from Salonitae and Leugae.

EDCS-27700126.text_edition: \n Caio Albucio Cai filio Tromentina Menippo decurioni Salonis aedili marito et Caio Albucio Cai filio Tromentina Prociliano decurioni Salonis et Issae aedili defuncto nnorum IXXX et Caio Albucio Cai filio Sergia Menippo decurioni Issae defuncto nnorum IXX filiis et Caio Ligurio Titiano defuncto nnorum XXX fratri Liguria Procilla quae et Albucia et sibi viva posuit\n
EDCS-27700126.translation: For Gaius Albucio, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe, Menippus, decurion of Salonissae, aedile, and husband; and for Gaius Albucio, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe, Procilianus, decurion of Salonissae and Issae, aedile, deceased at eighty years; and for Gaius Albucio, son of Gaius, of the Sergius tribe, Menippus, decurion of Issae, deceased at seventy years, his sons; and for Gaius Ligurio Titianus, deceased at thirty years, his brother. Liguria Procilla, who was also Alburcia, set this up while alive for herself.
EDCS-27700126.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Liguria Procilla (also known as Alburcia) for her deceased husband, sons, brother, and herself, listing their roles and ages.

EDCS-27700127.text_edition: \n Tertullae annorum XIIX Antoniae Primigeniae filius Lucio Antonio Luci filio Tromentina Firmo aedili IIIIviro iure dicundo praefecto fabrum procuratori Augusti filius Lucio Antonio Expectato frater vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-27700127.translation: Lucius Antonius Expectatus, son of Lucius Antonius Tromentinus, brother, erected this for himself and his family to Tertulla, aged 18, and to Antonia Primigenia, his father, who was quaestor, aedile, duovir, prefect of engineers, and procurator of Augustus.
EDCS-27700127.summary: This funerary monument was erected by a son for his deceased parents and sister.

EDCS-27700128.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Aufidio Epicteto decurioni et \n
EDCS-27700128.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Aufidius Epictetus, decurion...
EDCS-27700128.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication to Lucius Aufidius Epictetus, a decurion.

EDCS-27700129.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Aureli Hermogenis equitis Romani decurionis aedilis curulis defuncti annorum XX dierum III Marcus Aurelius Auggustorum libertus Hermes procurator piisimo filio\n
EDCS-27700129.translation: Marcus Aurelius Hermes, freedman of the Augusti and procurator, [erected this] to his most dutiful son, Marcus Aurelius Hermogenes, Roman equestrian, decurion, curule aedile, who died at the age of 20 years and 3 days.
EDCS-27700129.summary: A procurator dedicated this funerary monument to his son, who held the office of decurion and curule aedile.

EDCS-27700130.text_edition: \n Aurelio Severiano decurioni Salonitanorum et Aufidiae Soteridi parentibus Aufidia Rustica et Aurelius Rufinus filii\n
EDCS-27700130.translation: To Aurelius Severianus, decurion of the people of Salona, and Aufidia Soteris, their children Aufidia Rustica and Aurelius Rufinus [erected this].
EDCS-27700130.summary: This inscription records the dedication of children to their deceased parents, both of whom were decurions.

EDCS-27700131.text_edition: \n Caio Clodio Cai filio Sergia Fadieno IIIIviro iure dicundo Fadia Cai filia Marcellina mater locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-27700131.translation: Fadia Marcellina, daughter of Gaius, mother, [dedicated this] to Gaius Clodius Fadienus, son of Gaius, duovir, [in accordance with] a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-27700131.summary: A mother dedicated this monument to her son, who held the office of duovir, by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-27700132.text_edition: \n Cai libertus Eupor Cai fil- decuri C THEVR COI\n
EDCS-27700132.translation: Gaius Eupor, freedman of Gaius, son of Gaius, decurion...
EDCS-27700132.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording the name and affiliation of a decurion.

EDCS-27700133.text_edition: \n Papiria Rhome viva fecit sibi et Papiriae Cladillae nnorum XXIX et Publio Papirio Proculo nnorum XIX filiis condidit hic miseri mater duo funera partus ossaque non iustis intulit exequiis tegula nam Romae Proculum prolapsa peremit pressit Sipunti sic pressa Cladilla rogum vota parens nocuere tibi qui numina saeva ut plura eriperent plura dedere bona et Publio Papirio Clado viro et Publio Papirio Celerioni et Papiriae Hispanillae libertis Quintus Nassius Quinti filius Tromentina Certus Draco aedilis IIIIvir iure dicundo vivus fecit sibi et Tulliae Intumae matri hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-27700133.translation: Papiria Rhome, alive, erected this for herself and for her sons Publius Papirius Proculus, aged 19, and Publius Papirius Proculus, aged 20 years and 3 days, who died tragically. The mother, in her grief, provided two burials and brought the bones, but not with proper rites, for the tomb. For a tile fell on Proculus in Rome, and Cladilla was crushed in the same way on her pyre at Sipontum. The parent's vows were harmful to you, as if the savage gods wished to take more and gave more good things. And for Publius Papirius Cladus, her husband, and for Publius Papirius Celerion and Papiria Hispanilla, freedmen. Quintus Nassius Certus Draco, son of Quintus, of the Tromentina tribe, aedile, duovir, erected this monument for himself while alive, and for his mother Tullia Intuma. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-27700133.summary: This is a complex funerary monument erected by a grieving mother for her deceased sons, husband, and freedmen, detailing the tragic deaths of her sons and asserting the monument's non-hereditary status.

EDCS-27700134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Terentio Caesiano decurioni aedili defuncto annorum XXIII Terentius Primus libertus\n
EDCS-27700134.translation: To the departed spirits of Titus Terentius Caesianus, decurion, aedile, who died at the age of 23 years. Terentius Primus, his freedman, [erected this].
EDCS-27700134.summary: A freedman erected this funerary monument to his patron, Titus Terentius Caesianus, who was a decurion and aedile.

EDCS-27700135.text_edition: \n Tito Turranio Titi filio Vero aedili Scardonae Arria Cai filia Maxima filio pientissimo fecit et sibi et Caio Maecio Maximo coniugi libertis libertabusque suis\n
EDCS-27700135.translation: Arria Maxima, daughter of Gaius, made this for her most devoted son, Titus Turranius Verus, aedile of Scardona, and for herself, and for her husband Gaius Maecius Maximus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-27700135.summary: A mother dedicated this monument to her son, an aedile, and also for herself, her husband, and their freedpersons.

EDCS-27700136.text_edition: \n DDis MManibus Valeriae Ursinae Titi filiae coniugi incomparabili defunctae annorum mensium VI dierum V Aurelius Aquila decurio Patavissensis negotiator ex provincia Dacia bene merenti posuit et sibi cum qua vixit annos VII sine ulla querella\n
EDCS-27700136.translation: To the departed spirits of Valeria Ursinia, daughter of Titus, an incomparable wife, who died at the age of 6 months and 5 days. Aurelius Aquila, decurion from Patavium, a merchant from the province of Dacia, placed this for her, who deserved well, and for himself, with whom he lived for 7 years without any complaint.
EDCS-27700136.summary: A decurion and merchant from Dacia erected this monument to his wife, who died as an infant, and also for himself.

EDCS-27700138.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record dedicatory information for a monument, possibly related to a decurion, with names and titles mostly lost.

EDCS-27700140.text_edition: \n Titus Ancharius Anthus IIIIIIvir Augustalis titulum fieri iussit ex HS VIsex milibus sibi et Petroniae Prothesi uxori et libertis heredibusque Fortunato Tito Zosimo VIviris Zopyro Epaphrodito Substituto Abascanto Petroniae Iucundae Anchario Iusto et omnibus libertis libertabusque\n
EDCS-27700140.translation: Titus Ancharius Anthus, a member of the six-man board of Augustales, ordered the monument to be made at a cost of 6,000 sesterces for himself and for Petronia Prothes, his wife, and for their freedmen and heirs: Fortunatus, Titus Zosimus, a member of the six-man board of Augustales, Zopyrus, Epaphroditus, Substitute, Abascanthus, Petronia Iucunda, Ancharus Justus, and for all their freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-27700140.summary: This inscription records the construction of a funerary monument by Titus Ancharius Anthus, a sevir Augustalis, for himself, his wife, and their freedmen and heirs, detailing the cost and naming many individuals.

EDCS-27700141.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Basso Augustali Claudia Tiberi f(ilia) Thetis marito bene merenti et Titus Flavius Felix pro parte quinta patrono bono hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes I-X in agro pedes XXV\n
EDCS-27700141.translation: Claudia Thetis, daughter of Tiberius, for her deserving husband Titus Flavius Bassus, freedman of the emperor, a member of the Augustales, and Titus Flavius Felix, for his fifth share, [erected this monument for his] good patron; this monument shall not follow the heir; in front 10 feet, in depth 25 feet.
EDCS-27700141.summary: This inscription records the erection of a funerary monument by Claudia Thetis for her husband Titus Flavius Bassus, an Augustalis, and mentions Titus Flavius Felix, specifying dimensions and noting it is not inheritable.

EDCS-27700142.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Sceptus Admeti Augusti liberti libertus IIIIIIvir Augustalis vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Coetonidi uxori Caio Iulio Cai filio Tromentina Admeto filio Caio Iulio Cai filio Tromentina Aquilae filio Lucio Iulio Cai filio Tromentina Scepto filio Iuliae Cai filiae Tromentina Admetidi filiae Caio Iulio |(mulieris) liberto Triumphali filio Iuliae Cai libertae Romanae filiae Iuliae |(mulieris) libertae Scepsidi filiae Iuliae |(mulieris) libertae Primae uxori defunctae Niso Tiberi Claudi Augusti servo genero defuncto in hoc monumentum veto aliter ossua deponi quam Primae et nisi sunt in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XXX\n
EDCS-27700142.translation: Gaius Julius Sceptus, freedman of Admetus, freedman of the emperor, a member of the six-man board of Augustales, made this while alive for himself and for his wife Iulia Coetonis, for his son Gaius Julius Tromentinus, son of Gaius, for Admetus, son of Gaius Julius Tromentinus, for Aquila, son of Gaius Julius Tromentinus, for Sceptus, son of Lucius Julius Tromentinus, for his daughter Iulia, daughter of Gaius, for Admetis, daughter of Gaius Julius, son of a woman, for Triumphalis, son of a freedman of Iulia Romana, daughter of a woman, for Scepsis, daughter of a freedman of Iulia, daughter of a woman, and for his deceased wife Prima, daughter of Iulia, daughter of a woman, and for his deceased son-in-law Niso, son of Tiberius Claudius, freedman of the emperor. Within this monument, I forbid bones to be deposited other than those of Prima and Niso. In front 20 feet, in depth 30 feet.
EDCS-27700142.summary: This lengthy inscription details the construction of a family tomb by Gaius Julius Sceptus, an Augustalis, for himself, his wife, numerous children, and in-laws, with specific burial restrictions and measurements.

EDCS-27700143.text_edition: \n Decimus Petronius Dasius IIIIIIvir Decimus Petronius Cilo IIIIIIvir\n
EDCS-27700143.translation: Decimus Petronius Dasius, member of the six-man board of Augustales; Decimus Petronius Cilo, member of the six-man board of Augustales.
EDCS-27700143.summary: This brief inscription simply records the names and offices of two individuals, Decimus Petronius Dasius and Decimus Petronius Cilo, both members of the sevirate Augustales.

EDCS-27700144.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Pomponius Draco Augustalis vivus posuit sibi et Iuliae Anniae coniugi suae veto autem in hac arca alium corpus inferri aut ossua poni si quis autem intulerit dabit rei publicae Salonitanorum HS nummum Xmilia et ne quis ante arcam hanc aliam ponat\n
EDCS-27700144.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Pomponius Draco, an Augustalis, erected this while alive for himself and for his wife Iulia Annia. I forbid, however, any other body or bones to be placed in this tomb; whoever does so shall pay the public treasury of the people of Salona 10,000 sesterces, and no one shall place another tomb before this one.
EDCS-27700144.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Lucius Pomponius Draco, an Augustalis, for himself and his wife, including a strict prohibition against further burials and a penalty for violations, intended for the public treasury of Salona.

EDCS-27700145.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Vettius Augustalis Vettia coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-27700145.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Vettius, an Augustalis, erected this for his wife Vettia.
EDCS-27700145.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary dedication by Titus Vettius, an Augustalis, for his wife Vettia.

EDCS-27700146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Vettio Eutycheti IIIIIIviro Augustali Caius Vettius Eucharistus patri pientissimo et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-27700146.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Vettius Eucharistus made this for his most devoted father Caius Vettius Eutychetes, a member of the six-man board of Augustales, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-27700146.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Gaius Vettius Eucharistus for his father Caius Vettius Eutychetes, an Augustalis, and for himself.

EDCS-27700147.text_edition: \n Tito Vettio Titi liberto Fortunato Augustali Vettia Agape marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700147.translation: Vettia Agape for her deserving husband Titus Vettius Fortunatus, freedman of Titus, an Augustalis.
EDCS-27700147.summary: This inscription records a funerary dedication by Vettia Agape for her husband Titus Vettius Fortunatus, who was a freedman and an Augustalis.

EDCS-27700148.text_edition: \n Lucius Naevius Luci libertus Diogenes magister conlegii sevirum quinquennalis aedem de sua pecunia fecit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-27700148.translation: Lucius Naevius Diogenes, freedman of Lucius, master of the guild of the quinquennial sevirate, made and dedicated this temple from his own funds.
EDCS-27700148.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple by Lucius Naevius Diogenes, a freedman who held the important position of quinquennial magistrate in the guild of the sevirate.

EDCS-27700149.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27700149.text_edition: \n Aurelius Ursacius Salonitanus ex collegio Veneris et Aurelia Vitalia vivis sibi posuerunt hanc arca tradita ab Aurelia Lupa Atilio Primo\n
EDCS-27700149.translation: Aurelius Ursacius of Salona from the college of Venus and Aurelia Vitalia erected this chest for themselves while alive, handed over by Aurelia Lupa to Attilus Primus.
EDCS-27700149.summary: This inscription records the erection of a chest by Aurelius Ursacius and Aurelia Vitalia, possibly related to the college of Venus.

EDCS-27700150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Augustiano magistro symforariorum bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700150.translation: To Augustianus, master of the symphorarii, well deserving.
EDCS-27700150.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Augustianus, a well-regarded master of the symphorarii, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27700151.text_edition: \n Aurelia Vernilla plumbaria sibi et Aurelio Lucio marito suo et Aureliae Stercoriae filiae posuit quod si qui supeper haec corpora posuerit inferet rei publicae |(denarios) Ccentum milia\n
EDCS-27700151.translation: Aurelia Vernilla, a plumber, erected this for herself, her husband Aurelius Lucius, and her daughter Aurelia Stercoria; if anyone places bodies over this, they will incur a penalty of one hundred thousand denarii to the state.
EDCS-27700151.summary: This funerary inscription by Aurelia Vernilla for her family also serves as a legal warning against disturbing the burial site, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27700152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caniae Urbanae vilicae qua<e=F> vixit nnos LV Cania Ursina filia et Attius Verecundianus gener matri incomparabili \n
EDCS-27700152.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Cania Urbana, the farm manager, who lived 55 years; her daughter Cania Ursina and her son-in-law Attius Verecundianus erected this for their incomparable mother.
EDCS-27700152.summary: This tombstone honors Cania Urbana, a farm manager, set up by her daughter and son-in-law, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27700153.text_edition: \n Caius Cassius Urbanus Cassia Valentina vivi fecerunt s<i=T>bi concessum locum a Flavio Marcellino magistro introito sic libero \n
EDCS-27700153.translation: Caius Cassius Urbanus and Cassia Valentina made this for themselves while alive, a place granted by Flavius Marcellinus, the master, with free access.
EDCS-27700153.summary: This inscription records a burial plot reserved by Caius Cassius Urbanus and Cassia Valentina, granted by a magistrate named Flavius Marcellinus.

EDCS-27700154.text_edition: \n Decimus Cornelius Herma et Cornelia Tyche domo Ravenna parentes infelicissimi filiabus pientissimis Corneliae Helpidi filiae defunctae nnorum XVIII Corneliae Helpiste filiae defunctae nnorum XVIIII Restutae filiae defunctae nnorum VII libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-27700154.translation: Decimus Cornelius Herma and Cornelia Tyche, from Ravenna, most unhappy parents, for their most dutiful daughters, Cornelia Helpis, daughter deceased aged 18, Cornelia Helpiste, daughter deceased aged 19, and Restuta, daughter deceased aged 7, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-27700154.summary: This is a family funerary monument set up by grieving parents for their deceased daughters and future descendants, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27700155.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Ursiliano iuveni infelicissimo defuncto nnorum plus minus XXVIII artis medicinae industriae primae Aemiliea Spifar<i>lla con<i=l>ugi pientissimo et infelicissimo cum quo vixit annis IIII et sibi posuit \n
EDCS-27700155.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Flavius Ursilianus, a most unhappy youth deceased aged more or less 28, of the first skill in the art of medicine; Aemilia Spifarella set this up for her most dutiful and unhappy husband, with whom she lived for 4 years, and for herself.
EDCS-27700155.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Aemilia Spifarella for her young, skilled physician husband, Flavius Ursilianus, and herself, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27700156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Aprilis mensor\n
EDCS-27700156.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Caius Julius Aprilis, the surveyor.
EDCS-27700156.summary: This is a brief funerary dedication to Caius Julius Aprilis, a surveyor, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-27700157.text_edition: \n Lucius Iulius Cai filius Tromentina Clemens negotiator titulum fieri iussit sibi et Caio Iulio Cai filio Tromentina Maximo patri et Noniae Lacri filiae Marullae matri et Lucio Iulio Luci filio Tromentina Maximo filio et Iuliae Luci filiae Marullae filiae et Iuliae Luci filiae Clementillae filiae\n
EDCS-27700157.translation: Lucius Julius Clemens, son of Caius, of the Tromentina tribe, a merchant, ordered this memorial to be made for himself, his father Caius Maximus, son of Caius, of the Tromentina tribe; his mother Nonia Marulla, daughter of Lacris; his son Lucius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe; his daughter Julia Marulla, daughter of Lucius; and his daughter Julia Clementilla, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-27700157.summary: This inscription records a merchant, Lucius Julius Clemens, commissioning a family memorial for himself and several relatives across multiple generations, probably from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27700158.text_edition: \n Novesis servus agens et Iuventilla Suriscae filiae infelicissimae quae vixit annis II mensibus III bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700158.translation: Novensis, the acting slave, and Iuventilla, for her most unhappy daughter Surisca, who lived 2 years and 3 months, well deserving.
EDCS-27700158.summary: This funerary inscription by Novensis and Iuventilla is for their young daughter Surisca, indicating a possible parent-child or master-slave relationship.

EDCS-27700159.text_edition: \n Placentino retiario qui vicxit sic annos XX posuit retiaarius Bacchius colliberto de suo sibi\n
EDCS-27700159.translation: To Placentinus, a retiarius (net-fighter) who lived 20 years, his fellow-soldier Baccius erected this net for himself from his own resources.
EDCS-27700159.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Placentinus, a retiarius, erected by his comrade Baccius.

EDCS-27700160.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Publicius Aemilianus rhetor nationem Afer vixit annos XLVII menses VIIII dies VII horas noctis V\n
EDCS-27700160.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Publicius Aemilianus, a teacher of rhetoric from Africa, lived 47 years, 9 months, 7 days, and 5 night hours.
EDCS-27700160.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Quintus Publicius Aemilianus, an African rhetorician, and specifies his lifespan.

EDCS-27700161.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sempronio Fortunato mensori aedificiorum natione Kampanus defuncto nnorum XXVIII mensium IIII dierum XI Appia Victoria coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700161.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Appia Victoria placed this for her well-deserving husband, Sempronius Fortunatus, a builder from Campania, who died at the age of 28 years, 4 months, and 11 days.
EDCS-27700161.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sempronius Fortunatus, a builder from Campania, erected by his wife Appia Victoria.

EDCS-27700162.summary: This is a short inscription identifying Venustus, likely a tombstone, as an archer (pharetrarius).

EDCS-27700163.text_edition: \n Caius Villius Sexti <f=T>ilius domo Aquileia monumentum vivos fecit \n
EDCS-27700163.translation: Gaius Villius, son of Sextus, of the Tilian tribe, from Aquileia, made this monument while alive.
EDCS-27700163.summary: This is an inscription for a self-made tombstone (monumentum vivos fecit) by Gaius Villius from Aquileia.

EDCS-27700164.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Claudiano defuncto nnorum IIII dierum XLVIII Publius Aelius Ursinus et Claudia Festiva parentes filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-27700164.translation: To Publius Aelius Claudianus, who died at the age of 4 years and 48 days. Publius Aelius Ursinus and Claudia Festiva, his parents, [erected this] for their most unhappy son.
EDCS-27700164.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young child, Publius Aelius Claudianus, erected by his grieving parents.

EDCS-27700165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Abanto defuncto conserva posuit\n
EDCS-27700165.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Abanto, who died, his female companion placed this.
EDCS-27700165.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Abanto, placed by his female companion (conserva).

EDCS-27700166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fresia Lamia Fresiae Acceptae alumnae vixit nnos II menses III dies XV\n
EDCS-27700166.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Fresia Lamia, nursling of Fresia Accepta, lived 2 years, 3 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-27700166.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Fresia Lamia, a nursling, who died at age 2.

EDCS-27700167.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Actes Lupulus et Felix et Firminus matri piissimae \n
EDCS-27700167.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lupulus and Felix and Firminus [erected this] for their most devoted mother.
EDCS-27700167.summary: This funerary inscription records Lupulus, Felix, and Firminus dedicating a monument to their mother.

EDCS-27700168.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aebutio Tertullino defuncto nnorum XXV Amindianus filius posuit\n
EDCS-27700168.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Amindianus, his son, placed this for Aebutius Tertullinus, who died at the age of 25.
EDCS-27700168.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aebutius Tertullinus at age 25, with the monument erected by his son Amindianus.

EDCS-27700169.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aebutriae Quartae defunctae annorum XXIIII Barbius Vindemio coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700169.translation: To the Divine Shades. Barbius Vindemius erected this for his deserving wife, Aebutria Quarta, who died at the age of twenty-four.
EDCS-27700169.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aebutria Quarta, set up by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700170.text_edition: \n Lucio Aelio Pederoti Amaranthus Luci Aelii filius clementissimus bene merenti hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-27700170.translation: Amaranthus, son of Lucius Aelius, erected this monument for his most kind master, Lucius Aelius Pederos, who is not to be succeeded by an heir.
EDCS-27700170.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Aelius Pederos, dedicated by his freedman Amaranthus, indicating it is a family burial plot not passing to collateral heirs.

EDCS-27700171.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Publi liberto Satyro Lyris defuncto nnorum XL Papiria Helpidys coniugi rarissimo et incomparabilis pietatis loco concesso a Fulvio Hermete in fronte pedes IIII semissem et sibi\n
EDCS-27700171.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Publius Aelius Satyros, freedman of Publius, who died at the age of forty, a most rare and incomparably devoted husband. Granted in lieu by Fulvius Hermetes, four and a half feet in front and for himself.
EDCS-27700171.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Satyros, a freedman, dedicated by his wife Papiria Helpidys, with specific land dimensions.

EDCS-27700172.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Aelia.
EDCS-27700172.summary: A very brief funerary inscription to Aelia, likely part of a larger monument or a simple grave marker.

EDCS-27700173.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Marciae coniugi dulcissimae bene merenti quae bene vixit annis XXXV Turranius Firminianus maritus et Aelia Saturnina filia matri piissimae\n
EDCS-27700173.translation: To the Divine Shades. For her most sweet and deserving wife, Aelia Marcia, who lived a good life for thirty-five years. Erected by her husband Turranius Firminianus and her most devoted daughter Aelia Saturnina.
EDCS-27700173.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Marcia, set up by her husband and daughter, noting her virtuous life and age at death.

EDCS-27700174.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Salviae maritae Ulpius Primus et Ulpia Supera matri infelicissimae quae vicsit! cum Aelia Felicula annorum LV bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700174.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Aelia Salvia, his wife. Ulpius Primus and Ulpia Supera erected this for their most unhappy mother, who lived with Aelia Felicula for fifty-five years, deserving [of this memorial].
EDCS-27700174.summary: A funerary inscription for Aelia Salvia, erected by her husband Ulpius Primus and daughter Ulpia Supera, also mentioning Aelia Felicula.

EDCS-27700175.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Tertullinae filiae piae defunctae annorum XII mensium VIII C Ursulus et Aelia Telesphoris parentes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27700175.translation: To the Divine Shades. For their deserving daughter, Aelia Tertullina, pious, who died at the age of twelve years and eight months. Erected by her parents, C. Ursulus and Aelia Telesphoris.
EDCS-27700175.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Tertullina, a twelve-year-old girl, erected by her parents.

EDCS-27700176.text_edition: \n Marco Iunio Iusto decurioni coloniae Salonitanorum aedili IIviro pontifici Marcus Iunius Dafnus \n
EDCS-27700176.translation: For Marcus Junius Justus, decurion of the colony of Salona, twice aedile, pontiff. Marcus Junius Dafnus.
EDCS-27700176.summary: An inscription honoring Marcus Junius Justus for his public offices in Salona, likely set up by a relative.

EDCS-27700177.text_edition: \n Caio Orchivio Amempto decurioni nnorum XIIX Orchivia Phoebe mater fecit sibi et Amempto Caesaris Augusti dispensatori coniugi et libertis libertabusque posterisque suis et eorum et Rhodino Amempti Caesaris in fronte cum taberna pedes LII in agro pedes XLV hoc monumentum sive sepulchrum est extran<e=I>um heredem non sequetur \n
EDCS-27700177.translation: Orchivia Phoebe erected this monument or tomb for her deserving son, Caio Orchivio Amempto, decurion, aged eighteen, for herself and for Amemptus, freedman of Caesar Augustus, her husband, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and for those of Rhodinus Amemptus, freedman of Caesar; fifty-two feet in front with a shop, forty-five feet of land. This monument or tomb is outside and not to be succeeded by an heir.
EDCS-27700177.summary: A funerary monument for Caio Orchivio Amempto, erected by his mother Orchivia Phoebe, also for herself, her husband, and other relatives, specifying property dimensions and excluding collateral heirs.

EDCS-27700178.text_edition: \n Publius Caetennius Agathias vivus fecit sibi et Caetenniae Publi libertae Venus<t=I>ae filiae et Publio Caetennio Venus<t=I>o VIviro filio eius \n
EDCS-27700178.translation: Publius Caetennius Agathias erected this alive for himself, and for Caetennia Venusia, freedwoman of Publius, his daughter, and for Publius Caetennius Venusus, son of his freedman, a duovir.
EDCS-27700178.summary: A funerary monument erected by Publius Caetennius Agathias for himself, his daughter, and his freedman's son, indicating a family burial plot.

EDCS-27700179.text_edition: \n Caius Clodius Felix IIIIIIvir Augustalis vivus fecit sibi et Clodiae Primigeniae filiae posterisque suis\n
EDCS-27700179.translation: Caius Clodius Felix, a priest of the imperial cult, erected this for himself, his daughter Clodia Primigenia, and their descendants during his lifetime.
EDCS-27700179.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caius Clodius Felix, a priest of the imperial cult, for himself, his daughter, and their descendants, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700180.text_edition: \n Publius Fundanius Philologus Augustalis titulum fieri iussit sibi et Fundaniae Egloge libertae ex uxori Zosimus et Felix liberti et heredes faciundum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27700180.translation: Publius Fundanius Philologus, an Augustalis priest, ordered this monument to be made for himself and for Fundania Egloge, his freedwoman and former wife. Zosimus and Felix, freedmen, and his heirs supervised its construction.
EDCS-27700180.summary: This is a funerary monument initiated by Publius Fundanius Philologus, a priest of the imperial cult, for himself, his freedwoman/wife, and others, with construction overseen by his freedmen and heirs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700181.text_edition: \n Dibus Manibus Tulliae Ianuariae defunctae nnum XL Caius Albucius Publius sexvir Augustalis coniugi rarissimae et sibi posuit locus datus a Flavio Sozonte\n
EDCS-27700181.translation: To the Divine Shades: Caius Albucius Publius, a sexvir Augustalis, erected this for his most rare wife Tullia Ianuaria, who died at 40 years of age, and for himself. The plot was granted by Flavius Sozontes.
EDCS-27700181.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caius Albucius Publius, a local religious official, for his deceased wife Tullia Ianuaria and himself, with the burial plot provided by Flavius Sozontes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700182.text_edition: \n Aurelius Candianus ex collegio Veneris sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-27700182.translation: Aurelius Candianus, of the college of Venus, erected this for himself and his family.
EDCS-27700182.summary: This is a dedication erected by Aurelius Candianus, a member of a religious association dedicated to Venus, for himself and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700183.text_edition: \n Aurelius Quintianus decurio collegii fabrum et centonariorum qui vixit nnos plus minus LI menses V dies X vivus sibi posuit et Aureliae Ianuariae coniugi suae cot sic si quis aeam sic arcam post mortem eorum aperire voluerit inferet decuriae meae |(denarios) XXV\n
EDCS-27700183.translation: Aurelius Quintianus, a decurion of the college of builders and tent-makers, who lived for about 51 years, 5 months, and 10 days, erected this tomb for himself during his lifetime, and for Aurelia Ianuaria, his wife. Should anyone dare to open this tomb after their death, they will incur a penalty of 25 denarii payable to my decury.
EDCS-27700183.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Aurelius Quintianus, a leader in a guild of builders and tent-makers, for himself and his wife, including a financial penalty for desecration, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700184.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lupioni Magnus collegae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-27700184.translation: To the Divine Shades: This was erected by his colleague for Lupio Magnus, a deserving colleague.
EDCS-27700184.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a colleague for the deceased Lupio Magnus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700185.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Agrius Plocamus Caio Agrio Claro paedagogo bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-27700185.translation: To the Divine Shades: Caius Agrius Plocamus erected this monument for his deserving teacher, Caius Agrius Claros.
EDCS-27700185.summary: This is a funerary dedication erected by Caius Agrius Plocamus for his well-deserving teacher, Caius Agrius Claros, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700186.text_edition: \n Asidonius Agathopus et Aurelia Luxuria qui in se bene lavoraverunt sic quae vixit annis XXII vivi sibi fecerunt loco concesso a Iulio Clementiano Asidonius ceriolarius\n
EDCS-27700186.translation: Asidonius Agathopus and Aurelia Luxuria, who worked well for us, erected this while alive for themselves. Asidonius, a wax-maker, was granted this plot by Iulius Clementianus.
EDCS-27700186.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Asidonius Agathopus and Aurelia Luxuria for themselves, with the plot granted by Iulius Clementianus, a wax-maker, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Rufina Helpidi ornatrici bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700187.translation: To the Divine Shades: Aurelia Rufina erected this monument for Helpidius, a deserving ornament-maker.
EDCS-27700187.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Aurelia Rufina for a deserving colleague, Helpidius, likely a craftsman, in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700188.text_edition: \n C A vilico vixit annos bene merenti posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-27700188.translation: C. A., a deserving overseer, lived for [number of years]; he erected this for [himself] and for himself.
EDCS-27700188.summary: This is a funerary dedication for C. A., a deserving overseer (vilicus), who erected it for himself and likely for others, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700189.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Arcadiae Onesimus Asclepi vicarius coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700189.translation: To the Divine Manes of Aelia Arcadia, her freedman Asclepius, her deputy, set this up for his deserving wife.
EDCS-27700189.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a freedman to his deceased wife, Aelia Arcadia, who is described as deserving.

EDCS-27700190.text_edition: \n Aeliae Felicissimae coniugi karissimae Platorius Agrippa bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27700190.translation: Platorius Agrippa set this up for his dearest wife, Aelia Festissima, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700190.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Platorius Agrippa for his beloved wife, Aelia Festissima.

EDCS-27700191.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of Publius Aemilius.

EDCS-27700192.text_edition: \n R COL quaestori IIviro ECCAL nto \n
EDCS-27700192.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription that seems to record career titles of an official.

EDCS-27700194.text_edition: \n Lucius Varius Luci libertus Ver IIIIIIvir Augustalis libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum hoc monumentum sive sepulcrum heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes\n
EDCS-27700194.translation: Lucius Varius Verus, freedman of Lucius, who was an Augustalis and duovir of freedmen, set up this monument or tomb for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants; it will not pass to his heir; in front 20 feet, in the field...
EDCS-27700194.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman named Lucius Varius Verus, detailing his status and provisions for his descendants' burials.

EDCS-27700195.text_edition: \n Eutycheti Vetti Bathylli servo domo Aquileiae Menelaus et Peculiaris conservo bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700195.translation: For Eutyches, servant of Vettius Bathyllus, from Aquileia, Menelaus and Peculiaris set this up for their deserving fellow servant.
EDCS-27700195.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by fellow servants for a deceased servant named Eutyches from Aquileia.

EDCS-27700196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturnino Augusti nsotri vernae mensori Florentina coniux bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700196.translation: To the Divine Manes of Saturninus, our Emperor's freedman and measurer, his wife Florentina set this up for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700196.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Saturninus, an imperial freedman and measurer, by his wife Florentina.

EDCS-27700197.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valentinae alumnae et delicatae filiae Valentionis serbi sic vilici infelicissimae quae vixit nnos IIII Celerinus dominus posuit\n
EDCS-27700197.translation: To the Divine Manes of Valentina, the cherished foster-child and daughter of Valention, a slave, indeed a steward, most unfortunate, who lived 4 years, Celerinus her master set this up.
EDCS-27700197.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a master for his slave girl, Valentina, who died at a young age and served as a steward.

EDCS-27700198.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Cai filio Restituto filio Caio Valerio Cai liberto Restituto patri negotiatori vinario Marcus Nassius Sotericus amico bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700198.translation: For Gaius Valerius Restitutus, son of Gaius Valerius Restitutus, and for Gaius Valerius Restitutus, freedman of Gaius, his father, a wine merchant, Marcus Nassius Sotericus set this up for a deserving friend.
EDCS-27700198.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Marcus Nassius Sotericus for a friend, honoring his father and the friend himself, who was a wine merchant.

EDCS-27700199.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Primitivo marito pientissimo Calventia Septimina bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700199.translation: To the Divine Manes of Aelius Primitivus, her most devoted husband, Calventia Septimina set this up for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-27700199.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Calventia Septimina to her devoted husband, Aelius Primitivus.

EDCS-27700200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Rufinae coniugi incomparabili quae vixit nnos XXXVII Iulius Menander et sibi\n
EDCS-27700200.translation: To the Divine Manes of Aelia Rufina, my incomparable wife, who lived 37 years. Iulius Menander (dedicated this) for himself and her.
EDCS-27700200.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Iulius Menander to his wife Aelia Rufina, including his own provision for burial.

EDCS-27700201.text_edition: \n Aeliae Ursinae Umbria Maxima matri piissimae posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-27700201.translation: Umbria Maxima placed (this) for her most dutiful mother, Aelia Ursinia, deserving of all praise.
EDCS-27700201.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Umbria Maxima for her mother Aelia Ursinia.

EDCS-27700203.text_edition: \n Aelia Candida se viva posuit sibi et coniugi Publio Oct(avio) Fortunato\n
EDCS-27700203.translation: Aelia Candida placed (this) for herself while alive and for her husband Publius Octavius Fortunatus.
EDCS-27700203.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Aelia Candida for herself and her husband Publius Octavius Fortunatus.

EDCS-27700204.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Constantilla et Aelia Concordia <h=A>eredes Gnaeo Pompeio Threptioni posuaerunt vixit annos XXXX \n
EDCS-27700204.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia Constantilla and Aelia Concordia, heirs, placed (this) for Gnaeus Pompeius Threption, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-27700204.summary: This is a funerary epitaph placed by the heirs Aelia Constantilla and Aelia Concordia for Gnaeus Pompeius Threption.

EDCS-27700205.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Victoriae defunctae annorum VIIII dierum XXX mater filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-27700205.translation: To the Divine Manes of Aelia Victoria, deceased, aged 9 years and 30 days. Her most dutiful mother (dedicated this) for her most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-27700205.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her young daughter, Aelia Victoria.

EDCS-27700206.text_edition: \n N VLRF INF E annorum XXVI Fulvius Hermes collega et consacranius bene merenti et Eucida soror\n
EDCS-27700206.translation: [---]. For the colleague and fellow priest Eucidas, aged 26 years. Fulvius Hermes (dedicated this) for the deserving one and Eucida, his sister.
EDCS-27700206.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eucidas, a priest, who died at 26, dedicated by his colleague Fulvius Hermes and his sister Eucida.

EDCS-27700207.text_edition: \n Aurelius Peculiaris magister conquiliarius et Aurelia Urbica vivi sibi posuerunt si quis autem post obitum nostrum aliut sic corpus ponere voluerit inferat ecclesiae argenti pondo quinquaginta\n
EDCS-27700207.translation: Aurelius Peculiaris, master dyer, and Aurelia Urbica placed (this) for themselves while alive. If anyone after our death wishes to place a body differently, let him contribute fifty pounds of silver to the church.
EDCS-27700207.summary: This is a funerary monument purchased by Aurelius Peculiaris and Aurelia Urbica for themselves, with a stipulation for monetary contribution to the church for alternate burials.

EDCS-27700208.text_edition: \n Lucio Fabio Cai filio Clementi scribae nnorum XXX Cordia Auli filia Quarta mater posuit\n
EDCS-27700208.translation: For Lucius Fabius Clemens, son of Caius, a scribe, aged 30 years. Cordia Quarta, daughter of Aulus, his mother, placed (this).
EDCS-27700208.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Cordia Quarta for her son Lucius Fabius Clemens, a scribe.

EDCS-27800055.text_edition: \n Marco Veratio Severino defuncto annorum V Marcus Veratius Severus veteranus legionis XXX Ulpiae victricis et Licinia vivi posuerunt filio suo et sibi\n
EDCS-27800055.translation: For Marcus Veratius Severinus, deceased, aged 5 years. Marcus Veratius Severus, a veteran of the 30th Ulpia Victrix legion, and Licinia placed (this) for their son and for themselves.
EDCS-27800055.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Veratius Severus, a veteran, and Licinia for their young son Marcus Veratius Severinus and for themselves.

EDCS-27800056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agrio Secundino omnibus ornato moribus incomparabili quia peccavit laudatur ubi cessit quaeritur pretiosus quia non est liberti bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27800056.translation: To the Divine Manes of Agrius Secundinus, adorned with all virtues, incomparable, whose absence is sought where he erred and is praised. He is precious because he is not. (Dedicated by his freedmen) who deserved well.
EDCS-27800056.summary: This is a funerary epitaph praising the deceased Agrius Secundinus and stating it was dedicated by his freedmen.

EDCS-27800057.text_edition: \n Aufidio Felicissimo Aufidius Felicio fratri posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-27800057.translation: Aufidius Felicio dedicated this to his brother Aufidiusissimo and to himself.
EDCS-27800057.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Aufidius Felicio for his brother and himself.

EDCS-27800515.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Lusius Cerdo sibi et coniugi suae vivis posuit\n
EDCS-27800515.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Lusius Cerdo dedicated this for himself and his wife while they were alive.
EDCS-27800515.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Lusius Cerdo for himself and his wife.

EDCS-27800686.translation: Marcus Domitius Salvianus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-27800686.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and filiation of Marcus Domitius Salvianus.

EDCS-27800689.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27800689.text_edition: \n Titulum positum est Suetonio Iovino do<m=N>o Durrachio nnorum LX ovit sic mense Ianuario III \n
EDCS-27800689.translation: The inscription was set up for Suetonius Iovinus, a man of Durrachium, aged 60 years, in the month of January, on the 3rd day.
EDCS-27800689.summary: This inscription records the death of Suetonius Iovinus of Durrachium at the age of 60.

EDCS-27800690.text_edition: \n Dis Ascaenis et Matri Magnae Ianae libens merito\n
EDCS-27800690.translation: To the Ascaenian Gods and to the Great Mother Goddess, Iana, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-27800690.summary: This is a dedication to the Ascaenian Gods and the Great Mother Goddess Iana.

EDCS-27900001.text_edition: \n Caecinia Calpurnia vixit annos VI dies XXX Calpurnius et Rufina parentes filiae dulcissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-27900001.translation: Caecinia Calpurnia lived 6 years and 30 days. Calpurnius and Rufina, her parents, dedicated this to their dearest daughter.
EDCS-27900001.summary: This is a funerary dedication by parents Calpurnius and Rufina for their daughter Caecinia Calpurnia, who died at the age of 6 years and 30 days.

EDCS-27900002.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesidiae Romanae Pomponius Crescens coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900002.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Pomponius Crescens for his deserving wife, Caesidia Romana.
EDCS-27900002.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Pomponius Crescens for his wife Caesidia Romana.

EDCS-27900003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Caesionio Felici Coelia Firma marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900003.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Coelia Firma for her deserving husband, Gaius Caesionius Felix.
EDCS-27900003.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Coelia Firma for her husband Gaius Caesionius Felix.

EDCS-27900004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesoniae Firminae coniugi karissimae defunctae annorum XXI Urgulanius Faustus maritus bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900004.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Caesonia Firmina, her dearest wife, who died at the age of 21 years. Her husband, Urgulanius Faustus, dedicated this for her deserving character.
EDCS-27900004.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Urgulanius Faustus for his wife Caesonia Firmina, who died at age 21.

EDCS-27900005.text_edition: \n Publius Caetennius Publi libertus Aeschines vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Ithaces libertae Pantherae filiae defunctae nnorum XXVIII Marcus Subulnius Aprio\n
EDCS-27900005.translation: Publius Caetennius Aeschines, freedman of Publius, made this while alive for himself and for Iulia Ithaces, freedwoman of Panthera, his deceased daughter, aged 28 years. Marcus Subulnius Aprio.
EDCS-27900005.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Publius Caetennius Aeschines for himself and his deceased daughter, Iulia Ithaces, who died at age 28.

EDCS-27900006.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Caetennio Caeliano Publius Caetennius Caetennia[nus ------\n
EDCS-27900006.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Caetennius Caelianus, Publius Caetennius Caetennianus [restored name and relationship].
EDCS-27900006.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Publius Caetennius Caelianus and another, likely family member, with partially restored names, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900007.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Caetennius Onesimus.
EDCS-27900007.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Publius Caetennius Onesimus, likely a freedman or dependent, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900008.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Caetennio Primo Publio Caetennio Pudenti filiis Publius Caetennius Firmus \n
EDCS-27900008.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Caetennius Primo and Publius Caetennius Pudenti, sons; Publius Caetennius Firmus set this up.
EDCS-27900008.summary: This funerary inscription records Publius Caetennius Firmus setting up a monument for his sons, Primo and Pudenti, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900009.text_edition: \n Publius Caetennius Ursio Marciae Veneriae coniugi et Caetennio Ursino filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-27900009.translation: Publius Caetennius Ursio to Marcia Veneria, his wife, and to Caetennius Ursunus, his most devoted son.
EDCS-27900009.summary: This epitaph commemorates the wife and son of Publius Caetennius Ursio, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Callisto nnorum II Cassius Faventinus alumno bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900010.translation: To the departed spirits. For Callisto, aged 2 years, Cassius Faventinus to his well-deserving pupil.
EDCS-27900010.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a two-year-old child named Callisto, dedicated by Cassius Faventinus, likely a teacher or guardian, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Callisteni Caius Aelius Epictetianus matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900011.translation: To the departed spirits. For Callistenes, Caius Aelius Epictetianus to his well-deserving mother.
EDCS-27900011.summary: This funerary dedication records Caius Aelius Epictetianus honoring his mother, Callistenes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900012.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cameriae Heroidi defunctae annorum XVIII Maltinia Bonosa filiae infelicissimae \n
EDCS-27900012.translation: To the departed spirits. For Cameria Herois, deceased at 18 years, Maltinia Bonosa to her most unhappy daughter.
EDCS-27900012.summary: This epitaph records Maltinia Bonosa lamenting her 18-year-old daughter, Cameria Herois, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900013.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Campano defuncto nnorum XXX Aurelius Valentinus fratri du<l=I>cissimo posuit \n
EDCS-27900013.translation: To the departed spirits. For Campanus, deceased at 30 years, Aurelius Valentinus placed this for his most courageous brother.
EDCS-27900013.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelius Valentinus setting up a monument for his brother Campanus, who died at 30, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Candido defuncto nnorum XXI sodales bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900014.translation: To the departed spirits. For Candidus, deceased at 21 years, the colleagues to their well-deserving member.
EDCS-27900014.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by colleagues for a deceased member named Candidus, who died at 21, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900015.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Candidae coniugi bene merenti nnorum plus minus XXX quae mecum vixit nnos plus minus VII quae est cruciata ut pariret diebus IIII et non peperit et est ita vita functa Iustus conservus posuit\n
EDCS-27900015.translation: To the departed spirits. For Candida, his well-deserving wife, who lived with me about 7 years, who suffered greatly to give birth for 4 days and did not give birth and thus died. Iustus, a fellow slave, set this up.
EDCS-27900015.summary: This is a poignant funerary inscription by Iustus for his wife Candida, detailing her death during childbirth and their seven-year marriage, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Carpophorus Soteriae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900016.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Carpophorus placed this for Soteria, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-27900016.summary: A funerary dedication by Carpophorus to Soteria, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900017.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Cassio Colono defuncto annorum XIIII Quintus Cassius Tertullinus et Lucia Severina filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-27900017.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Quintus Cassius Tertullinus and Lucia Severina placed this for their most unhappy son, Quintus Cassius Colonus, who died at the age of 14.
EDCS-27900017.summary: A funerary monument for a young boy, Quintus Cassius Colonus, erected by his parents, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900018.text_edition: \n Quinto Cassio Eutichi sic Cassia Eutichia sic mater\n
EDCS-27900018.translation: To Quintus Cassius Eutychus, his mother Cassia Eutichia.
EDCS-27900018.summary: A funerary dedication by Cassia Eutichia to her son Quintus Cassius Eutychus, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Cassio Felicioni Quintus Cassius Tertullinus alumnus posuit\n
EDCS-27900019.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Quintus Cassius Tertullinus placed this for Quintus Cassius Felicioni, his ward.
EDCS-27900019.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Cassius Tertullinus for his ward Quintus Cassius Felicioni, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassio Minerbino sic defuncto nnorum XLVII Aelia Supera coniux marito incomparabili cum quo vixit nnos XXVII bene merenti posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-27900020.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Aelia Supera placed this for her incomparable husband, Cassius Minerbinus, who died at the age of 47, with whom she lived for 27 years, and for herself.
EDCS-27900020.summary: A funerary monument for Cassius Minerbinus, erected by his wife Aelia Supera, who also claims a space for herself, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900022.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassiae Concordiae Caelia Bonosa ex testamento ipsius\n
EDCS-27900022.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Caelia Bonosa placed this for Cassia Concordia, in accordance with her will.
EDCS-27900022.summary: A funerary dedication by Caelia Bonosa for Cassia Concordia, executed according to Concordia's will, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900023.text_edition: \n Cassia Constantilla Cassiae Secundae matri posuit in fronte pedes IV in agro pedes V\n
EDCS-27900023.translation: Cassia Constantilla placed this for her mother Cassia Secunda, 4 feet in front, 5 feet in the field.
EDCS-27900023.summary: A funerary marker set up by Cassia Constantilla for her mother Cassia Secunda, with specified dimensions for the burial plot, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900025.text_edition: \n Caio Catenio Phileto patrono Caius Catenius Demetrius et Caius Catenius Primus et Catenia Fusca bene merenti amici optimi avete et valete felix lapis\n
EDCS-27900025.translation: Caius Catenius Demetrius, Caius Catenius Primus, and Catenia Fusca salute you, Caius Catenius Philetus, their patron, who deserved well of them; greetings and farewell, happy stone.
EDCS-27900025.summary: A dedication by freedmen and women to their patron Caius Catenius Philetus, expressing good wishes and proclaiming the stone itself fortunate, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900026.translation: Cervia Siliciana for Maritima, her ward.
EDCS-27900026.summary: A dedication by Cervia Siliciana for her ward Maritima, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.
