EDCS-27900027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cingius Iustinus fecit piscinam in horto Aeliae Liciniae coniugi incomparabili et sibi\n
EDCS-27900027.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Cingius Iustinus built this pool in the garden for his incomparable wife Aelia Licinia, and for himself.
EDCS-27900027.summary: A funerary dedication by Cingius Iustinus for his wife Aelia Licinia and himself, recording the construction of a pool in a garden, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27900028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cirrotis Patrinus M R I posuit\n
EDCS-27900028.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Patrinus, freedman of Marcus, erected this.
EDCS-27900028.summary: A funerary monument erected by Patrinus, a freedman, for the deceased Cirrotis.

EDCS-27900029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Tiberius Claudius Cerdo sibi et Flaviae Quetillae coniugi pientissimae\n
EDCS-27900029.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Cerdo for himself and for Flavia Quetilla, his most devoted wife.
EDCS-27900029.summary: A funerary dedication by Tiberius Claudius Cerdo for himself and his wife Flavia Quetilla.

EDCS-27900030.text_edition: \n ori C PRI Publi Claudi Severi \n
EDCS-27900030.translation: To C. Pri. ... of Publius Claudius Severus.
EDCS-27900030.summary: A fragmentary dedication likely to a deity or a deceased person, associated with Publius Claudius Severus.

EDCS-27900031.text_edition: \n Claudiae Bonosae defunctae nnorum plus minus XXX Gelasius maritus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900031.translation: To Claudia Bonosa, deceased, aged more or less 30 years. Gelasius, her husband, erected this for her, his deserving wife.
EDCS-27900031.summary: A tombstone erected by Gelasius for his wife Claudia Bonosa, who died around the age of 30.

EDCS-27900032.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Ca<m=N>pana Claudio Peregrino marito qui annos LXXX bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-27900032.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Campana erected this for her husband Claudius Peregrinus, deserving of 80 years.
EDCS-27900032.summary: A tombstone erected by Claudia Campana for her husband Claudius Peregrinus, who lived to be 80.

EDCS-27900033.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Claudia Ianuaria.
EDCS-27900033.summary: A funerary dedication to Claudia Ianuaria.

EDCS-27900034.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Claudia Iusta. M.
EDCS-27900034.summary: A funerary dedication to Claudia Iusta, possibly with an initial or abbreviation 'M'.

EDCS-27900035.text_edition: \n annis VIII Clodius Adiutor pater infelicissimus filio piissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900035.translation: For eight years old. Clodius Adiutor, most unhappy father, for his most devoted son, deserving.
EDCS-27900035.summary: A tombstone erected by Clodius Adiutor, a grieving father, for his son who died at the age of eight.

EDCS-27900036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Clodio Hermeti Clodia Felicia patri\n
EDCS-27900036.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Clodia Felicia for her father Clodius Hermes.
EDCS-27900036.summary: A funerary monument erected by Clodia Felicia for her father Clodius Hermes.

EDCS-27900037.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Clodio Philargo Clodia Chara patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900037.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Clodia Chara for her deserving patron Clodius Philargus.
EDCS-27900037.summary: A funerary monument erected by Clodia Chara for her deserving patron Clodius Philargus.

EDCS-27900038.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Clodio Secundino defuncto annorum XXVI infelicissimo Aurelius Secundus et Clodia Firmina parentes\n
EDCS-27900038.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Clodius Secundinus, who died at the most unfortunate age of 26, his parents Aurelius Secundus and Clodia Firmina erected this.
EDCS-27900038.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their deceased son, Clodius Secundinus, who died at age 26.

EDCS-27900039.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Clodia Helpiduti matri Ancharia posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900039.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ancharia erected this to her well-deserving mother, Clodia Helpidus.
EDCS-27900039.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a daughter, Ancharia, for her deserving mother, Clodia Helpidus.

EDCS-27900040.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudio Polite Claudius Gemellinus patri pientissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900040.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudius Gemellinus erected this to his most devoted and well-deserving father, Claudius Polites.
EDCS-27900040.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a son, Claudius Gemellinus, for his devoted father, Claudius Polites.

EDCS-27900041.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of Claudia Primitiva, possibly on a tomb.

EDCS-27900042.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Coelio Quintiano infanti amantissimo defuncto nnorum III mensium IX Obultronia Corinthia alumno dulcissimo\n
EDCS-27900042.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Publius Coelius Quintianus, a most loving infant who died at 3 years and 9 months, his most beloved foster-son Obultronia Corinthia erected this.
EDCS-27900042.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Obultronia Corinthia for her beloved foster-son, the infant Publius Coelius Quintianus, who died at age 3 years and 9 months.

EDCS-27900043.text_edition: \n Coeliae Peregrinae Publi libertae defunctae nnum XVII Saecularis et Maria Coeli Balbini parentes filiae piissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-27900043.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Saecularis and Maria, parents of the most dutiful daughter Coelia Peregrina, freedwoman of Publius, who died at age 17, erected this.
EDCS-27900043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents, Saecularis and Maria, for their deceased daughter, Coelia Peregrina, a freedwoman.

EDCS-27900044.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Considius Viator qui et Gargilius vixit plus minus nnos XL memoriam posuit Amantius signu sic Simplici\n
EDCS-27900044.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Considius Viator, also known as Gargilius, who lived about 40 years, set up this memorial to Amantius, in the manner of Simplicius.
EDCS-27900044.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Considius Viator (Gargilius) to commemorate Amantius, who lived approximately 40 years.

EDCS-27900046.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelia Aquilina Felicissimo defuncto nnorum \n
EDCS-27900046.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelia Aquilina Felicissimus, who died at the age of...
EDCS-27900046.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the name Cornelia Aquilina Felicissimus and an incomplete age.

EDCS-27900048.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Crescen...
EDCS-27900048.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph that begins with 'To the spirits of the departed' and the name Crescen...

EDCS-27900049.text_edition: \n ------] Curio Ingenuo - Curio Secundo Caio Curio Ami Curia Syntyche\n
EDCS-27900049.translation: To Curio Ingenuus, Curio Secundus, Gaius Curio Ami, and Curia Syntyche.
EDCS-27900049.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary, listing the names of several individuals, possibly a family group.

EDCS-27900050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Curiatio Rufino defuncto annorum XXXVI Curiatia Soteris soror fecit\n
EDCS-27900050.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Curiatia Soteris made this for her brother Titus Curiatius Rufinus, who died at the age of 36.
EDCS-27900050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a sister to her deceased brother, Titus Curiatius Rufinus, who died at 36.

EDCS-27900051.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Curtio Eutycho Caesonia Thalassa coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900051.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caesonia Thalassa placed this for her deserving husband, Gaius Curtius Eutyches.
EDCS-27900051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deserving husband, Gaius Curtius Eutyches.

EDCS-27900052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dalmati qui vixit annos Dis Manibus Valentinae defunctae annorum plus minus trium Claudia L mater FNPAR \n
EDCS-27900052.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudia, daughter of FNPAR (son of...), placed this for her mother Valentina, who died at just over three years of age.
EDCS-27900052.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a daughter for her deceased mother, Valentina, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-27900053.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dassio homini infelicissimo defuncto nnorum XXVI mensium VIII Honesime sic soror posuit\n
EDCS-27900053.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Honesime, his sister, placed this for Dassius, a most unfortunate man, who died at the age of 26 years and 8 months.
EDCS-27900053.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a sister for her deceased brother, Dassius, who died at 26 years and 8 months.

EDCS-27900054.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dato filio bene merenti parentes fecerunt Severa e<t=I> Datus qui vixit annis X mensibus IIII \n
EDCS-27900054.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Severa and Datus, his parents, made this for their deserving son, Dato, who lived for 10 years and 4 months.
EDCS-27900054.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their deserving son, Dato, who died at 10 years and 4 months.

EDCS-27900055.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decembro Iucunda amico incomparabili qui vixit plus minus nnos XXXV\n
EDCS-27900055.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iucunda placed this for her incomparable friend, December, who lived for about 35 years.
EDCS-27900055.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a friend for her incomparable friend, December, who died at approximately 35 years of age.

EDCS-27900056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Domitiae Pinniae defunctae nnorum XXII Clodius Artemidorus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900056.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Clodius Artemidorus [placed this] for his deserving wife, Domitia Pinnia, who died at the age of 22.
EDCS-27900056.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his deserving wife, Domitia Pinnia, who died at 22.

EDCS-27900057.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Donatae filiae quae vixit nnos XXII mater infelicissima posuit\n
EDCS-27900057.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Her most unhappy mother placed this for her daughter Donata, who lived for 22 years.
EDCS-27900057.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a grieving mother for her deceased daughter, Donata, who died at 22.

EDCS-27900058.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Donatae Ursinae coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900058.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. [This was placed] for his deserving wife, Donata Ursinus.
EDCS-27900058.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up for a deserving wife, Donata Ursinus.

EDCS-27900061.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Epiteuxi defunctae annorum? Marcus Arruntius Zosimus Rufo filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-27900061.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Arruntius Zosimus Rufo placed this for his most devoted son Epiteuchus, who died at the age of [?].
EDCS-27900061.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a father, Marcus Arruntius Zosimus Rufo, for his devoted son, Epiteuchus, with an uncertain age at death.

EDCS-27900062.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Heuodia sic alumnae Scriboniae Corneliae annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-27900062.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Hevodia, dearly departed, to her foster-daughter Scribonia Cornelia, aged 18.
EDCS-27900062.summary: A funerary inscription for Scribonia Cornelia, who died at age 18, set up by Hevodia.

EDCS-27900063.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Heusebio sic defuncto nnorum XXX Sura conservo piissimo\n
EDCS-27900063.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Eusebius, who died aged 30, Sura, his most devoted fellow slave, [set this up].
EDCS-27900063.summary: A funerary inscription for Eusebius, who died at age 30, set up by his fellow slave Sura.

EDCS-27900064.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eutyches vixit nnos XIII menses II dies XX Fabricius Asclepiades alumni sic bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900064.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Eutyches lived 13 years, 2 months, and 20 days. Fabricius Asclepiades set this up for his well-deserving foster-child.
EDCS-27900064.summary: A funerary inscription for Eutyches, who died at age 13 years, 2 months, and 20 days, set up by his foster-father Fabricius Asclepiades.

EDCS-27900065.text_edition: \n ? posuit o in fronte pedes VIII |(uncias quattuor) Marci liberta Eutychia in agro pedes XXV\n
EDCS-27900065.translation: ?: Eutychia, freedwoman of Marcus, set this up in front [of the tomb], 8 feet [high] by 4 inches [wide], and 25 feet [deep] in the land.
EDCS-27900065.summary: A funerary boundary marker specifying dimensions, set up by Eutychia, freedwoman of Marcus.

EDCS-27900066.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Faberia Eutychia Faberiae Fortunatae patronae bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-27900066.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Faberia Eutychia set up this tombstone for Faberia Fortunata, her well-deserving patroness.
EDCS-27900066.summary: A funerary inscription for Faberia Fortunata, set up by her freedwoman and client Faberia Eutychia.

EDCS-27900067.text_edition: \n Fabricius Agatho posuit Agathino filio suo annuculo et mensum sic sex\n
EDCS-27900067.translation: Fabricius Agatho set this up for Agathinus, his son, aged one year and six months.
EDCS-27900067.summary: A funerary inscription for Agathinus, son of Fabricius Agatho, who died at the age of one year and six months.

EDCS-27900069.text_edition: \n Feresiae Quinti et Cai libertae Speratae Quintus Feresius Quinti libertus Chrestus fecit et \n
EDCS-27900069.translation: For Feresia, freedwoman of Quintus, and Sperata, freedwoman of Quintus. Quintus Feresius, freedman of Quintus Chrestus, made this and [...].
EDCS-27900069.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Quintus Feresius for Feresia and Sperata, both freedwomen of Quintus.

EDCS-27900070.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marronia Firma [set this up].
EDCS-27900070.summary: A funerary inscription for Marronia Firma.

EDCS-27900071.summary: A funerary inscription identifying the burial place of Firmanus, with an unclear initial element 'AFALX'.

EDCS-27900072.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Firmiae Soteridi nnorum XXXI Exuperatus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900072.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Exuperatus set this up for his well-deserving wife, Firmia Soteris, aged 31.
EDCS-27900072.summary: A funerary inscription for Firmia Soteris, who died at age 31, set up by her husband Exuperatus.

EDCS-27900074.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Primitivos sic Paramone matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900074.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Primitivos to Paramone, his well-deserving mother.
EDCS-27900074.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Flavius Primitivos to his mother Paramone, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flabius sic Saecundinus Iuliae Maiae \n
EDCS-27900075.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flabius to Julia Maia.
EDCS-27900075.summary: A funerary dedication by Flabius to Julia Maia, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Silvanus Hispani filius Quarto filio pientissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900076.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Silvanus, son of Hispanius, to his most devoted son Quarto, well-deserving.
EDCS-27900076.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Flavius Silvanus to his son Quarto, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Thallioni Cossonia Eutychia coniugi bene m(erenti) p(osuit)\n
EDCS-27900077.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cossonia Eutychia set this up for her well-deserving husband Flavius Thallio.
EDCS-27900077.summary: A funerary dedication by Cossonia Eutychia to her husband Flavius Thallio, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Vero defuncto nnorum XXIII Rufus fratri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-27900078.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Rufus set this up for his most devoted brother Titus Flavius Verus, who died at the age of 23.
EDCS-27900078.summary: A funerary dedication by Rufus to his brother Titus Flavius Verus, who died at 23, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900079.text_edition: \n Flavia Pardalis se viva sibi posuit\n
EDCS-27900079.translation: Flavia Pardalis set this up for herself while alive.
EDCS-27900079.summary: A funerary dedication made by Flavia Pardalis for herself during her lifetime, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900080.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Florae coniugis karissimae Barbario conservus\n
EDCS-27900080.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Barbario, fellow slave, to Flora, his most beloved wife.
EDCS-27900080.summary: A funerary dedication by Barbario, a fellow slave, to his wife Flora, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900081.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata mater sibiibi Gentill- fil(i-) infelicissim- qu- vixit \n
EDCS-27900081.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fortunata, mother, for herself, [---] Gentillus, her unhappy son, who lived [---].
EDCS-27900081.summary: A fragmented funerary dedication by Fortunata for herself and her son Gentillus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900082.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunato obsequentissimo et innocentissimo alumno annorum plus minus XVII Marcus Lectinius Cornelianus bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900082.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Lectinius Cornelianus set this up for his most obedient and innocent pupil Fortunatus, aged about 17.
EDCS-27900082.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Lectinius Cornelianus for his pupil Fortunatus, aged about 17, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortum Fortunato Caesaris nostri vernae Chreste contubernalis cum filio bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900083.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Chrestus, fellow slave of our Caesar, to Fortunatus, his son, well-deserving.
EDCS-27900083.summary: A funerary dedication by Chrestus, a slave of the emperor, to his son Fortunatus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-27900084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata et Abarto parentes Saturae filiae infelicissemae sic defunctae annorum X\n
EDCS-27900084.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Fortunata and Abarto, parents, for their most unfortunate daughter Satura, who died at the age of ten.
EDCS-27900084.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by parents to their young daughter Satura, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata coniugi Parthinopeo et filiis infelicissimis posuit\n
EDCS-27900085.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Fortunata dedicated this for her husband Parthinopoeus and their most unfortunate children.
EDCS-27900085.summary: A funerary monument set up by Fortunata for her husband Parthinopoeus and their children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900087.translation: Gaius Fuficius Sabinus, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-27900087.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Fuficius Sabinus, a freedman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900088.inscription_type: carmina, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-27900088.text_edition: \n Fuficia Agre crudeles Parcae nimium matercula dicit quoius in hoc tumulo filia parva iacet debueram prius ipsa mori sed numina vestra qui coluere cadunt neglexere manent infernae vobis commendo virtute satactam circu hoc tumulum quae cinis occulitur S\n
EDCS-27900088.translation: Cruel Fates, too cruel! My dear mother says that my little daughter lies in this tomb. I should have died first, but those who worship you, O deities, fall; those who neglect you endure. I commend to you her who is gathered here with virtue, whose ashes are hidden within this tomb.
EDCS-27900088.summary: A funerary inscription expressing grief and lament from a mother for her deceased young daughter, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Galliae Secundae Baebilius Genethlius Iulius Zmaragdus heredes bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900089.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Gallia Secunda, a well-deserving heir. Dedicated by Baebilius Genethlius and Julius Zmaragdus.
EDCS-27900089.summary: A funerary dedication for Gallia Secunda by her heirs, Baebilius Genethlius and Julius Zmaragdus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gamus Laeliae Liberali coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900090.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gamus dedicated this for Laelia Libera, his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-27900090.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Gamus for his wife Laelia Libera, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900091.text_edition: \n Sextus Gellius Primus et Caetennia Artimisia Gelliae Ursae libertae suae bene meritae posuerunt annorum XX hic sita est\n
EDCS-27900091.translation: Sextus Gellius Primus and Caetennia Artimisia dedicated this for Gellia Ursa, their well-deserving freedwoman, who died at the age of twenty. She is buried here.
EDCS-27900091.summary: A funerary epitaph by patrons for their freedwoman Gellia Ursa, who died at age 20, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900092.text_edition: \n Sextus <G=C>ellius Primus Lucio Vario Attico amico bene merito posuit \n
EDCS-27900092.translation: Sextus Gellius Primus dedicated this for Lucius Varius Atticus, his well-deserving friend.
EDCS-27900092.summary: A funerary dedication by Sextus Gellius Primus for his friend Lucius Varius Atticus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900093.text_edition: \n Gerulus Gemellus Aureliae For coniugi carissimae quae vixit nnos XXXXX bene merenti et sibi \n
EDCS-27900093.translation: Gerulus Gemellus dedicated this for Aurelia For, his dearest wife, who lived 50 years, and for himself, a well-deserving person.
EDCS-27900093.summary: A funerary epitaph by Gerulus Gemellus for his wife Aurelia For, who died at 50, and for himself, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900094.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Helpidis Sextus Baebius Valentio sorori piissimae posuit\n
EDCS-27900094.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valentio dedicated this for his most devoted sister Helpidis.
EDCS-27900094.summary: A funerary epitaph by Sextus Baebius Valentio for his sister Helpidis, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27900095.text_edition: \n Herennio Apelti Iulia Herbula marito posuit\n
EDCS-27900095.translation: Iulia Herbula placed this for her husband Herennius Apeltes.
EDCS-27900095.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Iulia Herbula dedicated a monument to her husband, Herennius Apeltes.

EDCS-27900096.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Herennio Papirio defuncto annorum XXVI sodales sodali bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-27900096.translation: To the Divine Shades. The members of the association placed this for their much deserving comrade Quintus Herennius Papirius, who died at the age of 26.
EDCS-27900096.summary: This funerary epitaph records that the members of an association dedicated a monument to their deceased comrade, Quintus Herennius Papirius, aged 26.

EDCS-27900097.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Herenniae Eutychiae Sellius Valentinus matri et Lucius Sellius Hermes coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-27900097.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sellius Valentinus for his mother Herennia Eutychia, and Lucius Sellius Hermes for his beloved wife.
EDCS-27900097.summary: This funerary epitaph records a joint dedication by Sellius Valentinus for his mother, Herennia Eutychia, and Lucius Sellius Hermes for his wife.

EDCS-27900098.text_edition: \n Hermeti arcam et titulum Euhodus servus posuit\n
EDCS-27900098.translation: Euhodus the slave placed this chest and inscription for Hermes.
EDCS-27900098.summary: This epitaph records that the slave Euhodus dedicated a burial chest and inscription for Hermes.

EDCS-27900099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titio Hilarioni marito bene merenti Cassia Maxima coniux\n
EDCS-27900099.translation: To the Divine Shades. For her devoted husband Titus Hilarion. Cassia Maxima, his wife.
EDCS-27900099.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Cassia Maxima dedicated a monument to her devoted husband, Titus Hilarion.

EDCS-27900100.text_edition: \n Homonoea Zosime viva sibi posuit et Septimio Lucio marito suo\n
EDCS-27900100.translation: Homonoea Zosime erected this for herself while alive, and for her husband Septimius Lucius.
EDCS-27900100.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Homonoea Zosime dedicated this monument for herself and her husband, Septimius Lucius.

EDCS-27900102.translation: Anemia placed this for her most loving husband Honorius.
EDCS-27900102.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Anemia dedicated a monument to her loving husband, Honorius.

EDCS-27900103.translation: For Horia Prima, freedwoman of Marcus.
EDCS-27900103.summary: This epitaph records the dedication of a monument to Horia Prima, freedwoman of Marcus.

EDCS-27900104.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hospitali infelicis<si>mo Maritima amica bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-27900104.translation: To the Divine Shades. Maritima placed this for her unfortunate friend Hospitalis, who deserved well.
EDCS-27900104.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Maritima dedicated a monument to her unfortunate but deserving friend, Hospitalis.

EDCS-27900105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ianuarius infantem sic infelici lapidem fecit\n
EDCS-27900105.translation: To the Divine Shades. January made this stone for an infant, thus in misfortune.
EDCS-27900105.summary: This funerary epitaph records that January erected this stone for an infant, lamenting their unfortunate fate.

EDCS-27900106.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Isiciae Firminae coniugi Flavius Fortunatus maritus bene merenti posuit defunctae nnorum plus minus XXXV\n
EDCS-27900106.translation: To the Divine Shades. Flavius Fortunatus, husband, erected this for his deserving wife, Isicia Firmina, who died at about 35 years of age.
EDCS-27900106.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his deceased wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Philadelphi qui et Polydapanus Stratonice conserva bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000001.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Philadelphus, also known as Polydapanus, and his deserving fellow slave, Stratonice.
EDCS-28000001.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to a freedman and a fellow slave, characteristic of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000002.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Filinae defunctae nnorum XVIII nondum libertae Cassia Constantilla posuit\n
EDCS-28000002.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Filina, who died at 18 years of age, not yet freed. Cassia Constantilla erected this.
EDCS-28000002.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Cassia Constantilla for Filina, a young woman who died before being granted freedom, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000003.text_edition: \n Lucio Plotio Luci filio Pollia Basso\n
EDCS-28000003.translation: To Lucius Plotus Bassus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-28000003.summary: A funerary epitaph naming Lucius Plotus Bassus, son of Lucius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000004.text_edition: \n Plotiae Secundinae Lucius Plotius Eutyches libertus patronae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000004.translation: For Plotia Secundina. Lucius Plotius Eutyches, her freedman, erected this for his deserving patroness.
EDCS-28000004.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by a freedman for his deceased patroness, Plotia Secundina, typical of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000005.text_edition: \n Pompeiae Hermione coniugi bene merenti et Pompeiae Secundae filiae pientissimae defunctae nnum XX mensium VIII dierum XX Marcus Pompeius Liberalis posuit\n
EDCS-28000005.translation: For his most dutiful daughter, Pompeia Secunda, who died at 20 years, 8 months, and 20 days, and for his wife, Pompeia Hermione. Marcus Pompeius Liberalis erected this.
EDCS-28000005.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Pompeius Liberalis for his deceased wife and daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000006.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Pomponius.
EDCS-28000006.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph dedicated to Pomponius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Pomponio Andronico Messia Lamia marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000007.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Publius Pomponius Andronicus. Messia Lamia erected this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-28000007.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Messia Lamia for her husband, Publius Pomponius Andronicus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000008.text_edition: \n Lucius Pomponius Extricatus vivus fecit sibi et suis et Caio Oppio Viviano amico bene merenti in fronte pedes VI in agro pedes VI\n
EDCS-28000008.translation: Lucius Pomponius Extricatus erected this for himself and his family while alive, and for his deserving friend Gaius Oppius Vivianus; 6 feet in front, 6 feet in the field.
EDCS-28000008.summary: A funerary monument erected during the lifetime of Lucius Pomponius Extricatus for himself, his family, and a friend, also including burial plot dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000009.text_edition: \n Publio Pomponio Paulo Caecilia Quinti filia Paula mater posuit\n
EDCS-28000009.translation: For Publius Pomponius Paulus. His mother, Caecilia Paula, daughter of Quintus, erected this.
EDCS-28000009.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Caecilia Paula for her son, Publius Pomponius Paulus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000010.text_edition: \n Pomponiae Alce Pomponia Arisbe sorori et libertae carissimae\n
EDCS-28000010.translation: To Pomponia Alce, her dearest sister and freedwoman, Pomponia Arisbe.
EDCS-28000010.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication by Pomponia Arisbe to her sister and freedwoman, Pomponia Alce.

EDCS-28000011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pomponiae Sabinae defunctae annorum IIII Flaviae Aciliae et Pomponiae Thalassiae alumnae infelicissimae\n
EDCS-28000011.translation: To the Divine Manes of Pomponia Sabina, who died at the age of four, the most unfortunate foster-child of Flavia Acilia and Pomponia Thalassia.
EDCS-28000011.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to the memory of Pomponia Sabina, a four-year-old child, by her foster mothers.

EDCS-28000012.text_edition: \n Pomponia Semne et Pomponia Quarta libertae patron- bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000012.translation: To their freedwoman and patron, deserving of all good things, Pomponia Semne and Pomponia Quarta.
EDCS-28000012.summary: This funerary epitaph honors a patron by her freedwomen, Pomponia Semne and Pomponia Quarta.

EDCS-28000013.text_edition: \n cum qua concorditer vixit per annos X quae decessit nnos dies XXX Pontius Trofimus maritus ob merita eius posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-28000013.translation: Pontius Trofimus erected this for his wife, with whom he lived in harmony for 10 years, who died after 30 days, and for himself.
EDCS-28000013.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Pontius Trofimus for his wife, recording their years of marriage and her death, and also for himself.

EDCS-28000014.text_edition: \n Pontia<n=H>ae PANONESA sibi e<t=I> con<iug=NS>i suo pos<t=I>e<r=P>(is)<q=O>(ue) eorum \n
EDCS-28000014.translation: To Pontia, and for herself and her husband, and later for their children.
EDCS-28000014.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication to Pontia, the dedicator, her husband, and their children.

EDCS-28000015.translation: To the Divine Manes of Pontia.
EDCS-28000015.summary: This funerary epitaph is a dedication to the memory of Pontia.

EDCS-28000016.text_edition: \n Pontiena Cale viva fecit sibi et Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Suavi viro Quinto Pontieno Suavi filio nnorum X\n
EDCS-28000016.translation: Pontiena Cale made this while alive for herself, and for Titus Flavius Suavius, freedman of Augustus, and for her son Quintus Pontienus Suavius, aged ten.
EDCS-28000016.summary: This funerary epitaph was self-funded by Pontiena Cale for herself, her husband Titus Flavius Suavius, and their son Quintus Pontienus Suavius.

EDCS-28000017.text_edition: \n cemeni annorum XX Postumae matri Secundinae et Corneliae Feliculae libertis libertabusque Corneli Agathangeli \n
EDCS-28000017.translation: To his mother Postuma Secundina, aged twenty, and to the freedmen and freedwomen, freedmen and freedwomen of Cornelius Agathangelus.
EDCS-28000017.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication to Postuma Secundina, aged twenty, and to the freedmen and freedwomen of Cornelius Agathangelus.

EDCS-28000018.translation: Praesentilla erected this for her obedient father.
EDCS-28000018.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Praesentilla for her obedient father.

EDCS-28000019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primillae infelici defunctae nnorum XVIII infelicissimus coniunx\n
EDCS-28000019.translation: To the Divine Manes of Primiilla, unfortunate, who died at the age of eighteen, her most unfortunate husband.
EDCS-28000019.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated by an unfortunate husband to his deceased wife, Primiilla, who died at eighteen.

EDCS-28000020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primitivo defuncto nnorum XXV bene merenti Saturnina mater\n
EDCS-28000020.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Primitivus, who died at the age of 25, a deserving son, his mother Saturnina placed this.
EDCS-28000020.summary: A tombstone dedicated by a mother, Saturnina, to her deceased son, Primitivus, probably from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Prima mater filiae Fructulae infelicissimae quae vixit annos XXXV bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000021.translation: To the Divine Shades. Prima, mother, placed this for her most unhappy daughter Fructula, who lived 35 years, a deserving one.
EDCS-28000021.summary: A tombstone set up by Prima for her deceased daughter Fructula, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000022.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Sextus Barbins Proculus.
EDCS-28000022.summary: A tombstone dedicated to Sextus Barbins Proculus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000023.text_edition: \n Dulcis Proserii coniux Vincentia quondam sarcofago hoc tegitur caro condente marito hanc post bis quinos sequitur Piruntia mater annos et aeterno iungit pia membra cubili\n
EDCS-28000023.translation: Sweet Vincentia, wife of Proserius, is covered by this sarcophagus; her loving husband is interred here, and after twice five years, her mother Piruntia follows, joining her limbs in eternal rest.
EDCS-28000023.summary: An epitaph on a sarcophagus, recording the burial of Vincentia and her mother Piruntia, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-28000024.text_edition: \n Prostinia Cai filia Procula viva fecit sibi et Faventinae delicatae defunctae annorum X\n
EDCS-28000024.translation: Procula, daughter of Caius Prostinius, made this while alive for herself and for her beloved Faventina, who died at the age of 10.
EDCS-28000024.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Procula for herself and her deceased young daughter Faventina, probably from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000025.text_edition: \n Privatae annorum duum mensium V Onesime mater\n
EDCS-28000025.translation: Onesime, mother [of someone named Privatus], aged two years and five months.
EDCS-28000025.summary: A tombstone commemorating a very young child named Privatus, erected by his mother Onesime, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000026.text_edition: \n Prudenti nnorum XX Proba mater posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000026.translation: To the deserving Prudentius, aged 20, his mother Proba placed this.
EDCS-28000026.summary: A tombstone dedicated by Proba to her son Prudentius, who died at age 20, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000027.text_edition: \n Lucius Publicius Eutychus vivus fecit Abascanto filio defuncto nnorum XVIII\n
EDCS-28000027.translation: Lucius Publicius Eutychus, while alive, made this for his deceased son Abascantus, aged 18.
EDCS-28000027.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Lucius Publicius Eutychus for his son Abascantus, probably from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Publicio Onesimiano defuncto nnorum XL Iulia Procilla marito <b=R>ene merenti et Aelia Corinthia posuerunt \n
EDCS-28000028.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Publicius Onesimianus, deceased at the age of 40. Julia Procilla for her deserving husband, and Aelia Corinthia, placed this.
EDCS-28000028.summary: A tombstone dedicated by Julia Procilla and Aelia Corinthia to Publius Publicius Onesimianus, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000029.text_edition: \n Publius Publicius Philetus vivus fecit sibi et Tersiniae Pantherae libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28000029.translation: Publius Publicius Philetus, while alive, made this for himself and for Tersinia Panthera, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants; he will not be succeeded by an heir.
EDCS-28000029.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Publius Publicius Philetus for himself, his freed slaves, and their descendants, indicating the owner intended to prevent future inheritance disputes, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000030.text_edition: \n Lucius Publicius Luci libertus Trophimus vivus fecit sibi et Lucio Publicio Iasoni patrono defuncto Iuliae Vendoni uxori eius Publiciae Gorge coniugi suae Publiciae Inventae Publicio Taurioni filiis suis in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XVI hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28000030.translation: Lucius Publicius Trophimus, freedman of Lucius, made this monument for himself while alive, and for his patron Lucius Publicius Iason, deceased, for his wife Julia Vendon, for his wife Publicia Gorge, for his children Publicia Inventa and Publicius Taurion. In the front, 12 feet; in the land, 16 feet. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-28000030.summary: This funerary monument, likely a tomb, was erected by Lucius Publicius Trophimus for himself and his family, specifying dimensions and stipulating that it is not inheritable.

EDCS-28000031.translation: Rupia Crescentilla placed this for herself while alive.
EDCS-28000031.summary: This is a funerary marker erected by Rupia Crescentilla for herself during her lifetime.

EDCS-28000032.text_edition: \n Quintae alumnae defunctae nnorum XXVI Lollius Cerdo\n
EDCS-28000032.translation: For Quintae, foster-child, 26 years of age. Lollius Cerdo [placed this].
EDCS-28000032.summary: This inscription records the dedication by Lollius Cerdo for his foster-child Quintae, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-28000033.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintio Ursae conservae quae vixit nnos XLIIII quae mecum vixit annos XI \n
EDCS-28000033.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintia Ursa, a fellow slave, who lived 44 years and lived with me for 11 years.
EDCS-28000033.summary: This dedication is made by an unnamed individual to the 'Dis Manibus' (spirits of the dead) for their fellow slave, Quintia Ursa, noting her age and the duration of their companionship.

EDCS-28000034.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Raeciae Marciae Aplius Karus maritus posuit\n
EDCS-28000034.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Raecia Marcia. Aplius Karus, her husband, placed this.
EDCS-28000034.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' for Raecia Marcia, erected by her husband Aplius Karus.

EDCS-28000035.text_edition: \n Raia Valeria Vi nn marito pientissimo defuncto nnorum plus minus L cum quo vixit innocentissime\n
EDCS-28000035.translation: To Raia Valeria, for her most devoted husband, deceased, about 50 years of age, with whom she lived most innocently.
EDCS-28000035.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a deceased husband, described as devoted and approximately 50 years old, by his wife Raia Valeria.

EDCS-28000036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ravoniae Eucarpiae Ravonia Placontis matri et Niceni delicatae defunctae nnrum XIII\n
EDCS-28000036.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ravonia Eucarpia, mother of Ravonia Placontis, and for Nicen, beloved child, deceased, 13 years of age.
EDCS-28000036.summary: This dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' is for Ravonia Eucarpia, mother of Ravonia Placontis, and for their deceased daughter Nicen, who died at 13.

EDCS-28000038.text_edition: \n Romania Sabina Titi liberta Firmioni et Martiali et Quintioni filiis viva se fecit\n
EDCS-28000038.translation: Romania Sabina, freedwoman of Titus, made this for herself while alive, and for her sons Firmio, Martialis, and Quintio.
EDCS-28000038.summary: This monument was erected by Romania Sabina, a freedwoman of Titus, for herself and her sons Firmio, Martialis, and Quintio.

EDCS-28000039.text_edition: \n Romania Thalia viva fecit sibi et Romanio Secundino et Romanio Surioni filio et Romaniae Saloninae filiae et Chrissio liberto Lupae libertae et Sabinae Sabiniano Secundae filiis\n
EDCS-28000039.translation: Romania Thalia made this for herself while alive, and for Romanio Secundino, and for her son Romanio Surio, and for her daughter Romaniae Salonia, and for Chrissius, freedman of Lupa, and for freedwoman Sabina, and for the sons of Sabinianus Secundus.
EDCS-28000039.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Romania Thalia for herself and various family members and freedpersons, including sons, a daughter, and freedmen.

EDCS-28000040.text_edition: \n Romula filio defuncto nnnorum V mensium III et L Salonius libertus et C Epicharis nutricio suo dulcissimo\n
EDCS-28000040.translation: For [their] son, 5 years and 3 months of age, deceased. Salonius, freedman, and his wet nurse Epicharis, most beloved.
EDCS-28000040.summary: This inscription records the passing of a child who died at the age of 5 years and 3 months, dedicated by his freedman father Salonius and his beloved wet nurse Epicharis.

EDCS-28000041.translation: To Rufina, a devoted wife, to her deserving husband.
EDCS-28000041.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her deserving husband, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000042.text_edition: \n Rusticia Clodiana filio carissimo Rusticiano qui vixit annos XXI ego misera mater emi ex proprio e meo peto bos sic fratres ne qui sic alium ponatis super\n
EDCS-28000042.translation: To Rusticia Clodiana, to her dearest son Rusticiano, who lived 21 years. I, a miserable mother, bought this from my own property. I beg my brothers, that no one place another [grave] upon this one.
EDCS-28000042.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from a grieving mother to her son, dating likely to the Imperial period, with a plea to her brothers regarding the burial plot.

EDCS-28000043.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabinus Sabinae matri dulcissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000043.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Sabinus placed this for his most beloved and deserving mother, Sabina.
EDCS-28000043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from a son to his deserving mother, dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus', likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000044.translation: To Sabina, wife of Hermes of Novus Persicus.
EDCS-28000044.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, honoring Sabina, wife of Hermes, who was possibly a freedman of a master named Novus Persicus.

EDCS-28000045.text_edition: \n Saccario ? coniugi inconparabili --- sic Hic ego --- sum perpetuus SEMICI qui MEDLI ux ADI vivos sic autem SA et HIIhospitia vestra A BAINTRA et benefac E si vos at sic me venietis cum Leptiana \n
EDCS-28000045.translation: [To] Saccarius? To my incomparable spouse --- Here I --- am forever SEMICI who MEDLI wife ADI alive. But thus SA and HII your hospitality A BAINTRA and do good E if you at thus me will come with Leptiana.
EDCS-28000045.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, dedicated to an 'incomparable spouse', possibly by a husband named Saccarius, with a plea for reciprocal hospitality and good deeds.

EDCS-28000046.text_edition: \n Satrenio Victorino filio infelicissimo qui vixit nnos V menses V dies VI Satrenius Victor et Sempronia Maxima parentes\n
EDCS-28000046.translation: To Satrenius Victorinus, a most unfortunate son, who lived 5 years, 5 months, 6 days. Satrenius Victor and Sempronia Maxima, his parents.
EDCS-28000046.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from parents to their young son, likely dating to the Imperial period, recording his short lifespan.

EDCS-28000048.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Scantiae Zoini Fyrmia sic Paeraegrina sic ANNAE bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000048.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Scantia Zoinus, Firmius Paeraegrinus for Annae, who deserved well.
EDCS-28000048.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, dedicated to Scantia Zoinus and Annae, possibly by Firmius Paeraegrinus.

EDCS-28000049.text_edition: \n Secundae filiae infelicissimae quae vixit nnos plus minus Dassius et Rosula parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000049.translation: To Secunda, an most unfortunate daughter, who lived more or less [number] years. Dassius and Rosula, her parents, placed this.
EDCS-28000049.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from parents to their daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period, lamenting her untimely death.

EDCS-28000050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Secundinus et Florentina parentes Severo filio annorum IIII infelicissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000050.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Secundinus and Florentina, parents, placed this for Severus, their son, 4 years old, most unfortunate.
EDCS-28000050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from parents to their young son, likely dating to the Imperial period, recording his age and lamenting his death.

EDCS-28000051.text_edition: \n annorum XVIII fratri piissimo Secundinus item Secundino bene merenti Miliesis fecit\n
EDCS-28000051.translation: Aged 18 years, to the most devoted brother. Secundinus, also to Secundinus, who deserved well, Miliesis made this.
EDCS-28000051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, dedicated by Miliesis to his brother Secundinus, who died at 18.

EDCS-28000052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Secundioni iuveni infelicissimo qui vixit nnos XXVII Secundus posuit\n
EDCS-28000052.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Secundus erected this for his most unhappy young kinsman Secundioni, who lived 27 years.
EDCS-28000052.summary: A funerary inscription for Secundioni, erected by Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000053.text_edition: \n Seio Dalmatae homini bono <q=O>ui incendio <o>ppressus est Aurelia Victorina uxor \n
EDCS-28000053.translation: Aurelia Victorina, his wife, erected this for Seius Dalmata, a good man, who was crushed by fire.
EDCS-28000053.summary: A funerary inscription for Seius Dalmata, erected by his wife Aurelia Victorina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000054.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Gellio Artorio defuncto nnrum VIII mensium VIIII Artoria Secundina et Gellius Felix pro pietate filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-28000054.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Gellius Artorius, who died at 8 years and 9 months. Artoria Secundina and Gellius Felix placed this for their most unhappy son out of duty.
EDCS-28000054.summary: A funerary inscription for the young child Lucius Gellius Artorius, erected by his parents Artoria Secundina and Gellius Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sentio Celeri Domitia Aphrodisia et Virius Stephanus amico bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000056.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Domitia Aphrodisia and Virius Stephanus erected this for their well-deserving friend Sentius Celer.
EDCS-28000056.summary: A funerary inscription for Sentius Celer, erected by his friends Domitia Aphrodisia and Virius Stephanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000057.text_edition: \n Seppienae Philomenae vivae Seppiena Restituta liberta VI M matri fecit\n
EDCS-28000057.translation: Seppiena Restituta, freedwoman, erected this for Seppiena Philomena, living, mother, 6 months ago.
EDCS-28000057.summary: A funerary inscription for Seppiena Philomena, erected by her freedwoman Seppiena Restituta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimo bene merenti Urgulanius Profuturus et Vesidia Cassia amico posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000059.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Urgulanius Profuturus and Vesidia Cassia erected this for their well-deserving friend Septimus.
EDCS-28000059.summary: A funerary inscription for Septimus, erected by his friends Urgulanius Profuturus and Vesidia Cassia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Servilio Apolloniano et Caio Servilio Proculo Iulia Restuta filio et nepoti\n
EDCS-28000060.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Servilius Apollonianus and Gaius Servilius Proculus. Julia Restuta [placed this] for her son and grandson.
EDCS-28000060.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Servilius Apollonianus and Gaius Servilius Proculus, erected by Julia Restuta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000061.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Servilio Eutycheti defuncto nnorum XXXIII Aurelia Maxima coniugi inconparabili sic bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000061.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Servilius Eutychetus, who died at 33 years. Aurelia Maxima erected this for her incomparable and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-28000061.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Servilius Eutychetus, erected by his wife Aurelia Maxima, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000062.text_edition: \n Marco Servilio Narcissso Stephanus fratri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28000062.translation: Stephanus erected this for his most devoted brother, Marcus Servilius Narcissus.
EDCS-28000062.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Servilius Narcissus, erected by his brother Stephanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000063.text_edition: \n Caio Sevio Tertio Caio Sevio Tempestivo filio Celandus\n
EDCS-28000063.translation: For Gaius Sevius Tertius. Celandus [erected this] for his son, Gaius Sevius Tempstivus.
EDCS-28000063.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Sevius Tempstivus, erected by his father Celandus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000064.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextiliae Secundinae coniugi sanctissimae Lucius Baebilius Hyginus\n
EDCS-28000064.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Baebilius Hyginus to his most virtuous wife, Sextilia Secundina.
EDCS-28000064.summary: A funerary dedication from Lucius Baebilius Hyginus to his wife, Sextilia Secundina, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000065.text_edition: \n Caio Sossio Cai liberto Philomuso testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Cai Sossi Cai liberti Severi et Luci Genuci Luci liberti Rustici\n
EDCS-28000065.translation: Caio Sossius Philomusus, freedman of Gaius, ordered this to be made in his will, under the supervision of Gaius Sossius Severus, freedman of Gaius, and Lucius Genucius Rusticus, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-28000065.summary: A funerary monument ordered by will by the freedman Caio Sossius Philomusus, executed by two other freedmen as arbitrators, probably dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000066.text_edition: \n Lucius Egnatius Sotericus hic situs est\n
EDCS-28000066.translation: Lucius Egnatius Sotericus is buried here.
EDCS-28000066.summary: A simple tombstone indicating the burial place of Lucius Egnatius Sotericus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000067.translation: Statius Clementianus dedicated this to the spirit of the place.
EDCS-28000067.summary: A dedication to the genius loci (spirit of the place) by Statius Clementianus, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000068.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Statio Vitali et Statiae Fortun<a>tae qui vixerunt nns XXXXV in se Publius Statius Vetustus filius posuit\n
EDCS-28000068.translation: To the Divine Manes. Publius Statius Vetustus, son, dedicated this to Publius Statius Vitalis and Statia Fortunata, who lived 45 years, for themselves.
EDCS-28000068.summary: A funerary inscription from Publius Statius Vetustus to his parents, Publius Statius Vitalis and Statia Fortunata, indicating their lifespan, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000069.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Statia Prima Statio Ursino coniugi bene merenti ex pietate posuit cum quo vixit annis XXX\n
EDCS-28000069.translation: Statia Prima dedicated this out of devotion to her husband Ursinnus, who deserved well, with whom she lived 30 years.
EDCS-28000069.summary: A funerary monument erected by Statia Prima for her deserving husband Ursinnus, noting their 30 years of marriage, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000070.text_edition: \n Lucius Sthenius L(uci)? l(ibertus) Creticus sibi et suis [posuit ------\n
EDCS-28000070.translation: Lucius Sthenius Creticus, freedman of Lucius (?), dedicated this for himself and his family.
EDCS-28000070.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Sthenius Creticus, a freedman, for himself and his family, with the rest of the inscription lost, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000071.text_edition: \n Steniae Securae Lucius Publicius Trophimus uxori optimae\n
EDCS-28000071.translation: Lucius Publicius Trophimus to his excellent wife, Stenia Secura.
EDCS-28000071.summary: A funerary dedication from Lucius Publicius Trophimus to his wife, Stenia Secura, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000072.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Sullio Communi Sullia Beronice bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-28000072.translation: Sullia Beronice made this for the Divine Manes of Gaius Sullius Communis, who deserved well.
EDCS-28000072.summary: A funerary dedication from Sullia Beronice to Gaius Sullius Communis, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000073.translation: To Supera, my sweetest Callista, aged...
EDCS-28000073.summary: A fragmented funerary dedication, possibly to a woman named Callista, noting her sweetness and age, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000074.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sym defunct- annorum VIIII Artemisia filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-28000074.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Sym, who lived 9 years, to his most unlucky son. Artemisia [erected this].
EDCS-28000074.summary: This is a tombstone for Sym, a boy of nine, set up by his mother Artemisia.

EDCS-28000075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Symforus Primae sorori bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000075.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To his deserving sister Primae. Symforus [erected this].
EDCS-28000075.summary: This is a tombstone for Primae, set up by Symforus, likely her brother.

EDCS-28000077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio T B Pon Zo C bene merenti SO EIP infelicissim-\n
EDCS-28000077.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Gaius T. B. Pon Zo C, who deserved well. [erected by] SO EIP, most unlucky.
EDCS-28000077.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Gaius T. B. Pon Zo C by SO EIP, presumably a relative or loved one, and records his unfortunate passing.

EDCS-28000079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Thaliae nnorum plus minus XXX Primus gener posuit\n
EDCS-28000079.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Thalia, who lived more or less 30 years. Primus, her son-in-law, placed this.
EDCS-28000079.summary: This tombstone commemorates Thalia, who died at approximately 30 years old, and was set up by her son-in-law, Primus.

EDCS-28000080.text_edition: \n Quintius Tanagro Flavia Marina uxor viva fecit\n
EDCS-28000080.translation: Quintius Tanagro. Flavia Marina, his wife, while alive, made this.
EDCS-28000080.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Flavia Marina for her husband Quintius Tanagro during her lifetime.

EDCS-28000081.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tampio Saeculari defuncto nnorum plus minus LXX Tampius Marcellus filius patri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000081.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Tampius Saecularis, who died at more or less 70 years. Tampius Marcellus, his son, placed this for his deserving father.
EDCS-28000081.summary: This tombstone honors Tampius Saecularis, who died around the age of 70, and was erected by his son, Tampius Marcellus.

EDCS-28000082.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tar Tertia Nigrino filio pientissimo infelicissimo vixit annos XVI menses dies XV mater filio posuit\n
EDCS-28000082.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Nigrinus, his most devoted and most unlucky son, who lived 16 years, [some] months, and 15 days. His mother placed this for her son.
EDCS-28000082.summary: This tombstone records the death of Nigrinus, who lived 16 years, some months, and 15 days, and was set up by his grieving mother.

EDCS-28000083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tattiae Laemotinae defunctae nnorum XXIII Caetennia Amullina bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000083.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Tattia Laemotina, who died at 23 years. Caetennia Amullina, who deserved well, [placed this].
EDCS-28000083.summary: This tombstone commemorates Tattia Laemotina, who died at the age of 23, and was set up by Caetennia Amullina.

EDCS-28000084.text_edition: \n Titus Aurelius Castus filius patri pientissimo\n
EDCS-28000084.translation: Titus Aurelius Castus, to his most devoted father.
EDCS-28000084.summary: This is a dedication set up by Titus Aurelius Castus for his devoted father.

EDCS-28000085.translation: Tersinia Panthera made this for Agon.
EDCS-28000085.summary: This monument was erected by Tersinia Panthera for Agon.

EDCS-28000086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentiae Titiae Publius Novius Laurus coniugi rarissimae posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000086.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Novius Laurus set this up for his exceedingly rare and deserving wife, Terentia Tita.
EDCS-28000086.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Publius Novius Laurus for his wife, Terentia Tita, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000087.translation: Tertius, ... aged seven years, his father... son...
EDCS-28000087.summary: A damaged funerary epitaph fragment recording the name and age of a young boy, Tertius, and indicating a paternal dedication.

EDCS-28000088.translation: To the departed spirits of Tertius.
EDCS-28000088.summary: A simple funerary epitaph dedicated to Tertius, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tertulliano vixit nnos V Aurelius Tertullus nepoti posuit\n
EDCS-28000089.translation: To the departed spirits. To Tertullus, who lived 5 years. Aurelius Tertullus set this up for his grandson.
EDCS-28000089.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Aurelius Tertullus for his grandson, Tertullus, who died at the age of five, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000090.text_edition: \n Tertullino dedefuncto nnorum VLLI Diogenes pater filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-28000090.translation: For Tertullinus, deceased, aged [---] years. Diogenes, his father, for his most unhappy son.
EDCS-28000090.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Diogenes to his son, Tertullinus, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000091.text_edition: \n Caio Tess<e=P>rio Mascellioni Visellia Felicula marito inconparabili sic posuit \n
EDCS-28000091.translation: Visellia Felicula set this up for her incomparable husband, Gaius Tessius Mascellio.
EDCS-28000091.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Visellia Felicula for her husband, Gaius Tessius Mascellio, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000092.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Thetidi Auggustorum libertae Secundinus filius et Sabinus collibertus coniugi dulcissimae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000092.translation: To the departed spirits. For Thetis, freedwoman of the emperors. Secundinus, her son, and Sabinus, her co-freedman, for their sweetest and deserving wife/companion.
EDCS-28000092.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Secundinus and Sabinus to Thetis, a freedwoman of the emperors, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000093.text_edition: \n Aulus Tiburtius Campester nnorum XL hic situs est filii posuerunt \n
EDCS-28000093.translation: Aulus Tiburtius Campester, aged 40 years, lies here. His sons set this up.
EDCS-28000093.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aulus Tiburtius Campester, aged 40, set up by his sons, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000094.text_edition: \n Lucio Tigidio Proculo Tigidia Tertulla <pa=ER>tri posuit \n
EDCS-28000094.translation: Tigidia Tertulla set this up for her father, Lucius Tigidius Proculus.
EDCS-28000094.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Tigidia Tertulla for her father, Lucius Tigidius Proculus, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000095.text_edition: \n CESIOInae alumnae Titi Firmi defunctae nnorum XXIIX Publius Coelius Eutyches uxori bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000095.translation: Publius Coelius Eutyches set this up for his deserving wife, Cesinia, foster-daughter of Titus Firmus, deceased, aged 28 years.
EDCS-28000095.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Publius Coelius Eutyches for his wife, Cesinia, who died at 28, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28000096.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titiae Heliadi Lucius Titius Callistus collibertae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000096.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Titius Callistus set this up for his deserving freedwoman Titia Heliadis.
EDCS-28000096.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Lucius Titius Callistus for his freedwoman Titia Heliadis.

EDCS-28000097.summary: A dedication from Titia Lyccus to Titia Iadia, likely a funerary context.

EDCS-28000098.text_edition: \n Titia Rufina matri Varronae pientessemae sic quae vissit sic annos LXXV\n
EDCS-28000098.translation: Titia Rufina to her most pious mother Varrona, who lived 75 years.
EDCS-28000098.summary: An epitaph for Varrona, commemorating her life of 75 years, dedicated by her daughter Titia Rufina.

EDCS-28000099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Threptioni Papinia Fesstia sic coniugi \n
EDCS-28000099.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Papinia Fesstia to her husband Threption, with a sigh.
EDCS-28000099.summary: A funerary dedication by Papinia Fesstia to her husband Threption.

EDCS-28000100.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Turpinae uxori Tertius maritus et Eutyches frater posuerunt animae innocenti sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-28000100.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tertius, her husband, and Eutyches, her brother, set this up for Turpina, their wife and sister, an innocent soul. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-28000100.summary: A funerary monument for Turpina, erected by her husband Tertius and brother Eutyches.

EDCS-28000104.text_edition: \n Valeri vivus fecit sibi et Felici co Clodiae P Iuliae T Valeriae Publi libertae suis posterisque \n
EDCS-28000104.translation: Valerius made this for himself while alive, and for Felix, his freedman, and for Clodia, daughter of Publius Iulius, and for Valeria, freedwoman of Publius, and for his descendants.
EDCS-28000104.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Valerius for himself, his freedmen, and descendants.

EDCS-28000105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Adaucto patrono bene merenti Caius Valerius Euplus libertus posuit\n
EDCS-28000105.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Valerius Euplus, his freedman, set this up for his deserving patron, Gaius Valerius Adauctus.
EDCS-28000105.summary: A funerary dedication by the freedman Caius Valerius Euplus for his deserving patron, Caius Valerius Adauctus.

EDCS-28000133.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iucunda Tarconio Agathemero conservo defuncto annorum XXX infelici posuit\n
EDCS-28000133.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Tarconius Agathemerus, his fellow slave, who died at the unfortunate age of 30. [---] set this up.
EDCS-28000133.summary: A funerary dedication by a fellow slave for Tarconius Agathemerus, who died at age 30.

EDCS-28000135.text_edition: \n Lucio Iulio Amiantho Iulia Elpis coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000135.translation: Iulia Elpis to her deserving husband, Lucius Iulius Amiantus.
EDCS-28000135.summary: A dedication by Iulia Elpis to her deserving husband, Lucius Iulius Amiantus.

EDCS-28000136.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Atiliano posuerunt parentes filio carissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000136.translation: To the spirits of the departed. His parents set this up for Iulius Atilanus, their dearest son, well-deserving.
EDCS-28000136.summary: A funerary monument erected by parents for their beloved son, Iulius Atilanus.

EDCS-28000137.text_edition: \n Iulio Crescenti infanti Marcus Iulius Cronius? ---\n
EDCS-28000137.translation: To infant Iulius Crescens, Marcus Iulius Cronius [dedicated].
EDCS-28000137.summary: This is a funerary dedication for an infant named Iulius Crescens, set up by Marcus Iulius Cronius, likely a parent.

EDCS-28000138.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Epiforeto defuncto filia patri pientissimo\n
EDCS-28000138.translation: To the Divine Shades. His daughter set this up to her most devoted father, Iulius Epiforetes, deceased.
EDCS-28000138.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a daughter for her deceased father, Iulius Epiforetes.

EDCS-28000139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Eutyches Papiriae Felicitati et sibi\n
EDCS-28000139.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iulius Eutyches set this up for Papiria Felicitas and himself.
EDCS-28000139.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Iulius Eutyches for Papiria Felicitas and for himself.

EDCS-28000140.text_edition: \n Iulio Grato defuncto annorum XVIIII Iulia Luci liberta Eleutheris filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-28000140.translation: To Iulius Gratus, deceased at nineteen years of age. Iulia Eleutheris, freedwoman of Lucius, set this up to her most unfortunate son.
EDCS-28000140.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulius Gratus, who died at nineteen, set up by his mother Iulia Eleutheris.

EDCS-28000141.text_edition: \n CTL a<n=T>norum VIIII pater Iulius Ianuarius et Urbulania Secuindina filiae infelicissimae posuerunt \n
EDCS-28000141.translation: To their most unfortunate daughter, aged nine years. Father Iulius Ianuarius and Urbulania Secundina set this up.
EDCS-28000141.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a nine-year-old daughter, set up by her parents Iulius Ianuarius and Urbulania Secundina.

EDCS-28000142.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Iasoni Pomponia Ammia coniugi pientissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000142.translation: To the Divine Shades. Pomponia Ammia set this up to her most devoted husband, Iulius Iason, well-deserving.
EDCS-28000142.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife, Pomponia Ammia, for her deserving husband, Iulius Iason.

EDCS-28000143.text_edition: \n Iulio Messori Phoebus avomculo sic bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000143.translation: To Iulius Messorius, well-deserving uncle, Phoebus [dedicated].
EDCS-28000143.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Iulius Messorius, set up by his uncle Phoebus.

EDCS-28000144.text_edition: \n Lucius Iulius Luci libertus Narcissus vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Helpidi coniugi et libertae et Lucio Iulio Ceriali filio et liberto et Lucio Iulio libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum ex p d\n
EDCS-28000144.translation: Lucius Iulius Narcissus, freedman of Lucius, made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Iulia Helpidis, his freedwoman, and for his son and freedman Lucius Iulius Cerialis, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, by his own provision.
EDCS-28000144.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Lucius Iulius Narcissus for himself, his wife, son, and descendants, as well as his freedmen and their descendants.

EDCS-28000145.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Filocyrio Flavia Soteris marito pientissimo\n
EDCS-28000145.translation: To the Divine Shades. Flavia Soteris [set this up] to her most devoted husband, Gaius Iulius Filocyrius.
EDCS-28000145.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife, Flavia Soteris, for her devoted husband, Gaius Iulius Filocyrius.

EDCS-28000146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Primitivo Ravonia Placontis marito bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000146.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ravonia Placontis [set this up] to her well-deserving husband, Iulius Primitivus.
EDCS-28000146.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife, Ravonia Placontis, for her deserving husband, Iulius Primitivus.

EDCS-28000147.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Primitivo defuncto annorum V Primus et Septimina filio piissimo et Ursula alumno\n
EDCS-28000147.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Julius Primitivus, who died aged 5. Primus and Septimina erected this for their most devoted son and adopted son Ursula.
EDCS-28000147.summary: A tombstone for a young boy, Julius Primitivus, set up by his parents and adopted son, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000149.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Iulius S Psalmus Iuliae Stratoniceni coniugi \n
EDCS-28000149.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Iulius Psalmus to his wife Iulia Stratonicena.
EDCS-28000149.summary: A funerary dedication from Lucius Iulius Psalmus to his wife Iulia Stratonicena, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Valentino nutrix et Flavia Iustina virginia ipsius pientissimo defuncto annoru<m=N> plus minus XXV \n
EDCS-28000150.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Julius Valentinus, who died aged more or less 25. His nurse and Flavia Iustina, his virgin companion, erected this for him, most devotedly.
EDCS-28000150.summary: A tombstone for Julius Valentinus, who died in young adulthood, erected by his nurse and a female companion, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuli Vari Saturisci defuncti nnorum XI Lucius Varius Saturiscus et Avidia Bestia filio pientissimo fecerunt\n
EDCS-28000151.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Iulius Varius Saturiscus, who died aged 11. Lucius Varius Saturiscus and Avidia Bestia erected this for their most devoted son.
EDCS-28000151.summary: A tombstone for Iulius Varius Saturiscus, a young boy, erected by his parents, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000152.text_edition: \n Lucillae defunctae nnorum XXVIII Iulius Ursulus coniugi rarissimae et infelicissimae S\n
EDCS-28000152.translation: For Lucilla, who died aged 28. Iulius Ursulus erected this for his most rare and most unfortunate wife.
EDCS-28000152.summary: A tombstone for Lucilla, erected by her husband Iulius Ursulus, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000153.text_edition: \n Iulio I Urso Anulia Tegiviva coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000153.translation: Anulia Tegiviva erected this for her husband Iulius Urso, well deserving.
EDCS-28000153.summary: A funerary dedication from Anulia Tegiviva to her husband Iulius Urso, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Ursus et Clodius Firminus Papinio T<e>rentio amici bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000154.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulius Ursus and Clodius Firminus erected this for their well-deserving friend Papinius Tarentius.
EDCS-28000154.summary: A tombstone for Papinius Tarentius, erected by his friends Iulius Ursus and Clodius Firminus, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000155.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Ursus Rosulae coniugi quem duxi annorum XII vixit mecum annos XIIII bene merenti posui\n
EDCS-28000155.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Rosula, wife to whom I was married for 12 years and with whom I lived for 14 years, most deserving. I set this up.
EDCS-28000155.summary: A tombstone erected by a husband for his wife Rosula, detailing their marriage duration, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000156.text_edition: \n e sibi et Iulipatrronae cognato filio Publio Turpilio o et suis\n
EDCS-28000156.translation: For himself and for his relative Iulipatron, son Publius Turpilius, and for his own people.
EDCS-28000156.summary: A tombstone recording a person setting up a monument for themselves, their relative Iulipatron, Publius Turpilius, and other family members, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000157.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Aquilinae defunctae nnorum XVIIII\n
EDCS-28000157.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Iulia Aquilina, who died aged 19.
EDCS-28000157.summary: A tombstone for Iulia Aquilina, who died in her late teens, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000158.text_edition: \n Iuliae Aquilinae Dionisius sic coniugi incomparabili con sic qua vixit annos L et sibi\n
EDCS-28000158.translation: Dionisius to his incomparable wife Iulia Aquilina, who lived for 50 years, and to himself.
EDCS-28000158.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Dionisius to his wife Iulia Aquilina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000159.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Chariteni locum donaverunt qui pertinebat at sic Cassios et Atiutorem sic et Saecularem et Fidelem loco qui appellatur in Epetina in fronte pedes numero XXXIII in agro pedes numero XXXIII praecipio autem post obitum meum ut liberti libertasque ponantur sine ulla controversia\n
EDCS-28000159.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. The Cassii, Atiutor, Saecularis, and Fidelis donated to Iulia Charis a plot of land in Epetina, measuring 33 feet in front and 33 feet in depth, which belonged to them; I give the instruction that after my death my freedmen and freedwomen shall be buried there without any dispute.
EDCS-28000159.summary: This is a funerary inscription where a group of individuals donate a burial plot to Iulia Charis, specifying future burial rights for her freedmen and freedwomen, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000160.text_edition: \n Iuliae Chreste Agatho amicae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000160.translation: To Iulia Chresta, a deserving friend, Agatho made this.
EDCS-28000160.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Agatho to his friend Iulia Chresta, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000161.text_edition: \n Iuliae - f(iliae) Epetinae matri Iulia Tina bene m(erenti) p(osuit)\n
EDCS-28000161.translation: Iulia Tina erected this to her deserving mother, Iulia Epetina, daughter of Iulia.
EDCS-28000161.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulia Tina to her mother Iulia Epetina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000162.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Fortunata Iuliae Zosimini sic filiae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000162.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Zosimini's daughter, Iulia Fortunata, erected this to her deserving daughter.
EDCS-28000162.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulia Fortunata to her daughter Iulia Zosimini, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000163.text_edition: \n Iuliae Gratae nnorum XXV Caius Iulius Nerva contubernali et libertae bene merenti et sibi\n
EDCS-28000163.translation: Caius Iulius Nerva to his companion and freedwoman Iulia Grata, aged 25, who deserved well, and to himself.
EDCS-28000163.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Caius Iulius Nerva to his companion and freedwoman Iulia Grata, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000164.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Heliadi defunctae nnorum VIII mensium II dierum XX Iulius Helius pater filiae infelicissimae et Corellia Diceosyne mater\n
EDCS-28000164.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For his most unhappy daughter Iulia Heliadis, who died at 8 years, 2 months, and 20 days, Iulius Heliades, father, and Corellia Diceosyne, mother.
EDCS-28000164.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulius Heliades and Corellia Diceosyne to their young daughter Iulia Heliadis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Heracleae defunctae nnorum XX Plo<t=P>ius Eu<t>yches coniugi bene merenti \n
EDCS-28000165.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Plo(t)ius Eutyches to his deserving wife Iulia Heraclea, who died at 20 years.
EDCS-28000165.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Plo(t)ius Eutyches to his wife Iulia Heraclea, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Leuce Verginio Philippo marito bene merenti \n
EDCS-28000166.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Verginius Philippus, her deserving husband, Iulia Leuce.
EDCS-28000166.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulia Leuce to her husband Verginius Philippus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000167.text_edition: \n Iuliae Lucidae libertae Veranilla patrona fecit\n
EDCS-28000167.translation: Veranilla, patroness, made this for her deserving freedwoman Iulia Lucida.
EDCS-28000167.summary: This is a funerary dedication by the patroness Veranilla to her freedwoman Iulia Lucida, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28000168.text_edition: \n I M A I P A\n
EDCS-28000168.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Julia Lupula, mother of Sextus.
EDCS-28000168.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Julia Lupula, the mother of Sextus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000169.text_edition: \n Iulia Maritima Popilio Popiliano pientissimo marito posuit\n
EDCS-28000169.translation: Iulia Maritima erected this for her most devoted husband, Popilius Popilianus.
EDCS-28000169.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Iulia Maritima for her husband, Popilius Popilianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000170.text_edition: \n Iulia Muntana sibi viva posuit et Aurelio Patienti coniugi suo inter areas in medio in horto et viam prospicientem inter Aurelium Vettium et Calventiam Ingenuam loco concesso ab Aelio Marullino\n
EDCS-28000170.translation: Iulia Muntana erected this for herself while alive, and for her husband Aurelius Patientius, in the middle of the plots in the garden, and facing the road, in a space granted by Aelius Marullinus.
EDCS-28000170.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a woman, Iulia Muntana, who built her own tomb and that of her husband Aurelius Patientius in a plot granted by Aelius Marullinus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000171.text_edition: \n Iuliae Procillae Luci Iuli Epaphroditi filiae nnorum XXII Lucius Iulius Crescens et Lucius Iulius Doryphorus Luci liberti patronae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000171.translation: To Julia Procilla, daughter of Lucius Iulius Epaphroditus, aged 22. Lucius Iulius Crescens and Lucius Iulius Doryphorus, freedmen of the patroness, erected this for their well-deserving patroness.
