EDCS-28000171.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Julia Procilla, aged 22, and was erected by her freedmen, Lucius Iulius Crescens and Lucius Iulius Doryphorus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000172.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Restutae infelicissimae interfectae nnum X causa ornamentorum Iulius Restutus et Statia Pudentilla parentes\n
EDCS-28000172.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Julia Restuta, most unhappy, killed at the age of 10 for the sake of her ornaments. Her parents Iulius Restutus and Statia Pudentilla.
EDCS-28000172.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Julia Restuta, who died tragically at age 10, set up by her parents, Iulius Restutus and Statia Pudentilla, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000173.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Sabina nepoti infelicissimo et Raia Marcella filio infelicissimo Marco Ampeio Sabino filio nnorum VII mens<ium=ER> VI \n
EDCS-28000173.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Iulia Sabina, my most unhappy granddaughter, and Raia Marcella, my most unhappy son, aged 7 years and 6 months.
EDCS-28000173.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Iulia Sabina and Marco Ampeius Sabinus, children who died young, by Raia Marcella, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000174.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Sarapia Claudiae Antonillae filia matri piissimae\n
EDCS-28000174.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Iulia Sarapia, daughter of Claudia Antonilla, her most devoted mother.
EDCS-28000174.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Claudia Antonilla to her devoted daughter, Iulia Sarapia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000175.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Cai filiae Severinae Iulia Vindemia matronae\n
EDCS-28000175.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Iulia Severina, daughter of Caius, from Iulia Vindemia, her devoted patroness.
EDCS-28000175.summary: This funerary dedication is from Iulia Vindemia, a patroness, to her daughter Iulia Severina, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000176.text_edition: \n Iulia Cai filia Tertulla viva fecit sibi et Caio Iulio Cai filio Sabino fratri et Iuliae Ceunae matri libertis libertabusque suis omnibus in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28000176.translation: Iulia Tertulla, daughter of Caius, erected this while alive for herself, and for her brother Gaius Iulius Sabinus, son of Caius, and for her mother Iulia Ceuna, and for all her freedmen and freedwomen, with 12 feet in front and 20 feet in the field. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-28000176.summary: This is an inscription by Iulia Tertulla establishing a tomb for herself, her brother, her mother, and her freedmen, specifying dimensions and stipulating it's not inheritable, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000177.text_edition: \n Iulia Valentina patronae et matri pientissimae bene merenti annorum sexaginta et Iulia Caesia liberta et alumna matri et patronae pientissimae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000177.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Iulia Valentina, her most devoted patroness and mother, aged sixty, and for her most devoted freedwoman and foster-daughter and patroness, Iulia Caesia.
EDCS-28000177.summary: This funerary inscription honors Iulia Valentina, a patroness and mother figure aged sixty, and her freedwoman and foster-daughter Iulia Caesia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000178.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Valentinae Serenus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000178.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Serenus to his well-deserving wife, Julia Valentina.
EDCS-28000178.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Serenus to his wife, Julia Valentina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000179.text_edition: \n Iuliae Valeriae co<n=L>iugi Quelius Euvelpistus sic quae vixit mecum concorditer annis XVIII \n
EDCS-28000179.translation: Quelius Evvelpistus to his wife, Julia Valeria, who lived harmoniously with me for 18 years.
EDCS-28000179.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Quelius Euvelpistus to his wife, Julia Valeria, who lived with him for 18 years, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000180.text_edition: \n Lucio Iunio Epicteto Iunia Trofime delicato suo posuit annorum V\n
EDCS-28000180.translation: Iunia Trofime placed this for her beloved Lucius Iunius Epictetus, aged 5.
EDCS-28000180.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Iunia Trofime for her young son, Lucius Iunius Epictetus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000181.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuniae Nigrinae Iulius Victorinus uxori bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000181.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Iulius Victorinus to his well-deserving wife, Iunia Nigrina.
EDCS-28000181.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Iulius Victorinus to his wife, Iunia Nigrina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000182.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lae quae vix<i>it annos XXXV Pompeius Fortis coniugi bene B merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000182.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Pompeius Fortis placed this for Lae, his well-deserving wife, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-28000182.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Pompeius Fortis to his wife, Lae, who lived for 35 years, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000183.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Laelia Laudice Laelio Messio fratri carissimo qui vixit annos XXXIIII menses V\n
EDCS-28000183.translation: To the Divine Spirits: To Laelius Messius, dearest brother, who lived 34 years and 5 months, from Laelia Laudice.
EDCS-28000183.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Laelia Laudice to her brother, Laelius Messius, who died at the age of 34 years and 5 months, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000184.text_edition: \n Laelia Maximina posuit delicato Felicissimo defuncto annorum III\n
EDCS-28000184.translation: Laelia Maxima placed this for her beloved Felicissimus, deceased, aged 3.
EDCS-28000184.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Laelia Maxima for her young son, Felicissimus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000185.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Laelia Sexta Vitali annorum VII dierum X delicatae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000185.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Laelia Sexta placed this for her well-deserving beloved Vitalis, aged 7 years and 10 days.
EDCS-28000185.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Laelia Sexta for her young daughter, Vitalis, who died at the age of 7 years and 10 days, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Laeliae Zoe Coelius Euvodus sic coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000186.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Coelius Euvodus placed this for his well-deserving wife, Laelia Zoe.
EDCS-28000186.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Coelius Euvodus to his wife, Laelia Zoe, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000187.text_edition: \n Lucius Lartius Terpinus vivus fecit sibi et Corelliae Nice coniugi suae sanctissimae et Corelliae Melete delicatae suae et libertis libertabusque suis in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XXX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28000187.translation: Lucius Lartius Terpinus made this while alive for himself, and for his most cherished wife, Corellia Nice, and his beloved Corellia Melete, and for his freedmen and freedwomen; the front is 20 feet, the land 30 feet. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-28000187.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument from Lucius Lartius Terpinus for himself, his wife, his daughter, and his freedmen, stipulating it is not to be inherited, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28000188.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aulo Laxaeo Hermadioni Lucius Papinius Ursulus filius patri piissimo\n
EDCS-28000188.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Papinius Ursulus, son, erected this for his most devoted father, Aulus Laxaeus Hermadion.
EDCS-28000188.summary: A tombstone erected by Lucius Papinius Ursulus for his father, Aulus Laxaeus Hermadion, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000189.text_edition: \n Liciniae Valeriae quae vixit annis plus minus XXXX filii matri memoriam posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000189.translation: Her sons erected this memorial for Licinia Valeria, who lived more or less 40 years.
EDCS-28000189.summary: A memorial erected by sons for their mother, Licinia Valeria, who died around the age of 40, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000190.text_edition: \n Caius Ligustius Felix vivus posuit sibi et Agriae Hermogeniae coniugi piissimae\n
EDCS-28000190.translation: Gaius Ligustius Felix erected this for himself and for his most devoted wife, Agria Hermogenia, while he was alive.
EDCS-28000190.summary: A funerary monument set up by Gaius Ligustius Felix for himself and his wife Agria Hermogenia, indicating he was alive at the time of its erection, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000191.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Livio Pontiano Tribonia Eutychis coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000191.translation: To the Divine Shades. Tribonia Eutychis for her most dutiful husband, Livius Pontianus.
EDCS-28000191.summary: A tombstone dedicated by Tribonia Eutychis to her deserving husband, Livius Pontianus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000192.text_edition: \n Lollia Luci liberta Musa hic sita\n
EDCS-28000192.translation: Musa, freedwoman of Lucius, lies here.
EDCS-28000192.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of Musa, a freedwoman of someone named Lucius, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000193.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lollia Rhodope.
EDCS-28000193.summary: A tombstone dedicated to Lollia Rhodope, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000194.summary: A memorial for a daughter named Lollia Secunda, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000195.translation: For the sons of Iotinus Neritus.
EDCS-28000195.summary: A dedication or memorial for the sons of Iotinus Neritus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lupae filiae defunctae annorum VIII mensium V Epetinus et Calliste parentes\n
EDCS-28000196.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Lupa, daughter, who died at 8 years and 5 months. Epetinus and Calliste, parents.
EDCS-28000196.summary: A tombstone erected by parents Epetinus and Calliste for their daughter Lupa, who died at the age of 8 years and 5 months, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000197.translation: For Lucius Lupulanus Lybius, her most dutiful husband.
EDCS-28000197.summary: A dedication by an unnamed person for their dutiful husband, Lucius Lupulanus Lybius, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28000198.text_edition: \n Maltinia Pudentilla Publio Aelio Catandioni marito pientissimo con sic quo sic vixit annos XXX\n
EDCS-28000198.translation: To Maltinia Pudentilla, for her most devoted husband Publius Aelius Catandion, with whom she lived for 30 years.
EDCS-28000198.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased husband, noting their lifespan together.

EDCS-28000199.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Manii Monimuani vixit annos XV dies XVII Sexaria Primitiva mater filio piissimo\n
EDCS-28000199.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Cai Manius Monimuanus, who lived 15 years and 17 days. His mother, Sexaria Primitiva, for her most devoted son.
EDCS-28000199.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, set up by his mother.

EDCS-28000200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcio Artemisio Marcia Fo<r>tunata marito bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000200.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcia Fortunata placed this for her well-deserving husband, Marcius Artemis(i)us.
EDCS-28000200.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a wife for her deceased husband.

EDCS-28000201.text_edition: \n Marcia Charite Marciae Nigellae filiae defunctae infelicissimae nnorum XXVII et Caio Aprio Maximo marito \n
EDCS-28000201.translation: Marcia Charite, for her most unfortunate deceased daughter Marciae Nigellae, aged 27, and for her husband Gaius Aprius Maximus.
EDCS-28000201.summary: This epitaph commemorates a deceased daughter and a husband, set up by Marcia Charite.

EDCS-28000202.text_edition: \n Caio Mario Cai liberto Herme Mariae Cai libertae Prepusae Vitoria Cai et Marci liberta Severa posuit\n
EDCS-28000202.translation: Vitoria, freedwoman of Gaius and Marcus, placed this for Gaius Marius Herme, freedman of Caius, and for Maria Prepusia, freedwoman of Caius.
EDCS-28000202.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a freedwoman for two other freedpersons.

EDCS-28000203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Mario Marino Marcia Tertulla coniu<g=C>i bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-28000203.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcia Tertulla placed this for her well-deserving husband, Caius Marius Marinus.
EDCS-28000203.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a wife for her deceased husband.

EDCS-28000204.text_edition: \n Mariae Rectinae coniugi optimae Caius Laepicus Secundus maritus et Marius Novatus libertus\n
EDCS-28000204.translation: To Maria Rectina, her excellent wife, from her husband Caius Laepicus Secundus and her freedman Marius Novatus.
EDCS-28000204.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a wife, jointly set up by her husband and her freedman.

EDCS-28000206.text_edition: \n - Egnatio Maximo filio et Egnatiae Liberali filiae et Egnatiae Verae filiae et Egnatio Luciensi filio et Egnatiae Secundae filiae in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-28000206.translation: [for] Egnatius Maximus, son, and Egnatia Liberalis, daughter, and Egnatia Vera, daughter, and Egnatius Lucius, son, and Egnatia Secunda, daughter, in the field, 20 feet.
EDCS-28000206.summary: This inscription appears to record land division or inheritance for multiple children, potentially in a funerary context.

EDCS-28000207.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus mater Melitinini filiae defunctae annorum VII\n
EDCS-28000207.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Melitinina, daughter, deceased, aged 7.
EDCS-28000207.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young deceased daughter.

EDCS-28000208.text_edition: \n Gaius Metellius Restutus et Noniae sic Euhodiae sic coniugi sic vivis sibi posuerunt\n
EDCS-28000208.translation: Gaius Metellius Restutus and Nonia Euhodia, for themselves while living, placed this.
EDCS-28000208.summary: This is a funerary monument intended for the couple themselves, set up during their lifetime.

EDCS-28000209.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Moenius Herma Murcidiae Hilarae coniugi rarissimae posuit et sibi con sic qua per multos annos bene vixit\n
EDCS-28000209.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Moenius Herma erected this for his most rare wife, Murcidia Hilara, and for himself, because he lived well with her for many years.
EDCS-28000209.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Moenius Herma for his wife Murcidia Hilara and himself, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000210.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Murrecius Valentinus se vivo posuit sibi et coniugi Valeriae Secundinae et Murrecio Valerio filio infelicissimo defuncto nnrum XL\n
EDCS-28000210.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Murrecius Valentinus set this up for himself while alive, and for his wife Valeria Secundina, and for his most unfortunate son Murrecius Valerius, who died at 40 years of age.
EDCS-28000210.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Murrecius Valentinus for himself, his wife Valeria Secundina, and his deceased son, dating possibly to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000212.text_edition: \n homini incomparabili qui vixit nnos XXXV locus concessus a Naevio Aulo Mario Fortunato signo Asterio\n
EDCS-28000212.translation: For an incomparable man who lived 35 years. The place was granted by Naevius Aulus Marius Fortunatus, nicknamed Asterius.
EDCS-28000212.summary: An epitaph recording the burial place granted for an 'incomparable man' who died at 35, with the grant made by Naevius Aulus Marius Fortunatus, possibly from the late 1st century BCE or early 1st century CE.

EDCS-28000213.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Neulae puellae quae vixit annos V menses V dies IIII Anteus pater\n
EDCS-28000213.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Neula, a girl who lived 5 years, 5 months, and 4 days. Anteus, her father.
EDCS-28000213.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl named Neula, set up by her father Anteus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000214.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nigrina CONSTRO bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000214.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Nigrina CONSTRO, who deserved well.
EDCS-28000214.summary: A simple funerary epitaph dedicated to Nigrina CONSTRO, implying she was a deserving individual, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000216.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Noviae Lepidae uxoris et Auli Persi Lepidi <f=E>ili Aulus Persius Etruscus \n
EDCS-28000216.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Novia Lepida, his wife, and for Aulus Persius Lepidus, his son. Aulus Persius Etruscus.
EDCS-28000216.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Aulus Persius Etruscus for his wife Novia Lepida and son Aulus Persius Lepidus, likely from the mid-1st century CE, given the name of the famous poet Aulus Persius Flaccus.

EDCS-28000217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Obultronio Hermeti Aulus Obultronius Hermias filio pientissimo bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-28000217.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aulus Obultronius Hermias erected this monument for his most devoted son Hermetus, who deserved well.
EDCS-28000217.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Aulus Obultronius Hermias for his devoted son Hermetus, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000218.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Obultronio Paenia Plaetoria \n
EDCS-28000218.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Obultronius. Paenia Plaetoria.
EDCS-28000218.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Obultronius, set up by Paenia Plaetoria, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000219.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Oclatio Socrati Lucius Iulius Primus patri dulcissimo et Iuliae Agatetyche\n
EDCS-28000219.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Oclatius Socratus. Lucius Iulius Primus for his dearest father and for Iulia Agatetyche.
EDCS-28000219.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Lucius Iulius Primus for his father Lucius Oclatius Socratus and for Iulia Agatetyche, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28000220.translation: For Marcus Octavius, son of Marcus, of the Tromentina tribe.
EDCS-28000220.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Octavius, son of Marcus, belonging to the Tromentina tribe, possibly from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-28000221.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Octavius Menodotus se vivo et Aeliae Apuleiae coniu<g=C>i in fronte pedes X in aggro pedes X \n
EDCS-28000221.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Octavius Menodotus for himself while alive, and for his wife Aelia Apuleia, 10 feet in front, 10 feet in the ground.
EDCS-28000221.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording land allocated for a tomb by Lucius Octavius Menodotus for himself and his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000222.text_edition: \n testamento fieri iussit sibi et suis arbitratu Octaviae Thallusae uxoris\n
EDCS-28000222.translation: He ordered it to be made by his will for himself and his family, under the supervision of his wife Octavia Thallusae.
EDCS-28000222.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating that the tomb was constructed according to the deceased's will, with his wife Octavia Thallusae overseeing the process, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000223.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Oppiae Trofimae defunctae nnorum XXII coniugi bene merenti Flavius Castor et sibi\n
EDCS-28000223.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Oppia Trofima, deceased, aged 22, his well-deserving wife. Flavius Castor and himself.
EDCS-28000223.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a tomb to Oppia Trofima, who died at 22, by her husband Flavius Castor, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000224.text_edition: \n Marco Publio Chryseroti et Publiae Calliopeni Marcus Publius Rufus parentibus pientissimis posuit\n
EDCS-28000224.translation: Marcus Publius Rufus erected this for his most dutiful parents, Marcus Publius Chryseros and Publia Calliope.
EDCS-28000224.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the erection of a monument by Marcus Publius Rufus for his parents, Marcus Publius Chryseros and Publia Calliope, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000225.text_edition: \n Pacuvio Lucido defuncto annorum VIII Pacuvia Tryfera alumno\n
EDCS-28000225.translation: For Pacuvius Lucidus, deceased, aged 8. Pacuvia Tryfera, as guardian.
EDCS-28000225.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Pacuvia Tryfera, acting as guardian, for Pacuvius Lucidus, who died at the age of 8, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000226.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Paeani Cavari Frontonis Synodus fecit\n
EDCS-28000226.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Synodus made this for Paean Cavari Frontonis.
EDCS-28000226.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Synodus made a monument for Paean Cavari Frontonis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000227.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or memorial for Paenius Urso, who is referred to as a 'deity', possibly a deified individual or a cultic context, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Panes filiae infelicissimae T T T P A F\n
EDCS-28000228.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Panes, his most unhappy daughter. He gave, he made, he left (this).
EDCS-28000228.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Panes, described as the 'most unhappy daughter', by an unspecified male relative, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000229.text_edition: \n Lucius Papinius Hermes Lucio Papinio Luci filio Martiali defuncto infanti annuculo fecit\n
EDCS-28000229.translation: Lucius Papinius Hermes made this for his son Lucius Papinius Martialis, deceased, an infant of one year.
EDCS-28000229.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Lucius Papinius Hermes made a monument for his infant son, Lucius Papinius Martialis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000230.text_edition: \n defunct- nnum XI Papiniae Secundianae Lucio Papinio Crescenti filio Papinia Reddita liberta heres fecit\n
EDCS-28000230.translation: For Papinia Secundiana, deceased, aged 11. Papinia Reddita, freedwoman and heir, made this for her son Lucius Papinius Crescens.
EDCS-28000230.summary: This funerary inscription records that Papinia Reddita, acting as heir and freedwoman, erected a monument for her son Lucius Papinius Crescens and her daughter Papinia Secundiana, who died at 11, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000231.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Papirio Fortunato defuncto annorum XXV Papiria Eutychia liberto bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000231.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Publius Papirius Fortunatus, who died at 25 years old. Papiria Eutychia, to her well-deserving freedman.
EDCS-28000231.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Papirius Fortunatus, set up by his freedwoman, Papiria Eutychia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000232.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papiriae Fortunatae Antonius Cosmus maritus sibi et coniugi suae inconparabili sic posuit\n
EDCS-28000232.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonius Cosmus erected this for himself and his incomparable wife, Papiria Fortunata.
EDCS-28000232.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Papiria Fortunata, set up by her husband Antonius Cosmus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000233.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Petronia.
EDCS-28000233.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary epitaph for someone named Petronia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000234.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Musa Lucio Petronio Diomanio bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000234.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Valeria Musa, to Lucius Petronius Diomanus, well-deserving.
EDCS-28000234.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating a relationship between Valeria Musa and Lucius Petronius Diomanus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000235.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Petronio Maritimo sancto Fabia Procula bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000235.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Quintus Petronius Maritimus, the saintly. Fabia Procula, to him, well-deserving.
EDCS-28000235.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Petronius Maritimus, set up by Fabia Procula, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000236.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Petronio Primitivo filio pientissimo RPVVDI et Iuliae Probae filiae infelicissimae parentes posuerunt defunctis annorum XX\n
EDCS-28000236.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For their most dutiful son, Petronius Primitivus, and their most unfortunate daughter, Iulia Proba, who died at 20 years old. Their parents set this up.
EDCS-28000236.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their two children, Petronius Primitivus and Iulia Proba, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000237.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Petronius Secundus Primitivae uxori bene merenti\n
EDCS-28000237.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Petronius Secundus, to his well-deserving wife Primitiva.
EDCS-28000237.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Primitiva, set up by her husband Lucius Petronius Secundus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000238.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Petronio Turanniano iuveni infelicissimo qui vixit annos XXVII Quarta coniux et mater pientissima\n
EDCS-28000238.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Petronius Turannianus, the most unfortunate youth, who lived 27 years. Quarta, his wife and most dutiful mother, [set this up].
EDCS-28000238.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Petronius Turannianus, set up by his wife Quarta, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28000239.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Petronia Pastorilla Masurio Baiano coniugi pientissimo Petroniae Floe matri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28000239.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Petronia Pastorilla, to her most dutiful husband Masurius Baianus, and to Petronia Floe, her mother, well-deserving. [She] set this up.
EDCS-28000239.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Masurius Baianus and Petronia Floe, set up by Petronia Pastorilla, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100079.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Marcus Valerius Clemens, likely a funerary marker from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100080.text_edition: \n Valerio Hilaro defuncto annorum XVII Valeria Euthycala alumno posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-28100080.translation: To Valerius Hilarus, who died at 17 years of age. Valeria Euthycula dedicated this to her foster-child and to herself.
EDCS-28100080.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Valeria Euthycula to her foster-child, Valerius Hilarus.

EDCS-28100081.text_edition: \n V VL M Valeri- Max libertis libertabusque hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes in agro pedes XXI\n
EDCS-28100081.translation: To Valerius Max, his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument shall not follow the heir. In front, XX feet; in the plot, XXI feet.
EDCS-28100081.summary: This is an epitaph for Valerius Max, establishing a monument for his freedmen and their descendants, with dimensions specified.

EDCS-28100082.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Primigenio Caio Valerio Cai filio Metallico Plotia Amanda viro et filio\n
EDCS-28100082.translation: To Gaius Valerius Metallicus, son of Gaius Valerius, with Plotia Amanda for her husband and son.
EDCS-28100082.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Valerius Metallicus, erected by Plotia Amanda, possibly his wife, for him and their son.

EDCS-28100083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Protati Valeria Marcia patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100083.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Gaius Valerius Protatus, from Valeria Marcia, for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-28100083.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the deified spirits of Gaius Valerius Protatus by Valeria Marcia, his patron.

EDCS-28100084.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Vero vixit nnos XVI menses V dies XVI posuerunt Caius Valerius E Valeria V parentes filio pientissimo et sibi\n
EDCS-28100084.translation: To Gaius Valerius Verus, who lived 16 years, 5 months, and 16 days. Gaius Valerius and Valeria V, his parents, dedicated this to their most devoted son and to themselves.
EDCS-28100084.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Valerius Verus, erected by his parents, who also dedicated it to themselves.

EDCS-28100085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Valerio Zosimo Publius Valerius Zosimus fratri et Felicissima coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100085.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Publius Valerius Zosimus, from Publius Valerius Zosimus for his well-deserving brother and for his wife, Felicissima.
EDCS-28100085.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Publius Valerius Zosimus and his wife Felicissima by Publius Valerius Zosimus, possibly his brother.

EDCS-28100086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Bassillae et Caio Iulio Sereno Caius Iulius Valerianus filius parentibus bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100086.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Valeria Bassilla and Gaius Julius Sereno. Gaius Julius Valerianus, their son, dedicated this to his well-deserving parents.
EDCS-28100086.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Valeria Bassilla and Gaius Julius Sereno by their son, Gaius Julius Valerianus.

EDCS-28100087.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Clementinae defunctae nnorum XXII mensium III Cincia Histrica filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-28100087.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Valeria Clementina, who died at 22 years of age, 3 months. Cincia Histrica, her devoted daughter, dedicated this.
EDCS-28100087.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Clementina, dedicated by her devoted daughter, Cincia Histrica.

EDCS-28100088.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Crescentillae defunctae nnorum XXX Ulpia Severa patrona una cum P\n
EDCS-28100088.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Valeria Crescentilla, who died at 30 years of age. Ulpia Severa, her patron, together with P...
EDCS-28100088.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Crescentilla, dedicated by her patron Ulpia Severa.

EDCS-28100089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Ianuaria pro duobus fratribus infelicissimis Bitidius suae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28100089.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valeria Januaria dedicated this to her two unfortunate brothers and to her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-28100089.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Valeria Januaria for her two brothers and her wife.

EDCS-28100090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Splocinis posuit Vinicius Echio coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100090.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vinicius Echo set this up for his well-deserving wife, Valeria Splocina.
EDCS-28100090.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100091.text_edition: \n Varius Valentinus et Flavia Licentina Iulio Cyrano et Variae Flaviae Saloniae filiis infelicissimis qui vixerunt Iulius annos XXVIII menses II et Salonia annos XVI menses VIII parentes infelicissimi filiis carissimis posuerunt\n
EDCS-28100091.translation: Valentinus Varius and Licentina Flavia set this up for their most unfortunate children, Julius, who lived 28 years and 2 months, and Salonia, who lived 16 years and 8 months; their most unfortunate parents set this up for their dearest children.
EDCS-28100091.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by parents to their young children, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100092.text_edition: \n Variae Barbarae Atilia Prima mater filiae piissimae fecit\n
EDCS-28100092.translation: Atilia Prima made this for her most devoted daughter, Varia Barbara.
EDCS-28100092.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100093.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Variae Leonini matri pientissimae Firmus filius posuit\n
EDCS-28100093.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Her son Firmus set this up for his most dutiful mother, Varia Leonina.
EDCS-28100093.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a son to his mother, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100094.summary: A simple funerary dedication for Varia Maxima, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100095.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Variae Regillae matri piissimae\n
EDCS-28100095.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For his most devoted mother, Varia Regilla.
EDCS-28100095.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to a mother, Varia Regilla, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100096.text_edition: \n Varoniae Germanae et quae vixit nnos XXVII Aurelia Caesia mater filiae infelicissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28100096.translation: Aurelia Caesia, her mother, set this up for her daughter Varonia Germana, who lived 27 years, her most unfortunate and well-deserving child.
EDCS-28100096.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100097.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vediae Ursinae defunctae nnorum LXXX Vedius Augustalis matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100097.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vedius Augustalis set this up for his well-deserving mother, Vedia Ursinna, who died at the age of 80.
EDCS-28100097.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a son for his elderly mother, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100098.text_edition: \n Lucius Verulanus Ampliatus et Bellia Chrysorhoe vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-28100098.translation: Lucius Verulanus Ampliatus and Bellia Chrysorhoe, while alive, set this up for themselves and their family.
EDCS-28100098.summary: A funerary monument established by a couple for themselves and their descendants, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Verus patri qui vixit annos LXXX bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100099.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For his well-deserving father, Verus, who lived 80 years.
EDCS-28100099.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a son to his father, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100100.text_edition: \n Vesio Fortunato annorum XIIX Vesia liberta Vitalis viva fecit\n
EDCS-28100100.translation: Vesia Vitalis, freedwoman, made this for Vesio Fortunatus, aged eighteen, while she was alive.
EDCS-28100100.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication of a monument by Vesia Vitalis for Vesio Fortunatus, who died at eighteen.

EDCS-28100101.text_edition: \n Vettiae Marcellae coniugi rarissimae Publius Aelius Philippus maritus eius posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100101.translation: Publius Aelius Philippus placed this for his most rare wife, Vettia Marcella, honoring her for her deserving nature.
EDCS-28100101.summary: This funerary epitaph records a husband's loving dedication to his deceased wife, Vettia Marcella.

EDCS-28100102.text_edition: \n Vibio Gemello Clodius Ianuarius et Ancharia Valerilla fratri pientissimo\n
EDCS-28100102.translation: Clodius Ianuarius and Ancharia Valerilla for their most devoted brother, Vibius Gemellus.
EDCS-28100102.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Clodius Ianuarius and Ancharia Valerilla to their brother, Vibius Gemellus.

EDCS-28100103.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Vibio Primitivo Vibia Sabina \n
EDCS-28100103.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Marcus Vibius Primitivus, from Vibia Sabina.
EDCS-28100103.summary: This funerary epitaph is a dedication to the departed spirit of Marcus Vibius Primitivus by Vibia Sabina.

EDCS-28100104.text_edition: \n Vibio Restituto defuncto annorum XIX Vibia Tallusa mater imfelicissuma sic filio pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28100104.translation: Vibia Tallusa, a most unhappy mother, placed this for her most devoted son, Vibius Restitutus, who died at nineteen.
EDCS-28100104.summary: This funerary epitaph records an epitaph set up by a mother for her deceased son, Vibius Restitutus, who died at nineteen.

EDCS-28100106.text_edition: \n Vibiae Aproniae Maximus nepotiae suae posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100106.translation: Maximus placed this for his granddaughter, Vibiae Aproniae, honoring her for her deserving nature.
EDCS-28100106.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Maximus for his granddaughter, Vibiae Aproniae.

EDCS-28100107.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vibiae Tychini Veianius Turranianus C \n
EDCS-28100107.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Vibia Tychini, from Veianius Turranianus.
EDCS-28100107.summary: This funerary epitaph is a dedication to the departed spirit of Vibia Tychini by Veianius Turranianus.

EDCS-28100108.text_edition: \n D IV Victori infelicissimo filio vixit annos III menses X dies VII\n
EDCS-28100108.translation: For Victor, a most unfortunate son, who lived 3 years, 10 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-28100108.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of a deceased son named Victor, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-28100109.text_edition: \n Aplinae nnissimae Victoricus pater posuit de<f=S>uncti sic ANNVCLI menserum sic octo \n
EDCS-28100109.translation: Victoricus, the father, placed this for his most [---] daughter, departed, aged eight months.
EDCS-28100109.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by a father for his deceased daughter, who died at approximately eight months of age.

EDCS-28100111.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vincentio Hilariano homini bono qui vixit annis plus minus XXXV Florentia uxor proprio sumptu ATEED \n
EDCS-28100111.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Vincentius Hilarianus, a good man, who lived more or less thirty-five years. His wife Florentia [placed this] at her own expense.
EDCS-28100111.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by a wife, Florentia, for her husband, Vincentius Hilarianus, who died around the age of thirty-five.

EDCS-28100112.text_edition: \n Viria Secundina viva fecit sibi et Sestio Victori coniugi et libertis libertabusque suis Aelia Quintina viva fecit sibi et libertis libertabusque suis\n
EDCS-28100112.translation: Viria Secundina made this while alive for herself, for her husband Sestius Victor, and for her freedmen and freedwomen. Aelia Quintina made this while alive for herself and for her freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-28100112.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording two women, Viria Secundina and Aelia Quintina, who dedicated plots for themselves and their freedpersons.

EDCS-28100113.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Visciae Maximinae Annia Maxima mater\n
EDCS-28100113.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Annia Maxima, mother, set this up for Viscia Maxima.
EDCS-28100113.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Annia Maxima commemorates her daughter Viscia Maxima.

EDCS-28100114.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitalioni conservo qui vixit nnos plus minus XXXX Maritima coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-28100114.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Maritima set this up for her husband Vitalio, a fellow slave who lived approximately 40 years.
EDCS-28100114.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vitalio, a fellow slave, erected by his wife Maritima.

EDCS-28100115.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitalis Ianuario conservo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28100115.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Vitalis set this up for his well-deserving fellow slave Ianuarius.
EDCS-28100115.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ianuarius, a fellow slave, erected by Vitalis.

EDCS-28100116.inscription_type: carmina, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-28100116.text_edition: \n Vitalis Pieridi Luci Pomponi Pietatis vernae bene merenti Pieris hoc tumulo tegitur de matre Venusta sexto nata loco quae fuit a reliquis nondum viginti iuvenis compleverat annos quoi sic quoque virginitas nuper adempta fuit grataque florebat cunctis mortalibus aetas quam Fors ad superos noluit esse diu non pudor huic aberat pietas non grata parenti non amor in fratres eius avarus erat cara fuit mater fuerant caraeque sorores et pia coniugio grataque semper erat invida Pieridi cum venit letifera hora qua cubuit molli languida saepe toro hanc Atropos rapuit Lachesisque et tertia Clotho infelix mater tollit ad astra manus incusatque deos incusat denique Parcas quae vitam pensant quaeque futura canunt inplerunt fratres magnis mugitibus auras et cuncti flebant nec minus ante rogum haec fuit at sic tumulum miserae vox ultima matris ossa simul vidit tabida Pieridis hanc humus excepit leviter precor illa prematque infantem ex utero quae quoque sustinuit coniunx Pieridi supremum munus amatae hunc titulum scripsit pro pietate sua\n
EDCS-28100116.translation: Vitalis is buried in this tomb. Pieris, daughter of Venusta, was the sixth born of her mother. She had not yet completed twenty years of age, and her virginity was recently taken away; her age was pleasing to all mortals, but Fate did not wish it to last long. She lacked neither modesty nor piety towards her parents, nor was she avaricious in her love for her brothers. She was dear to her mother and sisters, and always pleasing in marriage. Cruel Pieris, when the deadly hour came, she lay weak on her soft couch, Atropos, Lachesis, and the third Clotho snatched her away. Her unhappy mother raises her hands to the stars, blaming the gods and finally the Fates, who weigh life and sing of future events. Her brothers filled the air with loud groans, and all wept before the pyre. Such was she, but this is the last cry of her miserable mother when she saw the ashes of Pieris. May the earth receive her lightly, and may it press lightly upon her, as she bore an infant in her womb. His beloved wife Pieris performed the final duty for her husband. She wrote this epitaph out of her piety.
EDCS-28100116.summary: This is a lengthy, metrical funerary epitaph for Pieris, recording her virtues, untimely death, and the grief of her family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100117.text_edition: \n Marco Vivio Obelliano Samanna et Caio LucretioCX \n
EDCS-28100117.translation: For Marcus Vivius Obellianus Samanna and Gaius Lucretius CX.
EDCS-28100117.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly recording dedications or names for two individuals, Marcus Vivius Obellianus Samanna and Gaius Lucretius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100118.translation: For Vivia Honorata, mother, who deserved well.
EDCS-28100118.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vivia Honorata, erected by a descendant.

EDCS-28100119.text_edition: \n Ulpius Paulinus infans innocentissimus qui vixit annis novem diebus quadraginta et duobus parentes Paulus et Filomele filio dulcissimo merenti pos(uerunt\n
EDCS-28100119.translation: For Ulpius Paulinus, most innocent infant, who lived nine years and forty-two days. His parents Paulus and Filomele set this up for their sweetest deserving son.
EDCS-28100119.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the infant Ulpius Paulinus, set up by his grieving parents.

EDCS-28100120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Veratio filio pientissimo defuncto nnorum XXI Arria Ias et Veratius Eutyches parentes infelicissimi posuerunt\n
EDCS-28100120.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: For Marcus Ulpius Veratius, most devoted son, who died at 21 years of age. His most unhappy parents Arria Ias and Veratius Eutyches set this up.
EDCS-28100120.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Veratius, erected by his grieving parents.

EDCS-28100121.text_edition: \n Ulpia Ingenua Ulpio Ingenuo filio infelicissimo defuncto nnorum XVI\n
EDCS-28100121.translation: Ulpia Ingenua for her most unhappy son Ulpius Ingenuus, who died at 16 years of age.
EDCS-28100121.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ulpius Ingenuus, erected by his mother Ulpia Ingenua.

EDCS-28100122.text_edition: \n Ulpia Cai filia Prima viva fecit sibi et Caio Ulpio Cai filio Quartino patri et Maximillae Poppiae matri et Sextilio Saturnino fratri et C Clementillae filio sic et libertis libertabusque COM E V\n
EDCS-28100122.translation: Ulpia Prima, daughter of Caius, made this while alive for herself, and for her father Gaius Ulpius Quartinus, son of Caius, and for her mother Maximilla Poppia, and for her brother Sextilius Saturninus, and for her son Clementillus; and for freedmen and freedwomen. COM E V.
EDCS-28100122.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Ulpia Prima for herself and her family members, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28100123.text_edition: \n Condidit hic natum infelix Bocontia sic mater functa EEPQVON nimis SVOSSOCIVM magno <d=A>olore nomine et ipsuo <hic> situs est Bocontius sic infans qui tulit bis binos <me=VF>ns<e=F>s Q qua<t=E>tu<or=E>que I <di=IN>es qu<e=L> effecit HVLASTISBATI suae domu <n=V>e ulla <c=V>orpo<ra=CR> <iac=T>ent \n
EDCS-28100123.translation: Unhappy Bocontia buried her son here, her mother having died. He is buried here, named Bocontius, an infant who lived for 2 months and 4 days, who made his mother, Hylas Bati, mourn with great grief. He lies here, no body of his own household.
EDCS-28100123.summary: This epitaph laments the death of an infant named Bocontius, who died after only two months and four days, placed by his mother Bocontia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100124.text_edition: \n Volsiae Pyrallidi Mammio Quartioni viro Volsio Saturnino filio Iuliae Craterillae filiae Octavio Mammiano nepoti Volsio Pyramo filio et Octavio Nardo adfini in fronte pedes XXV in agro pedes XXX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28100124.translation: For Volsia Pyrallida, for her husband Mammio Quartio, for her son Volsius Saturninus, for her daughter Iulia Craterilla, for her grandson Octavius Mammianus, for her son Volsius Pyramus, and for her relative Octavius Nardo. In front 25 feet, in the land 30 feet. This monument will not bind an heir.
EDCS-28100124.summary: This is a property deed and funerary monument for the Volsia family, specifying dimensions and stipulating that it cannot be inherited, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100125.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Volussia sic Laetina Iulio Apro alumno defuncto annorum VII bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100125.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Volussia Laetina for her well-deserving foster-son Iulius Apro, who died at seven years of age.
EDCS-28100125.summary: This dedication to the underworld spirits is for a deceased foster-son, Iulius Apro, aged seven, by Volussia Laetina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100126.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Urbica Fronemo sic patri suo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28100126.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Urbica erected (this) for her well-deserving father, Fronemo.
EDCS-28100126.summary: This is a dedication to the underworld spirits for a father, Fronemo, erected by his well-deserving daughter Urbica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100127.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Urgulanio Euvodio sic nnorum XXII Urgulania Nice fratri posuit\n
EDCS-28100127.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Urgulania Nice erected (this) for her well-deserving brother Publius Urgulanius Euvodius, who died at twenty-two years of age.
EDCS-28100127.summary: This dedication to the underworld spirits is for Publius Urgulanius Euvodius, who died at twenty-two, erected by his sister Urgulania Nice, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100128.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Urgulaniae Valentinae Publius Urgulanius Primitivus matri rarissimae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100128.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Urgulanius Primitivus for his most rare mother Urgulania Valentina, well-deserving.
EDCS-28100128.summary: This dedication to the underworld spirits is for Urgulania Valentina, described as 'most rare', by her son Publius Urgulanius Primitivus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100129.text_edition: \n Ursae Castrici Cladi libertae Lupa verna\n
EDCS-28100129.translation: For Ursa, freedwoman of Castricius Cladus. Lupa, the spring one.
EDCS-28100129.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Ursa, a freedwoman of Castricius Cladus, by Lupa Verna, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100130.text_edition: \n Ursicino defuncto nnorum IIII parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-28100130.translation: For Ursicinus, deceased at four years of age. His parents set this up.
EDCS-28100130.summary: This epitaph records the death of a child named Ursicinus at the age of four, erected by his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursulae annorum XXX Muntana mater fecit bene merenti\n
EDCS-28100131.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Muntana, his mother, made (this) for her well-deserving Ursula, who died at thirty years of age.
EDCS-28100131.summary: This dedication to the underworld spirits is for Ursula, who died at thirty, erected by her mother Muntana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100132.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Zosimo defuncto nnorum XX Lucius Pompeius Chresimus frater fratri pi<entissimo>\n
EDCS-28100132.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Zosimus, who died at the age of 20. Lucius Pompeius Chresimus, his brother, placed this for his most loving brother.
EDCS-28100132.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, commemorating Zosimus and dedicated by his brother.

EDCS-28100133.text_edition: \n Zotico <I=L>ulia Fortunata coniugi inconparabili sic qui vixit mecum nnos plus minus XXX bene merenti posuit et sibi \n
EDCS-28100133.translation: For Zoticus. Iulia Fortunata placed this for her incomparable wife, who lived with me for more or less 30 years, well deserving.
EDCS-28100133.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated by Iulia Fortunata to her deceased wife Zoticus.

EDCS-28100134.text_edition: \n Uxor fecit marito suo bene merenti titulum fecit\n
EDCS-28100134.translation: His wife made this for her deserving husband. She made the monument.
EDCS-28100134.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording a wife's dedication to her well-deserving husband.

EDCS-28100137.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, possibly recording a measurement related to a tomb or property boundary.

EDCS-28100144.text_edition: \n ALL ilios tres et vixit nnos XXV menses V dies XIII libertae carissimae merenti posu---\n
EDCS-28100144.translation: [Alilius?]... and lived 25 years, 5 months, 13 days. Placed by [her freedman?] for his most dear freedwoman.
EDCS-28100144.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording the lifespan of a freedwoman and the dedication by her lover.

EDCS-28100145.text_edition: \n menses IIII dies XXIII Nlacrumare sic suorumnat ferat haec parentes SP tulit hanc\n
EDCS-28100145.translation: [... four months, twenty-three days. [It is sad that] her parents bore this.]
EDCS-28100145.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, possibly part of a funerary text lamenting the deceased.

EDCS-28100146.text_edition: \n pienentissimo qui vicxit sic mecum annos XXVIII posuit ucxor sic ma<r=P>ito suo \n
EDCS-28100146.translation: [...] for the most loving [...] who lived with me for 28 years. His wife placed this for her husband.
EDCS-28100146.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated by a wife to her husband.

EDCS-28100147.text_edition: \n oria carissimo co<n=I>iu<g=C>i <p=R>osuit qui vixit annorum <p=R>lus minus numero quinqua<g=C>inta \n
EDCS-28100147.translation: For Orius, dearest husband. Placed by [him] who lived for approximately fifty years.
EDCS-28100147.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated to a husband named Orius.

EDCS-28100148.translation: Casius, his son, for his most loving father.
EDCS-28100148.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated by a son to his father.

EDCS-28100150.text_edition: \n qui vixit annis XXVI diebuu(s) XXXX MEPIA posuerunt MIRCODRLEO EXAEIRA\n
EDCS-28100150.translation: [...] who lived 26 years, 30 days. Mepia and Mirco and Drleus and Exaira placed [this?].
EDCS-28100150.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording the lifespan of the deceased and listing several dedicators.

EDCS-28100151.summary: This inscription, likely a dedication or an epitaph, records a tree dedicated to a person named Ae Felix.

EDCS-28100153.translation: To Su..., consul, to the one who deserved well.
EDCS-28100153.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning a consul and a beneficiary, possibly a dedication or epitaph, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28100154.summary: This very short inscription records a personal name, Ario Vse Hayas, possibly an epitaph or ownership mark.

EDCS-28100155.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nyurus socrae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28100155.translation: To the Divine Shades. Nyurus erected [this] to his well-deserving mother-in-law.
EDCS-28100155.summary: This is a dedication to the Manes, recording that Nyurus erected a monument for his deserving mother-in-law, likely an epitaph from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28100157.summary: This inscription consists of a series of seemingly personal names, possibly forming a list of individuals or place names, with no clear context provided.

EDCS-28100158.summary: This is a fragmented phrase, possibly part of a larger epitaph or dedication, mentioning 'all the living', with an uncertain context and date.

EDCS-28100166.text_edition: \n VIII Idus Ianuarias I vir- clarissim- \n
EDCS-28100166.translation: On the eighth day before the Ides of January, from a clarissimus...
EDCS-28100166.summary: This fragment records a date (January 5th) and refers to a person of senatorial rank (clarissimus), likely part of a formal inscription from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28100169.text_edition: \n Innocenti puero Constantio qui in nnis VIII caruit minas saeculi Barbas et Haeraclia sic parentes\n
EDCS-28100169.translation: To Innocens, a boy, Constantio, who lacked eight years of the age of the world, Barbas and Haeraclia, his parents, [set this up].
EDCS-28100169.summary: This epitaph records the death of a young boy named Constantio, who died at the age of eight, and was erected by his parents, Barbas and Haeraclia, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-28100173.text_edition: \n VITREA voque et natae nata uti comite OVTVIMRC comitata necavit um sprevit nulloque cupidine eius MVC fuit uno contenta marito nomen erit a summo NVII VERS bene meritae Sabinus et sibi\n
EDCS-28100173.translation: Vitrea, and her daughter, a daughter born, accompanied by the companion Ovtvimrc, killed herself, scorned no one, and with no desire for another, content with one husband, her name will be from the highest NVII VERS, well-deserving, Sabinus and for himself.
EDCS-28100173.summary: This is a complex and partially restored epitaph detailing the life and death of a woman named Vitrea, who seemingly committed suicide after a life of fidelity and contentment with her husband, erected by Sabinus.

EDCS-28300001.text_edition: \n Pullia Cai filia Surisca Nimphis sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28300001.translation: Pullia Surisca, daughter of Caius, fulfilled her vow to the Nymphs willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28300001.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Pullia Surisca to the Nymphs, likely from a rural or provincial context during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28300002.text_edition: \n Lucius Stallius Secundus VIvir Augustalis et Stallia Callirhoe uxor cum liberis aedem Matri Magnae ex voto suscepto fecerunt sua pecunia\n
EDCS-28300002.translation: Lucius Stallius Secundus, a Six-member official of the Augustan cult, and his wife Stallia Callirhoe, with their children, built this temple to the Great Mother from a vow they had undertaken, at their own expense.
EDCS-28300002.summary: A dedication of a temple to the Great Mother by a local magistrate and his family, funded by themselves.

EDCS-28300003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Titulenio Luci filio Respecto veterano beneficiario legionis X geminae Titia Myrsine marito karissimo\n
EDCS-28300003.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Titia Myrsine dedicated this to her dearest husband, Lucius Titulenius Respectus, son of Lucius, a veteran serving as a specialist officer in the Tenth Gemina Legion.
EDCS-28300003.summary: A funerary dedication for a veteran officer of the Tenth Gemina Legion, set up by his wife.

EDCS-28300004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Vibius Cai filius Pomptina Pudens domo Arretio |(centurio) legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis vivos sic fecit sibi et Iuliae Cai filiae Pollae uxori\n
EDCS-28300004.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Vibius Pudens, son of Gaius, of the Pomptina tribe, from Arretium, a centurion of the Seventh Claudian Legion, known for its piety and loyalty, erected this while alive for himself and his wife Julia Polla, daughter of Gaius.
EDCS-28300004.summary: A tomb inscription for a centurion of the Seventh Claudian Legion, dedicated by himself for himself and his wife.

EDCS-28300005.text_edition: \n Aurelio Longiniano decurioni coloniae Drobetensium Aelia Balbina coniux obsequentissima qui liberos suos SCERISTANVPATITIOSS mater quae vixit cum eo annis XX\n
EDCS-28300005.translation: To Aurelius Longinianus, decurion of the colony of Drobeta. Aelia Balbina, his most obedient wife, who lived with him for 20 years. The mother who [---].
EDCS-28300005.summary: A funerary inscription for a decurion of Drobeta, set up by his wife, potentially mentioning a mother.

EDCS-28300006.text_edition: \n Titus Rubrius Titi libertus Philonomus IIIIIIvir testamento fieri iussit sibi et Baureae ||(mulierum) libertae Rufae et Tito Rubrio Titi filio Albae\n
EDCS-28300006.translation: Titus Rubrius Philonomus, freedman of Titus, a Six-member official, ordered this to be made by his will for himself, his freedwoman Baurea, and his son Titus Rubrius Alba.
EDCS-28300006.summary: A tomb inscription for a freedman and former official, established by his will for himself and his family members.

EDCS-28300007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliis Geminae matri et Adiutori Aeliis Valentinae Primo fratribus Tertia posuit\n
EDCS-28300007.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Tertia dedicated this to her mother Aelia Gemia and her brother Adiutor Aelius Valentinus Primus.
EDCS-28300007.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Tertia for her mother and brother.

EDCS-28300008.text_edition: \n Aeliae Ursae quae vixit annos XXII menses VII dies XVI Iulius Hermes pater posuit et Statius Dionysocles maritus infelicissimae\n
EDCS-28300008.translation: To Aelia Ursa, who lived 22 years, 7 months, and 16 days. Her father Iulius Hermes and her husband Statius Dionysocles, set this up for his most unfortunate wife.
EDCS-28300008.summary: A tomb inscription for Aelia Ursa, set up by her father and husband.

EDCS-28300009.text_edition: \n Antonio Vibiano nnorum XXX Antonia Valeria soror pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28300009.translation: To Antonius Vibianus, aged 30 years. His sister Antonia Valeria set this up for her most dutiful brother.
EDCS-28300009.summary: A funerary dedication for Antonius Vibianus, aged 30, set up by his sister.

EDCS-28300010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Firminus et Flavia Prima parentes infelicissimi filiabus suis Stercoriae annorum VIII Iovino annorum IIII Lucio annorum IIII indulgentissimis\n
EDCS-28300010.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aurelius Firminus and Flavia Prima, the most heartbroken parents, for their daughters Stercoria, aged 8, and Iovinus, aged 4, and Lucius, aged 4, their most loving children.
EDCS-28300010.summary: A funerary dedication by parents for their young children who died at ages 8 and 4.

EDCS-28300011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Saturninae defunctae nnorum LXV Mucius Crescis uxori bene merenti et sibi posuit loco concesso a Ulpio Varrone\n
EDCS-28300011.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Mucius dedicated this, with permission from Ulpius Varrone, for his well-deserving wife Aurelia Saturnina, who died at 65 years of age, and for himself.
EDCS-28300011.summary: A tomb inscription for Aurelia Saturnina, set up by her husband for her and himself, with permission for the burial site.

EDCS-28300012.text_edition: \n Caius Bennius uxori suae Caesiae Optatae non lubens sic fecit sed fatum sic fecit\n
EDCS-28300012.translation: Gaius Bennius made this for his wife Caesellius Optata not willingly, but fate willed it so.
EDCS-28300012.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Caesellius Optata, commissioned by her husband Gaius Bennius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300013.text_edition: \n Lucius Caesellius Publi filius Tromentina Maximus Caesellia Publi filia Quarta\n
EDCS-28300013.translation: Lucius Caesellius Maximus, son of Publius, of the tribe Tromentina, and Caesellia Quarta, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-28300013.summary: This inscription records the names and tribal affiliations of Lucius Caesellius Maximus and Caesellia Quarta, possibly a married couple or siblings, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Coelius Probus coniugi Mariae Marcellae bene merenti et Marius Marcellus matri piissimae defunctae annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-28300014.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Coelius Probus for his deserving wife Maria Marcella, and Marius Marcellus for his devout mother, deceased at 35 years.
EDCS-28300014.summary: This funerary inscription honors Maria Marcella, wife of Publius Coelius Probus, and her mother by Marius Marcellus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300015.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunio defuncto nnorum III mensium XI Caetennianus et Victorina alumno carissimo\n
EDCS-28300015.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Fortunius, deceased at 3 years and 11 months. Caetennianus and Victorina for their dearest foster-child.
EDCS-28300015.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Fortunius, a young child, set up by Caetennianus and Victorina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Iulius Alexander vivus fecit sibi et Sextiae Prim(a)e coniugi rariissimae\n
EDCS-28300016.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Iulius Alexander made this while alive for himself and for his most rare wife, Sextia Prima.
EDCS-28300016.summary: This funerary inscription is a self-dedication and for his wife Sextia Prima, made by Lucius Iulius Alexander, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300017.text_edition: \n Quintus Tullius Crescens Liberali nepotiae defunctae nnorum XXV\n
EDCS-28300017.translation: For Liberalis, deceased granddaughter, 25 years old. Quintus Tullius Crescens.
EDCS-28300017.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Liberalis, a granddaughter aged 25, set up by Quintus Tullius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300018.text_edition: \n Gai Iuli Queti verna nomine Primitivus nnorum V posuit Abascantus pater\n
EDCS-28300018.translation: Gaius Iulius Quetus, a slave named Primitivus, aged 5 years. His father Abascantus erected this.
EDCS-28300018.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Primitivus, the 5-year-old son of the slave Gaius Iulius Quetus, erected by his father Abascantus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300019.text_edition: \n Iuliae Lascivae et Iuliae Lupae nnorum XVIII Iulia Panthera posuit sorori et matri\n
EDCS-28300019.translation: For Iulia Lasciva and Iulia Lupa, 18 years old. Iulia Panthera set this up for her sister and mother.
EDCS-28300019.summary: This funerary inscription honors two individuals, Iulia Lasciva and Iulia Lupa (aged 18), as sister and mother, by Iulia Panthera, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Musculo Orestes suo delicato annorum V posuit\n
EDCS-28300020.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Musculus, their pet, 5 years old. Orestes set this up.
EDCS-28300020.summary: This funerary inscription is for a beloved pet named Musculus, aged 5, dedicated by Orestes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Ovio Maximo viro honesto marito optimo Valeria Pomponia marito suo et sibi fecit et titulum posuit et lorici(am) inpendio suo fecit\n
EDCS-28300021.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Publius Ovius Maximus, an honorable husband, the best of husbands. Valeria Pomponia made this for her husband and for herself, and erected the monument and provided the stone casing at her own expense.
EDCS-28300021.summary: This funerary inscription records Valeria Pomponia's dedication for her late husband Publius Ovius Maximus, and also for herself, including the erection of the monument and casing, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300022.text_edition: \n Pompeiae Maximinae vixit nnos XI Valeria Titia filiae infelicissimae et Valeriae Titiae vixit nnos XXXV Caius Pompeius Cerdo coniugi incomparabili bene merenti\n
EDCS-28300022.translation: To Pompeia Maximina, who lived 11 years. Valeria Titia, her most unhappy daughter, and Valeria Titia, who lived 35 years, [erected this] to Gaius Pompeius Cerdo, her incomparable and well-deserving husband.
EDCS-28300022.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of Pompeia Maximina, Valeria Titia, and Gaius Pompeius Cerdo, with dedications from family members.

EDCS-28300023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pompeiae Phyebae sic Lucius Epidius Valens posuit sibi et uxori bene merenti et libertis libertabusque suis in fronte pedes VIII in agro pedes VIIII\n
EDCS-28300023.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Epidius Valens erected this to Pompeia Phyeba, his wife, to himself, his well-deserving wife, and his freedmen and freedwomen, extending 8 feet in front and 9 feet in depth.
EDCS-28300023.summary: This funerary inscription by Lucius Epidius Valens dedicates a plot to his wife Pompeia Phyeba, himself, and his freed dependents.

EDCS-28300024.text_edition: \n Primitivae filiae defunctae annorum XXXIII parentes Lartius et Hermogenia miserrimae filiae posuerunt\n
EDCS-28300024.translation: To Primitiva, deceased daughter, 33 years old. Her parents Lartius and Hermogenia erected this for their most miserable daughter.
EDCS-28300024.summary: This epitaph records the death of Primitiva at age 33, with the monument erected by her grieving parents.

EDCS-28300025.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito T Victori defuncto nnorum XVI Titus T Privatus et Servilia Chrysis parentes infelicissimi posuerunt\n
EDCS-28300025.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Titus T. Victor, deceased, 16 years old. His most unhappy parents Titus T. Privatus and Servilia Chrysis erected this.
EDCS-28300025.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Titus T. Victor at age 16, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-28300026.text_edition: \n T<e=F>rentia viva fecit sibi Quinto Statio Publi filio Crescenti? --- Marco Statio Publi filio S<e=F>vero? --- \n
EDCS-28300026.translation: Terentia, while alive, made this for herself, for Quintus Sthenius Crescentes, son of Publius [---], and for Marcus Sthenius Severus, son of Publius [---].
EDCS-28300026.summary: This inscription records the self-funded funerary arrangements made by Terentia for herself and possibly two other individuals, Quintus and Marcus Sthenius.

EDCS-28300027.text_edition: \n Turiae Artemisiae quae vixit nnos XXXVII menses VIII Iulius Artemisianus matri dulcissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28300027.translation: To Turia Artemisia, who lived 37 years and 8 months. Iulius Artemisianus erected this for his dearest and well-deserving mother.
EDCS-28300027.summary: This epitaph records the death of Turia Artemisia at age 37 years and 8 months, dedicated by her son.

EDCS-28300028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitalioni posuit Maxima coniux <q=O>ui vixit c<u=O>(m) <e=L>a annus sic XXIII posuit bene merenti \n
EDCS-28300028.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Maxima erected this to Vitalio, her well-deserving husband, who lived with her for 23 years.
EDCS-28300028.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Vitalio, dedicated by his wife Maxima, noting their 23 years of marriage.

EDCS-28300030.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-28300030.text_edition: \n Sarcofagum Ursicli et Tertiae depositio Ursicli die III Idus Decembres indictione XIIII si quis vero voluerit sine conscientia supra sibi praesumere det poenae sic nomine sanctae aecclesiae sic ante litis ingressum auri uncias IIII\n
EDCS-28300030.translation: Sarcophagus of Ursiclius and Tertia. Deposit of Ursiclius on the 13th day before the Kalends of December, in the 14th indiction. If anyone wishes to take space above for themselves without conscience, they shall pay a penalty of 4 ounces of gold in the name of the holy church before entering litigation.
EDCS-28300030.summary: This inscription concerns the burial arrangements and associated penalties for Ursiclius and Tertia, with a specific mention of a donation to the church and a date in the 14th indiction.

EDCS-28300031.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Drosus Charini conservae bene merenti posuuit\n
EDCS-28300031.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Drosus erected this to Charinus, his well-deserving fellow slave.
EDCS-28300031.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Charinus, dedicated by his fellow slave Drosus.

EDCS-28300032.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio Marcus Ippius Luci filius Stellatina Benevento Vitalis |(centurio) cohortis VIII voluntariorum [Phi][lippianae] votum libens posuit Imperatore domino nostro [Philippo] et Tittiano consulibus III Nonas Ianuarias signo Equitii\n
EDCS-28300032.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the Genius. Marcus Ippius Vitalis, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, from Benevento, a centurion of the 8th cohort of volunteers of Philippi, willingly set up this vow during the consulship of our Emperor Lord Philippus and Tittianus, on the 3rd day before the Nones of January, by the sign of Equitius.
EDCS-28300032.summary: This inscription records a military votive offering by Marcus Ippius Vitalis, a centurion, to Jupiter and the Genius, dated to the consulship of Philippus and Tittianus.

EDCS-28300034.text_edition: \n Lucius Allius Luci filius Fabia signifer legionis XI nnum XXX stipendiorum X hic situs est Lucius Statienus Luci filius Fabia Catulus posuit\n
EDCS-28300034.translation: Lucius Allius, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe, standard-bearer of the 11th legion, aged 30, with 10 years of service, lies here. Lucius Statienus, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe, Catulus, erected this.
EDCS-28300034.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Allius, a standard-bearer in the 11th legion, erected by Lucius Statienus.

EDCS-28300035.text_edition: \n Titus Ancharenus Titi filius Sergia domo Laranda miles legionis VII annorum XLV stipendiorum XXIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-28300035.translation: Titus Ancharenus, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, from Laranda, a soldier in the 7th legion, aged 45, with 23 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-28300035.summary: A tombstone for Titus Ancharenus, a soldier in the 7th legion, originally from Laranda.

EDCS-28300036.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Domitius Cnaei filius Velina Pessinunte annorum XLIV stipendiorum XXV veteranus ex legione VII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-28300036.translation: Gnaeus Domitius, son of Gnaeus, of the Velina tribe, from Pessinus, aged 44, with 25 years of service, a veteran of the 7th legion, lies here. He ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-28300036.summary: A tombstone for Gnaeus Domitius, a veteran of the 7th legion from Pessinus, who requested it be built in his will.

EDCS-28300037.text_edition: \n Publius Apulanus Publi filius Pollia Sabinus domo Eporedia tribunus militum legionis XI N IM\n
EDCS-28300037.translation: Publius Apulanus, son of Publius, of the Pollia tribe, Sabinus, from Eporedia, a military tribune of the 11th legion N IM.
EDCS-28300037.summary: An inscription commemorating Publius Apulanus, a military tribune of the 11th legion, from Eporedia.

EDCS-28300038.text_edition: \n Melvadius Macri filius eques alae Claudiae novae domo Cugernus nnorum XXX stipendiorum XII testamento fieri iussit Tiberius Claudius Aurelius heres posuit\n
EDCS-28300038.translation: Melvadius, son of Macrinus, an equestrian of the newly formed Claudia cavalry, from Cugernus, aged 30, with 12 years of service, ordered this to be made in his will. Tiberius Claudius Aurelius, the heir, erected this.
EDCS-28300038.summary: A tombstone for Melvadius, an equestrian in the Claudia cavalry, from Cugernus, erected by his heir Tiberius Claudius Aurelius.

EDCS-28300039.text_edition: \n Caius Helvius Cai filius Macer Ul<t=P>(inia) sic miles \n
EDCS-28300039.translation: Caius Helvius Macer, son of Caius, of the Ultinia tribe, died.
EDCS-28300039.summary: A tombstone for Caius Helvius Macer, a soldier of the Ultinia tribe.

EDCS-28300040.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Ultinia sic Clistinna miles legionis VII anorum XL stipendiorum XIIX hic situs est\n
EDCS-28300040.translation: Caius Iulius, son of Caius, of the Ultinia tribe, Clistinna, a soldier in the 7th legion, aged 40, with 12 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-28300040.summary: A tombstone for Caius Iulius Clistinna, a soldier in the 7th legion, from the Ultinia tribe.

EDCS-28300041.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Marci filius Trernahensis miles legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis annorum XXXXV stipendiorum XX Marcus Arruntius frater et heres posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-28300041.translation: Marcus Iulius, son of Marcus, from Trerena, a soldier in the 7th Claudian legion, loyal and faithful, aged 45, with 20 years of service. Marcus Arruntius, brother and heir, erected this. Lies here.
EDCS-28300041.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Iulius, a soldier in the 7th Claudian legion, erected by his brother and heir Marcus Arruntius.

EDCS-28300042.text_edition: \n Quintus Oppius Quinti filius Pollia Foro Cornelii signifer le<g=C>(ionis) VII nnorum XXX stipendiorum VIII hic situs est heres posuit \n
EDCS-28300042.translation: Quintus Oppius, son of Quintus, of the Pollia tribe, from Forum Cornelii, standard-bearer of the 7th legion, aged 30, with 8 years of service, lies here. The heir erected this.
EDCS-28300042.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Oppius, a standard-bearer in the 7th legion, from Forum Cornelii, erected by his heir.

EDCS-28300043.text_edition: \n Marcus Vireius Titi filius Celer Leo domo Augusta Bagiennorum donatus a Tiberio Caesare torquibus armillis faleris sic\n
EDCS-28300043.translation: Marcus Vireius Celer, son of Titus, from Leo in Augusta Bagiennorum, was awarded torcs, armbands, and a harness by Tiberius Caesar.
EDCS-28300043.summary: An inscription recording military awards given by Tiberius Caesar to Marcus Vireius Celer, an inhabitant of Augusta Bagiennorum.

EDCS-28300044.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Alienus, likely a freedman or former slave, for himself or a deceased relative, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300046.text_edition: \n Marcus Annonius AELIAS C IV nnrum XXX aetate infelix SITAS et filii duo Caius Iulius posuit coniugi suae bene meritae de suo\n
EDCS-28300046.translation: Marcus Annonius Aelianus, unhappy in the thirty-first year of his age, and his two sons Caius Iulius, erected (this) to his well-deserving wife, from his own means.
EDCS-28300046.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Marcus Annonius Aelianus and his sons to his deceased wife, noting her age and the cost of the monument, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300047.text_edition: \n Caius Laberius filius es nnorum VII hic situs est hunc titulum posuit tibi fidus amicus ultuma sic quae potui debita persolvi non dolere mater faciundum fuit properavit aetas voluit hoc astrum meum vale et priores aetate te tollant hunc luctum tibi\n
EDCS-28300047.translation: Caius Laberius, son of Esnorum, of the seventh year, lies here; a faithful friend erected this monument for you. I have paid the last dues I could; the mother's grief was necessary; youth hurried it; fate willed my star thus. Farewell, and may the elders carry away this grief from you.
EDCS-28300047.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for Caius Laberius, a young boy, with a lament from a friend and possibly a mother, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300048.text_edition: \n Honesimus sic Titi Septumi sic servus nnorum XV hic situs est dominus bene merito posuit\n
EDCS-28300048.translation: Honesimus, a slave of Titus Septumius, of the fifteenth year, lies here; his master erected (this) to his well-deserving slave.
EDCS-28300048.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Honesimus, a fifteen-year-old slave, erected by his master, Titus Septumius, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300049.text_edition: \n Caio Statio Syriaco et Iuliae Natali \n
EDCS-28300049.translation: To Caius Statius Syricus and Iulia Natalia.
EDCS-28300049.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription, possibly incomplete, for Caius Statius Syricus and Iulia Natalia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus e Tertullin<a=N>ae coniugi simae et incomparabili et Aureliae Acutae filiae simae atque pientissimae parum felicibus Aurelius Maximus coniugi iimae et filiae dulcissimae posuit et sibi \n
EDCS-28300050.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Maximus erected (this) to his most loving and incomparable wife Tertullina and his most loving and most dutiful daughter Aurelia Acuta, and to himself; alas, for their unhappy lives.
EDCS-28300050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Aurelius Maximus for his wife Tertullina and daughter Aurelia Acuta, expressing his grief and devotion, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300051.translation: To Titia Procilla, of the thirty-sixth year.
EDCS-28300051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titia Procilla, who died at age 36, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300053.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Plaetorius Albanus posuit\n
EDCS-28300053.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Plaetorius Albanus erected (this).
EDCS-28300053.summary: This is a religious dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Plaetorius Albanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300054.summary: This is an incomplete religious dedication to Jupiter by Lucius Se..., dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300055.text_edition: \n Libero Augusto sacrum Lucius Aebutius Luci filius Sergia Celer aedilis IIvir iuri dicundo ex pecunia publica\n
