EDCS-28600120.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto omnipotenti Mithrae aeterno sacrum Atilius Tertius ex voto libiens posuit\n
EDCS-28600120.translation: To the all-powerful, eternal, unconquered god Sol Mithras, sacred. Atilius Tertius, willingly and according to his vow, set this up.
EDCS-28600120.summary: A votive dedication to the god Sol Invictus Mithras by Atilius Tertius.

EDCS-28600121.text_edition: \n Trivis Caius Aristius Clemens ex voto libens solvit merito\n
EDCS-28600121.translation: To Trivia. Caius Aristius Clemens willingly paid his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-28600121.summary: A votive dedication to Trivia by Caius Aristius Clemens.

EDCS-28600128.text_edition: \n Surus Sparuci filius domo Tribocus eques alae Claudiae novae nnorum XXX stipendiorum XIII hic situs est testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Celati sesquiplicarii heredis\n
EDCS-28600128.translation: Surus, son of Sparucus, from Tribocus, a cavalryman of the ala Claudia Nova, aged 30 years and 13 years of service, is buried here; he ordered this to be made by testament at the arbitration of Celatus, a sesquiplicarius.
EDCS-28600128.summary: A funerary inscription for Surus, a cavalryman of the ala Claudia Nova, detailing his age, service length, and burial instructions, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28600129.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Titinio Iuliano gybernatori sic IIII Fortuna natione Paraetonio vixit annos XLII militavit annos XIX Valeria Zosime coniugi bene merenti ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600129.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Titus Titinius Iulianus, governor, from Paraetonia by nation, who lived 42 years and served 19 years. Valeria Zosime, his deserving wife, arranged for this to be set up.
EDCS-28600129.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Titinius Iulianus, a governor, set up by his wife Valeria Zosime, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28600138.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae Falernae nepotillae et Claudio Proculo filio eius defuncto annorum VIII et Antoniae Clucerae sic filiae et Octavio Papiriano nepoti defuncto annorum VII Antonia Fencis sic mater infelicissima ob pietatem posuit\n
EDCS-28600138.translation: To the divine shades. Antonia Fencis, mother most unhappy, dedicated this to Antonia Falerna, granddaughter, and to her son Claudius Proculus, deceased at eight years of age, and to Antonia Clucera, her daughter, and to her grandson Octavius Papirianus, deceased at seven years of age, out of devotion.
EDCS-28600138.summary: This funerary monument was erected by a grieving mother for her deceased children and grandchildren.

EDCS-28600148.text_edition: \n II pientissimae Lucius Epidius Rufinianus maritus\n
EDCS-28600148.translation: To Lucius Epidius Rufinianus, most devoted husband.
EDCS-28600148.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a devoted husband, likely from his wife.

EDCS-28600149.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Flavio Primo Flavius Epictetus patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-28600149.translation: To the divine shades. Flavius Epictetus dedicated this to Marcus Flavius Primus, his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-28600149.summary: This is a dedication from a freedman to his patron.

EDCS-28600156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Claudiano filio annorum V \n
EDCS-28600156.translation: To the divine shades. To Iulius Claudianus, son, aged five.
EDCS-28600156.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy.

EDCS-28600168.text_edition: \n Marcus Poblicius Marci filius Aquila annorum II\n
EDCS-28600168.translation: Marcus Poblicius Aquila, son of Marcus, aged two.
EDCS-28600168.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, parentage, and age of a deceased child.

EDCS-28600173.text_edition: \n Marco Servenio Punico posuit Marcus Servenius Doctus filius\n
EDCS-28600173.translation: Marcus Servenius Doctus dedicated this to Marcus Servenius Punicus, his son.
EDCS-28600173.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by a father to his son.

EDCS-28600177.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Toria Secunda Publicio Menofilo coniugi suo bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-28600177.translation: To the divine shades. Toria Secunda dedicated this monument to Publicius Menophilus, her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-28600177.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by a wife to her deserving husband.

EDCS-28600182.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vesidiae Crescentillae AE defuncta<e=I> annorum XXV Vesidia Cassia mater filiae infelicissima<e=I> \n
EDCS-28600182.translation: To the divine shades. To Vesidia Crescentilla, deceased at 25 years of age. Vesidia Cassia, mother, to her most unhappy daughter.
EDCS-28600182.summary: This funerary monument was erected by a grieving mother for her deceased daughter.

EDCS-28600185.text_edition: \n Caius Volusius Primigenius VIvir vivos sic fecit sibi et Caio Volusio Euhemero conliberto VIviro Augustali et Caio Volusio Primigenio filio defuncto nnum VIII libertis libertabusque suis ossibus inferendis extraniorum nequis ossa inferre velit et tu et tibi in fronte pedes XXX in agro pedes XXX\n
EDCS-28600185.translation: Caius Volusius Primigenius, a *VIvir*, made this while alive for himself, and for Caius Volusius Euhemerus, a fellow freedman and *VIvir Augustalis*, and for Caius Volusius Primigenius, son, deceased at eight years of age, and for his freedmen and freedwomen, that no outsider might deposit bones for burial; and for you [implied recipient of the plot] and for yourself, 30 feet in front, 30 feet in the land.
EDCS-28600185.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument and columbarium inscription from a *VIvir* for himself, relatives, freedmen, and specifying burial plot dimensions, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28600193.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus Brittannicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator VI consul IIII pater patriae pontem Hippi fluminis vetustate corruptum restituit sumptum et operas subministrantibus Novensibus Delminensibus Riditis curante et dedicante Lucio Iunio Rufino Proculiano legato pro praetore\n
EDCS-28600193.translation: The emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus Britannicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, restored the bridge over the Hippi river, corrupted by age, with the expenses and labor supplied by the Novenses, Delminenses, and Riditae, under the supervision and dedication of Lucius Iunius Proculus Proculianus, legate pro praetore.
EDCS-28600193.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bridge by Emperor Commodus, with details of the communities that contributed and the imperial legate in charge, likely dating to around 177-180 CE.

EDCS-28600194.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul II proconsul pater patriae et Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar \n
EDCS-28600194.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, father of the fatherland, and to Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-28600194.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Philip the Arab and his son, Philip the Younger, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28600195.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Claudio Tacito Pio Felici Invicto Augusto nostro IIII\n
EDCS-28600195.translation: To our Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul for the 4th time.
EDCS-28600195.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Tacitus, dating to his reign in 275-276 CE.

EDCS-28600197.text_edition: \n Gaio Valerio Constantio et Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobilissimis Caesaribus\n
EDCS-28600197.translation: To Gaius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-28600197.summary: This inscription honors the Caesars Gaius Valerius Constantius (Maximian) and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-28600198.summary: This inscription honors Caesar Maximinus Daza, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-28600201.translation: To Emperor Caesar [[Marcus Antonius Gordianus]] Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions of the town for the 23rd year.
EDCS-28600201.summary: This damaged inscription honors Emperor Gordian III, dating it to his 2nd year of tribunician power and likely the 23rd year of the local calendar, probably mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28600203.text_edition: \n DDominis nnostris Galerio Valerio Maximiano et Flavio Severo invictis Auggustis\n
EDCS-28600203.translation: To our lords Galerius Valerius Maximianus and Flavius Severus, unconquered Augusti.
EDCS-28600203.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Galerius Maximian and Severus, likely dating to their joint rule in 306-307 CE.

EDCS-28700936.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Varroni veterano cohortis I |(miliariae) Aurelius Victorinus et Iulia filii heredes posuerunt\n
EDCS-28700936.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Victorinus and Iulia, children, and heirs, set this up for Marcus Aurelius Varro, a veteran of the 1st Cohort of Dacians.
EDCS-28700936.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by children and heirs for Marcus Aurelius Varro, a veteran, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700938.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Vitalis qui vixit nnos plus minus XIIII Aurelius Ianuarius miles legionis Italicae et Quirilla mater parentes filio infelicissimo miserrimio posuuerunt\n
EDCS-28700938.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Ianuarius, a soldier of the Italian Legion, and Quirilla, his mother, set this up for their most unfortunate and sorrowful son, Aelius Vitalis, who lived for more or less 14 years.
EDCS-28700938.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelius Vitalis, a young boy, erected by his soldier father and mother, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700940.text_edition: \n Asclepiade Domestico et Crescentilla adito libero in agro pedes VI\n
EDCS-28700940.translation: Asclepiades Domestus and Crescentilla, with their freeborn child, in the field, 6 feet.
EDCS-28700940.summary: This inscription likely marks a property boundary or a small burial plot belonging to Asclepiades Domestus and Crescentilla, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28700941.text_edition: \n Caius Asurius miles legionis VII centuria Trini nnorum XXXV stipendiorum XIV domo Florentia Severa liberta posuit\n
EDCS-28700941.translation: Caius Asurius, a soldier of the VII Legion, from the century of Trinus, aged 35, with 14 years of service, from Florence, erected this for Severa, his freedwoman.
EDCS-28700941.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Severa, a freedwoman, erected by her soldier master Caius Asurius, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700942.translation: Sextus Aelius, princeps of the legion...
EDCS-28700942.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording Sextus Aelius, princeps of a legion, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28700943.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-28700943.text_edition: \n Aurelius Alexsander sic beneficiarius numero frumentariorum legionis XI Claudiae vibus sic hanc sibi suo nomine iussit testamento arcam poni\n
EDCS-28700943.translation: Aurelius Alexander, a specially appointed soldier (beneficiarius) in the grain service of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, ordered this tomb chamber to be built for himself in his own name by his will.
EDCS-28700943.summary: This tomb inscription records Aurelius Alexander, a soldier in the grain service of the XI Claudia legion, who commissioned his own tomb by will, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700945.text_edition: \n ------ Au]reliae Ianuariae infelicissimae <f=E>iliae quae vixit nnos III menses X Aurelius Ianuarius miles cohortis VIII pater eius Aurelia Ursina mater posu<e=I>runt \n
EDCS-28700945.translation: Aurelia Ianuaria, his most unhappy daughter, who lived 3 years and 10 months. Aurelius Ianuarius, a soldier of the Eighth Cohort, her father, and Aurelia Ursina, her mother, erected this.
EDCS-28700945.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Aurelia Ianuaria, the young daughter of a soldier named Aurelius Ianuarius and Aurelia Ursina, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28700946.text_edition: \n Veneria Quartilla Aurelio Materno coniugi incomparabili et innocentissimo veterano legionis II Italicae natione Norico qui vixit nnos XLVIIII posuit coniunx bene merenti\n
EDCS-28700946.translation: Veneria Quartilla erected this for her husband, Aurelius Maternus, an incomparable and most innocent veteran of the Second Italica Legion, a Norican by birth, who lived 49 years.
EDCS-28700946.summary: This epitaph honors Aurelius Maternus, a veteran of the II Italica legion and a Norican, erected by his wife Veneria Quartilla, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700947.text_edition: \n ------ Au]reli legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis stipendiorum XXX filia Ioventia \n
EDCS-28700947.translation: Aurelia... of the Seventh Claudian Legion, loyal and faithful, with 30 years of service, her daughter Ioventia.
EDCS-28700947.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records Aurelia, a loyal soldier of the VII Claudia legion with 30 years of service, possibly associated with her daughter Ioventia.

EDCS-28700948.text_edition: \n Publio Bennio Sabino IIIIviro iure dicundo auguri IIIIviro iure dicundo nni flamini Augustali praefecto cohortis II Lusitanorum equitatae\n
EDCS-28700948.translation: To Publius Bennius Sabinus, IIIIvir for dispensing justice, augur, IIIIvir for dispensing justice, flamen of Augustus, prefect of the Second Lusitanian Cohort (cavalry).
EDCS-28700948.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors Publius Bennius Sabinus, listing his various civic and military offices including IIIIvir, augur, flamen Augustalis, and cohort prefect, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700949.summary: A simple epitaph recording the burial of Beres, an archer, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700952.text_edition: \n Quintus Cassius Quinti filius Tromentina Constans aedilis IIIIvir iure dicundo augur praefectus fabrum tribunus militum cohortis IIII voluntariorum civium Romanorum Quintus Cassius Cerdo libertus et heres faciundum curavit hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28700952.translation: Quintus Cassius Constans, son of Quintus, of the Tromentina tribe, aedile, IIIIvir for dispensing justice, augur, prefect of the artisans, military tribune of the Fourth Cohort of Roman Citizen Volunteers, had this made by Quintus Cassius Cerdo, his freedman and heir; this monument will not pass to the heir.
EDCS-28700952.summary: This extensive epitaph records the career of Quintus Cassius Constans, highlighting his local political and military roles, and specifies that his freedman and heir, Quintus Cassius Cerdo, commissioned the monument and that it is not entailed.

EDCS-28700953.text_edition: \n decurioni equitum praefecto cohortis III Alpinae et Quinto Ennio Severo nnorum XV fecit Lutatia Veneria viro et filio orba queror genetrix miseri post funera nati raptaque et ante premit me mea cara soror intra ter quinos infelix occidit annos sic illi coniunx sic toga pura data est nullaque propago manibus succepta parentis nullus honos fasces in mea tecta tulit omnia Ditis habet praeterquam nomen eorum nec comitem nati me sinit esse mei o genitor felix qui nec tua funera vidit nec filium filiam FRIII\n
EDCS-28700953.translation: To the decurion of the cavalry, prefect of the Third Alpine Cohort, and to Quintus Ennius Severus, aged 15. Lutatia Veneria, mother mourning her husband and son, left alone after the birth of her miserable son and taken away before her time, unfortunately died within five years; thus his wife, thus the toga virilis was given to him, and no offspring was conceived by his parents; no honor, no fasces brought to my home; Hades has all but their name, and does not allow me to be the companion of my child. Oh, fortunate father who saw neither your funeral nor that of your son, daughter...
EDCS-28700953.summary: This complex and emotionally charged epitaph, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century CE, laments the premature deaths of Quintus Ennius Severus and possibly his father and sister, commissioned by his mother Lutatia Veneria, who describes her profound grief.

EDCS-28700954.text_edition: \n Quinto Fabio Pacato Voltinia Viennae militi legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis nnum XXXVII Fabius Fabianus frater posuit\n
EDCS-28700954.translation: To Quintus Fabius Pacatus, soldier of the Eleventh Claudian Legion, loyal and faithful, aged 37. His brother Fabius Fabianus erected this.
EDCS-28700954.summary: This funerary inscription honors Quintus Fabius Pacatus, a 37-year-old soldier of the XI Claudia legion, erected by his brother Fabius Fabianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28700955.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Apuleio Montano veterano ex ordinato decurionum et IIviro coloniae Aequensium homini bono Octavia Rufina marito incomparabili et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-28700955.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Octavia Rufina set this up for her husband Lucius Apuleius Montanus, a veteran, former member of the town council and duovir of the colony of Aequa, a good man, and for herself.
EDCS-28700955.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by a wife for her deceased husband, a local magistrate and veteran, and for herself.

EDCS-28701027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iu<l>ius Probinu(s QVVV F C NBI posui<t=I> M V CXXX sibi et suis natis LX SN YZS |XSXZ X \n
EDCS-28701027.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulius Probinus set this up for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-28701027.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with unclear abbreviations, likely set up by Iulius Probinus for himself and his family.

EDCS-28701300.text_edition: \n Marcus Attius Cinna ob honorem Augustalitatis \n
EDCS-28701300.translation: Marcus Attius Cinna, because of the office of Augustalis.
EDCS-28701300.summary: This inscription records Marcus Attius Cinna holding the office of Augustalis.

EDCS-28701301.text_edition: \n Petronius Cai filius Rufus IIIIvir testamento fieri iussit sibi et Caio Petronio\n
EDCS-28701301.translation: Petronius Rufus, son of Gaius, ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for Gaius Petronius.
EDCS-28701301.summary: A will dictates the construction of a funerary monument for Petronius Rufus and Gaius Petronius.

EDCS-28701302.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus infelicissimae et Publius Atilius matri \n
EDCS-28701302.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Atilius set this up for his most unhappy mother.
EDCS-28701302.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Publius Atilius to his mother.

EDCS-28701304.text_edition: \n Lucius Cervius Restitutus vivus fecit Antoniae Secundae uxori et Statiae Ingenuae matri eius\n
EDCS-28701304.translation: Lucius Cervius Restitutus made this while alive for Antonia Secunda, his wife, and for her mother, Statia Ingenua.
EDCS-28701304.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Lucius Cervius Restitutus for his wife and her mother.

EDCS-28701305.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Cincio Fortunato Cincia Vitalis coniugi et Cincia Valeria filia patri piissimo\n
EDCS-28701305.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cincia Vitalis for her husband Gaius Cincinius Fortunatus, and Cincia Valeria, their daughter, for her most dutiful father.
EDCS-28701305.summary: This funerary epitaph is jointly dedicated by a wife and daughter to their deceased husband and father.

EDCS-28701306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crescenti defuncto annorum IIII dierum IV Messia Paula alumno bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28701306.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Messia Paula set this up for Crescens, deceased at four years of age and four days, for his well-deserved merits.
EDCS-28701306.summary: This funerary epitaph honors a young child, Crescens, and was set up by Messia Paula.

EDCS-28701307.text_edition: \n Lucius Domitius Agathopus testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-28701307.translation: Lucius Domitius Agathopus ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-28701307.summary: This inscription indicates that Lucius Domitius Agathopus made arrangements for a monument in his will.

EDCS-28701308.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Florius Cornelianus Corneliae Satriae matri carissimae posuit\n
EDCS-28701308.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Florius Cornelianus set this up for his dearest mother, Cornelia Satria.
EDCS-28701308.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Publius Florius Cornelianus to his mother.

EDCS-28701309.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fuscioni Floria Hilara mater filio\n
EDCS-28701309.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Floria Hilara, mother, to her son Fusconius.
EDCS-28701309.summary: A funerary inscription from a mother to her son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701310.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hilara<e> matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-28701310.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Hilara, mother, well-deserving.
EDCS-28701310.summary: A funerary dedication by an unknown person to a deserving mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701311.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Cai filio Nin \n
EDCS-28701311.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Julius Nin, son of Caius.
EDCS-28701311.summary: A funerary inscription for Julius Nin, son of Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701313.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Valentino qui vixit annos XXXXV Iulia Patrona\n
EDCS-28701313.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Julius Valentinus, who lived 45 years. Iulia Patrona.
EDCS-28701313.summary: A funerary monument recording the death of Julius Valentinus at age 45, dedicated by Iulia Patrona, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701314.text_edition: \n Iulia Ide Iulio Prim<ig>enio coniugi et patrono bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-28701314.translation: Iulia Ide erected this to her husband and patron, Iulius Primigenius, well-deserving.
EDCS-28701314.summary: A dedication by Iulia Ide to her husband and patron, Iulius Primigenius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701315.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Licinius Messalinus bene merens a liberto merenti\n
EDCS-28701315.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Licinius Messalinus, well-deserving, from a deserving freedman.
EDCS-28701315.summary: A funerary dedication by a freedman to his well-deserving former master, Lucius Licinius Messalinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701318.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Petronio Urso Lucius Petronius Ursus filius Petronius Augustio filius patri pio posuerunt\n
EDCS-28701318.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Petronius Ursus and his son Petronius Augustius erected this to their pious father.
EDCS-28701318.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Lucius Petronius Ursus and his son Petronius Augustius to their father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701319.summary: A simple dedication by Publius, likely marking a tomb or a small structure, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701320.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus patronae alumnae carae pientissimae Secundinae posuerunt annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-28701320.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Secundina, most devoted patroness and dear foster-child, who lived 18 years. They erected this.
EDCS-28701320.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by unknown persons to Secundina, their 18-year-old patroness and foster-child, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701321.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Stenio Tacito Byrria sic Lupula coniugi sanctissimo\n
EDCS-28701321.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Stenius Tacitus Byrrhus. To his most sacred wife, Lupula.
EDCS-28701321.summary: A funerary inscription dedicating a monument from Stenius Tacitus Byrrhus to his wife Lupula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701322.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Stercorio infelicissimo annorum plus minus annorum VIIII mater et pater posuerunt\n
EDCS-28701322.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: to Stercorius, most unlucky, aged more or less nine years, his mother and father placed this.
EDCS-28701322.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Stercorius, set up by his parents.

EDCS-28701323.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tigrini Tiburtinus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28701323.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Tiburtinus placed this for Tigrinus, who deserved well.
EDCS-28701323.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tigrinus, set up by Tiburtinus, likely a friend or relative, honoring his merit.

EDCS-28701324.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Varro Luci defunctus annorum XXX Panico mater et Dasto matertera pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-28701324.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: for Varro, Lucius, deceased, aged thirty years; his mother Panico and his aunt Dasto placed this for the most devoted.
EDCS-28701324.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Varro, deceased at thirty, set up by his mother and aunt.

EDCS-28701325.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Veneriae Petroniae Sucundus sic coniugi suae infelicissimae quae vixit nnos plus minus XL bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28701325.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: for Veneria Petronia, to his wife, most unlucky, who lived more or less forty years; Sucundus placed this for the one who deserved well.
EDCS-28701325.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Veneria Petronia, set up by her husband Sucundus, lamenting her early death.

EDCS-28701326.text_edition: \n Vipsanae Lupae Vivius Hyla filius bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28701326.translation: For Vipsana Lupa, her son Vivius Hyla placed this, who deserved well.
EDCS-28701326.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vipsana Lupa, set up by her son Vivius Hyla.

EDCS-28701327.text_edition: \n Dis bonis Manibus Ursinae conservae Silvinus conservus bene merenti posu annorum XX it\n
EDCS-28701327.translation: To the good spirits of the underworld: Silvinus placed this for Ursina, his fellow slave, who deserved well, aged twenty years.
EDCS-28701327.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ursina, a fellow slave, set up by Silvinus, honoring her merit and noting her age.

EDCS-28701329.text_edition: \n III annorum XXXXX P AT et bene merenti\n
EDCS-28701329.translation: Aged fifty years and three; [set up] for the one who deserved well.
EDCS-28701329.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, who was fifty-three years old.

EDCS-28701334.text_edition: \n Apollini sancto Antonius Castor IIvir coloniae Salonae ex praecepto dei posuit\n
EDCS-28701334.translation: For the holy Apollo: Antonius Castor, as aedile of the colony of Salona, placed this according to the god's command.
EDCS-28701334.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo by Antonius Castor, an official in the colony of Salona, acting under divine instruction.

EDCS-28701335.translation: Sacred to Augusta Diana: Quintus Valerius [---]
EDCS-28701335.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana Augusta, initiated by Quintus Valerius, but it is incomplete.

EDCS-28701338.text_edition: \n Iovi --- Titus Rom vir perfectissimus MV M \n
EDCS-28701338.translation: For Jupiter: Titus Rom, a most accomplished man, [---]
EDCS-28701338.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Titus Rom, an accomplished man, but the inscription is fragmentary.

EDCS-28701339.text_edition: \n Iovi Aeterno Flavius Apoleius sic Verocia Marcella et filia eorum et Appuleia Marcella votum libentes solverunt\n
EDCS-28701339.translation: To Jupiter the Eternal, Flavius Apoleius, along with Verocia Marcella and their daughter, and Appuleia Marcella, gladly paid their vow.
EDCS-28701339.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Jupiter the Eternal by Flavius Apoleius and his family, likely a familial altar or offering.

EDCS-28701340.text_edition: \n Titus Pinarius Eros Iovi Caelesti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701340.translation: Titus Pinarius Eros gladly paid his vow, as was deserved, to Jupiter Caelestis.
EDCS-28701340.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Jupiter Caelestis by Titus Pinarius Eros, likely a personal offering.

EDCS-28701343.text_edition: \n V V B sacrum Marcus Ulpius Attalus collector cognationis ex pecunia multaticia\n
EDCS-28701343.translation: Sacred to the gods. Marcus Ulpius Attalus, collector of the association, made this from confiscated money.
EDCS-28701343.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Ulpius Attalus, likely for a collegium, funded by a fine.

EDCS-28701345.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Lucius Cornelius Apalaustus pro salute Marci Vivi Cresti amici karissimi ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28701345.translation: Lucius Cornelius Apalaustus erected this monument to the Unconquered God Mithras, according to his vow, for the safety of his dearest friend Marcus Vivius Crestus.
EDCS-28701345.summary: This inscription records a votive monument dedicated to Mithras by Lucius Cornelius Apalaustus for the health of his friend Marcus Vivius Crestus.

EDCS-28701347.text_edition: \n Nymphis Marcus Thorius Valens votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701347.translation: Marcus Thorius Valens gladly paid his vow, as was deserved, to the Nymphs.
EDCS-28701347.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to the Nymphs by Marcus Thorius Valens, likely a dedication at a water source.

EDCS-28701348.translation: Sempronia Ursa erected an altar to Priapus.
EDCS-28701348.summary: This inscription records an altar dedicated to Priapus by Sempronia Ursa, likely for fertility or protection.

EDCS-28701349.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum voto suscepto pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Nervae Traiani optimi Augusti Germanici Dacici nostri Trophimus servus Amandianus dispensator a solo fecit et aquam induxit loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-28701349.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Having undertaken a vow for the safety of our Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Trophimus, servant of Amandianus, the steward, made this from the ground up and brought in water, on land granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-28701349.summary: This inscription records the construction of a shrine to Silvanus Augustus by the steward Trophimus, funded by a decree of the local council, for the emperor's well-being, likely during the reign of Trajan.

EDCS-28701350.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. C. Sabinus [made this].
EDCS-28701350.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Augustus made by C. Sabinus, likely a simple votive offering.

EDCS-28701351.text_edition: \n Soli deo Sextus Cornelius Antiochus stellam et fructiferam ex visu libens posuit\n
EDCS-28701351.translation: Sextus Cornelius Antiochus gladly set up a star and a fruit-bearing [image or representation] to the Sun god, as seen in a vision.
EDCS-28701351.summary: This inscription records a votive offering, possibly a cult image, dedicated to the Sun god Sol by Sextus Cornelius Antiochus based on a vision.

EDCS-28701353.text_edition: \n Veneri Bacchae sacrum Aurelia Primula ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28701353.translation: Sacred to Bacchic Venus. Aurelia Primula dedicated this according to her vow.
EDCS-28701353.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Venus as Bacchus by Aurelia Primula, likely a personal vow related to revelry or fertility.

EDCS-28701356.translation: C. Aquilinus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28701356.summary: This inscription records a person named C. Aquilinus fulfilling a vow, likely on a small monument or altar.

EDCS-28701357.text_edition: \n Clodia Maxima votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701357.translation: Clodia Maxima willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-28701357.summary: This inscription records Clodia Maxima fulfilling a vow, likely on a small monument or altar.

EDCS-28701358.text_edition: \n Publius Cloelius miles cohortis Campanae custos Traguri votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701358.translation: Publius Cloelius, soldier of the Campanian cohort and custos of Tragurium, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28701358.summary: This inscription records Publius Cloelius, a soldier and official from Tragurium, fulfilling a vow, likely on a small monument or altar.

EDCS-28701359.translation: Iucunda willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-28701359.summary: This inscription records a woman named Iucunda fulfilling a vow, likely on a small monument or altar.

EDCS-28701360.text_edition: \n Paxaea Elpis cum suisque filiabus hoc monumentum ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28701360.translation: Paxaea Elpis, with her daughters, erected this monument in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28701360.summary: This inscription records Paxaea Elpis and her daughters dedicating a monument in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-28701361.text_edition: \n Caius Seius Nym votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701361.translation: Caius Seius Nym willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28701361.summary: This inscription records Caius Seius Nym fulfilling a vow, likely on a small monument or altar.

EDCS-28701366.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Valeri<o=C> Diocletiano Pio Felici Augusto res publica Salonitanorum \n
EDCS-28701366.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Augustus, the community of Salona.
EDCS-28701366.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the community of Salona to Emperor Diocletian, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-28701367.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-28701367.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Constantio victoriosissimo semper Augusto Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius vir clarissimus praeses provinciae Dalmatiae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-28701367.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantius, most victorious, ever Augustus, Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, most distinguished man and praeses of the province of Dalmatia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-28701367.summary: This inscription records Flavius Iulius Rufinus Sarmentius, a provincial governor, dedicating something to Emperor Constantius, likely on a public building or statue.

EDCS-28701368.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, invested with tribunician power...
EDCS-28701368.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicating something to an Emperor Caesar, noting his tribunician power.

EDCS-28701369.text_edition: \n Antonio Tauro ex dua<b=R>us centenariis ducenario post facto qui vixit annis LV Aelia Saturnina clarissima femina marito benignissimo \n
EDCS-28701369.translation: To Antonio Taurus, who served as a centenarius for twenty years and died after completing his service, who lived 55 years, Aelia Saturnina, most distinguished woman, to her most kind husband.
EDCS-28701369.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Aelia Saturnina to her husband Antonio Taurus, who was a centenarius.

EDCS-28701370.text_edition: \n Caeciliae Logianae eminentissimi viri filiae et Tullio Callipiano equiti Romano Tullia Logiana filia parentibus suis fecit in fronte pedes XXVI in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-28701370.translation: Tullia Logiana, daughter of the most eminent man Tullio Callipiano, a Roman knight, erected this for her parents, Caecilia Logiana and Tullio Callipiano, twenty-six feet in front and twenty feet in the field.
EDCS-28701370.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Tullia Logiana to her parents, Caecilia Logiana and Tullio Callipiano, a Roman knight.

EDCS-28701371.text_edition: \n Caio Pontilio Fregelliano consuli patrono decreto decurionum publice\n
EDCS-28701371.translation: Publicly, by decree of the local council, for Gaius Pontilius Fregellianus, consul and patron.
EDCS-28701371.summary: This inscription records a public dedication made by decree of the local council to Gaius Pontilius Fregellianus, who held the office of consul and patron.

EDCS-28701375.translation: To Jupiter, from his son Certus [---]
EDCS-28701375.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter from his son Certus, with the rest of the inscription missing.

EDCS-28701376.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Caius Beriu Aurelius Maximus venator immunis cohortis V Mil numini eius cum suis posuit donum dedit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701376.translation: Caius Beriu Aurelius Maximus, hunter and immunis of the Fifth Cohort of the Milians, placed this gift and offered it willingly and deservedly to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator, in honor of his divinity, along with his family.
EDCS-28701376.summary: This is a votive offering placed by Caius Beriu Aurelius Maximus, a hunter and immunis of the Fifth Cohort of the Milians, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator.

EDCS-28701377.text_edition: \n Laribus Augustis familia Publi Ulpi Publi Valenti[ni ------\n
EDCS-28701377.translation: To the August Lares, the family of Publius Ulpius Publi Valentini [---]
EDCS-28701377.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the August Lares by the family of Publius Ulpius Publi Valentini, with the remainder missing.

EDCS-28701379.text_edition: \n Matri Magnae cognationis Caius Turranius Cronius IIIIIIvir Augustalis voto suscepto aedem et aram de sua pecunia fecit et expolivit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-28701379.translation: Gaius Turranius Cronius, a IIIIIIvir Augustalis, built and decorated this temple and altar from his own money, having undertaken the vow to the Great Mother, and dedicated it himself.
EDCS-28701379.summary: This records the construction and dedication of a temple and altar to the Great Mother by Gaius Turranius Cronius, a IIIIIIvir Augustalis, who made the offering voluntarily.

EDCS-28701380.text_edition: \n Prima Nimphis sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701380.translation: Prima Nimphis thus fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28701380.summary: This inscription records Prima Nimphis fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-28701381.summary: This inscription records Mummia Glycinna fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-28701383.text_edition: \n us Sergio sic Sulpicio Galbae Imperatori Caesari\n
EDCS-28701383.translation: [This is for/to] Sulpicius Galba, Emperor Caesar. [---]
EDCS-28701383.summary: This inscription seems to be an dedication or dedication fragment to the Emperor Sulpicius Galba.

EDCS-28701384.text_edition: \n Imperatori Nervae Caesari Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III consuli III\n
EDCS-28701384.translation: To Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, chief priest, in his third year of tribunician power, and consul for the third time.
EDCS-28701384.summary: This inscription records the titles and offices of Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-28701385.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco sic Aurelio Severo Antonino Pio Felici Augusto patri patriae Magno\n
EDCS-28701385.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the fatherland, the Great.
EDCS-28701385.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28701386.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Probo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae proconsuli Aurelius Marcianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Delmatiae sic devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-28701386.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul. Aurelius Marcianus, a most accomplished man, governor of the province of Dalmatia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-28701386.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Probus, likely from the late 3rd century CE, by Aurelius Marcianus, the governor of Dalmatia.

EDCS-28701387.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name Sorianus, a freedman, with some illegible text, possibly from a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-28701389.translation: Symphorus willingly fulfilled his vow as deserved.
EDCS-28701389.summary: This is a votive inscription by Symphorus, likely from the Imperial period, expressing gratitude for a fulfilled vow.

EDCS-28800844.text_edition: \n Flavio Iuliano ex protectore et ex praepositis qui vixit annis LI et Aureliae Sapriciae uxori obseqeuntissimae quae vixit annis XXXV\n
EDCS-28800844.translation: To Flavius Iulianus, formerly a guardsman and formerly a prefect, who lived 51 years, and to Aurelia Sapricia, his most obedient wife, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-28800844.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Flavius Iulianus and his wife Aurelia Sapricia, likely from the 3rd or 4th century CE.

EDCS-28800845.text_edition: \n p Grato beneficiario consularis legionis Bononia a coniugi \n
EDCS-28800845.translation: To Gratus, a subordinate of the consular legate of Legion Bononia, for his wife.
EDCS-28800845.summary: This is a dedication to Gratus, a subordinate official associated with a legionary legate, for his wife, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-28800846.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Dolens veteranus legionis X geminae ex beneficiario consularis et Caelia Bonosa vivi sibi posuerunt\n
EDCS-28800846.translation: Gaius Julius Dolens, a veteran of the 10th Gemina Legion, formerly a subordinate of the consular legate, and Caelia Bonosa, set this up for themselves while alive.
EDCS-28800846.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a veteran, Gaius Julius Dolens, and Caelia Bonosa for themselves, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28800847.text_edition: \n Publi fili-- Malleol? et Placentina? XXI \n
EDCS-28800847.translation: To Publius' son Malleolus? and Placentina? (aged) 21.
EDCS-28800847.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the names and age of one or more individuals, possibly a funerary monument.

EDCS-28800848.text_edition: \n Caius Vatinius Sexti filius Capito veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis domo Arretio vivus fecit sibi et Vatiniae Feliculae libertae\n
EDCS-28800848.translation: Gaius Vatinius Capito, son of Sextus, a veteran of the 7th Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion, from Arretium, made this for himself while alive, and for Vatinia Felicula, his freedwoman.
EDCS-28800848.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Gaius Vatinius Capito, a veteran of the 7th Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion, for himself and his freedwoman Vatinia Felicula.

EDCS-28800849.text_edition: \n filius veteranus legionis domo Ateste testamento fieri ius[sit ------\n
EDCS-28800849.translation: His son, a veteran of the legion, from Ateste, ordered this to be made in his will [---].
EDCS-28800849.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating that the monument was ordered in the will of a veteran from Ateste, with the rest of the text illegible.

EDCS-28800851.text_edition: \n Lucius R CI IIIIvir? --- tribunus mi[litum ------\n
EDCS-28800851.translation: Lucius R., aedile? ... tribune of soldiers...
EDCS-28800851.summary: This inscription appears to record the career of a Roman official named Lucius R., possibly an aedile and a military tribune, with much of the text lost.

EDCS-28800855.text_edition: \n egiae Longinae rius beneficiarius onianus DVL et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-28800855.translation: ...egiae Longinae, onianus, a beneficiary, dedicated this to himself and to DVL.
EDCS-28800855.summary: This is a dedication set up by 'onianus', a beneficiary, for himself and for 'egiae Longinae' and 'DVL', with the full context and date unclear.

EDCS-28800856.text_edition: \n II CHI corniculario consularis marito incomparabili Lucilia na si quis in hoc aliut sic corpu[s ------\n
EDCS-28800856.translation: ... a cornicularius of a consular official, for his incomparable husband Lucilia na. If anyone... this body...
EDCS-28800856.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to an 'incomparable husband' by his wife Lucilia, mentioning a 'cornicularius' (an administrative assistant) and possibly an attempt to preserve the body, with the recipient's name partially obscured.

EDCS-28800857.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus T Nepos miles? cohortis VIII [voluntariorum ------\n
EDCS-28800857.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Nepos, a soldier? of the 8th cohort of volunteers...
EDCS-28800857.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Departed) for Nepos, a soldier of the 8th cohort of volunteers, with further details missing.

EDCS-28800858.text_edition: \n Octaviae Carae dominae et tecusae rarissimae summae sanctimoniae et benignitatis feminae coniugi Saloni Sabiniani veterani ex corniculario consularis legionis I adiutricis signo Scammati e<q=O>(uitis) Romani cum quo concorditer vixit nnos XXX ob merita Ulpius Asclepius confraterfrater |(Christo posuit \n
EDCS-28800858.translation: To Octavia Cara, dearest and most virtuous mistress, a wife of utmost sanctity and kindness, for Salvius Sabinianus, a veteran from the 'cornicularius' of the consular of Legio I Adiutrix, named Scammatus, with whom he lived in harmony for 30 years; Ulpius Asclepius, his fellow brother in Christ, set this up in consideration of his merits.
EDCS-28800858.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Ulpius Asclepius for his wife Octavia Cara and her husband Salvius Sabinianus, a veteran and former administrator, detailing their harmonious marriage of 30 years and mentioning a Christian context.

EDCS-28800859.text_edition: \n Quintiae C stolatae feminae Quinti quondam Germani primipilaris filiae quae ddidit nnum XXX Flavius Valens beneficiarius consularis nniae superioris coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-28800859.translation: For Quintia, a woman of high status, daughter of Quintus Germanus, formerly a primipilaris, who gave 30 years; Flavius Valens, a beneficiary of a consular official, for his most beloved wife Nnia Superior.
EDCS-28800859.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintia, daughter of a former primipilaris, set up by her husband Flavius Valens, a beneficiary of a consular official, and mentions her age or lifespan of 30 years.

EDCS-28800860.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Calpurniae Nymphae Quintus Servilius Statianus |(centurio) cohortis I Belgarum coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-28800860.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Calpurnia Nympha, a well-deserving wife, by Quintus Servilius Statianus, a centurion of the 1st Cohort of Belgae.
EDCS-28800860.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Departed) for Calpurnia Nympha, set up by her husband Quintus Servilius Statianus, a centurion.

EDCS-28800861.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Sornius C miles cohortis VIII voluntariorum Calpurnia Pro mater filio ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-28800861.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Sornius C., a soldier of the 8th cohort of volunteers. Calpurnia Pro, his mother, had this set up for her son.
EDCS-28800861.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Departed) for Lucius Sornius C., a soldier, set up by his mother Calpurnia Pro.

EDCS-28800862.text_edition: \n Torentio signifero qui vixit annos XXV piissimo Catulus spathario suo posuit\n
EDCS-28800862.translation: For Torentius, standard-bearer, who lived 25 years. His most devoted Catulus, his spatharius, set this up.
EDCS-28800862.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Torentius, a standard-bearer who lived 25 years, and was set up by Catulus, who was his 'spatharius' (a type of guard or attendant).

EDCS-28800863.text_edition: \n Lucio Trebonio Valenti militi legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis et Lucio Trebonio Constanti filio et Treboniae Tertullae sorori Lucius Trebonius Successus libertus patronis suis bene merentibus vivus fecit sibi et suis libertis libertabusque in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-28800863.translation: Lucius Trebonius Successus, his freedman, made this for his well-deserving patrons Lucius Trebonius Valens, a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, pious and faithful, and for Lucius Trebonius Constanti, his son, and for Trebonia Tertulla, his sister, during his lifetime, for himself and his freedmen and freedwomen. In front 20 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-28800863.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a freedman, Lucius Trebonius Successus, for his patrons and their family, including a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, specifying the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-28800864.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius filius Azinas Proculus eques cohortis III Alpinorum vexillarius equitum cohortis I Belgarum decurio equitum cohortis eiusdem hunc sic sepulcrum vivos sic paravi mihi et Apuleiae Sabinae uxori meae libertis libertabusque hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28800864.translation: Gaius Valerius Proculus Azinas, son of Valerius, an equestrian of the III Cohort of the Alpinis, standard-bearer of the cavalry of the I Cohort of the Belgae, and decurion of the cavalry of the same cohort, prepared this tomb for himself while alive and for his wife Apuleia Sabina; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-28800864.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Proculus Azinas, a military officer, who prepared his own tomb and that of his wife. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800867.text_edition: \n tius Quinti filius Galeria? veteranus legionis sibi et suis \n
EDCS-28800867.translation: Titus, son of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, a veteran of the legion, for himself and his family.
EDCS-28800867.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus, a legionary veteran, who dedicated the monument for himself and his family. The approximate date is Roman Imperial.

EDCS-28800875.text_edition: \n enus Augustalis vivus fecit --- sibi? etArbusculae coniugi et Caio Iulio \n
EDCS-28800875.translation: Augustalis made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Arbuscula, and for Gaius Julius [---].
EDCS-28800875.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Augustalis, who built the monument for himself, his wife, and possibly another person. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800876.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Germanus Barlaha sacerdos Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni vivus sibi posuit et Syrae coniugi\n
EDCS-28800876.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Germanus Barlaha, priest of Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, placed this for himself while alive and for his wife Syria.
EDCS-28800876.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Germanus Barlaha, a priest of Jupiter Dolichenus, for himself and his wife. It likely dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800877.text_edition: \n Caius Caesius Cai libertus Amaranthus IIIIIIvir Augustalis vivus fecit sibi et Clodiae Publi libertae Blandae uxori et Caesiae Lepidillae defunctae nnum trium et Caio Caesio Cai filio Tromentina Vindici aedili IIIIviro iure dicundo et Corneliae Publi filiae Clementillae uxori\n
EDCS-28800877.translation: Gaius Cassius Amaranthus, freedman of Gaius, an Augustalis Seviri, made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Clodia Blända, freedwoman of Publius, and for Caesius Lepidilla, deceased, aged 33, and for Gaius Cassius Vindicus, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe, aedile, a duumvir with judicial power, and for Cornelia Clementilla, wife of Publius.
EDCS-28800877.summary: This is a large funerary inscription by Gaius Cassius Amaranthus, an Augustalis Seviri, who prepared the monument for his family, including his deceased wife and son, and a son-in-law. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800878.text_edition: \n Decimo Campanio Publi filio Tromentina Varo aedili IIIIviro iure dicundo IIIIviro iure dicundo quinquennali auguri flamini praefecto fabrum publice\n
EDCS-28800878.translation: To Decimus Campanicus, son of Publius, of the Tromentina tribe, aedile, duumvir with judicial power, quinquennial duumvir, augur, flamen, and prefect of the artisans, publicly.
EDCS-28800878.summary: This is a public dedication honouring Decimus Campanicus for his various civic and religious offices. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800879.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Canius Placidus Augustalis vivus fecit sibi et suis posterisque eorum in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XXX\n
EDCS-28800879.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Canius Placidus, an Augustalis, made this while alive for himself and his family and their descendants; in front 20 feet, in the field 30 feet.
EDCS-28800879.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Titus Canius Placidus, an Augustalis, for himself and his descendants, with specified dimensions for the plot. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800880.text_edition: \n Sexto? Fulvio Sexti filio Tromentina Alfiano filio decurioni Sexto? Fulvio Pietati filio Sexto? Fulvio Pietati patri ae Proculae matri iae Syrelae coniugi Sextus? Fulvius Alfianus suis\n
EDCS-28800880.translation: To Sextus Fulvius Alfianus, son of Sextus, of the Tromentina tribe, decurion; to Sextus Fulvius Pietas, son of Sextus; to Sextus Fulvius Pietas, father; to Procula, mother; to Syrela, wife; Sextus Fulvius Alfianus, for his family.
EDCS-28800880.summary: This is a funerary inscription listing multiple family members, with Sextus Fulvius Alfianus dedicating it for his family. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800882.text_edition: \n Iulia Agathe MERIS Nassio Iuliano defuncto VIviro Augustali \n
EDCS-28800882.translation: Iulia Agatha, for Nassius Julianus, an Augustalis Seviri, deceased.
EDCS-28800882.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Iulia Agathe, commemorating Nassius Julianus, an Augustalis Seviri. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800883.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honouring Malleolus for his office as aedile. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800884.text_edition: \n ntius Mer IIIIIIvir Augustalis se vivo fecit sibi et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum in fronte pedes XXXV \n
EDCS-28800884.translation: Gaius Pontius, Augustalis and member of the college of six, had this built in his lifetime for himself and for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, 35 feet in front.
EDCS-28800884.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Gaius Pontius, an Augustalis, dedicating a space for himself and his freedmen, likely for a tomb or columbarium.

EDCS-28800885.text_edition: \n Ti(berius) Iulius Maximus Cai Iuli Thiridatis filius decurio alae Pharthorum annorum XXVI domo Roma hic situs est Sextus Coelius NAVIB\n
EDCS-28800885.translation: Tiberius Iulius Maximus, son of Caius Iulius Thiridates, decurion of the Wing of Parthia, 26 years old, from Rome, lies here. Sextus Coelius [erected this monument].
EDCS-28800885.summary: This is a tombstone for Tiberius Iulius Maximus, a 26-year-old decurion of a cavalry unit, who was from Rome.

EDCS-28800887.text_edition: \n Lucio Veturioo equiti cohortis octavae praetoriae nnorum XVIII Aelia Maximina mater\n
EDCS-28800887.translation: To Lucius Veturios, an equestrian of the eighth Praetorian cohort, 18 years old. His mother Aelia Maximina [dedicated this].
EDCS-28800887.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aelia Maximina to her son, Lucius Veturios, an 18-year-old equestrian serving in the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-28900124.text_edition: \n IO OC NO coloniae aedili IIviro patrono coloniae sacerdoti designato \n
EDCS-28900124.translation: [Dedicated to] ... , aedile, duovir, patron of the colony, and designated priest of the colony.
EDCS-28900124.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication to a local magistrate who held several important civic and religious offices in the colony.

EDCS-28900125.text_edition: \n Orbia Sexti filia Paulla titulum fieri iussit sibi et Sexto Orbio Paullo IIIIIIviro patri Cordiae Afae matri Sexto Orbio Praeconino pontifici fratri Publio Grattio Campano filio\n
EDCS-28900125.translation: Orbia Paula, daughter of Sextus, ordered this monument to be made for herself, for her father Sextus Orbius Paulllus, Augustalis, for her mother Cordia Afa, and for her brother Publius Grattius Praeconinus, a pontifex.
EDCS-28900125.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Orbia Paula for herself and her family members, including her father who was an Augustalis and her brother who was a pontifex.

EDCS-28900126.text_edition: \n isidio? ---icto quaestori defuncto annorum Papinia Filumena uxor cum filiis suis marito pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28900126.translation: To ... idius, quaestor, deceased, aged [---]. His wife Papinia Filumena, with her sons, erected this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-28900126.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a wife and sons for their deceased husband and father, who held the office of quaestor.

EDCS-28900127.text_edition: \n ? Petronio Auli filio Tromentina Postumo aedili cunda mater fecit\n
EDCS-28900127.translation: [Dedicated to] ... Petronius Postumus, son of Aulus, aedile of the Tromentina tribe. His mother ... cunda made this.
EDCS-28900127.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a mother to her son, Petronius Postumus, who held the office of aedile.

EDCS-28900128.text_edition: \n ------ Qui]ntilium Mescenium menses III dies XV decessit Salonis astrosum Marcus Quintilius us pater infelix H\n
EDCS-28900128.translation: [Marcus Quintilius] Quintilius Mesecenus died after 3 months and 15 days. His unhappy father Marcus Quintilius [erected this].
EDCS-28900128.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a father for his son, Marcus Quintilius Mesecenus, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-28900130.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that likely refers to a deceased aedile, son of someone named Rufus.

EDCS-28900131.text_edition: \n Caius Saufeius Moderatus Augustalis testamento fieri iussit sibi et Luciliae Rhodiae libertis libertabusque suis Caius Saufeius Thalamus et Caius Saufeius Moderatianus heredes faciundum curaverunt in fronte pedes XXX in agro pedes LXV\n
EDCS-28900131.translation: Gaius Saufeius Moderatus, an Augustalis, ordered this to be made in his will for himself and for Lucilia Rhodia, and for their freedmen and freedwomen. His heirs, Gaius Saufeius Thalamus and Gaius Saufeius Moderatianus, saw to its construction, 30 feet in front and 65 feet in the field.
EDCS-28900131.summary: This is a funerary inscription established by Gaius Saufeius Moderatus through his will, for himself, his freedwoman Lucilia Rhodia, and their freedpersons, with specific dimensions for the plot.

EDCS-28900132.text_edition: \n ------ cum co]mpare suo annis XXX - Silicius Tertius IIIIIIvir Augustalis coloniae Salonitanorum\n
EDCS-28900132.translation: With his partner for 30 years - Silicius Tertius, one of the six duoviri Augustales of the colony of Salona.
EDCS-28900132.summary: This inscription likely records the funerary honors or career of Silicius Tertius, a magistrate in the colony of Salona, who served alongside a partner for 30 years.

EDCS-28900134.text_edition: \n Caius Vibius Leo IIIIIIvir vivus fecit sibi et Caeliae Tertiae coniugi et Antestiae Messiae libertis libertabusque suis hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-28900134.translation: Caius Vibius Leo, one of the six duoviri, made this monument for himself and his wife Caelia Tertia and his freedmen and freedwomen, while alive; it shall not bind his heir.
EDCS-28900134.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Caius Vibius Leo, a magistrate, for himself and his family, explicitly stating it is not inherited.

EDCS-28900137.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription recording the title 'priest' (sacerdos) and potentially the name of a deity.

EDCS-28900139.text_edition: \n fabrum et centonariorum ae quae in aere scripta est \n
EDCS-28900139.translation: ...the craftsman and of the fullers, which is written on bronze.
EDCS-28900139.summary: This fragment refers to a record of craftsmen and fullers, possibly detailing regulations or membership lists, inscribed on bronze.

EDCS-28900140.text_edition: \n Proc ---? IIIvir iure dicundo IIII[vir quinquennalis ------\n
EDCS-28900140.summary: This fragmentary inscription lists Roman magistracies, including a IIIvir (a three-man board, possibly judicial) and a IIIIvir quinquennalis (a four-man board with quinquennial powers, likely responsible for census and public works).

EDCS-28900144.text_edition: \n F Q F ia Publi filia F--- A P Aedem et signa de sua pecunia fecerunt voto suscepto\n
EDCS-28900144.translation: F. Q. F., daughter of Publius F. Q. F., [with her husband?] A. P., made the temple and statues with their own money, having undertaken a vow.
EDCS-28900144.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple and statues funded by a woman and possibly her husband, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-28900145.text_edition: \n Aebu L erius ACCEP magister collegii fabrum D PIN castra SLCANI et S P V AFESIEI\n
EDCS-28900145.translation: Aebus L. [...], the master of the guild of craftsmen, [donated?] the buildings of the camp and...
EDCS-28900145.summary: This inscription records an official, Aebus L., as the master of a guild of craftsmen, making a donation related to a camp and other structures.

EDCS-28900146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Soterae obstetrici defunctae annorum XXXV Aelius Antonianus Themistocles libertae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28900146.translation: To the divine spirits. For Aelia Sotera, a midwife, deceased at 35 years of age. Aelius Antonianus Themistocles erected this for his well-deserving freedwoman.
EDCS-28900146.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Aelia Sotera, a midwife, set up by her former owner in recognition of her service.

EDCS-28900147.text_edition: \n ------ Ag]athopus rcae Primigeni annorum duorum S Ravennae\n
EDCS-28900147.translation: ...Agathopus, son of Primigenius, aged two years... in Ravenna.
EDCS-28900147.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a young child named Agathopus, indicating the place of death or burial.

EDCS-28900151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crinito secutori natione Afer annorum XX pugnarum I<I=L> @(obito) Afidia posuit de sua sibi frugalitate bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-28900151.translation: To the divine spirits. For Crinitus, a hunter by nationality from Africa, aged 21 years. Afidia erected this for him, remembering his deservingness with her frugality.
EDCS-28900151.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Crinitus, an African hunter, set up by Afidia, who recognized his merit and erected the monument with her own limited means.

EDCS-28900152.summary: This is a dedication to Epictesis Fortunata, an Augustalis, likely inscribed on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-28900155.text_edition: \n Maximiano <q=C>ui et Aureo secutori nnorum XXII pugnarum V deceptus a latron<i=E>b<u=O>s frater fratri carissimo Gemina coniux desideratissimo \n
EDCS-28900155.translation: To Maximianus Aureus, a wrestler of 22 years and 5 fights, deceived by brigands; his brother sets this up for his dearest brother; Gemina, his wife, for her most desired.
EDCS-28900155.summary: This epitaph records the death of Maximianus Aureus, a wrestler who died at 22, and was likely erected by his brother and wife.

EDCS-28900156.translation: Misceius Ampliatus makes this in Salona.
EDCS-28900156.summary: This inscription states that Misceius Ampliatus commissioned or built something in Salona.

EDCS-28900157.text_edition: \n Onesimo Marci Iuli Macrini dispensatori Successus vicarius\n
EDCS-28900157.translation: Successus, the deputy, for Onesimus, freedman of Marcus Julius Macrinus, the steward.
EDCS-28900157.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Successus, a deputy, to Onesimus, a freedman and steward of Marcus Julius Macrinus.

EDCS-28900158.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Placidiae Damale quae et Rufinae matri vernaculorum optimae et incomparabili feminae uxori fidelissimae et piissimae vixit annos XXXVIII menses V dies X Marcus Plautius Severus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28900158.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Placidia Damale, who was also Rufina, mother of household slaves, most excellent and incomparable woman, most faithful and most devoted wife, who lived 38 years, 5 months, 10 days. Marcus Plautius Severus set this up for her who well deserved it.
EDCS-28900158.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Placidia Damale (Rufina), erected by her husband Marcus Plautius Severus, highlighting her virtues as a mother and wife.

EDCS-28900159.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Secundo servo bono defuncto nnorum XX bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28900159.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Secundus, the good slave, who died at 20 years. Erected by the one who well deserved it.
EDCS-28900159.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a slave named Secundus, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-28900161.text_edition: \n Sossius hic situs est Successus iure peritus Ravvennae genitus Salonis funere raptus libera que<m=N> ratio semper deduxit ad aequum \n
EDCS-28900161.translation: Sossius is buried here. Successus, skilled in law, born in Ravenna, snatched away in funeral rites from Salona, whom reason always led to what is right.
EDCS-28900161.summary: This epitaph commemorates Sossius, buried in Salona, who was skilled in law and known for his just character.

EDCS-29000267.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Terentio Mercuriali annorum XXXI vexxillario collegii fabrum Terentius Mercurius pater et patronus filio infelicissimo dolens posuit\n
EDCS-29000267.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Titus Terentius Mercurialis, 31 years old, standard-bearer of the guild of smiths. Terentius Mercurius, father and patron, set this up for his most unhappy son with grief.
EDCS-29000267.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Titus Terentius Mercurialis, a standard-bearer for a smiths' guild, erected by his grieving father.

EDCS-29000268.text_edition: \n ------ ---]rio natione qui vixit annos plus minus XXVI S intravi simul et exxivi eptus OCS\n
EDCS-29000268.translation: [...]rio by nationality, who lived more or less 26 years. I entered and I exited OCS.
EDCS-29000268.summary: This fragmentary epitaph records the age of an individual and mentions their entry and exit, possibly related to a military or official context.

EDCS-29000269.text_edition: \n Quinto Turranio Mercurio liberto Turrani Successi toreuticensis ab urbe qui vixit nnos filio bene merenti \n
EDCS-29000269.translation: For Quintus Turranius Mercurius, freedman of Turranius Successus, a worker in toreutic art from the city, who lived [age missing] years. Erected for the deserving son.
EDCS-29000269.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Quintus Turranius Mercurius, a freedman and toreutic artisan, likely erected by his patron or a relative.

EDCS-29000282.text_edition: \n matri annorum Acubio Maximo annorum XVIII et Acubio Valentino fratri Acubius Sabidianus suis posuit\n
EDCS-29000282.translation: Acubius Sabidianus dedicated this to his mother, bearing the name Acubius Maximus, aged eighteen, and to his brother, Acubius Valens.
EDCS-29000282.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Acubius Sabidianus to his mother and brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000283.text_edition: \n Caio Aebutio Secundino defuncto nnorum V mensium III Caius Aebutius Crescens et Octavia Secundina parentes infelicissimi\n
EDCS-29000283.translation: For Gaius Aebutius Secundinus, who died at the age of five years and three months. Gaius Aebutius Crescens and Octavia Secundina, most unhappy parents.
EDCS-29000283.summary: This is a tombstone for a young child, Gaius Aebutius Secundinus, set up by his grieving parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000284.translation: [The inscription likely names] Aetia, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-29000284.summary: This fragment, possibly from a tombstone or a dedication, names Aetia, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-29000285.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that mentions Aelius, likely a personal name, and appears to be part of a larger text.

EDCS-29000286.text_edition: \n Aeliae Proculae defunctae annorum XXX posuit Alasinius F<e=I>rox coniugi incomparabili \n
EDCS-29000286.translation: Alasinius Ferox dedicated this to his incomparable wife, Aelia Procula, who died at the age of thirty.
EDCS-29000286.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Alasinius Ferox to his wife, Aelia Procula, who died at age thirty.

EDCS-29000287.text_edition: \n Aeliae I L defunctae annorum Anicetus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-29000287.translation: Anicetus dedicated this to his deserving wife, Aelia I. L., who died at [an unspecified] age.
EDCS-29000287.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Anicetus to his wife, Aelia I. L., described as deserving.

EDCS-29000288.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Primo us tia\n
EDCS-29000288.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Publius Aelius Primus...
EDCS-29000288.summary: This inscription begins with a standard invocation to the underworld deities (Dis Manibus) for Publius Aelius Primus, likely a funerary dedication.

EDCS-29000291.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia ARTO nnorum V Iuli-- \n
EDCS-29000291.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Aelia Arto, aged five years, [name of dedicator] Iuli...
EDCS-29000291.summary: This funerary inscription invokes the underworld spirits for Aelia Arto, who died at age five, and names a dedicator starting with 'Iuli'.

EDCS-29000292.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29000292.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Alexandriae infelicissimae virgini quae vixit nnos plus minus XV parentes infelicissimi infelicissimae filiae memoriam\n
EDCS-29000292.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Aelia Alexandria, a most unhappy virgin who lived about fifteen years. Her most unhappy parents [set this up] in memory of their most unhappy daughter.
EDCS-29000292.summary: This funerary monument was set up by the grieving parents of Aelia Alexandria, a virgin who died around age fifteen.

EDCS-29000293.text_edition: \n mno annorum I mensium II dierum Aelia Fortunata filio pientissimo IFEIAO \n
EDCS-29000293.translation: ...aged one year, two months. Aelia Fortunata [dedicated this] to her most devoted son.
EDCS-29000293.summary: This funerary dedication, likely from Aelia Fortunata, commemorates her son who died at the age of one year and two months.

EDCS-29000294.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Aeronia Primigenia Atticus, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29000295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agathini defuncti annorum XVI mater posuit\n
EDCS-29000295.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. His mother placed [this] for Agathinus, deceased at sixteen years of age.
EDCS-29000295.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a sixteen-year-old named Agathinus, set up by his mother.

EDCS-29000296.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agria Primitiva fratri et filio Iu\n
EDCS-29000296.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Agria Primitiva [placed this] for her brother and son Iu[lius?]...
EDCS-29000296.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Agria Primitiva for her brother and son, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-29000297.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Aelia Ni[a] AF [restored]
EDCS-29000297.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Aelia Ni[a].

EDCS-29000298.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning a freedman named Albus Caius.

EDCS-29000302.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription where Amullina set up a monument for Servilius Primitivus.

EDCS-29000306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Filocalo patrono et Antoniae Glycerae sorori Antonia Fingis bene merenti\n
EDCS-29000306.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Antonia Fingis [placed this] for her patron Antonio Filocalus and for Antonia Glycera, her sister, who deserved well.
EDCS-29000306.summary: This funerary inscription by Antonia Fingis honors her patron Antonio Filocalus and her sister Antonia Glycera.

EDCS-29000307.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae Hedone maritus uxsori sic infelicissimae defunctae annorum XVIIII\n
EDCS-29000307.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Her husband [placed this] for Antonia Hedone, his wife, so unhappy, deceased at nineteen years of age.
EDCS-29000307.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Antonia Hedone at nineteen, set up by her grieving husband.

EDCS-29000308.text_edition: \n Antoniae Sabinae <quae=CVI> et MegNHmae defunctae annorum XVIIII Antonius Sabinianus et Octavia Eyagria parentes filiae dulcissimae posuerunt et sibi \n
EDCS-29000308.translation: Antonius Sabinianus and Octavia Eyagria, parents, placed [this] for their most sweet daughter Sabina, who [died as if she were] MegNma, at seventeen years of age, and for themselves.
EDCS-29000308.summary: This funerary inscription by parents Antonius Sabinianus and Octavia Eyagria honors their daughter Sabina and includes themselves.

EDCS-29000309.text_edition: \n ------] MAP me A libert-- V sibi et mar[ito ------\n
EDCS-29000309.translation: [restored] MAP for herself and her husband [restored]...
EDCS-29000309.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating a dedication by an individual for herself and her husband.

EDCS-29000310.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollonius, son of Aulus.

EDCS-29000312.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record a dedication to Apro.

EDCS-29000314.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Apuleiae Iucundae Lucius Petilius Luci libertus Privatus sorori bene merenti\n
EDCS-29000314.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Petilius Privatus, freedman of Marcus, to his deserving sister Apuleia Iucunda.
EDCS-29000314.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Apuleia Iucunda, dedicated by her brother Lucius Petilius Privatus.

EDCS-29000315.text_edition: \n IIIRO Aquiliae Marci libertae Chresti concubinae suae\n
EDCS-29000315.translation: To Chrestus, freedman of Marcus, for his concubine Aquilia...
EDCS-29000315.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record a dedication by a man to his concubine, Aquilia.

EDCS-29000318.translation: To Arce T, with his wife...
EDCS-29000318.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to mention a person named Arce T and his wife.

EDCS-29000319.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Arethusa Zosimae.
EDCS-29000319.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Arethusa Zosimae.

EDCS-29100352.text_edition: \n Arriae Venaeriae sic matri dulcissimae Arrius Venerianus filius\n
EDCS-29100352.translation: To Arria Venaeria, his dearest mother, from her son Arrius Venerianus.
EDCS-29100352.summary: This epitaph records Arrius Venerianus dedicating a monument to his beloved mother, Arria Venaeria.

EDCS-29100354.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Artemisiae Restutus matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-29100354.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Restutus to his deserving mother Artemisia.
EDCS-29100354.summary: This funerary epitaph records Restutus dedicating a monument to his deserving mother, Artemisia.

EDCS-29100355.text_edition: \n Aselia Lucida Lucio Aselio Halemo et Steniae Tychini parentibus posuit\n
EDCS-29100355.translation: Aselia Lucida dedicated this to her parents, Lucius Aselius Halemus and Stenia Tychine.
EDCS-29100355.summary: This funerary epitaph records Aselia Lucida dedicating a monument to her parents, Lucius Aselius Halemus and Stenia Tychine.

EDCS-29100358.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Asinio Vitali Lollia Privata coniugi pientissimo\n
EDCS-29100358.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lollia Privata to her most devoted husband, Gaius Asinius Vitalis.
EDCS-29100358.summary: This funerary epitaph records Lollia Privata dedicating a monument to her devoted husband, Gaius Asinius Vitalis.

EDCS-29100359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Astrio Secundino qui et Salto TO\n
EDCS-29100359.translation: To the Divine Manes of Gaius Astrius Secundinus, also called Salto.
EDCS-29100359.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Astrius Secundinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100361.text_edition: \n ticus Atiliae uxori suae bene merenti et sibi \n
EDCS-29100361.translation: To Atilia, his wife who deserved well, and to himself.
EDCS-29100361.summary: This funerary inscription honors Atilia and the dedicator, possibly a husband setting up a tomb for himself and his deserving wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100363.text_edition: \n ------ A]tilia Hospita E Lereia Ceniavia sibi et viro Luci filius Verecundus et fratri <F=E>irmo obito et Vitali filiae suae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur \n
EDCS-29100363.translation: Atilia Hospita, E Lereia Ceniavia, for herself, and for her husband Lucius Verecundus, son of Lucius, and for her brother (Firminus?) who died, and for her daughter Vitalis. This monument will not pass down to an heir.
EDCS-29100363.summary: This funerary epitaph records Atilia Hospita, E Lereia Ceniavia, and family members, establishing a monument that is not inheritable, suggesting a private tomb construction likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100364.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atiliae Quartae Atilius Basilides filius matri pientissimae\n
EDCS-29100364.translation: To the Divine Manes of Atilia Quarta. Atilius Basilides, her son, placed this for his most devoted mother.
EDCS-29100364.summary: This funerary epitaph records Atilius Basilides dedicating a monument to his devoted mother, Atilia Quarta, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100365.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexto Attio Tertullo defuncto nnorum III mensium II dierum XIII parentes filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-29100365.translation: To the Divine Manes of Sextus Attius Tertullus, deceased, aged 3 years, 2 months, and 13 days. His parents placed this for their sweetest son.
EDCS-29100365.summary: This funerary epitaph records the premature death of Sextus Attius Tertullus, aged 3 years, 2 months, and 13 days, with his parents dedicating the monument, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100366.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph dedicating a monument to Ienia Augurina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100371.text_edition: \n naufragio exisse annum vertentem vixisse post annum man<u=O> humana sublatum esse Aurelius Aeladi pater filio pientissimo posuit \n
EDCS-29100371.translation: Having perished in a shipwreck, having lived for a year, and having been taken away by human strength, Aurelius Aeladi placed this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-29100371.summary: This funerary epitaph records Aurelius Aeladi dedicating a monument to his son who died tragically after surviving a shipwreck and living for a year, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29100372.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Epicteto defuncto filia patri pientissimo bene merenti posuit\n
