EDCS-29500212.translation: Popilius Philus to his wife, aged 24.
EDCS-29500212.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a wife who died at the age of 24, dedicated by Popilius Philus.

EDCS-29500213.text_edition: \n M Pothinae vixit nnos XVII - Collius Pothinus filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-29500213.translation: Collius Pothinus to his most devoted daughter Pothina, who lived for 17 years.
EDCS-29500213.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Collius Pothinus dedicating a monument to his devoted daughter Pothina, who died at age 17.

EDCS-29500215.text_edition: \n ------ Aure]liae Primulae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29500215.translation: ...to his deserving wife Aurelia Primula, erected this.
EDCS-29500215.summary: This is a funerary inscription by an unnamed man dedicating a monument to his deserving wife, Aurelia Primula.

EDCS-29500216.translation: Titus Priminius Potus lived for ... husband.
EDCS-29500216.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Priminius Potus, likely set up by his husband.

EDCS-29500217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primitio et Primitae Primus et Callistus amicis bene merentibus\n
EDCS-29500217.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Primus and Callistus for their dear and well-deserving friends Primitio and Primita.
EDCS-29500217.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by Primus and Callistus to their friends Primitio and Primita, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500218.translation: For Primitivos, who deserved so well.
EDCS-29500218.summary: A funerary inscription, likely a tombstone or epitaph, dedicated to a person named Primitivos.

EDCS-29500219.translation: To Primitiva, from Primitiva, her daughter -- Valeri -- her foster-child.
EDCS-29500219.summary: A funerary inscription for Primitiva, dedicated by her daughter and foster-child.

EDCS-29500220.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to someone named Prisca, identified as a son.

EDCS-29500221.text_edition: \n Proba filiae infelicissimae parentes posuerunt defunctae nnrum XX \n
EDCS-29500221.translation: Parents set this up for their most unfortunate daughter, dead at 20 years.
EDCS-29500221.summary: A funerary epitaph for a daughter who died at the age of 20, set up by her parents.

EDCS-29500222.text_edition: \n Proculae filiae et Caio Caesio Donato et Autonoe vernulae \n
EDCS-29500222.translation: For Procula, [their] daughter, and for Gaius Caesius Donatus and Autonoe, their slaves.
EDCS-29500222.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating a daughter named Procula, and two slaves, Gaius Caesius Donatus and Autonoe.

EDCS-29500224.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Publicio Asclepio coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500224.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Titus Publicius Asclepius, a well-deserving husband.
EDCS-29500224.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Titus Publicius Asclepius, identified as a deserving husband.

EDCS-29500225.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Publicius Trophimus heres institutus ab Valeria Philete uxore mea in memoriam karae coniugis et mi posui stilibatam causa arae viatoribus bene concordes vivite vivendo fit minus una dies commemora tecum nil stomachandi haec nobis domus perpetua\n
EDCS-29500225.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Publicius Trophimus, appointed heir by his wife Valeria Philete, set this up in memory of his dear wife and himself. Live harmoniously while living, for living makes one less alone. Remember that this house is eternal for us.
EDCS-29500225.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Lucius Publicius Trophimus, appointed heir by his wife Valeria Philete, commemorating both himself and his wife, with a reflective message on life and eternity.

EDCS-29500228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ianiae Quarta matri - bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500228.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Iania Quarta, a deserving mother.
EDCS-29500228.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Iania Quarta, honoring her as a deserving mother.

EDCS-29500230.text_edition: \n Quintae coniugi pientissimae Verna maritus posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500230.translation: For Quinta, a most devoted wife. Verna the husband set this up for a well-deserving [wife].
EDCS-29500230.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Verna for his devoted wife Quinta, honoring her as well-deserving.

EDCS-29500234.text_edition: \n lius Publi filius Restutus vivus sibi fecit\n
EDCS-29500234.translation: Restutus, son of Publius, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-29500234.summary: A funerary dedication by Restutus for himself, likely a tombstone, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500235.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Romula mater Amantillae filiae infelic[issimae ------\n
EDCS-29500235.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Romula, mother, for her most unhappy daughter Amantilla [---]
EDCS-29500235.summary: A funerary dedication by Romula for her daughter Amantilla, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500237.text_edition: \n Quisquis es atventum sic nostrum contempla sepulcrum siste precor paucis perlege fata mea me mater genuit gremio nutritque laborem spemque et opem vitae prodideratque suae hic iaceo infelix cinis et combusta favilla hic sunt discordi stamina ducta colo desine iam de me dulcis matercula flere et sine nos uno deinde iacere loco c Rufus\n
EDCS-29500237.translation: Whoever you are, as you approach our tomb, please stop and read my fate for a few moments. My mother bore me, nurtured me in her womb, and brought forth the hope and sustenance of her life. Here I lie, unhappy ashes and burnt cinders; here the threads of discord were spun for me by fate. Cease now, dear mother, to weep for me, and let us lie together in one place. Rufus.
EDCS-29500237.summary: A funerary epitaph by Rufus, lamenting his unhappy fate and asking his mother to stop grieving, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500238.text_edition: \n Finis loci qui est Quinti S C in fronte secus flumen pedes XXXXV item secus monimentum sic in agro pedes XXXXII item a capite sinistro longum pedes XLVII semis item ab aquilone in agro pedes XVI at quem locum iter ambitum actum aditum sacrificiique causa quibus esse oportet oportebit D AP HOR\n
EDCS-29500238.translation: Boundary of the land which belongs to Quintus, son of the Senate. In front, along the river, 45 feet; likewise along the monument, in the field, 42 feet; likewise from the left head, long 47.5 feet; likewise from the north, in the field, 16 feet. To which place access, passage, and entry, and for the purpose of sacrifice, as there should be and will be. D. AP HOR.
EDCS-29500238.summary: A boundary marker defining land owned by Quintus, son of the Senate, including dimensions and rights of passage, dated to the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-29500244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sa<t=I>ria Flora Onesimeni NVMM DE \n
EDCS-29500244.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Satria Flora, daughter of Onesimenus. NUMM DE
EDCS-29500244.summary: A funerary dedication to the Spirits of the Underworld for Satria Flora, daughter of Onesimenus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500247.text_edition: \n libert et Publio Saufeio soror libert\n
EDCS-29500247.translation: freedwoman, and Publius Saufeius's sister, freedwoman.
EDCS-29500247.summary: A fragmented inscription identifying two individuals, likely freedwomen, one related to Publius Saufeius, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500250.translation: Secunda to her son Ursinus. M
EDCS-29500250.summary: A dedication by Secunda to her son Ursinus, likely a funerary monument, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500251.text_edition: \n Secundino filio suo defuncto annorum XVIII mater posuit\n
EDCS-29500251.translation: Secundina placed [this] for her deceased son, 18 years old.
EDCS-29500251.summary: A funerary monument set up by Secundina for her son who died at the age of 18, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500253.text_edition: \n io patri pientissimo L Secundina posuit\n
EDCS-29500253.translation: L. Secundina placed [this] for her most dutiful father.
EDCS-29500253.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by L. Secundina to her father, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500255.translation: Seia Marcellina for her most beloved mother.
EDCS-29500255.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Seia Marcellina to her mother, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29500257.text_edition: \n Lucius Septimius Obultronianus vivus monumentum aedificavit Lucio Septimio Obultroniano filio defuncto annorum XVII\n
EDCS-29500257.translation: Lucius Septimius Obultronianus, while alive, erected this monument for his son Lucius Septimius Obultronianus, deceased at seventeen years of age.
EDCS-29500257.summary: A funerary monument erected by a father for his deceased son, identified by name and age.

EDCS-29500258.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely recording a personal name, possibly funerary.

EDCS-29500259.translation: To the Divine Shades of Septimia Secunda Valeri.
EDCS-29500259.summary: A funerary dedication to the deceased Septimia Secunda Valeri.

EDCS-29500260.text_edition: \n Septimiae Ursae defunctae annorum VII liae F Septimi-- defunct- annorum \n
EDCS-29500260.translation: For Septimia Ursa, deceased at seven years of age; [---]lia F. Septimi[us ---], deceased at [---] years.
EDCS-29500260.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for a young girl and possibly another deceased individual.

EDCS-29500263.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Servilio defuncto nnorum XII Victorina alumno VIC posuit\n
EDCS-29500263.translation: To the Divine Shades of Servilius, deceased at twelve years of age; Victorina, his pupil, placed this.
EDCS-29500263.summary: A funerary monument erected by a pupil for her deceased teacher, Servilius.

EDCS-29500264.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sestius Clarus et Vettia Secundina parentes filio infelicissimo vixit annos VI\n
EDCS-29500264.translation: To the Divine Shades of Sestius Clarus and Vettia Secundina, parents, for their most unhappy son, who lived six years.
EDCS-29500264.summary: A funerary monument erected by parents for their young son.

EDCS-29500265.translation: [---] Severa Turrania Valentina, for her parents.
EDCS-29500265.summary: A funerary dedication by Severa Turrania Valentina for her parents.

EDCS-29500266.text_edition: \n Severin-- et Aemilius Hilarus posuerunt bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500266.translation: Severinus and Aemilius Hilarus placed this for one well-deserving.
EDCS-29500266.summary: A dedication by Severinus and Aemilius Hilarus for an unnamed, deserving individual.

EDCS-29500268.translation: [---] of Silvanus, deceased in the year [---]
EDCS-29500268.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription recording the death and age of Silvanus.

EDCS-29500269.text_edition: \n Sossiae Taminiae defunctae nnorum XXXV Caius Messius Alypus coniugi carissimae quam in matrimonio habuit nnos XVIIII\n
EDCS-29500269.translation: For Sossia Tamina, deceased at thirty-five years of age; Caius Messius Alypus for his dearest wife, whom he had in marriage for nineteen years.
EDCS-29500269.summary: A funerary monument erected by a husband for his deceased wife, mentioning her age and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-29500271.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a personal name, possibly Publius Suc... [---].

EDCS-29500272.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to contain part of a name, possibly Successus, with preceding text.

EDCS-29500277.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Titius Charelao sic Aurelia Pudens OIC \n
EDCS-29500277.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelia Pudens placed this for Quintus Titius Charelaus.
EDCS-29500277.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelia Pudens for Quintus Titius Charelaus.

EDCS-29500278.text_edition: \n Marco Terentio Marci filio Saturnino Terenti\n
EDCS-29500278.translation: To Marcus Terentius Saturninus, son of Marcus Terentius...
EDCS-29500278.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning to record the name and filiation of Marcus Terentius Saturninus.

EDCS-29500279.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that begins with a possible name, Tertia, and mentions a son.

EDCS-29500280.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tertia soror Vindemiae sorori suae infelicissimae defunctae annorum pientissimae fecit\n
EDCS-29500280.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Tertia, his most pious sister, made this for Vindemia, her most unhappy deceased sister, who died at years of age.
EDCS-29500280.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Tertia for her deceased sister Vindemia.

EDCS-29500281.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name Tertulla.

EDCS-29500282.text_edition: \n Theodoto Theodoti filio Theodote Galene patrono erga se bene merito et Theodoto et Lupulo filiis et sibi\n
EDCS-29500282.translation: To Theodotos, son of Theodotos, Galene, the patron, for having deserved well of him, and to Theodotos and Lupus, his sons, and for himself.
EDCS-29500282.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a patron named Galene, and also includes other family members: Theodotos, his sons Theodotos and Lupus, and himself.

EDCS-29500284.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titiseno Carico defuncto nnorum XL Hilaritas conservo \n
EDCS-29500284.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Titisemus Caricus, who died at 40 years of age. Hilaritas, his fellow slave...
EDCS-29500284.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Hilaritas, a fellow slave, for Titisemus Caricus, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-29500285.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Titio annorum LXI \n
EDCS-29500285.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Lucius Titius, who lived 61 years.
EDCS-29500285.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Titius, who died at the age of 61.

EDCS-29500286.text_edition: \n Lucio Titio Iucundo M Titia <G=C>lyicenna patrono suo fecit \n
EDCS-29500286.translation: M. Titia Clycennna dedicated this to her patron, Lucius Titius Iucundus.
EDCS-29500286.summary: A funerary dedication by M. Titia Clycennna to her patron Lucius Titius Iucundus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500287.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Titii Sedati Marronia Firma amico bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500287.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Marronia Firma dedicated this to her friend Lucius Titius Sedatus, who deserved well.
EDCS-29500287.summary: A funerary dedication by Marronia Firma to her well-deserving friend Lucius Titius Sedatus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500288.text_edition: \n I IL Titia Acte compari dulcissimo posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500288.translation: Titia Acte placed this for her dearest companion, who deserved well.
EDCS-29500288.summary: A funerary dedication by Titia Acte for her beloved companion, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500289.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Titia Secunda.
EDCS-29500289.summary: A funerary dedication by Titia Secunda to the spirits of the departed, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500293.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Tyche.
EDCS-29500293.summary: A funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed for Tyche, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500294.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valenti defuncto annorum IIII mensium III Barbius pater posuit\n
EDCS-29500294.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Barbius the father dedicated this for Valens, deceased at the age of four years and three months.
EDCS-29500294.summary: A funerary dedication by Barbius for his son Valens, who died at the age of four years and three months, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500295.summary: A funerary dedication for Valentia, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500297.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning a person named Valens, date uncertain.

EDCS-29500299.text_edition: \n Manibus Dis Valerio Frontoni veterano defuncto annorum LXXX Claudia Nigrina coniugi carissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29500299.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Claudia Nigrina placed this for her most beloved husband, Valerius Frontonus, a veteran, deceased at the age of eighty years, who deserved well.
EDCS-29500299.summary: A funerary dedication by Claudia Nigrina for her deceased husband, the veteran Valerius Frontonus, who died at eighty, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29500301.text_edition: \n Valeriae defunctae nnorum IIII dierum XLV Marcus Aurelius Seneca delicatae\n
EDCS-29500301.translation: Marcus Aurelius Seneca dedicated this for his darling Valeria, deceased at the age of four years and forty-five days.
EDCS-29500301.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Aurelius Seneca for his young daughter Valeria, who died at the age of four years and forty-five days, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500303.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus incomparabili feminae Valeriae Feliculae coniugi sanctissimae quae vixit annos AI \n
EDCS-29500303.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Valeria Felicula, my incomparable and most virtuous wife, who lived [AI] years.
EDCS-29500303.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Valeria Felicula, likely set up by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500304.text_edition: \n Valeria Trofime Flavio Victori marito pientissimo et sibi\n
EDCS-29500304.translation: Valeria Trofime [erected this] for Flavius Victor, her most devoted husband, and for herself.
EDCS-29500304.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Valeria Trofime for her husband Flavius Victor and herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500305.text_edition: \n AMII V -- defunct- annorum XXIII Valeria Valerilla fili- \n
EDCS-29500305.translation: [Erected by] Valeria Valerilla for her [---] son, who died at the age of twenty-three years.
EDCS-29500305.summary: A funerary epitaph for a son of Valeria Valerilla, who died at age 23, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Varius Euhodus CE \n
EDCS-29500306.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Lucius Varius Euhodus, decurion.
EDCS-29500306.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Varius Euhodus, a decurion, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500307.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Variae libertae lius posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500307.translation: To the spirits of the departed, [erected by]lius for Varia, his freedwoman, who deserved well.
EDCS-29500307.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by an unnamed man (lius) to his freedwoman Varia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500308.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Varia Secunda.
EDCS-29500308.summary: A funerary epitaph for Varia Secunda, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500309.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Varroni nnorum X-VIIII mensium VI Carpia Apra mater filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-29500309.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Varro, [aged] seventeen years and six months, [erected by] Carpia Apra, his most unfortunate mother, for her son.
EDCS-29500309.summary: A funerary epitaph for Varro, aged 17 years and 6 months, set up by his mother Carpia Apra, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500313.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vedio Secundino Aulus Vedius A Ca<e=I>cilia Fortunata parentes nnorum III mensium IIII infelicissimi \n
EDCS-29500313.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Vedius Secundinus, [erected by] Aulus Vedius and Caecilia Fortunata, his parents, [for their] most unhappy son, aged three years and four months.
EDCS-29500313.summary: A funerary epitaph for Vedius Secundinus, set up by his parents Aulus Vedius and Caecilia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500314.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Veiedius Iuvenalis vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Stochadi coniugi et Iuliae Theonoe sorori eius et Cnaeo Veiedio Cnaei filio Maximo nnorum XIIX\n
EDCS-29500314.translation: Gnaeus Veiedius Iuvenalis, while alive, made this for himself, and for his wife Iulia Stochadis, and for his sister Iulia Theonoe, and for Gnaeus Veiedius Maximus, son of Gnaeus, aged eighteen years.
EDCS-29500314.summary: A funerary monument established by Gnaeus Veiedius Iuvenalis for himself, his wife, sister, and son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500315.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Velius Mescenius Posidonius Mesc<e>niae Tycheitini coniugi pientissimae bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500315.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Lucius Velius Mescenius Posidonius [erected this] for Mescenia Tycheitini, his most devoted wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-29500315.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Lucius Velius Mescenius Posidonius to his wife Mescenia Tycheitini, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500320.translation: To the spirits of the departed of Vett--
EDCS-29500320.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Vett-- by an unknown dedicant, likely an epitaph.

EDCS-29500321.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Vettio Tertullino Coelius Gemellinus amico incomparabili\n
EDCS-29500321.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Coelius Gemellinus to his incomparable friend, Tito Vettio Tertullino.
EDCS-29500321.summary: A funerary inscription by Coelius Gemellinus to his friend Tito Vettio Tertullino, likely an epitaph.

EDCS-29500323.text_edition: \n eculatori legionis XIIII Pontius Viator fratri bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500323.translation: Pontius Viator, account keeper of the 14th Legion, to his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-29500323.summary: A funerary inscription by Pontius Viator to his brother, who served in the 14th Legion.

EDCS-29500325.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Vibio Firmo qui et Artorius Caius Vibius F \n
EDCS-29500325.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Vibius F. to Caius Vibius Firmus, who was also called Artorius.
EDCS-29500325.summary: A funerary inscription by Caius Vibius F. to Caius Vibius Firmus, also known as Artorius, likely an epitaph.

EDCS-29500327.translation: To the spirits of the departed of Vibi.
EDCS-29500327.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the departed of the Vibi family, likely an epitaph.

EDCS-29500328.text_edition: \n Vibia S fecit sibi et Himero filio \n
EDCS-29500328.translation: Vibia S. made this for herself and for her son Himero.
EDCS-29500328.summary: A funerary inscription by Vibia S. for herself and her son Himero, likely an epitaph.

EDCS-29500329.text_edition: \n vixit AVI item vixit nnos XVI Vibullis \n
EDCS-29500329.translation: He lived... also he lived 16 years. Vibullis.
EDCS-29500329.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of someone identified with Vibullis, with a missing age.

EDCS-29500332.text_edition: \n Victorino defuncto annorum VI dierum XV Iulianus et Ca \n
EDCS-29500332.translation: Victorinus, deceased at 6 years of age and 15 days. Iulianus and Ca--
EDCS-29500332.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of a child named Victorinus, with parents Iulianus and Ca--.

EDCS-29500333.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Victorinus C., recording that he lived.

EDCS-29500335.text_edition: \n Lucio Vipsanio Luci filio Valenti defuncto nnorum XLVII Lucius Vipsanius Valens filius et Baebidia Balbina uxor bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500335.translation: Lucius Vipsanius Valens, his son, and Baebidia Balbina, his wife, to the well-deserving Lucius Vipsanius Valens, deceased at 47 years of age.
EDCS-29500335.summary: A funerary inscription by a son and wife to the deceased Lucius Vipsanius Valens, who died at 47.

EDCS-29500339.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vivio Privationi Papinia Tyche coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-29500339.translation: To the divine spirits, Papinia Tyche to her well-deserving husband, Vivius Privationus.
EDCS-29500339.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to a husband by his wife, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-29500340.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Viviae Secundinae Iulius Polynices EC \n
EDCS-29500340.translation: To the divine spirits, Vivia Secundina. Iulius Polynices [erected this].
EDCS-29500340.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Vivia Secundina by Iulius Polynices, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-29500343.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpio Dacioni Aurelia Victorina marito bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29500343.translation: Aurelia Victorina set up [this monument] to her well-deserving husband, Ulpius Dacioni.
EDCS-29500343.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Aurelia Victorina for her husband, Ulpius Dacioni, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-29500344.translation: [This is the tomb] of Ulpius Lucius, son of Lucius, of the Domitian tribe.
EDCS-29500344.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph identifying Ulpius Lucius, son of Lucius.

EDCS-29500345.text_edition: \n ------] Vibullius T legionis VII et Lucius Salvius Marci Sueto centuriones legionis XI iudices dati ex co<n=M>ventione a Lucio Volusio Saturnino legato pro praetore Cai Caesaris Augusti Germanici inter RV \n
EDCS-29500345.translation: [Judges appointed from the association] by Lucius Volusius Saturninus, governor, of Caesar Augustus Germanicus, between [...].
EDCS-29500345.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording judicial appointments made by a governor, Lucius Volusius Saturninus, during the reign of Emperor Caligula.

EDCS-29600214.summary: A short inscription identifying Alexander as a minister of God, likely Christian.

EDCS-29600222.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600222.text_edition: \n Aurelius Secundus qui cunparabid sic ab Aurelio Alexsio sic piscinam at sic dua corpura sic deponenda meum et coniugem meam Renatam et nefas quadra<b=R>it nobis parentibus ut p<on=VR>eremus filiam nostram in hanc piscinam sane coiiurabit sic ut supra birginiam sic suam null[lum aliud corpus inferatur ------ \n
EDCS-29600222.translation: Aurelius Secundus bought this swimming pool and two bodies to be placed here: my own and my wife Renata's. It is forbidden for our parents to bury our daughter in this swimming pool. He will swear, as above, that no other body besides his Virginian is to be placed here. [Further text missing].
EDCS-29600222.summary: A funerary inscription detailing the purchase of a burial plot by Aurelius Secundus for himself and his wife, with specific instructions and oaths regarding its use, likely Christian.

EDCS-29600231.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600231.text_edition: \n Euassu in pace Valeria coniugi suo fecit memoria annis vixit LX\n
EDCS-29600231.translation: Euassus made this for his wife Valeria in peace. She lived for 60 years.
EDCS-29600231.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Euassus for his wife Valeria, who lived for 60 years, likely Christian.

EDCS-29600237.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600237.text_edition: \n Flavius Virgilianus qui bixit sic annus sic XXX et Aurelia Ursilla oxor sic eius qui se vivi sibi urdenaverunt sic hunc sar<c=T>ofagum si quis autem voluerit super haec duo corpora punere sic infere<t=H> sanctae ecclisiae sic argenti libras quinquem \n
EDCS-29600237.translation: Flavius Virgilianus, who lived for 30 years, and Aurelia Ursilla, his wife, who prepared this tomb for themselves while alive. Whoever wishes to bury two bodies above these will pay 5 pounds of silver to the holy church.
EDCS-29600237.summary: A funerary inscription where Flavius Virgilianus and his wife Aurelia Ursilla prepared their tomb and stipulated a payment to the church for additional burials, likely Christian.

EDCS-29600238.text_edition: \n Flaviae infanti dulcissimae quae sana mente salutifero die Paschae gloriosi fontis gratiam consecuta est super vixitque post baptismum sanctum mensibus quinque vixit nnos III menses X dies VII Flavianus et Archelais parent<e=I>s filiae piissimae depositio XV Kalendas Septembres \n
EDCS-29600238.translation: For Flavia, the sweetest infant, who received the grace of the glorious font on a healthy day of Easter, and lived five months after her holy baptism, 3 years, 10 months, 7 days. Flavianus and Archelais, most devoted parents, to their daughter. Burial on the 15th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-29600238.summary: A Christian funerary epitaph for a young child, Flavia, detailing her age at death and baptism, and the date of her burial.

EDCS-29600240.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600240.text_edition: \n Piscina Flavi Sabbati Flavia Sexta compari et Flavia Regulia <f=E>(ilia) sua qui communiter consentientes comparaverunt \n
EDCS-29600240.translation: The pool of Flavius Sabbatus. Flavia Sexta and Flavia Regulia, his daughter, who bought it together with common consent.
EDCS-29600240.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a pool by Flavia Sexta and Flavia Regulia for Flavius Sabbatus.

EDCS-29600241.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication of thanks by Gaudentius.

EDCS-29600245.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to be a memorial for someone named John.

EDCS-29600248.text_edition: \n ius Iubentius sic patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-29600248.translation: To Iubentius, thus to his deserving father.
EDCS-29600248.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Iubentius to his well-deserving father.

EDCS-29600249.text_edition: \n Viventiae coniugi suae dilectissimae et sibi Valerius Felix depositus VII Idus Novembres qui vixit annos LXV quod si quis eam arcam post obitum eius aperire voluerit inferre debebit ecclesiae de(nariorum) folles mille in qua sunt filiae Graecina et Proculina\n
EDCS-29600249.translation: To Viventia, his most beloved wife, and for himself, Valerius Felix, buried on the seventh day before the Ides of November, who lived 65 years. If anyone wishes to open this tomb after her death, he will have to pay the church one thousand coin bags, in which are his daughters Graecina and Proculina.
EDCS-29600249.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Felix and his wife Viventia, setting a penalty for disturbing the tomb.

EDCS-29600250.summary: This is a fragmented inscription honoring someone named Julius.

EDCS-29600258.summary: This is a brief epitaph wishing peace to the deceased, Palladius.

EDCS-29600262.text_edition: \n Deposita Petronia die III Kalendas Augustas Depositus Nereus die II Nonas Augustas Deposita Sofronia die XII Kalendas Iulias Pro nefas iste tuum loquitur Petronia nomen iam titulus nono vixdum lacrimabilis anno nata diu dulcis et longi pignus amoris vita brevis sed nunc istic iam longa parentum vulnera Nereus genitor genetrixque funesti Sofronia brevibus tumuli flevere querellis tu tamen hinc spera caelum pia mente fidelis\n
EDCS-29600262.translation: Petronia was laid to rest on the third day before the Kalends of August. Nereus was laid to rest on the second day before the Nones of August. Sofronia was laid to rest on the twelfth day before the Kalends of July. Alas, this epitaph speaks your name, Petronia, already in its ninth year. Yet, a sweet and long pledge of love, life was short but now here for a long time. The wounds of parents, Nereus the father and the mother Sofronia, mourned with brief laments in the tomb. Yet you, pious and faithful, hope for heaven from here.
EDCS-29600262.summary: This complex epitaph commemorates Petronia, Nereus, and Sofronia, possibly family members, with poetic verses lamenting loss and offering hope.

EDCS-29600267.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600267.text_edition: \n XNI arcam I et Aureli-- S \n
EDCS-29600267.translation: A tomb for XNI and Aurelius--.
EDCS-29600267.summary: This is a fragmented inscription indicating the allocation of a tomb for an individual or individuals named XNI and Aurelius.

EDCS-29600271.text_edition: \n rit Valeri i Valer et Valeria Merope filia annorum V\n
EDCS-29600271.translation: Valerius Felix, Valerius and Valeria Merope, daughter, aged 5.
EDCS-29600271.summary: This inscription records the deaths of Valerius Felix, Valerius, and a young girl named Valeria Merope, aged 5.

EDCS-29600272.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600272.text_edition: \n Valeria Hermogenia honesta femina die V Kalendarum Iuniarum quinquagesimo octavo anno finita est viva se arcam de suo poni iussit\n
EDCS-29600272.translation: Valeria Hermogenia, a woman of good standing, died on the 5th day before the Kalends of June in her fifty-eighth year; she herself ordered her tomb to be erected during her lifetime at her own expense.
EDCS-29600272.summary: This epitaph records the death and burial arrangements of Valeria Hermogenia, who died in her 58th year and had her tomb constructed during her lifetime.

EDCS-29600281.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29600281.text_edition: \n Divi Constanti pignus de nomine nomen iam lugenda diu condita progenies blanda suaeque aetatis victrix dulcis obit nimium sede beatorum recipit te lacteus orbis e gremio matris hoc tua digna fides\n
EDCS-29600281.translation: Offspring, sweet, victorious, and cherished, beloved pledge of Divine Constantius, you have departed from the world of the living; the milky orb of heaven receives you into the blessed seats, fitting for your devotion, from the embrace of your mother.
EDCS-29600281.summary: This is an epitaph lamenting the early death of a child, a pledge of Constantius, and celebrating the child's blessed reception into heaven.

EDCS-29600283.text_edition: \n miseri pia A mariti Q est potitu ESSIB in dom TIB urnat sic PIC tus geminus vit respicit O nomine NA S miseran TEOEPFI M IV S et I V INF \n
EDCS-29600283.translation: Unhappy A[---] of the husband, for whom Poti[---] was born, and in the house TIB urnat, thus PIC tus, Geminius, Vit [---] looks back with misery, O name NA S, miserable [---] and his wife [---].
EDCS-29600283.summary: This is a fragmented epitaph lamenting the death of a husband and perhaps a child, mentioning his wife and a possibly related person named Potius.

EDCS-29600289.text_edition: \n qua Deum me rem facinus M tas haec OC \n
EDCS-29600289.translation: What deed of god or what crime, this [---] has done.
EDCS-29600289.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that appears to be a lament or question about a deed or crime.

EDCS-29601484.text_edition: \n Praecipio libertis libertabusque meis nequis vestrum in hunc monumentum corpus aut ossa exterae inferat si quis contemserit sic tunc conlibertis dare devebet sic poenae nominae sic |(denarios) miliae sic\n
EDCS-29601484.translation: I command my freedmen and freedwomen, that none of you shall bring a body or bones of an outsider into this monument; if anyone disobeys, he will owe to the co-freedmen a penalty of one thousand denarii.
EDCS-29601484.summary: This is a funerary regulation inscribed on a monument, dictating burial restrictions and penalties for violating them, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-29601485.text_edition: \n IMAI vivi fecerunt sibi et filiis suis pientissimis quot si di aequi fuissent illi nobis facere debuerant parentibus infelicissimis quod heredum non fiet\n
EDCS-29601485.translation: IMAI made this for themselves and for their most devoted sons, who, if the gods had been just, should have made this for us, the most unfortunate parents, but it will not happen since there are no heirs.
EDCS-29601485.summary: This inscription expresses the sorrow of parents who erected a tomb for themselves and their deceased sons, lamenting that their sons could not reciprocate the honor due to the parents' own unhappiness and lack of surviving heirs.

EDCS-29601505.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sulliae Maximae defunctae annorum plus minus XXXV Sullius Felix \n
EDCS-29601505.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sullia Maxima, deceased, aged more or less 35 years. Sullius Felix [dedicated this].
EDCS-29601505.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Sullia Maxima, who died around age 35, made by Sullius Felix.

EDCS-29601516.text_edition: \n Ima hic sita Ia mater filiae\n
EDCS-29601516.translation: Ima lies here, mother of [her] daughter.
EDCS-29601516.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Ima as the mother of a daughter and marking her burial place.

EDCS-29601519.text_edition: \n D O QV RIVRI ILIS coniugi pro meritis\n
EDCS-29601519.translation: To [---]RI, for his services to his spouse.
EDCS-29601519.summary: This is a dedication to a spouse, likely a husband, for their merits.

EDCS-29601523.text_edition: \n SEL Hospiti patrono suo titulum posuit\n
EDCS-29601523.translation: SEL [dedicated] this monument to his patron, Hospitus.
EDCS-29601523.summary: This inscription records SEL setting up a monument for his patron, Hospitus.

EDCS-29601524.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a wife named Iailo, likely on a tombstone or a private monument.

EDCS-29601529.summary: This inscription, with significant lacunae, seems to record the name Ami[---]cus, possibly a personal name or part of a longer phrase.

EDCS-29601571.text_edition: \n Septimius Maximianus vir perfectissimus principalis coloniae Salon(itanorum) vivo sibi et coniugi suae dulcissimae posuit qui vicsit sic annis LXV depositus die III Nonas Februarias\n
EDCS-29601571.translation: Septimius Maximianus, a most accomplished man, principal of the colony of Salona, erected this for himself and his most beloved wife while alive, who lived 65 years and was laid to rest on the third day before the Nones of February.
EDCS-29601571.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Septimius Maximianus, a prominent official in Salona, for himself and his wife, recording his lifespan and date of death.

EDCS-29601572.text_edition: \n IESIOISV fer cun sic Vib<i=L>o civi Cuncordiensi sic \n
EDCS-29601572.translation: To Vibius Concordia, a citizen of Cuncurdiensis.
EDCS-29601572.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Vibius Concordia, a citizen of Cuncurdiensis, with some letters being uncertain or restored.

EDCS-29700325.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursioni defuncto annorum XXIII parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-29700325.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Ursioni, who died at the age of twenty-three, his parents erected this.
EDCS-29700325.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ursioni, who died at 23, erected by his parents.

EDCS-29900010.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Eponae Regin(a)e Genio loci Publius Bennius Egregius miles cohortis voluntariorum adiutor principis beneficiarius consularis votum solvit l(ibens)\n
EDCS-29900010.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Epona Regina, and to the Genius of the place, Publius Bennius Egregius, a soldier of the volunteer cohort, assistant to the princeps, and a beneficiarius consularis, gladly paid his vow.
EDCS-29900010.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter, Epona, and the local genius by Publius Bennius Egregius, a soldier holding several official roles, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-29900011.text_edition: \n Otaciliae Severae Augustae coniugi Philippi Augusti matri Philippi nobilissimi Caesaris res publica Docleatium decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-29900011.translation: To Otacilia Severa Augusta, wife of Philippus Augustus, mother of Philippus, the most noble Caesar, by decree of the decurions of the public council of Docleatia.
EDCS-29900011.summary: This is a dedication honoring Otacilia Severa, wife of Emperor Philippus, and mother of Caesar Philippus, set up by the town council of Docleatia.

EDCS-29900013.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Gordio Maximiano artis grammaticae Graecae peritissimo Quintus Flavius Helenus amico inconparabili sic\n
EDCS-29900013.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Caius Gordius Maximianus, most skilled in the art of Greek grammar, Quintus Flavius Helenus, his incomparable friend, set this up.
EDCS-29900013.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Gordius Maximianus, a Greek grammar teacher, erected by his friend Quintus Flavius Helenus.

EDCS-29900014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Silvio Aestivo sodali ROMP Iulius Iadestinus et Flavius Chresimus collegae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-29900014.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld, for Silvius Aestivus, a deserving colleague, Iulius Iadestinus and Flavius Chresimus, colleagues, set this up.
EDCS-29900014.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Silvius Aestivus, a colleague, erected by his fellow colleagues Iulius Iadestinus and Flavius Chresimus.

EDCS-29900015.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto sacrum Sextus Baberius Ianuarius votum posuit merito\n
EDCS-29900015.translation: Sacred to Sol Invictus, Sextus Baberius Ianuarius set up this vow, as deserved.
EDCS-29900015.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the unconquered sun god Sol Invictus by Sextus Baberius Ianuarius.

EDCS-29900018.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Lucio Domitio Aureliano Au[gusto] II et Capitolino consulibus Aurelius Verecundus vir egregius procurator argentariarum balneum vetustate conlapsum ad pristinam faciem reformare curavit\n
EDCS-29900018.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Augustus (second consulship) and Capitolino, Aurelius Verecundus, an outstanding man and procurator of the mines, saw to it that the bathhouse, collapsed with age, was restored to its former state.
EDCS-29900018.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bathhouse by Aurelius Verecundus, procurator of the mines, during the consulship of Emperor Aurelian, likely in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29900021.text_edition: \n I Mascelio maritus coniugi carissimae et sibi posterisque suis bene merentibus\n
EDCS-29900021.translation: Mascelio to his dearest wife and to himself and to their deserving descendants.
EDCS-29900021.summary: This is a dedication from Mascelio for his wife and descendants, likely on a tomb or funerary monument, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900022.text_edition: \n inter Nerastinos et Pituntinos termini recogniti et restituti a Pisone legato pro praetore Tiberi Claudi Caesaris Augusti Germanici per Caium Marium Maternum |(centurionem) legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis quos Lucius Volusius Saturninus legatus pro praetore statuendos curaverat\n
EDCS-29900022.translation: Between the Nerastini and the Pituntini, boundary markers were recognized and restored by Piso, governor and legate of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, through Caius Marius Maternus, a centurion of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, which Lucius Volusius Saturninus, legate and governor, had arranged to be set up.
EDCS-29900022.summary: This inscription records the re-establishment of boundary markers between two tribes, the Nerastini and Pituntini, by the governor Piso, under Emperor Claudius, with earlier arrangements made by Lucius Volusius Saturninus, dating to the mid-1st century AD.

EDCS-29900027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Aemilio Rufo beneficiario consularis Aemilia Aphrodite patrono\n
EDCS-29900027.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Quintus Aemilius Rufus, aide to the consular legate. By Aemilia Aphrodite, to her patron.
EDCS-29900027.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aemilia Aphrodite to her patron, Quintus Aemilius Rufus, an aide to a consular legate, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900028.text_edition: \n cohortis VIII voluntariorum mp Firmi coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-29900028.translation: To Firmi, to his deserving wife, of the VIII cohort of volunteers.
EDCS-29900028.summary: This is a dedication from Firmi to his deserving wife, likely a tombstone, from a member of the VIII cohort of volunteers, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900029.text_edition: \n Marcus Pomponius Zosimus negotians materiarius vivus fecit sibi et Pomponiae Semnae filiae optimae defunctae et Pomponio Secundino defuncto bene merentibus hic lapide lusit ponderibus his XXXX L C\n
EDCS-29900029.translation: Marcus Pomponius Zosimus, a merchant dealing in building materials, made this while alive for himself and for his deceased daughter Pomponia Semna, the excellent one, and for the deserving deceased Pomponius Secundinus; he competed here on this stone with weights of 40 pounds.
EDCS-29900029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Pomponius Zosimus for himself and his deceased family members, also recording his profession as a building materials merchant and a note about his competition with weights, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900033.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Marnus qui vixit annos Aurelia Tertulla coniugi incomparabili posuit et sibi\n
EDCS-29900033.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Marnus, who lived [age missing], erected this for Aurelia Tertulla, his incomparable wife, and for himself.
EDCS-29900033.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Marnus for his wife Aurelia Tertulla and himself, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900034.text_edition: \n o maritus quae vixit nnos XV dies XVIII\n
EDCS-29900034.translation: [Name missing], husband, [for his wife] who lived 15 years and 18 days.
EDCS-29900034.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph recording the age of a deceased woman and the grief of her husband, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900035.summary: This is a dedication from a child to their mother, Severa, likely on a tomb or monument, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900043.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29900043.text_edition: \n Aurelii Marcianus IIAHIN civis Afer et Quintina uxor eius vivi sibi hanc piscinam virginem a se conparatam constituerunt\n
EDCS-29900043.translation: The Aurelii Marcianus, a citizen from Africa, and Quintina, his wife, made this pool, new when they purchased it, for themselves while alive.
EDCS-29900043.summary: This inscription records the construction of a pool by Aurelius Marcianus and his wife Quintina, who were citizens from Africa, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900048.summary: This inscription records the name of Gaius Vat Polin Cast, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900049.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Valeria Emerita, with the rest of the text missing, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900053.text_edition: \n Invicto patri Aelius Aelianus eques praetorianus et Ulpius Licinius a scriniis praefecti sancxerunt sic\n
EDCS-29900053.translation: Aelius Aelianus, an equestrian soldier of the Praetorian Guard, and Ulpius Licinius, an imperial secretary, decreed the following to the unconquered father.
EDCS-29900053.summary: This inscription records a decree by Aelius Aelianus and Ulpius Licinius concerning the worship of an unconquered deity, likely a military or administrative monument.

EDCS-29900054.text_edition: \n Terrae MatriMI NOII ES calinio sup sic cura procuratoris Mercurius vilicus officinae ferrariae XI Kalendas Maias \n
EDCS-29900054.translation: To Mother Earth. Aelius Calinius, procurator, and Mercurius, foreman of the ironworks office, set this up by decree on the 21st of April.
EDCS-29900054.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Mother Earth by a procurator and a foreman of an ironworks, likely an agricultural or industrial monument.

EDCS-29900056.text_edition: \n Aplo Curbania Kabaleti filia annorum IIII\n
EDCS-29900056.translation: Aplo, daughter of Curbania and Kabaletos, aged 4.
EDCS-29900056.summary: This inscription records the name and age of a child, Aplo, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900057.text_edition: \n Aplo Aplinis Tizi filia annorum II\n
EDCS-29900057.translation: Aplo, daughter of Aplinys and Tizis, aged 2.
EDCS-29900057.summary: This inscription records the name and age of a child, Aplo, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900058.text_edition: \n Sexto Licinia Pinsi filia <a=H>nnorum XX hic sita est \n
EDCS-29900058.translation: Here lies Sextus Licinius, son of Pinsi, aged 20.
EDCS-29900058.summary: This inscription records the burial of Sextus Licinius, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900059.text_edition: \n Caius Lucilius Cai filius Ma C(aio) Lucilio Cai filio Volso patri et Luciliae \n
EDCS-29900059.translation: To Gaius Lucilius, son of Gaius, his father Volso, son of Gaius Lucilius, and to Lucilia [...].
EDCS-29900059.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a father and another individual, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-29900060.text_edition: \n Sub hac inscriptione Marcus Cupitius Marci filius Pollia Paulus Amasia miles legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis stipendiorum XVII Cupitius Pollio frater faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-29900060.translation: Under this inscription, Marcus Cupitius Pollus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, with 17 years of service, his brother Cupitius Pollio had this made.
EDCS-29900060.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Marcus Cupitius Pollus, a soldier, commissioned by his brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900061.text_edition: \n Invicto Mithrae spelaeum cum omne impensa Hermes Cai Antoni Rufi praefecti vehiculorum et conductoris publici portorii servus vilicus Fortunatianus fecit\n
EDCS-29900061.translation: Hermes, a slave and foreman belonging to Caius Antonius Rufus, prefect of transport services and public tax collector, made the cave for the unconquered Mithras with all expenses.
EDCS-29900061.summary: This inscription records the construction of a Mithraeum by a slave foreman under a prefect of transport services, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900062.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Vallia Veneria, a freedwoman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900103.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Sertorio Cai filio Broccho Aquilio Agricolae Pedanio Fusco Salinatori Iulio Serviano \n
EDCS-29900103.translation: To Gnaeus Sertorius Brocchus, son of Caius, Aquilius Agricola Pedanius Fuscus Salinator Julius Servianus.
EDCS-29900103.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Gnaeus Sertorius Brocchus, son of Caius, with a long string of gentilic and cognomen names, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900104.text_edition: \n ius Quirina Genialis viator consulum et praetorum sacerdos at sic aram Caesaris decurio testamento poni iussit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-29900104.translation: Genialis, a herald of consuls and praetors, a priest, ordered this altar of Caesar to be set up by his will; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-29900104.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar to Caesar by a man named Genialis, who held various civic and priestly roles, as authorized by the local council.

EDCS-29900105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelio Verzaiio liberto qui vixsit sic annis LXXXV\n
EDCS-29900105.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the departed. Aurelius Verzaius, freedman, who lived 85 years.
EDCS-29900105.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Verzaius, a freedman who lived to the age of 85.

EDCS-29900106.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records the names of two individuals, possibly Aemilius Maximus and Marcus Antonius, date uncertain.

EDCS-29900107.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cohortali Aurelius Lautus ex voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-29900107.translation: Aurelius Lautus willingly set up this altar to Jupiter, the Best and Greatest, Protector of the Cohort, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-29900107.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Lautus dedicating an altar to Jupiter Cohortalis, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900108.text_edition: \n Invicto Augusto Aurelius Argyrianus procurator Aug(usti) Orfito cons(ule) laetus libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-29900108.translation: Aurelius Argyrianus, procurator of the Emperor, willingly and deservedly set this up to the Invincible Augustus, in the consulship of Orfitus.
EDCS-29900108.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to the Invincible Augustus by Aurelius Argyrianus, an imperial procurator, during the consulship of Orfitus.

EDCS-29900109.text_edition: \n Servilia Marci filia Copiesilla aediculam Matris Magnae faciendam curavit ipsa inpensa sua quam voverat pro ea Marcus Cottius Certus vir eius\n
EDCS-29900109.translation: Servilia, daughter of Marcus, Copiesilla, saw to the construction of a small shrine to the Great Mother, at her own expense, which she had vowed for her husband, Marcus Cottius Certus.
EDCS-29900109.summary: This inscription records that Servilia Copiesilla funded the construction of a shrine to the Great Mother, as a vow made on behalf of her husband.

EDCS-29900110.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Coulio Leporio qui vixit nnos XII menses X dies VII Coulius Zoticus et Publicia Ursa filio infelicissimo posuerunt qui HACARIS \n
EDCS-29900110.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Coulio Leporius, who lived 12 years, 10 months, and 7 days. Coulius Zoticus and Publicia Ursa set this up for their most unhappy son, who [died at] HACARIS.
EDCS-29900110.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Coulio Leporius, a child who died at the age of 12, erected by his parents, with a place name mentioned.

EDCS-29900112.text_edition: \n Publius Attecius Publi filius Venimarus miles cohortis VIII centuria Attili domo Claudia Viruno annorum XXI stipendiorum II Marcus Ressius Bricomarus heres ex testamento ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-29900112.translation: Publius Attecius Venimarus, son of Publius, a soldier of the 8th Cohort, in the century of Attilius, from the municipium Claudia Virunum, aged 21, with 2 years of service. Marcus Ressius Bricomarus, his heir, saw to its erection according to the will.
EDCS-29900112.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Attecius Venimarus, a soldier from Claudia Virunum, erected by his heir.

EDCS-29900113.text_edition: \n LLV matri optimae et pientissimae vivae posuerunt locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-29900113.translation: His children placed this for their excellent and most dutiful mother while she was still alive, the site granted by decree of the town council.
EDCS-29900113.summary: This inscription records a dedication by children to their mother, noting it was authorized by a local council decree, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900114.text_edition: \n Aelii Nepos et Dasas Arbonis patri carissimo qui vixit nnos LXX bene merenti memoriam posuerunt\n
EDCS-29900114.translation: Aelius Nepos and Dasas erected this in memory of their father Arbo, most dear, who lived 70 years, for his well-deserved memory.
EDCS-29900114.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by sons for their father, Arbo, who lived to be 70 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900117.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Maxa annorum L infelicissimis po<su=VS>(it) Dis Manibus Aurelia Clemaentina sic annorum XL Sicu filius \n
EDCS-29900117.translation: To the divine shades, Aurelius Maxa, aged 50, placed [this]. To the divine shades, Aurelia Clementina, 40 years old. Thus her son.
EDCS-29900117.summary: This inscription is a double epitaph for Aurelius Maxa and Aurelia Clementina, recording their ages and erected by Clementina's son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900788.translation: To the divine shades of Ravonius Fortunatus. 101.
EDCS-29900788.summary: This is a very brief epitaph dedicated to Ravonius Fortunatus, possibly indicating his age or year of death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000155.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Ulpius Ianuarius, identified as a husband, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000156.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Onesimiano qui vixit annos XIII menses VIII dies III Marcus Ulpius Onesimus et Aurelia Lucilla parentes infelicissimi filio pientissimo et dulcissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-30000156.translation: Marcus Ulpius Onesimus and Aurelia Lucilla, most unhappy parents, erected this for their son, Marcus Ulpius Onesimianus, who lived 13 years, 8 months, and 3 days, a most dutiful and dearest son.
EDCS-30000156.summary: This inscription is a memorial set up by grieving parents for their young son, Marcus Ulpius Onesimianus, who died at 13 years old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000157.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Sabinae annorum XXVI Ulpius Candidus filiae posuit\n
EDCS-30000157.translation: To the divine shades of Ulpia Sabina, aged 26. Ulpius Candidus erected [this] for his daughter.
EDCS-30000157.summary: This inscription is an epitaph for Ulpia Sabina, aged 26, erected by her father, Ulpius Candidus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000160.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Urvicae sic defunctae nnorum XVII Urbana soro<r=P>i infelicisiimae \n
EDCS-30000160.translation: To the divine shades of Urvica, thus deceased, aged 17. Urbana, for her most unhappy sister.
EDCS-30000160.summary: This epitaph records the death of Urvica at age 17, erected by her sister Urbana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000161.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Urgulania Valentina matri et Tarquitius \n
EDCS-30000161.translation: To the divine shades of Urgulania Valentina, mother, and Tarquitius...
EDCS-30000161.summary: This inscription begins an epitaph for Urgulania Valentina, identified as a mother, and Tarquitius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000162.translation: To the divine shades of Ursinus...
EDCS-30000162.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph dedicated to Ursinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000163.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursino nnum VII Naebia sic <T=I>uta bene merenti \n
EDCS-30000163.translation: To the departed spirits. Naebia, in her seventh year, [erected this] for Ursinus, her deserving son.
EDCS-30000163.summary: A funerary dedication by a mother for her son, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursus Iulio patri defuncto annorum XXXX bene merenti\n
EDCS-30000165.translation: To the departed spirits. For Ursius, her deserving father, who died at the age of forty.
EDCS-30000165.summary: A funerary dedication for a father, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000167.translation: To the departed spirits. For Ursius, five years old.
EDCS-30000167.summary: A brief funerary dedication for a child named Ursius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000184.text_edition: \n s qui vixit annos nonaginta et lia Ammi qui sic vixit annos quod si quis supra ea corpora aliud voluerit ponere inferet AVR PRIMA LIB\n
EDCS-30000184.translation: who lived ninety years and Lia Ammi, who lived... If anyone wishes to place anything else above these bodies, he will incur [a penalty]. AVR Prima, freedwoman.
EDCS-30000184.summary: A funerary inscription with a warning against disturbing the grave, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000205.text_edition: \n Rutilia viva fecit sibi et Ar Tusco patri legionis VII et A Primioni filio et Rutiliae Festae defunctae \n
EDCS-30000205.translation: Rutilia, while alive, made this for herself, and for her father Ar Tuscus of the VII Legion, and for her son A. Primio, and for the deceased Rutilia Festa.
EDCS-30000205.summary: A family tomb inscription set up by Rutilia, likely from the Roman imperial period, commemorating herself and deceased relatives, including a legionary father.

EDCS-30000206.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Novellia sibi et Lucio Iulio marito optimo veterano cohortis VIII voluntariorum et cognationi suae et libertis liberisque eorum\n
EDCS-30000206.translation: To the departed spirits. Novellia for herself, and for her most excellent husband Lucius Julius, a veteran of the VIII Cohort of Volunteers, and for her extended family, and for their freedmen and their children.
EDCS-30000206.summary: A funerary inscription by a woman for herself, her husband who was a veteran, and their family, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000207.text_edition: \n Titus Octavius Titi filius Velina Hister veteranus legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis testamento fieri iussit et Acrini contubernali in fronte pedes X in agro pedes XI\n
EDCS-30000207.translation: Titus Octavius, son of Titus, of the Velina tribe, from Hister, a veteran of the XI Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, ordered this to be made by his will, and for Acrinia, his companion. Ten feet in front, eleven feet in the rear.
EDCS-30000207.summary: A tomb inscription for a veteran of the XI Legion, commissioned by will and specifying plot dimensions, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000208.text_edition: \n Lucius Pescennius Luci filius Falerna Saturninus veteranus legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis vivus fecit sibi et Mariae Spuri filiae Quintae coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-30000208.translation: Lucius Pescennius Saturninus, son of Lucius, of the Falerna tribe, a veteran of the XI Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, made this while alive for himself and for Maria Quinta, daughter of Spurius, his deserving wife.
EDCS-30000208.summary: A funerary monument set up by a veteran of the XI Legion for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000209.text_edition: \n Lucius Vegnonius -- Luci filius veteranus legionis VII domo Florentia opaena Fabricia se viva sibi et viro E hic situs est\n
EDCS-30000209.translation: Lucius Vegnonius, son of Lucius, a veteran of the VII Legion, from Florence, [with the tribe] Fabricia, set this up while alive for himself and for his husband E. He lies here.
EDCS-30000209.summary: A funerary inscription for a veteran of the VII Legion, indicating it was set up by himself and mentioning his husband, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30000210.text_edition: \n qui vixsit! annos XXII menses I INFICIN Romae M conius sic amantissima marito cum co sic vixsit! benem sic merenti po<s>ivit sic\n
EDCS-30000210.translation: [This inscription is for] who lived 22 years, 1 month. Rome. M. Conius placed this for his most loving husband, with whom he lived. [for the deserving husband].
EDCS-30000210.summary: A funerary inscription by a man for his deceased husband, likely from the Roman imperial period, including a note on the duration of their relationship.

EDCS-30000211.text_edition: \n Himero Himer pater et Stephane mater et Tranquillae filiae et Tranquillae matri\n
EDCS-30000211.translation: Himero, his father Himerus and mother Stephane, and their daughter Tranquilla, and their mother Tranquilla.
EDCS-30000211.summary: This inscription likely commemorates members of a family, with Himerus and Stephane dedicating it for their daughter Tranquilla and Himerus's mother, also named Tranquilla.

EDCS-30000212.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus vivus fecit Quintus Agrius Eutychides Agrio Barbaro <f=E>ilio et Agrio Eutycho patri et Flaviae Maximae coniugi bene merentibus \n
EDCS-30000212.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Agrius Eutychides, while alive, made this for his deserving son Agrius Barbarus, and his father Agrius Eutyches, and his wife Flavia Maxima.
EDCS-30000212.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Quintus Agrius Eutychides for his son, father, and wife, indicating a burial monument.

EDCS-30100396.translation: To the spirits of the departed for Marcia Helpidi.
EDCS-30100396.summary: A brief funerary inscription dedicated to Marcia Helpidi.

EDCS-30100397.text_edition: \n Sev ANI et Terentiae Marci filiae Te annorum Marcus Terentius Vale \n
EDCS-30100397.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sev[---]ani and Terentia, daughter of Marcus. Marcus Terentius Vale [erected this for them?]. Aged [---] years.
EDCS-30100397.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription that likely records the names of individuals and their ages, possibly set up by Marcus Terentius Vale.

EDCS-30100398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cnaeo Stenio Securo Stenia \n
EDCS-30100398.translation: To the spirits of the departed for Gnaeus Stenius Securus. Stenia [dedicated this].
EDCS-30100398.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by Stenia to Gnaeus Stenius Securus.

EDCS-30100402.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Maximae defunctae nnorum X dierum XXX Aurelius Tertius miles cohortis VIII voluntariorum Antoninianae et Aurelia Apuleia parentes filiae infelicissimae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30100402.translation: To the spirits of the departed for Aurelia Maxima, who died aged 10 years and 30 days. Aurelius Tertius, a soldier of the VIII cohort of Antoninian volunteers, and Aurelia Apuleia, parents, erected this for their most unfortunate daughter who deserved well.
EDCS-30100402.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Aurelius Tertius and Aurelia Apuleia for their young daughter Aurelia Maxima, who died as a child.

EDCS-30100403.text_edition: \n Quintus Baebius Quinti filius Sergia Conanis miles legionis VII nnorum XXXXV stipendiorum XX hic situs est quamvis lasse viator rogo ne graveris et tumulum contempla meum lege et moraris iam aliquid resciveris dum vixi hilaris iucundus amicis nunc torpens iaceo hic ossa requiescunt mea natus sum C<o=A>n<a=O>nis conditus in Ill<y=V>rico vale et bene facito vitae dum fatum venit frater fratri \n
EDCS-30100403.translation: Quintus Baebius, son of Quintus, of the Sergia tribe, from Conanis, a soldier of the VII legion, aged 45 years, with 20 years of service, lies here. Though weary traveler, I ask that you do not trouble yourself; contemplate my tomb, read it, and linger, and you will learn something. While I lived, I was cheerful and pleasant to friends; now I lie here numb, my bones rest here. I was born in Conanis, buried in Illyricum. Farewell, and fare well in life until fate came. Brother to brother.
EDCS-30100403.summary: A soldier of the VII legion, Quintus Baebius, recounts his life and requests travelers to read his tomb, which was set up by his brother.

EDCS-30100404.text_edition: \n Lucius Cornelius Luci filius Fabia Heraclea miles legionis VII nnorum XXXIII stipendiorum XI testamento fieri iussit Secundus heres posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100404.translation: Lucius Cornelius, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe, a soldier of the VII legion, aged 33 years, with 11 years of service, ordered this to be made by his will. Secundus, the heir, placed it. He lies here.
EDCS-30100404.summary: A soldier of the VII legion, Lucius Cornelius, stipulated in his will for his heir Secundus to erect his tomb, where he lies.

EDCS-30100405.text_edition: \n agenio Eucisi filio equiti alae Frontonianae annorum XL stipendiorum XX Nemis Sige hic situs est frateres sic frater sic fecerunt\n
EDCS-30100405.translation: [---]enio, son of Eucisus, a cavalryman of the Frontonian ala, aged 40 years, with 20 years of service, lies here. Nemis Sige, his brothers, thus, his brothers, made this.
EDCS-30100405.summary: A cavalryman of the Frontonian ala is commemorated by his brothers.

EDCS-30100406.text_edition: \n Caius Longinius Cai filius Cornelia Amblada miles legionis VII annorum XL stipendiorum XXI hic situs est frater fratri\n
EDCS-30100406.translation: Caius Longinius, son of Caius, of the Cornelia tribe, a soldier of the VII legion, aged 40 years, with 21 years of service, lies here. Brother to brother.
EDCS-30100406.summary: A soldier of the VII legion, Caius Longinius, is commemorated by his brother.

EDCS-30100407.text_edition: \n EMANS Platoris Daesitias vexillarius equitum cohortis I Belgarum turma Valeri Proculi nnorum XLV stipendiorum XXIV hic situs est fieri curavit Iulia Ves coniunx\n
EDCS-30100407.translation: Emans, standard-bearer of a troop of the 1st Cohort of Belgae under Valerius Proculus, aged 45, with 24 years of service, lies here. His wife Iulia Ves had him buried.
EDCS-30100407.summary: This is a tombstone for Emans, a standard-bearer of a cavalry cohort, erected by his wife.

EDCS-30100408.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Severo infelicissimo <q=O>ui decid<i>t in Pannonia cum duo<b=R>us filiis Misaucis Varronianus tata patronis be<n=IV>e meri<t=I>is <p=D>osuit \n
EDCS-30100408.translation: To the departed spirits. Varronianus set this up for Severus, most unfortunate, who died in Pannonia, with his two sons, Misaucis. For his well-deserved merits.
EDCS-30100408.summary: This is a tombstone for Severus and his two sons, likely erected by a relative named Varronianus.

EDCS-30100409.text_edition: \n Marcus Vibius Marci filius Aemilia D<y=V>rrachio miles legionis VII nnorum XXX stipendiorum VII hic situs est lius heres posuit \n
EDCS-30100409.translation: Marcus Vibius Dyrrachus, son of Marcus, of the Aemilia tribe, a soldier of the 7th Legion, aged 30, with 7 years of service, lies here. His heir Lius erected it.
EDCS-30100409.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Vibius Dyrrachus, a soldier in the 7th Legion, erected by his heir.

EDCS-30100410.text_edition: \n Pollia Aese miles legionis VII nnorum XXX stipendiorum VII hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100410.translation: Pollia Aese, a soldier of the 7th Legion, aged 30, with 7 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-30100410.summary: This is a tombstone for Pollia Aese, a soldier in the 7th Legion.

EDCS-30100411.text_edition: \n ITNAI LIVOS annorum et Faustus nnorum XII\n
EDCS-30100411.summary: This inscription records the ages of two individuals, Livos and Faustus.

EDCS-30100412.text_edition: \n Iulio Menandro Iulius infanti mensum sic Menander pater\n
EDCS-30100412.translation: To Iulius Menander. Iulius set this up for his infant son Menander, aged [...]. His father.
EDCS-30100412.summary: This is a tombstone for an infant named Menander, set up by his father, Iulius.

EDCS-30100413.translation: Lucius Signius Firmus set this up.
EDCS-30100413.summary: This is a dedication set up by Lucius Signius Firmus.

EDCS-30100416.text_edition: \n Iunoni Aurelius Nepos ob honorem duumveratus sic Firmi et Maximi filiorum votum pater libens solvit\n
EDCS-30100416.translation: Aurelius Nepos willingly fulfilled a vow to Juno on the occasion of his sons Firmius and Maximinus holding the office of duumvir.
EDCS-30100416.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Juno by Aurelius Nepos, commemorating his sons' holding of the duumvirate.

EDCS-30100417.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly dedicating something to Augustus.

EDCS-30100418.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Titius Euthimus sic Gabalus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-30100418.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. Titius Euthimus, a Gabalian, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100418.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater by Titius Euthimus.

EDCS-30100420.text_edition: \n Nymphis et Silvano VANECS voto libens fecit\n
EDCS-30100420.translation: To the Nymphs and Silvanus, Vanecs gladly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-30100420.summary: This is a votive dedication to the Nymphs and Silvanus made by Vanecs.

EDCS-30100421.text_edition: \n Triviis sacrum Titia Elpis votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-30100421.translation: Titia Elpis gladly set up this votive offering at the crossroads.
EDCS-30100421.summary: This inscription records a votive offering at a crossroads by Titia Elpis.

EDCS-30100423.translation: Lucius Eburnus Tatianus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100423.summary: This inscription records Lucius Eburnus Tatianus fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-30100425.text_edition: \n Marco Annio Vero II Cnaeo Arrio Augure consulibus Aelius Fronto Charito Nassius Iettus StaC Lucio Iulio Serviano Lucio Sollio \n
EDCS-30100425.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Annius Verus (II) and Gnaeus Arrius Augur, Aelius Fronto, Charito, Nassius Iettus, and Statius Lucius Iulius Servianus, Lucius Sollius [dedicated this].
EDCS-30100425.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, likely dedicators, under the consuls Marcus Annius Verus and Gnaeus Arrius Augur.

EDCS-30100426.text_edition: \n Burrius Betuloni filius Trebocus miles cohortis Aquitanorum annorum LV stipendiorum XXIX hic situs est heres posuit\n
EDCS-30100426.translation: Burrius Betulo's son, Trebocus, a soldier of the Aquitanian cohort, aged 55, with 29 years of service, is buried here; his heir set this up.
EDCS-30100426.summary: This is a tombstone for Burrius Betulo's son, Trebocus, a soldier, placed by his heir.

EDCS-30100427.text_edition: \n Marcus Varenus Marci filius Voltinia Valens veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Marci Vareni Secundi liberti idemque heredis\n
EDCS-30100427.translation: Marcus Varenus Valens, son of Marcus, a veteran of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, ordered this to be made in his will under the supervision of Marcus Varenus Secundus, his freedman and heir.
EDCS-30100427.summary: This inscription records the posthumous burial arrangement by Marcus Varenus Valens, a veteran of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, to be carried out by his freedman and heir.

EDCS-30100429.text_edition: \n IIviro designato Calpurnia Crispina marito pientissimo\n
EDCS-30100429.translation: To Calpurnia Crispina, his most devoted wife, [set up by her husband], designated duovir.
EDCS-30100429.summary: This inscription is a dedication from a husband to his wife, Calpurnia Crispina, who was the designated duovir.

EDCS-30100430.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Valerianus haruspex vivus sibi posuit\n
EDCS-30100430.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Iulius Valerianus, the haruspex, erected this for himself while alive.
EDCS-30100430.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulius Valerianus, a haruspex, made for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-30100431.text_edition: \n Marcus Marcius Anthus IIIIIIvir ex pecunia honoraria coloniae \n
EDCS-30100431.translation: Marcus Marcius Anthus, a member of the college of six, from the honorary funds of the colony.
EDCS-30100431.summary: This inscription records Marcus Marcius Anthus, a member of the college of six, funded by the colony's honorific money.

EDCS-30100432.text_edition: \n Munatia Primigenia nomine Munatiae Publi filiae Attianillae ob honorem Publi Munati Rustici auguris\n
EDCS-30100432.translation: Munatia Primigenia on behalf of Munatia Attianilla, daughter of Publius, for the honor of Publius Munatius Rusticus, the augur.
EDCS-30100432.summary: This inscription records Munatia Primigenia honoring Munatia Attianilla, daughter of Publius, in connection with the augur Publius Munatius Rusticus.

EDCS-30100664.translation: To Lusia Rufina, daughter of Maximu(s), aged IV.
EDCS-30100664.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lusia Rufina, daughter of Maximu(s), who died at the age of four.

EDCS-30100665.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus filia matri pientissimae fecit nnorum plus minus I \n
EDCS-30100665.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: a daughter made this for her most devoted mother, having lived more or less one year.
EDCS-30100665.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a daughter for her mother, noting the mother's short lifespan, likely indicating an infant's death.

EDCS-30100681.text_edition: \n Quintus Vettius Hospes miles cohortis Campanorum domo Suessa annorum XLV stipendiorum XXII testamento iussit fieri\n
EDCS-30100681.translation: Quintus Vettius Hospes, a soldier of the cohort of Campanians from Suessa, aged 45, with 22 years of service, ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-30100681.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Vettius Hospes, a soldier of the cohort of Campanians, indicating his place of origin, age, and length of service.

EDCS-30100684.translation: To Titia Procula, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-30100684.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Titia Procula, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-30100685.text_edition: \n Aelius MAus et Aelia Victoria mater infelicissimo filio qui vixit annos II\n
EDCS-30100685.translation: Aelius Maus and Aelia Victoria (made this) for their most unhappy son, who lived for 2 years.
EDCS-30100685.summary: This is a funerary inscription by parents, Aelius Maus and Aelia Victoria, for their son who died at the age of two.

EDCS-30100696.translation: Sacred to Bindo (and) Neptuno, from Casuni.
EDCS-30100696.summary: This is a dedication of a sacred object or place to the deities Bindo and Neptuno, likely by Casuni.

EDCS-30101114.text_edition: \n Sexto Aurelio Lupiano Lupi filio principi decuriones collegae et populares et peregrini incolae civi optimo ob merita posuerunt epulo dedicata loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-30101114.translation: For Sextus Aurelius Lupianus, son of Lupus, the principal: the council members, colleagues, local citizens, and foreigners, honored citizens, erected this for an excellent citizen, for his merits, with a banquet; dedicated by decree of the council members.
EDCS-30101114.summary: This is a public monument erected by local officials and citizens to honor Sextus Aurelius Lupianus, recognizing his merits and public service.

EDCS-30101160.translation: Sextus C. Iucundus (erected this) for Iucundus.
EDCS-30101160.summary: This is a dedication, likely funerary, by Sextus C. Iucundus for someone named Iucundus.

EDCS-30101161.text_edition: \n Quintus Valerius Quinti filius Quirina domo Iconio veteranus legionis VII annorum LV stipendiorum XXVIII hic situs est Quintus Portorius et Quintus Valerius Anteros posuerunt\n
EDCS-30101161.translation: Quintus Valerius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, from Iconium, a veteran of the Legion VII, aged 55, with 28 years of service, lies here; Quintus Portorius and Quintus Valerius Anteros erected this.
EDCS-30101161.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Valerius, a veteran of Legion VII, indicating his origin, age, and service, with the erectors named.

EDCS-30101163.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Epetinus Caius Avillius Clemens Lucius Coelius Capella Publius Raecius Libo Publius Valerius Secundus iudices dati a Marco Pompeio Silvano legato Augusti pro praetore inter rem publicam Asseriatium et inter rem publicam Alveritarum in re praesenti per sententiam suam determinaverunt\n
EDCS-30101163.translation: Tiberius Claudius Epetinus, Caius Avillius Clemens, Lucius Coelius Capella, Publius Raecius Libo, Publius Valerius Secundus, judges appointed by Marcus Pompeius Silvanus, governor with the rank of legate Augusti pro praetore, have determined in the present matter by their verdict between the public affairs of Asseriatum and the public affairs of Alverta.
EDCS-30101163.summary: This inscription records a legal judgment made by a panel of judges appointed by the governor Marcus Pompeius Silvanus, resolving a dispute between two communities.

EDCS-30101164.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Plaetorio Titi filio Postumino Iulia Severina filio\n
EDCS-30101164.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Titus Plaetorius Postuminus, son of Titus. Iulia Severina [dedicated this] to her son.
EDCS-30101164.summary: A funerary dedication (dis manibus) for Titus Plaetorius Postuminus, set up by his mother Iulia Severina, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30101165.text_edition: \n Clodia Turi filia Aeta sibi et Tito Safinio Septumi filio Rufo Safiniae Septumi filiae Secundae filiis suis viva fecit\n
EDCS-30101165.translation: Aeta, daughter of Turius Clodius, made this for herself and for Titus Safinius Rufus, son of Septumius, and for her sons and daughters, Safinia Secunda, while she was alive.
