EDCS-31400181.translation: To his son, the boy Ienuarius, who lived 7 years, 2 months, and 7 days, and to his most innocent grandson Ienuarius, who lived 11 years, 3 months, and 17 days, Secundianus and Ianuaria erected this for their daughter and grandson.
EDCS-31400181.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of a son and grandson, with detailed ages and a dedication from Secundianus and Ianuaria.

EDCS-31400182.text_edition: \n Quartioni Gai Flavi Iusti servo Plaia contubernalis\n
EDCS-31400182.translation: To Plaia, fellow soldier (contubernalis) of Gaius Flavius Iustus, slave of Quartio.
EDCS-31400182.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Plaia, a slave and fellow soldier of Gaius Flavius Iustus, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400186.text_edition: \n Libero Patri D - Aurelius Licinianus libens\n
EDCS-31400186.translation: To Liber Pater, Aurelius Licinianus willingly presented this.
EDCS-31400186.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater by Aurelius Licinianus, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400187.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Aurelia Zaca libens posuit\n
EDCS-31400187.translation: Aurelia Zaca willingly set this up for Liber Pater.
EDCS-31400187.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater by Aurelia Zaca, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400188.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Aurelius Gaanus beneficiarius consularis provinciae Pannoniae inferioris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400188.translation: To the August Diana, Aurelius Gaanus, a beneficiarius consularis of the province of Lower Pannonia, willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-31400188.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana Augusta by Aurelius Gaanus, a military official in Lower Pannonia, likely dating from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31400189.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci pro salute Imperatoris Marci Antonini Gordiani Pii Felicis Augusti NMAtus vir egregius procurator eius V \n
EDCS-31400189.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, N. Matus, an outstanding man and his procurator, [dedicated this].
EDCS-31400189.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the safety of Emperor Gordian III by N. Matus, his procurator, likely dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-31400190.text_edition: \n pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimii Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi patris patriae tribuniciae potestatis \n
EDCS-31400190.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, possessing tribunician power.
EDCS-31400190.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording titles of Emperor Septimius Severus, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-31400191.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Pio Augusto divi Severi filio Arabico Adiabencio Parthico Brittannico maximo patri patriae tribunicia potestate consuli IIII decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-31400191.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus, son of the deified Severus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Britannicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, with tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-31400191.summary: This inscription records the dedication to Emperor Caracalla by the town council, likely dating to his reign (198-217 AD).

EDCS-31400192.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Vibio Traeboniano sic Gallo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto ordo decurionum coloniae metallae Domavianae\n
EDCS-31400192.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, the town council of the mining colony of Domaviana.
EDCS-31400192.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Decius by the town council of Domaviana, likely dating to his reign (249-251 AD).

EDCS-31400193.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Vibio Traeboniano sic Veldumniano Volusiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto ordo decurionum coloniae metallae Domavianae\n
EDCS-31400193.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, the town council of the mining colony of Domaviana.
EDCS-31400193.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Trebonianus Gallus by the town council of Domaviana, likely dating to his reign (251-253 AD).

EDCS-31400199.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to an Augustan figure, possibly an emperor or empress, likely dating from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-31400201.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Hilarae vixit nnos L Lucius Barbius <V=I>atori coniugi bene merenti et sibi fecit \n
EDCS-31400201.translation: To the Divine Shades of Claudia Hilara, who lived 50 years. Lucius Barbius Vitalis made this for his deserving wife and himself.
EDCS-31400201.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Hilara, set up by her husband Lucius Barbius Vitalis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400202.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Salviae Here NIM Aurelius Fl DEONI coniugi A IIMI\n
EDCS-31400202.translation: To the Divine Shades of Salvia Here[---]. Aurelius Fl[---]DEONI made this for his wife A[---]IMI[---].
EDCS-31400202.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Salvia Here, set up by Aurelius Fl[---]DEONI for his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400207.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Marci filius Maximus quondam SC \n
EDCS-31400207.translation: Marcus Ulpius Maximus, son of Marcus, formerly [---].
EDCS-31400207.summary: A fragmented inscription recording the name and filiation of Marcus Ulpius Maximus, likely a citizen's tombstone from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400215.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Iustus decurio municipii Bistuensis et Aelia Procula coniux vivi sibi posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400215.translation: To the Divine Shades of Publius Aelius Iustus, decurion of the municipality of Bistuensis, and his wife Aelia Procula. They set this up for themselves while alive.
EDCS-31400215.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Iustus and Aelia Procula, set up by themselves while alive, recording his status as a decurion in Bistuensis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400217.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulminatori Flavius Seneca vir egregius libens posuit\n
EDCS-31400217.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Thunderer. Flavius Seneca, an excellent man, set this up willingly.
EDCS-31400217.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Flavius Seneca, likely a private votive offering from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400218.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Licinius Victorinus miles legionis II Aurelio Probo patri defuncto nnrum LV et Proculae matri defunctae piae bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-31400218.translation: To the Divine Shades of Licinius Victorinus, a soldier of the Legio II, for his deceased father Aurelius Probus, who lived 55 years, and for his deceased, pious, and deserving mother Procula. He set this up.
EDCS-31400218.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Probus and Procula, set up by their son Licinius Victorinus, a soldier of Legio II, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400219.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Titi filio Lucio decurioni municipii Bistuae et Aureliae Proculae Flavia Procilla viva fecit et sibi et suis\n
EDCS-31400219.translation: To the Divine Shades of Titus Flavius Lucius, son of Titus, decurion of the municipality of Bistua, and Aurelia Procula. Flavia Procilla made this for herself while alive, and for her family.
EDCS-31400219.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Lucius and Aurelia Procula, set up by Flavia Procilla for herself and her family, recording his status as a decurion in Bistua, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400221.text_edition: \n sacerdoti urbis Romae ssimo qui vixit coniugi et sibi viva fecit\n
EDCS-31400221.translation: [Dedicated to] the most excellent priest of the city of Rome, who lived [---]. Made for his wife and himself while alive.
EDCS-31400221.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording a priest of Rome and his wife, set up by himself while alive, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400222.text_edition: \n Aurelius Crescen<t>inus et Iuliana parentes F<e=I>liciano filio pien<t>issimo defuncto annorum XXX bene merenti et sibi vivis pro pietate e<t=I> posteris \n
EDCS-31400222.translation: Aurelius Crescentinus and Iuliana, parents, for their most devoted son Feliciano, deceased at the age of 30 years, well-deserving, and for themselves while alive, for piety and for posterity.
EDCS-31400222.summary: A funerary epitaph for Feliciano, set up by his parents Aurelius Crescentinus and Iuliana for him and themselves, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400224.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Proculae vixit annos III Aurelii Nepos et Procilla filiae posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400224.translation: To the Divine Shades of Aurelia Procula, who lived 3 years. Aurelius Nepos and Procilla set this up for their daughter.
EDCS-31400224.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Procula, set up by her parents Aurelius Nepos and Procilla, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400226.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Plator Batonis ann(orum) I?V?V et P \n
EDCS-31400226.translation: To the Divine Shades. Plator, son of Batonis, aged 15-19.
EDCS-31400226.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Plator, son of Batonis, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Posaulionis Iaci filiae piissimae defunctae annorum XX parentes\n
EDCS-31400227.translation: To the Divine Shades. Parents to Posaulionis, daughter of Iacius, most dutiful, who died at the age of 20.
EDCS-31400227.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their devoted daughter Posaulionis, who died at 20 years old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400228.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo - Publius Aelius Severus libens posuit\n
EDCS-31400228.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Publius Aelius Severus willingly erected this.
EDCS-31400228.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Severus, likely set up in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400231.text_edition: \n vixit nnos XIImius Bato etva parentes eius infelicissimi fecerunt\n
EDCS-31400231.translation: Lived 12 years. Bato and his parents, most unhappy, made this.
EDCS-31400231.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Bato who lived 12 years, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400233.text_edition: \n aria coniu[gi ------ ICA fecit A NISS A ISA L N T ON I I N B D\n
EDCS-31400233.translation: [---] to his wife Aria [---]. Ica made this.
EDCS-31400233.summary: A fragmented funerary monument recording the erection by Ica for his wife Aria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400235.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Terentio qui vixit annos LX et Vendoni coniugi aeius sic vivae Bessus parentibus pro pietate et sibi vivus posuit\n
EDCS-31400235.translation: To the Divine Shades. Bessus, out of duty and for himself while alive, erected this for Terentius who lived 60 years, and for Vendonia his wife, while she was alive.
EDCS-31400235.summary: A funerary monument erected by Bessus for his parents Terentius and Vendonia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400237.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Lucius P S posuit\n
EDCS-31400237.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Lucius P S erected this.
EDCS-31400237.summary: A dedication to Silvanus Augustus by Lucius P S, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400239.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attio Victori filio pientissimo vixsit sic annos XXXI\n
EDCS-31400239.translation: To the Divine Shades. Attius Victor, most dutiful son, lived 31 years.
EDCS-31400239.summary: A funerary epitaph for Attius Victor, a dutiful son who lived 31 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400245.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Maximinus.
EDCS-31400245.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Maximinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400246.text_edition: \n Aeliae Publi filiae Procillae sanctissimae feminae Caius Safinius Severus maritus ex volunta<t>e ipsius Procillae\n
EDCS-31400246.translation: To Aelia Procilla, daughter of Publius, most holy woman. Caius Safinius Severus, her husband, [erected this] from the wish of Procilla herself.
EDCS-31400246.summary: A funerary monument set up by Caius Safinius Severus for his wife Aelia Procilla, according to her own wishes, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400247.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tattaris Veneti filius et Temus Annaei filia Batoni filio annorum XX et Annaiae sic filiae annorum XXV filiis pientissimis vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-31400247.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tattaris son of Venetus and his wife Annaia daughter of Temus, made this for their son Batton, aged 20, and for their daughter Annaia, aged 25, their most dutiful children, while alive, for themselves and their family.
EDCS-31400247.summary: This funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, records the parents Tattaris and Annaia dedicating a tomb for their children Batton and Annaia.

EDCS-31400248.text_edition: \n Valerius Luci libertus Castor annorum XX Marcus Valerius Marci libertus Priscus annorum XII\n
EDCS-31400248.translation: Valerius Castor, freedman of Lucius, aged 20; Marcus Valerius Priscus, freedman of Marcus, aged 12.
EDCS-31400248.summary: This inscription, likely a tombstone from the Imperial period, records the names and ages of two individuals, possibly related or sharing a burial space.

EDCS-31400249.text_edition: \n O ICA beneficiarius consularis provinciae Pannoniae superioris posuit VII Idus Iunias Scapula Prisco et Tineio Clemente consulibus\n
EDCS-31400249.translation: O ICA, a beneficiary of the consular governor of the province of Pannonia Superior, erected this on the 7th day before the Ides of June, when Scapula Priscus and Tineius Clemens were consuls.
EDCS-31400249.summary: This dedication from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD records the erection of a monument by O ICA, a beneficiary official in Pannonia Superior, during the consulship of Priscus and Clemens.

EDCS-31400255.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae ac Geniis sacrorum Augustorum Rus Pinnes miles cohortis primae Belgarum immunis libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-31400255.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras and to the Genii of the sacred rites of the Augusti, Rus Pinnes, an immunis (tax-exempt soldier) of the first cohort of the Belgae, gladly set this up as a deserved offering.
EDCS-31400255.summary: This dedication from the Imperial period records a soldier, Rus Pinnes, making a voluntary offering to the god Mithras and the Genii of the emperors' sacred rites.

EDCS-31400256.translation: To... Vict]orius, the most dutiful son, who deserved it.
EDCS-31400256.summary: This fragmentary funerary epitaph from the Imperial period records a dedication to a son named Victorious.

EDCS-31400257.translation: To Plator son of Iulius Teutmeitis.
EDCS-31400257.summary: This simple funerary inscription from the Imperial period records the name of the deceased, Plator, son of Iulius Teutmeitis.

EDCS-31400258.text_edition: \n Caius Aponius Verus Silvano et Genio Epetinorun sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400258.translation: Gaius Aponius Verus paid his vow to Silvanus and the Genius of the people of Epeti, gladly and as deserved.
EDCS-31400258.summary: This votive inscription from the Imperial period records Gaius Aponius Verus fulfilling a vow to the gods Silvanus and the Genius of Epeti.

EDCS-31400259.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Vero decurioni municipii Rideri militi legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis militavit nnos XXV Aurelii Varro et Candida parentes\n
EDCS-31400259.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Verus, a decurion of the municipium of Rideri, a soldier of the 11th Claudian legion, pious and faithful, served for 25 years. His parents Aurelii Varro and Candida.
EDCS-31400259.summary: This funerary epitaph from the Imperial period records Aurelius Verus, a decurion and legionary, and is dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-31400260.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemiliae Dracont defunctae annorum XX illae Vibia Lione delicio bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400260.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aemilia Dracont, deceased at the age of 20; to Vibia Lione, beloved pet, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400260.summary: This funerary epitaph from the Imperial period is dedicated to a deceased woman, Aemilia Dracont, and her beloved pet, Vibia Lione.

EDCS-31400261.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Mercurialis delicato suo in infelicissimo annorum V bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400261.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Julius Mercurialis, your beloved pet, aged 5, who deserved well. Placed by [---].
EDCS-31400261.summary: This funerary epitaph from the Imperial period is dedicated to a beloved pet named Julius Mercurialis, aged five.

EDCS-31400262.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caetenniae Callirhoe Iulia Secundina matri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400262.translation: To the Divine Shades: Iulia Secundina set this up for her well-deserving mother, Caetennia Callirhoe.
EDCS-31400262.summary: This funerary epitaph records a daughter's dedication to her mother, Caetennia Callirhoe.

EDCS-31400264.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaudentiae Victorina<e=F> c<o=A>niu<g=C>i inc<o=A>mpara<b=R>i<l=I>i <q=A>ua<e=F> vixit annis XXX <Cr=VDT>escens maritus bene merenti \n
EDCS-31400264.translation: To the Divine Shades: for Gaudentia Victorina, his incomparable wife, who lived 30 years, her husband, well-deserving, set this up.
EDCS-31400264.summary: This funerary epitaph records a husband's dedication to his wife, Gaudentia Victorina, who died at age 30.

EDCS-31400265.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Supera filia matri pientissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400265.translation: To the Divine Shades: Iulia Supera set this up for her most dutiful daughter, a most deserving mother.
EDCS-31400265.summary: This funerary epitaph records a daughter's dedication to her mother, Iulia Supera.

EDCS-31400266.translation: To the Divine Shades: for Lollia Dracontilla.
EDCS-31400266.summary: This brief funerary epitaph records the deceased Lollia Dracontilla.

EDCS-31400267.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lolliae Saecundinae Lollia Lupa filia matri piissimae\n
EDCS-31400267.translation: To the Divine Shades: for Lollia Saecundina. Lollia Lupa, her daughter, for her most pious mother.
EDCS-31400267.summary: This funerary epitaph records a daughter's dedication to her mother, Lollia Saecundina.

EDCS-31400268.translation: To Lupus, perhaps of the Anicii family; perhaps of the Maecii family.
EDCS-31400268.summary: This fragmentary inscription may record the names of two individuals, Lupus and possibly their family affiliations.

EDCS-31400269.translation: To the Divine Shades: For Lutatius Firmus. Lutatia.
EDCS-31400269.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Lutatia, possibly to Lutatius Firmus.

EDCS-31400270.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Severae Laelia Ae<g=C>iale annae bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-31400270.translation: To the Divine Shades: Laelia Aigiale set this up for her well-deserving mother, Severa.
EDCS-31400270.summary: This funerary epitaph records a daughter's dedication to her mother, Severa.

EDCS-31400282.translation: For his family and himself, while alive.
EDCS-31400282.summary: This fragmentary funerary epitaph records a dedication by an individual for himself and his family during his lifetime.

EDCS-31400286.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Publius Aurelius M C votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400286.translation: Sacred to the August Goddess Diana. Publius Aurelius M C fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31400286.summary: This inscription records Publius Aurelius M C fulfilling a vow to the goddess Diana Augusta.

EDCS-31400289.text_edition: \n Silvano Viatori sacrum Quintus Anix votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400289.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Viator. Quintus Anix gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31400289.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus Viator, dedicated by Quintus Anix.

EDCS-31400290.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus Viator, begun by Iunepotianus, with an incomplete dedication.

EDCS-31400291.text_edition: \n Silvano Viatori sacrum Panes Pil votum solvit\n
EDCS-31400291.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Viator. Panes Pil fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31400291.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus Viator, dedicated by Panes Pil.

EDCS-31400292.translation: Panes Stul dedicated this to Silvanus.
EDCS-31400292.summary: A dedication to Silvanus, set up by Panes Stul.

EDCS-31400293.text_edition: \n Silvano Viatori sancto sacrum Augusto Quintus S Vo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400293.translation: Sacred to the holy Silvanus Viator. Quintus S. Vo gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Augustus.
EDCS-31400293.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus Viator and possibly Augustus, dedicated by Quintus S. Vo.

EDCS-31400299.text_edition: \n Aurelio Tullio Timammo marito obsequentissimo in[nocentissimo ------\n
EDCS-31400299.translation: To Aurelius Tullius Timamnus, my most obedient and most innocent husband [---]
EDCS-31400299.summary: A dedication to a husband named Aurelius Tullius Timamnus.

EDCS-31400301.text_edition: \n fecit sibi et Sextoni coniugi carissimae quae vixit annos LV bene merenti et suis\n
EDCS-31400301.translation: He made this for himself and for his most cherished wife Sextonai, who lived 55 years, well-deserving, and for his own [family].
EDCS-31400301.summary: A funerary inscription for the deceased wife Sextonai and the dedicator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400302.translation: To MES M MANTVR, while alive, to his wife
EDCS-31400302.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription mentioning MES M MANTVR and his wife.

EDCS-31400303.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for a wife named Messia.

EDCS-31400304.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription dedicated to a wife by Marcellius.

EDCS-31400305.translation: To the great VAV Maxima, his wife, and for himself.
EDCS-31400305.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a woman named Maxima and the dedicator, likely for a tomb or shrine.

EDCS-31400307.text_edition: \n ISI i U<r=P>si OBvero et filio et sibi posuit \n
EDCS-31400307.translation: Ursi erected this for himself, his son, and his son.
EDCS-31400307.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Ursi for himself and his two sons, likely for a tomb.

EDCS-31400308.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the burial of Valerius Ursus.

EDCS-31400318.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Cassianus libens votum solvit\n
EDCS-31400318.translation: Publius Aelius Cassianus gladly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-31400318.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Publius Aelius Cassianus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely on a shrine or altar.

EDCS-31400319.text_edition: \n FFllavis Apollinari et Honorio filiis carissimis et Frontino fratri et Maximae matri \n
EDCS-31400319.translation: To Flavius Apollinaris and Honorius, dearest sons, and to Frontinus, [their] brother, and to Maxima, [their] mother.
EDCS-31400319.summary: This inscription is a dedication to family members, likely for a tomb or memorial.

EDCS-31400320.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publiae Aeliae Tertullae filiae defunctae annorum III Aelius I et Cassia Maxima fecerunt\n
EDCS-31400320.translation: To the departed spirits of Publia Aelia Tertulla, [his] deceased daughter, aged 3 years. Aelius I and Cassia Maxima made this.
EDCS-31400320.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Publia Aelia Tertulla, made by her parents.

EDCS-31400322.text_edition: \n Militia insigni raptus trieteride sexta sedibus his situs est miserabilis Heliodorus Heliodorus et CIItianae parentes miseri fecerunt\n
EDCS-31400322.translation: Torn away in his sixth trieteric cycle, Heliodorus lies in these seats, alas. Heliodorus and Citiana, miserable parents, made this.
EDCS-31400322.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Heliodorus, who died young, made by his grieving parents.

EDCS-31400324.text_edition: \n Venndo Laezarpa Plaenis filius <c=Q>um infante suo hic situs \n
EDCS-31400324.translation: Venndo Laezarpa, son of Plaenis, lies here with his infant.
EDCS-31400324.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Venndo Laezarpa and his infant child.

EDCS-31400326.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci filius Voltinia Verus domo Luco Augusti miles legionis <X=V>I Claudiae piae <f=B>(idelis) nnorum I stipendiorum XVII hic situs est <t=I>(estamento) fieri <i=P>(ussit) Publius Annius Aquila faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-31400326.translation: Lucius Valerius Verus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinia tribe, from Luco Augusti, a soldier of the 16th Claudian Legion, dutiful and faithful, aged 21 years, with 17 years of service, lies here. He ordered this to be made by his will; Publius Annius Aquila took care of its construction.
EDCS-31400326.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription for a soldier, Lucius Valerius Verus, who specified its construction in his will.

EDCS-31400329.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valiae Maximae Veratius Maximus coniugi pientissimae <e>t Valius Maximus frater sorori carissimae quae vixit annos XX bene merenti I posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400329.translation: To the departed spirits of Valia Maxima, his most devoted wife. And Valius Maximus, her brother, to his dearest sister, who lived 20 years, deserving well. They placed this.
EDCS-31400329.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Valius Maximus to his sister Valia Maxima and her husband.

EDCS-31400331.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valenti Vatinio pientissimo puero annorum XVI dierum XVII\n
EDCS-31400331.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Valentinus Vatinus, a most devoted boy, aged 16 years and 17 days.
EDCS-31400331.summary: A funerary dedication to a young boy named Valentinus Vatinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400334.text_edition: \n nis OCIO infe<l=I>icissim Quin<t=I>io frater <ben=THE>e merit \n
EDCS-31400334.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. His brother [erected this] for the most devoted Ocius, deserving [of this].
EDCS-31400334.summary: A funerary dedication by a brother for the deceased Ocius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400336.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Divi filius Augustus parens coloniae murum turris dedit\n
EDCS-31400336.translation: The Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the Divine [Julius Caesar], father of the colony, gave the wall and a tower.
EDCS-31400336.summary: An imperial dedication recording Emperor Augustus's gift of a wall and tower to a colony, likely dating to his reign (27 BC - 14 AD).

EDCS-31400337.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus fecit Opia Lucida fecit Aeliano pientissimo coniugi? et Plotiae Festivae filiae ---II FE XXX\n
EDCS-31400337.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Opia Lucida made [this] for Aelianus, her most devoted husband, and for her daughter Plotia Festiva, 25 years old.
EDCS-31400337.summary: A funerary dedication by Opia Lucida for her husband Aelianus and daughter Plotia Festiva, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400338.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Callis[t]a, wife, and [her] daughter...
EDCS-31400338.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication for a woman named Callista and her daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400346.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Andes POPI
EDCS-31400346.summary: A very fragmentary funerary dedication, possibly naming Andes and a gentilicium like Popilius or a cognomen, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400347.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400348.translation: To Julia, wife of Iulius Niger.
EDCS-31400348.summary: A funerary dedication for Julia, wife of Iulius Niger, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400349.translation: Slator, son of Oeplus, aged 10 years.
EDCS-31400349.summary: A funerary epitaph for a boy named Slator, son of Oeplus, aged 10, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400350.text_edition: \n Vandano Volturia Triti filia annorum L\n
EDCS-31400350.translation: Vandanus, son of Volturia Tritus, aged 50 years.
EDCS-31400350.summary: A funerary epitaph for Vandanus, son of Volturia Tritus, aged 50, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400351.text_edition: \n Vendes Dennaia Andentis filia annorum XXX\n
EDCS-31400351.translation: Vendes Dennaia, daughter of Andentis, aged 30.
EDCS-31400351.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vendes Dennaia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400352.text_edition: \n us Denannorum XC hic est situs\n
EDCS-31400352.translation: Of the Denannorum, aged 90, is buried here.
EDCS-31400352.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of an individual from the Denannorum, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400357.translation: Proculus, damaged by age and decay.
EDCS-31400357.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to record the name Proculus and his state of decay, possibly on a tombstone or monument, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400358.text_edition: \n Caius Reius Alcimo sibi et Publiciae Faustinae uxori pientissimae fecit\n
EDCS-31400358.translation: Gaius Reius made this for himself and for his most devoted wife, Publicia Faustina.
EDCS-31400358.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Reius and his wife Publicia Faustina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400359.text_edition: \n Valentina mater et Crescentylia sic amita Preponti filio\n
EDCS-31400359.translation: Valentina, mother, and Crescentylia, aunt, [dedicate this] to Prepontius, son of Preponti.
EDCS-31400359.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a mother and aunt to a young boy, Prepontius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400361.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Fausta PA EIOPAMIA POSI EOONMFAM AECINPNSOOITA quae VISENAS\n
EDCS-31400361.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelia Fausta [---] who [---]
EDCS-31400361.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to Aelia Fausta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400362.text_edition: \n Proclo deefun<c>to annorum IIII C Pr<o>cla infelicissimo III \n
EDCS-31400362.translation: To Proclus, deceased at 4 years, and to C. Procla, most unhappy [at ---] 3 years.
EDCS-31400362.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two children, Proclus and C. Procla, highlighting their young ages at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400363.translation: Saufeia Ide, mother, [dedicates this to] Publius Saufeius.
EDCS-31400363.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a mother, Saufeia Ide, to her son, Publius Saufeius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400365.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Of Quintus Iulius.
EDCS-31400365.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription, likely a tombstone fragment, mentioning Quintus Iulius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400366.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publiae Quinti filiae Secundae Lucius Lurius Ursus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400366.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Lurius Ursus to his well-deserving wife, Publia Quinti filia Secunda.
EDCS-31400366.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a husband, Lucius Lurius Ursus, to his wife, Publia Quinti filia Secunda, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400367.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a milestone, indicating a distance of eight units.

EDCS-31400370.text_edition: \n Seneca sibi et suis Petilliae Primae uxori Quinto Petillio Marci filio Rufo Nunnuleiae Marci filiae Secundae Nunnuleio Marci filio Capitoni Nunnuleio Marci filio Lucano Nunnuleio Marci filio Senecae Sexto Nunnuleio Marci filio Pulchro\n
EDCS-31400370.translation: Seneca, for himself and his family, and for Petillia Prima, his wife, Quintus Petillius Rufus, son of Marcus, Nunnuleia Secunda, daughter of Marcus, Quintus Petillius, son of Marcus, Lucius Petillius, son of Marcus, and Sextus Petillius Pulcher, son of Marcus.
EDCS-31400370.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Seneca to himself, his family, and other named individuals, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-31400371.translation: To Galerius Valerius Maximinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-31400371.summary: This is a dedication to Maximinus Daia, who held the title of Caesar, likely on a public monument during his reign.

EDCS-31400373.text_edition: \n DDominis nnostris Impperatoribus Flavio Valerio Constantio Pio Felici Augusto et Flavio Valerio Severo nobilissimo Caesari Domino nostro MN N baeatissimo sic Caesari\n
EDCS-31400373.translation: To our Lords Emperors Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, and Flavius Valerius Severus, most noble Caesar, our Lord, and to the most blessed Caesar.
EDCS-31400373.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Constantine I and Severus, likely erected in a public space during their joint rule.

EDCS-31400375.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Trebonianus Gallus' son, Volusianus, who was also Augustus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-31400377.text_edition: \n Imperatori Quinto Messio Deccio sic Traiano\n
EDCS-31400377.translation: To Emperor Quintus Messius Decius Traianus.
EDCS-31400377.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Decius, likely on a public monument during his reign.

EDCS-31400378.translation: To Emperor Quintus Messius Decius Traianus.
EDCS-31400378.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Decius, likely on a public monument during his reign.

EDCS-31400380.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caessari sic Herennio Aetrusco sic Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-31400380.translation: To Emperor Caesar Herennius Etruscus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-31400380.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Herennius Etruscus, likely on a public monument during his reign.

EDCS-31400389.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul pater patriae a Salonis milia passuum XXXIIII XXXIIII\n
EDCS-31400389.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, 34 miles from Salona.
EDCS-31400389.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, records the titles and honors of Emperor Gordian III and his distance from the city of Salona.

EDCS-31400390.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus et Caius Herennius Etruscus Quintus Decius et Caius Val<e>ns Hostilianus Messius Quintus nobi<l>issimi Caessares\n
EDCS-31400390.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, and to Gaius Herennius Etruscus Quintus Decius, and to Gaius Valens Hostilianus Messius Quintus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-31400390.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Decius, Herennius Etruscus, and Hostilianus, likely erected in a public space during their reign.

EDCS-31400391.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Claudius Pius Felix Augustus XXXV\n
EDCS-31400391.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, for the 35th time.
EDCS-31400391.summary: This inscription records a titulature of Emperor Claudius, likely honoring him on an official monument dated to his 35th acclamation as imperator.

EDCS-31400392.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Pio <F=E>(elici) Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo \n
EDCS-31400392.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius, Pious, Felicis, Unconquered Augustus, chief priest.
EDCS-31400392.summary: This inscription records a titulature for Emperor Claudius II Gothicus, likely on a monument dating to his reign.

EDCS-31400395.summary: This inscription records the name of Flavius Valerius Constans, possibly as a dedication or on a tombstone from the late Roman period.

EDCS-31400398.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XIIII consul IV pater patriae censor XXXVI\n
EDCS-31400398.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor.
EDCS-31400398.summary: This inscription records the full imperial titulature of Emperor Claudius, highlighting his consulships and censorate, likely on an official monument.

EDCS-31400399.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XIIII consul IIII pater patriae censor XXXXIII\n
EDCS-31400399.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor.
EDCS-31400399.summary: This inscription records the full imperial titulature of Emperor Claudius, likely on an official monument, with a specific count for his censorate.

EDCS-31400400.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XIIII consul IIII pater patriae censor XXXXVI\n
EDCS-31400400.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor.
EDCS-31400400.summary: This inscription records the full imperial titulature of Emperor Claudius, likely on an official monument, with a specific count for his censorate.

EDCS-31400404.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XIIII consul IIII pater patriae censor LXIX\n
EDCS-31400404.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor.
EDCS-31400404.summary: This inscription records the full imperial titulature of Emperor Claudius, likely on an official monument, with a specific count for his censorate.

EDCS-31400417.translation: From the pasture of Sextus Mutellus Maximus.
EDCS-31400417.summary: This inscription marks the origin or ownership of a pasture belonging to Sextus Mutellus Maximus.

EDCS-31400418.translation: From the pasture of Sextus Mutellus Maximus.
EDCS-31400418.summary: This inscription marks the origin or ownership of a pasture belonging to Sextus Mutellus Maximus.

EDCS-31400580.text_edition: \n Marcus Atil<i=L>us Rufus Palpelliae Maximae uxori vivus fecit \n
EDCS-31400580.translation: Marcus Atilius Rufus made this while alive for his wife, Palpellia Maxima.
EDCS-31400580.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument erected by Marcus Atilius Rufus for his wife, Palpellia Maxima, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400581.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Opli filius Roesia Vesclevesis filia Septuma sic vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-31400581.translation: Marcus Aurelius, son of Oplius, and Roesia Vesclevesis, daughter of Septuma, made this tomb for themselves and their families.
EDCS-31400581.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription recording the names of the deceased and their familial relationships, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400582.text_edition: \n Lucio Licinio Veloci C Publi filiae Tertullae\n
EDCS-31400582.translation: To Lucius Licinius Velox, by C. Publius for his daughter Tertulla.
EDCS-31400582.summary: This is an epitaph for Tertulla, set up by her father C. Publius, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400583.text_edition: \n Caius Ortorius Praeconinus munus dedicat et Saturinae\n
EDCS-31400583.translation: Caius Ortorius Praeconinus dedicates the public games and Saturina.
EDCS-31400583.summary: This inscription records the dedication of public games by Caius Ortorius Praeconinus, possibly also honouring Saturina, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400584.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Valgi Proculi Rupia Marceillina uxor marito carissimo viva posuit\n
EDCS-31400584.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Rupia Marceillina placed this for her dearest husband, Caius Valgius Proculus, while she was alive.
EDCS-31400584.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400589.text_edition: \n \xce\x94\xce\xb5 \xce\xb4onis \xce\x94ei \xce\xb5t sancti Paulis sic Marius tribunos sic con sic coniogen sic suam VV etetiis suis votom sic suom sic solvet\n
EDCS-31400589.translation: To the gods. Paulis, devout and holy, discharges his vow to the tribunes, his wife, and his family.
EDCS-31400589.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Paulis to deities and officials, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400590.text_edition: \n Atilius Pudens Atiliae Sexti filiae Quartae coniugi annorum XXV a Sexti filia Clesta Atilio Maximo filio annorum XI vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-31400590.translation: Atilia Quartus, daughter of Sextus, wife of Atilius Pudens, aged 25, and her son Atilius Maximus, aged 11, made this while alive.
EDCS-31400590.summary: This funerary inscription records the names and ages of a wife and son who jointly dedicated the monument, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400594.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Emperor Augustus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400595.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caio Iulio Miaximino Pio Felici et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimi sic Caesari Auggustis\n
EDCS-31400595.translation: To the Emperor Gaius Julius Maximius Pius Felix and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesars, Augusts.
EDCS-31400595.summary: This inscription honors two Roman emperors, likely dating to the reign of the elder, Gaius Julius Maximius Pius Felix, in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31400596.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-31400596.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-31400596.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely dating from his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-31400599.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul II proconsul pater patriae et Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar consul curante Claudio Herenniano viro clarissimo legato Auggustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-31400599.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, consul; supervised by Claudius Herennianus, most distinguished man, legate of the Augusts, proconsul.
EDCS-31400599.summary: This official inscription records the titles and powers of Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, with the supervision of a legate, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-31400601.text_edition: \n Imperator Nerva Traianus divi Nervae filius Caesar \n
EDCS-31400601.translation: Emperor Nerva Traianus, son of the deified Nerva, Caesar.
EDCS-31400601.summary: This inscription records the name and titles of Emperor Trajan, son of Emperor Nerva, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-31400604.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Drusi filius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XV co(n)sul IIII pater patriae censor\n
EDCS-31400604.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 15th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor.
EDCS-31400604.summary: This inscription lists the full titulature of Emperor Claudius, highlighting his numerous offices and honors, likely on a public monument from his reign (c. 41-54 AD).

EDCS-31400619.text_edition: \n Marco Opellio Antonino Diadumeniano nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis Caesaris Marci Opellii Severi Macrini Pii Felicis Augusti filio pecunia publica decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-31400619.translation: To Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, son of Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, at public expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-31400619.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the young Caesar Diadumenianus, son of Emperor Macrinus, funded by public money and decreed by local councillors, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31400620.text_edition: \n Aelius Bessus et Aelia Ursa filiae pientissimae et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400620.translation: Aelius Bessus and Aelia Ursa set this up for their most dutiful daughter and for themselves while alive.
EDCS-31400620.summary: This inscription records parents setting up a monument for their deceased daughter and for themselves, functioning as a family tomb marker.

EDCS-31400621.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Valeriae Te quae vixit Aelius Qumatri \n
EDCS-31400621.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Valeria Te who lived... Aelius Qumatri.
EDCS-31400621.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the spirits of the underworld, likely for a woman named Valeria, set up by Aelius Qumatri, functioning as a tomb epitaph.

EDCS-31400624.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld.
EDCS-31400624.summary: This inscription is a standard dedication to the spirits of the underworld, indicating a funerary context.

EDCS-31400626.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Totia Proculi filia annorum XVIII Proculus et Marcella filiae pro pietate\n
EDCS-31400626.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Totia, daughter of Proculus, aged 18. Proculus and Marcella for their daughter, out of devotion.
EDCS-31400626.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Totia, set up by her parents Proculus and Marcella, mentioning her age and their devotion.

EDCS-31400628.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Ursinulae Aurelius Rufus sibi et suae coniugi vivus testamento faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-31400628.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Rufus took care to have this made while alive and by will for Ursinula, and for himself and his wife.
EDCS-31400628.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Rufus for his wife Ursinula and for himself and his wife, arranged by will, indicating a tomb.

EDCS-31400631.text_edition: \n Lucio Domitio I Eroti viro ex equestribus turmis egregio procuratori metallorum Pannoniorum et Delmatorum mirae integritatis et bonitatis Marcus Aurelius Rusticus vir egregius ducenarius amico praestantissimo\n
EDCS-31400631.translation: To Lucius Domitius Eros, a man of outstanding merit from the equestrian ranks, procurator of the Pannonian and Dalmatian mines, of admirable integrity and goodness, Marcus Aurelius Rusticus, an outstanding man, ducenarius, for his most excellent friend.
EDCS-31400631.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Marcus Aurelius Rusticus to his friend Lucius Domitius Eros, a high-ranking procurator in charge of mines, highlighting Eros's virtues.

EDCS-31400632.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Catilia Tryphena vixit nnos L Marcus Catilius Maximus filius et Iulius Atticus maritus bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400632.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Catilia Tryphena lived 50 years. Marcus Catilius Maximus, her son, and Julius Atticus, her husband, set this up for her who deserved well.
EDCS-31400632.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Catilia Tryphena, set up by her son and husband, recording her age and their affection.

EDCS-31400633.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caminiae Eutychiae coniugi vixit annos L Caminius Hermes maritus et Hermetianus filius eius posuerunt et sibi\n
EDCS-31400633.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caminius Hermes, her husband, and his son Hermetianus erected this for Caminiia Eutychia, his wife, who lived 50 years, and for themselves.
EDCS-31400633.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caminia Eutychia, set up by her husband and son, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400634.text_edition: \n Genio Liberi Patris Papinius Primigenius sacr(avit)\n
EDCS-31400634.translation: Papinius Primigenius dedicated (this) to the Genius of Liber Pater (Bacchus).
EDCS-31400634.summary: A dedication to the Genius of Liber Pater (Bacchus) by Papinius Primigenius, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400636.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cohortali eandem aram Iovi Cohortali Saturninus consecravit\n
EDCS-31400636.translation: Saturninus consecrated the same altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cohortalis and to Jupiter Cohortalis.
EDCS-31400636.summary: A dedication of an altar by Saturninus to a specific cult of Jupiter, Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cohortalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400640.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Super veteranus ex legione VIII Augusta memoriam sibi vivus et Verae coniugi Maximinae et Victorino filiis nostris filiam sic diffunctam sic vixit annos XXVIII\n
EDCS-31400640.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Super, a veteran from the Eighth Augustan Legion, for himself while alive, and for Vera, his wife, and for our sons Victorinus and Maximus, and for our daughter who died thus, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-31400640.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Super, a veteran of the Eighth Legion, and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400641.text_edition: \n Z B P io Ulpia iusa marito ico bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400641.translation: Ulpia set this up for her deserving husband.
EDCS-31400641.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed husband, set up by his wife Ulpia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400643.text_edition: \n Caius Manlius Cai libertus Honesimus nnorum L hic situs est\n
EDCS-31400643.translation: Caius Manlius Honesimus, freedman of Caius, lies here, aged 50 years.
EDCS-31400643.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Manlius Honesimus, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400647.text_edition: \n Ultima clauserunt Parcarum stamina filo Principii miserandi diem quem gloria nisi avus adque pater puerum dedere <p=R>raeclara militia patruoque suo iunxere fovendum cum primum pulchra lanugine sumeret annos spectantes magnum patriae columenque futurum heu miseri gloriari sibi laetamque senectam crudelem luctum domui Ravenna remisit hoc miseros titulo proprium signasse dolorem \n
EDCS-31400647.translation: The Fates closed the threads of life of the pitiable boy. His grandfather and father gave him to his paternal uncle to be cherished when he first began to take on beautiful down, looking to him as a great support and pillar of the homeland. Alas, wretchedly, they boasted of themselves and a happy old age, but Ravenna sent cruel grief to the house. This inscription marks their proper sorrow.
EDCS-31400647.summary: A complex funerary epigram lamenting the premature death of a young boy and the sorrow it brought his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400648.translation: To the spirits of the departed of Anicia IRCIS.
EDCS-31400648.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Anicia IRCIS, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400649.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Eudoxio et Eudoxiae Edoxius et Eudoxia posuerunt nnorum XXIII\n
EDCS-31400649.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Eudoxius and Eudoxia set this up for Eudoxius and Eudoxia, aged 23 years.
EDCS-31400649.summary: A funerary epitaph for Eudoxius and Eudoxia, set up by Eudoxius and Eudoxia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400650.text_edition: \n Lucio Aelio Aurelio Commodo Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani \n
EDCS-31400650.translation: Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus, son of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus...
EDCS-31400650.summary: The beginning of an inscription mentioning Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus, son of Emperor Hadrian, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-31400651.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caio S Lupo decurioni annorum L Claudia Maxima marito\n
EDCS-31400651.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius S. Lupus, a decurion, aged 50. Claudia Maxima [erected this] for her husband.
EDCS-31400651.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius S. Lupus, a decurion, erected by his wife Claudia Maxima, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400652.text_edition: \n D I D OI Flavius Aponianus qui tulit annos X BLLARIO uxori BNILV LVSI IVC XX marito et PRIO-BI MEF\n
EDCS-31400652.translation: Flavius Aponianus, who lived 10 years. To BLLARIO, his wife of 20 years and 11 months, and to his husband and PRIO-BI MEF.
EDCS-31400652.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavius Aponianus and his wife BLLARIO, with a partial mention of another individual, dating likely to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400653.text_edition: \n ONIIT Titus Iu<l=I>(ius) Cai <f=I>(ilius) Asiaritanus nnrum XXVIIII \n
EDCS-31400653.translation: Titus Iulius, son of Caius, from Asia, aged 29.
EDCS-31400653.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Iulius Asiaritanus, aged 29, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400654.text_edition: \n Caio Minidio Primigenio annorum XXXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-31400654.translation: For Gaius Minidius Primigenius, aged 40. He lies here.
EDCS-31400654.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Minidius Primigenius, aged 40, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400655.text_edition: \n Plaetoria Marci filia Posilla annorum III hic sita est\n
EDCS-31400655.translation: Plaetoria Posilla, daughter of Marcus, aged 3. She lies here.
EDCS-31400655.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Plaetoria Posilla, aged 3, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400661.text_edition: \n Lucio Attio Restuto equiti ex singularibus cohortis III Alpinorum stipendiorum XXI hic situs est\n
EDCS-31400661.translation: For Lucius Attius Restutus, an equestrian from the select men of the III Alpinorum cohort, with 21 years of service. He lies here.
EDCS-31400661.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Attius Restutus, an equestrian soldier of the III Alpinorum cohort, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400662.text_edition: \n Aurelia Maxima quae vixit annos XXXXII Aurelius Agathocles GVII maritus coniugi pientissimae quae concordi[ter ------\n
EDCS-31400662.translation: Aurelia Maxima, who lived 42 years. Aurelius Agathocles, her husband, for his most devoted wife, who lived harmoniously with him...
EDCS-31400662.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Maxima, erected by her husband Aurelius Agathocles, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400663.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci filius Lemonia domo Bononia miles le[gionis ------\n
EDCS-31400663.translation: Lucius Valerius, son of Lucius, from the Lemonia tribe, from Bononia, a soldier of the [X?] legion...
EDCS-31400663.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius, a soldier of a legion, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400665.text_edition: \n MAII vixerunt IIVI nnos XL Dassius Aeternalis miles cohortis VIII voluntariorum bene merentibus memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-31400665.translation: XL years lived. Dassius Aeternalis, a volunteer soldier of the VIII cohort, erected this memorial for those who deserved well.
EDCS-31400665.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Dassius Aeternalis, a volunteer soldier, for deserving individuals, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Rastoriano equo publico decurioni IIviro et quinquennali municipii Bistuatium dispunctori civitatis Naronensium quaestori municippiorum Azinatium Splonistarum Arupinorum et Aeliae Procilianae defunctae nnorum Albia Crispina coniugi incomparabili et filiae infelicissimae et sibi\n
EDCS-31400666.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Publius Aelius Rastorianus, an equestrian decurion, IIvir and quinquennial magistrate of the municipality of Bistuatium, dispunctor of the citizenship of Naronensium, quaestor of the municipalities of Azinatium, Splonista, and Arupinus, and for Aelia Prociliana, deceased, aged [...], for his incomparable wife, and his most unhappy daughter, and for himself.
EDCS-31400666.summary: This is a complex funerary monument dedicated by Publius Aelius Rastorianus to his wife, daughter, and himself, detailing his extensive civic and military career in various municipalities, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400670.text_edition: \n Lucius S Pudens Cereri votum solvit \n
EDCS-31400670.translation: Lucius S. Pudens paid his vow to Ceres.
EDCS-31400670.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Lucius S. Pudens fulfilling a vow to the goddess Ceres.

EDCS-31400671.text_edition: \n Maximinus sculpet sic Dianae Augustae sacrum Titus Aelius Messor votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-31400671.translation: Maximinus carved this sacred offering for Diana Augusta; Titus Aelius Messor willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-31400671.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Diana Augusta, with Titus Aelius Messor fulfilling a vow, and Maximinus identified as the sculptor.

EDCS-31400672.text_edition: \n Triviabus votum solvit libens Lucius S Pudens\n
EDCS-31400672.translation: Lucius S. Pudens willingly paid his vow to the Three Goddesses.
EDCS-31400672.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Lucius S. Pudens fulfilling a vow to the Triviae (Three Goddesses).

EDCS-31400673.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Augusti filio Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXVIII \n
EDCS-31400673.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 28th time...
EDCS-31400673.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning with the titles of an emperor, likely marking the start of a public dedication or building project.

EDCS-31400674.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Marcus Anneus EX um votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31400674.translation: Marcus Anneus willingly and deservedly paid his vow, sacred offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-31400674.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Marcus Anneus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-31400678.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating that Euvaristus erected the monument.

EDCS-31400679.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Nicala Avidia fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-31400680.text_edition: \n Ulpio Nepoti militi cohortis VIII voluntariorum bucinatori defuncto annorum XXX stipendiorum X Maximia Procula mater filio infelicissimo hic defunctus Est \n
EDCS-31400680.translation: To Nepos, soldier of the 8th cohort of volunteers, a horn-blower, who died at the age of 30 with 10 years of service. Maximia Procula, his mother, [erected this] for her most unfortunate son, who died here.
EDCS-31400680.summary: This tombstone records the death of Maximia Procula's son, a horn-blower in the army, detailing his age, service length, and her grief.

EDCS-31400681.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Ingenua mater et Aurelius pater urnam pientissimae filiae infelicissimae posuerunt quae vixit annos XVI\n
EDCS-31400681.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelia Ingenua, mother, and Aurelius [her husband], father, placed this urn for their most devoted, most unfortunate daughter, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-31400681.summary: This tombstone records the death of a 16-year-old girl, with her parents Aurelia Ingenua and Aurelius placing the urn and lamenting her fate.

EDCS-31400682.text_edition: \n Lupula Statili --? templum Matri Ma<gn=NG>aeaerum de suo restituit cum suis f<i=E>liis \n
EDCS-31400682.translation: Lupula Statilia restored the temple of the Great Mother of the Gods at her own expense with her children.
EDCS-31400682.summary: This inscription records Lupula Statilia and her children restoring a temple dedicated to the Great Mother of the Gods.

EDCS-31400683.summary: A funerary dedication to the gods of the underworld by Fulvia Primitiva, likely on her own tomb.

EDCS-31400686.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aemilius Hermosenax ex visu posuit\n
EDCS-31400686.translation: Aemilius Hermosenax, in accordance with a vision, dedicated this to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-31400686.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aemilius Hermosenax, prompted by a vision, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400688.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintiano filio annorum II dierum XXII Primula mater posuit\n
EDCS-31400688.translation: To Quintianus, aged 2 years and 22 days. His mother Primula dedicated this.
EDCS-31400688.summary: A funerary dedication for a young child, Quintianus, made by his mother Primula.

EDCS-31400689.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabinae vixit plus minus nnos XXX Athenio conserva<e> fecit\n
EDCS-31400689.translation: To Sabina, who lived approximately 30 years. Athenio her companion made this.
EDCS-31400689.summary: A funerary dedication for Sabina, who lived about 30 years, by her companion Athenio.

EDCS-31400690.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saufeiae Mamaeae nnorum <L>V Manius Saufeius Valentinus matri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400690.translation: To Saufeia Mameaea, aged 55 years. Manius Saufeius Valentinus dedicated this to his well-deserving mother.
EDCS-31400690.summary: A funerary dedication by Manius Saufeius Valentinus to his mother Saufeia Mameaea.

EDCS-31400691.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Julia Secundina, my wife.
EDCS-31400691.summary: A funerary dedication to the gods of the underworld for Julia Secundina, identified as the wife of the dedicator.

EDCS-31400692.text_edition: \n Lucio Stanio Secundo Lucius Stanius Marcellinus patri\n
EDCS-31400692.translation: To Lucius Stanius Secundus. Lucius Stanius Marcellinus, his father.
EDCS-31400692.summary: A funerary dedication for Lucius Stanius Secundus, made by his father Lucius Stanius Marcellinus.

EDCS-31400693.text_edition: \n Statio Silvino annorum LX Saufeius Valentinus patri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400693.translation: To Silvinus, aged 60 years. Saufeius Valentinus dedicated this to his well-deserving father.
EDCS-31400693.summary: A funerary dedication by Saufeius Valentinus to his father Silvinus.

EDCS-31400702.text_edition: \n Nymphis sacrum Quintus Trebonius Eutactus fecit\n
EDCS-31400702.translation: Sacred to the Nymphs. Quintus Trebonius Eutactus made this.
EDCS-31400702.summary: A dedication to the Nymphs by Quintus Trebonius Eutactus.

EDCS-31400703.translation: [Secundus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31400703.summary: A record of a vow fulfilled by someone named Secundus, the date and recipient are lost.

EDCS-31400704.translation: Venator gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31400704.summary: A dedication by Venator, likely a private votive offering, date unknown.

EDCS-31400706.summary: A fragment of an inscription mentioning the emperors (Augusti), likely part of a public monument, date uncertain.

EDCS-31400708.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atreccesianus miles legionis XIIII geminae stipendiorum XXVI posuit Aurelia Festa coniux bene merenti posuit <q=o>(ui) vixit annos XXXXVIII et At<r=K>ectia viva ma<m=N>mu<l=I>a filio pient[issimo ------ \n
EDCS-31400708.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Atreccesianus, a soldier of the 14th Gemina legion with 26 years of service, erected [this]. Aurelia Festa, his wife, erected [this] for him deserving of all good things, who lived 48 years, and for Atrectia, his living mother, most devoted.
EDCS-31400708.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aurelia Festa for her husband Atreccesianus, a soldier, and his mother Atrectia, dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE based on the legion.

EDCS-31400709.text_edition: \n Aurelio Dizae equiti ex singularibus stipendiorum XVI Titia Valentina coniugi carissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400709.translation: Titia Valentina erected [this] for her most cherished husband Aurelius Dizaeus, a cavalryman, one of the select troops with 16 years of service, deserving of all good things.
EDCS-31400709.summary: A funerary monument erected by Titia Valentina for her husband Aurelius Dizaeus, a cavalryman, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400711.text_edition: \n IIAE militi legionis I Italicae Severianae stipendiorum XXIII vixit annos Aurelius Marcianus miles legionis supra scriptae contubernalis bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400711.translation: to IIAE, a soldier of the I Italica Severiana legion with 23 years of service, who lived... Aurelius Marcianus, a soldier of the aforementioned legion, his tent-mate, erected [this] for him deserving of all good things.
EDCS-31400711.summary: A funerary monument for a soldier of the I Italica legion, erected by his contubernalis (tent-mate) Aurelius Marcianus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31400713.text_edition: \n ius Licinianus i centuria Serv dulcissim XIIII et \n
EDCS-31400713.translation: ius Licinianus, centurion of the troop of Serv[...], most beloved, 14 years...
EDCS-31400713.summary: A fragmented inscription possibly recording a military officer, Servius Licinianus, with partial service dates, date uncertain.

EDCS-31400714.text_edition: \n Iunius Rufus domo Heraclea veteranus legionis curam egit veteranus le[gionis ------\n
EDCS-31400714.translation: Iunius Rufus from Heraclea, a veteran of the legion, took charge of the care of the veteran of the legion...
EDCS-31400714.summary: An inscription concerning Iunius Rufus, a veteran, potentially detailing his responsibilities or a memorial he established, date uncertain.

EDCS-31400715.text_edition: \n ------ Si]lvanus veteranus cohortis VIII voluntariorum vivus fecit sibi et fratri defuncto cae coniugi meae vivae\n
EDCS-31400715.translation: [...] Silvanus, a veteran of the 8th cohort of volunteers, made [this] while alive for himself and for his deceased brother and for my living wife.
EDCS-31400715.summary: A funerary monument erected by the veteran Silvanus for himself, his deceased brother, and his living wife, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-31400721.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. ING, a veteran.
EDCS-31400721.summary: A very fragmentary funerary inscription for a veteran named ING, date unknown.

EDCS-31400726.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Desidieni Proculi decurionis coloniae Salonitanorum defuncti annorumIV mensium IIII dierum IIII Desidiena Primitiva nutrix infelicissima\n
EDCS-31400726.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Caius Desidienus Proculus, a decurion of the colony of Salona, deceased at the age of 4 years, 4 months, and 4 days. Desidiena Primitiva, his most unhappy nurse.
EDCS-31400726.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child, Caius Desidienus Proculus, set up by his nurse, likely from the Roman period given the style and mention of a colony.

EDCS-31400727.text_edition: \n Qunito? Aeronio Tertio VIviro Augustali Quintus Aeronius Helius patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400727.translation: To Quintus Aeronius Helius, Augustal official, for his father Quintus Aeronius Tertius, who served as a duovir, a father who deserved well.
EDCS-31400727.summary: This is a dedication by a son to his deceased father, likely an honorary monument, from the Augustal collegium context.

EDCS-31400728.text_edition: \n Lucio Statio Publi filio Ma aedili IIviro flamini divi Ve[spasiani ------\n
EDCS-31400728.translation: To Lucius Statius, son of Publius, aedile, duovir, and priest of the deified Vespasian...
EDCS-31400728.summary: This is an incomplete funerary or honorary inscription for Lucius Statius, detailing his civic and religious offices, likely dating to the Flavian period.

EDCS-31400744.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Arrius Saecularis Arriae Iucundae matri dulcissimae\n
EDCS-31400744.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Arrius Saecularis for his mother Arria Iucunda, most beloved mother.
EDCS-31400744.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a son to his mother, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-31400745.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Arsinoe Ursulo bene merenti coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-31400745.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arsinoe placed this for Ursulus, her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-31400745.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-31400747.translation: To the gods of the underworld... Asclepiades... husband...
EDCS-31400747.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription, possibly mentioning a person named Asclepiades and his role as a husband.

EDCS-31400748.text_edition: \n Attiae Florentiae Nebia sic Bysana amicae rarissimae \n
EDCS-31400748.translation: To Attia Flavia, from Nebia, called Bysana, for her most rare friend.
EDCS-31400748.summary: This is a dedication from a woman named Nebia to her friend Attia Flavia, likely a tombstone or memorial.

EDCS-31400749.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius, most unhappy...
EDCS-31400749.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription, expressing grief for Aurelius.

EDCS-31400750.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Messiano coniugi inconparabili annorum XXX <q=O>ui vixit m[ecum ------ \n
EDCS-31400750.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Aurelius Messianus, unparalleled husband, who lived 30 years with me...
EDCS-31400750.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, Aurelius Messianus, providing his age at death and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-31400751.text_edition: \n Aurelius Sa Miccae coniugi quae vixit cum eo plus minus annos et tio filio domino nostro III \n
EDCS-31400751.translation: Aurelius Sa for Micca, his wife, who lived with him for approximately... years, and for Tio, his son, our lord...
EDCS-31400751.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Sa for his wife Micca and son Tio, indicating their lifespans and his relationship to them.

EDCS-31400752.text_edition: \n VT SA ME tis et Aurelia soror \n
EDCS-31400752.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly mentioning two women, Vtsa Metis and Aurelia, and their familial relationship.

EDCS-31400753.text_edition: \n Aureliae Eupateriae qui sic vixit annos XXIII Aurelia Vera filiae posuit Aurelio Iovino innocentissimo posuit\n
EDCS-31400753.translation: To Aurelia Eupateria, who lived 23 years. Aurelia Vera placed this for her daughter. For Aurelius Iovinus, most innocent. Placed.
EDCS-31400753.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Aurelia Vera for her daughter Aurelia Eupateria and possibly also for Aurelius Iovinus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400754.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription likely dedicating something to Aurelia, daughter of Marcus, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400756.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Ursula Aurelia Secunda pro pietate posuisuerunt Iulio Labioni pientissimo parentibus amicibus sic fratri Iulio Tertio bene merentibus\n
EDCS-31400756.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Aurelia Ursula and Aurelia Secunda erected this out of piety for Julius Labio, most dutiful parent and friend, and for his brother Julius Tertius, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400756.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Aurelia Ursula and Aurelia Secunda for Julius Labio and Julius Tertius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400757.text_edition: \n DDis Manibus Batullino infelicissimo? VELDVAM dies XVIII Batullus pater et mater Salonina posuerunt\n
EDCS-31400757.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Batullinus, most unfortunate? For 18 days. Batullus and his mother Salonina placed this.
EDCS-31400757.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, set up by Batullus and his mother Salonina for the deceased Batullinus, possibly a son or close relative.

EDCS-31400758.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Baebidio Eleuthero Ponponia sic Secunda marito \n
EDCS-31400758.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Lucius Baebidius Eleutherus, her husband. Pomponia Secunda.
EDCS-31400758.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Pomponia Secunda for her husband Lucius Baebidius Eleutherus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400761.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly referring to heirs, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400762.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caetenniae Decimiae vixit annos XXXV Iulia Secundina mater et Firmius Eutyches patronus \n
EDCS-31400762.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Caetennia Decimia, who lived 35 years. Iulia Secundina, mother, and Firmius Eutyches, patron.
EDCS-31400762.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Iulia Secundina and Firmius Eutyches for Caetennia Decimia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400764.text_edition: \n Candida in fronte pedes XXXX in [agro ------\n
EDCS-31400764.translation: Candida in the front 40 feet in the field [------
EDCS-31400764.summary: This is a fragmentary boundary inscription, likely from the Imperial period, specifying dimensions of land.

EDCS-31400765.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antia Maximina Corneliae Secundinae materterae bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400765.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Antia Maximina, her aunt, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400765.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Cornelia Secundina for her aunt Antia Maximina, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400767.text_edition: \n Cassiae Titi filiae Pomponillae Cassia Pallas patronae bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400767.translation: For Cassia Pomponilla, daughter of Titus. Cassia Pallas, patroness, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400767.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Cassia Pallas for her client Cassia Pomponilla, daughter of Titus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400770.text_edition: \n RIAII filio infelici et incomparabili vixit annos Victorinus CF parent[es ------\n
EDCS-31400770.translation: To Victorinus, son of Ria, unhappy and incomparable, who lived [X] years, his parents [---]
EDCS-31400770.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Victorinus, a son of Ria, with the remaining text lost, likely placed by his parents.

EDCS-31400772.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Clodiae Marcae <L=I>(ucius) Clodius Germ coniugi bene merenti \n
EDCS-31400772.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Clodia Marca; Lucius Clodius Germanus to his deserving wife.
EDCS-31400772.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Clodius Germanus for his well-deserving wife, Clodia Marca.

EDCS-31400773.text_edition: \n Cluentio Fortunato infelicissimo qui vix[it annos ------\n
EDCS-31400773.translation: To Cluentius Fortunatus, most unhappy, who lived [X] years [---]
EDCS-31400773.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cluentius Fortunatus, noting his unfortunate life and unspecified age at death.

EDCS-31400774.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Coelio Epithymeto marito optimo Coelia Secunda liberta bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400774.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Publius Coelius Epithymetus, to his excellent husband; Coelia Secunda, his freedwoman, well-deserving.
EDCS-31400774.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Coelia Secunda, a freedwoman, for her excellent and well-deserving husband, Publius Coelius Epithymetus.

EDCS-31400777.summary: This fragment likely belongs to a funerary epitaph or a dedication, with the beginning and end of the text missing.

EDCS-31400779.text_edition: \n SECa Egnatio Maritimo patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400779.translation: To Seca, to Egnatius Maritimus, her well-deserving father.
EDCS-31400779.summary: This is an epitaph set up by Seca for her well-deserving father, Egnatius Maritimus.

EDCS-31400782.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Feliculae defunctae annorum XXVI Maturus coniug(i) optimae\n
EDCS-31400782.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Felicula, deceased aged 26; Maturus to his excellent wife.
EDCS-31400782.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Maturus for his excellent wife, Felicula, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-31400783.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Ael[---] Nayl[---]
EDCS-31400783.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph, likely for an individual named Ael[---] Nayl[---].

EDCS-31400785.text_edition: \n nnos XV loco dato gratuito a Flavio Sabino patrono in fronte pedes XV in agro pedes X\n
EDCS-31400785.translation: [---] 15 years, place granted free of charge by Flavius Sabinus, patron, 15 feet in front, 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-31400785.summary: This inscription records land allocated for a burial, measuring 15x10 feet, granted gratuitously by Flavius Sabinus.

EDCS-31400788.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly the beginning of an epitaph or dedication for Lucius Fulvius Mar[---].

EDCS-31400790.text_edition: \n ad vitam volui Celerem te crescere mater ad mortem ingemui te Celerem misera Publius Gallius Celer Flavia Vi[r------\n
EDCS-31400790.translation: I wished for you, Celer, to grow for life; wretchedly I mourned you, Celer, in death. Publius Gallius Celer [erected this for] Flavia Vi[---]
EDCS-31400790.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the grief of a parent, Publius Gallius Celer, for his son or relative named Celer, and possibly for a woman named Flavia Vi.

EDCS-31400791.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to a mother named Gelasia.

EDCS-31400793.text_edition: \n I Tito Haio coniugi inconparabili qui vixit annis XXX Aurelia Tertulla coniux c<um=ON> quo concorditer vixit annis X bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-31400793.translation: Aurelia Tertulla, his wife, who lived in concord with him for 10 years, erected this for Tito Haio, her incomparable husband, who lived for 30 years, a deserving man.
EDCS-31400793.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Aurelia Tertulla for her husband Tito Haio, detailing their lifespan and marital harmony.

EDCS-31400794.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Helvi-- Filin[us/a]
EDCS-31400794.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription dedicated to a person named Helvius or Helvia, likely addressed to the Manes.

EDCS-31400795.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hilarrio Val annis fratri \n
EDCS-31400795.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Hilarrio, [my beloved] brother.
EDCS-31400795.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Hilarrio, the brother of the dedicator.

EDCS-31400797.text_edition: \n BI qui vixit annos XLVI merenti Imiaria conserva\n
EDCS-31400797.translation: To the deserving Imiaria, his slave/freedwoman, who lived 46 years.
EDCS-31400797.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a deserving slave or freedwoman named Imiaria, who lived to be 46 years old.
