EDCS-31400798.translation: [Erected for] a most dutiful mother and Inacho F[---]
EDCS-31400798.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment, likely dedicated to a mother and another individual named Inacho.

EDCS-31400800.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Ir[e?]ne, his nurse.
EDCS-31400800.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to a nurse named Irene.

EDCS-31400803.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuli Carpophori a Bel<e=F>nia filio \n
EDCS-31400803.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Iulius Carpophorus, son of Belenia.
EDCS-31400803.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Iulius Carpophorus, son of Belenia.

EDCS-31400804.translation: To Decimus Iulius M. Hilarianus, most unfortunate [---]
EDCS-31400804.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment mentioning Decimus Iulius M. Hilarianus, described as most unfortunate.

EDCS-31400805.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Valens annorum XXVII \n
EDCS-31400805.translation: Gaius Julius Valens, son of Gaius, aged 27.
EDCS-31400805.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius Valens, who died at the age of 27.

EDCS-31400806.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Sabina Tito Iulio Ingenuo filio infelicissimo defuncto nnorum XIIII titulum posuit mater pientissima\n
EDCS-31400806.translation: To the Divine Manes. Julia Sabina erected this monument for her most unfortunate son, Titus Julius Ingenuus, who died at the age of 14. A most devoted mother.
EDCS-31400806.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Julia Sabina for her son Titus Julius Ingenuus, who died at age 14.

EDCS-31400808.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone mentioning Rectia and her son.

EDCS-31400809.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Publius Lurio.
EDCS-31400809.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to Publius Lurio.

EDCS-31400810.text_edition: \n Maria Helpis se viva sibi et Aurelio Secundo coniugi inconparabili posuit\n
EDCS-31400810.translation: Maria Helpis erected this for herself while still alive, and for her incomparable husband, Aurelius Secundus.
EDCS-31400810.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Maria Helpis for herself and her husband, Aurelius Secundus.

EDCS-31400814.text_edition: \n Natali et Pieridi liberto us in fronte pedes XX \n
EDCS-31400814.translation: For Natalius and Pieridus, freedmen, a frontage of 20 feet.
EDCS-31400814.summary: This inscription records the burial plot size for the freedmen Natalius and Pieridus.

EDCS-31400816.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nicini sic defunctae nnorum X mensium III dierum V Fortunatus nepotiae posuit\n
EDCS-31400816.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Nicini, who died at age 10 years, 3 months, and 5 days. Fortunatus erected this for his niece.
EDCS-31400816.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Fortunatus for his niece Nicini, who died at a young age.

EDCS-31400817.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Octavia Aeliae filiae vixit nnos titulum libens posuit\n
EDCS-31400817.translation: To the Divine Manes. Octavia erected this monument for her daughter Aelia, who lived...
EDCS-31400817.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Octavia for her daughter Aelia.

EDCS-31400821.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Publius Pinus.

EDCS-31400822.text_edition: \n et Plaetoriae Primitivae Plaetoria Victorina parentibus optimis posuit\n
EDCS-31400822.translation: Plaetoria Victorina erected this for her parents, Plaetoria Primitiva and [...]
EDCS-31400822.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Plaetoria Victorina for her parents.

EDCS-31400826.text_edition: \n Cai fili-- Rae et aviae karissimae Flavi[ae ------\n
EDCS-31400826.translation: To Caius, son of..., and to his dearest grandmother, Flavia [---]
EDCS-31400826.summary: This is a dedication to Caius, son of..., and his grandmother Flavia, likely from a funerary monument.

EDCS-31400827.summary: This records the age of Renatus, 14, likely on a funerary marker.

EDCS-31400828.text_edition: \n PRCV Claudia Ressituta tatae suo bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400828.translation: Claudia Ressituta to her father, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400828.summary: Claudia Ressituta dedicates this to her well-deserving father, possibly on a tomb.

EDCS-31400829.summary: This incomplete inscription likely records Quintus, a freedman or slave, associated with Epaphroditus.

EDCS-31400830.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Septimio Salutari G P C bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400830.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Lucius Septimius Salutaris, a Roman citizen, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400830.summary: This is a dedication to the deceased Lucius Septimius Salutaris, a Roman citizen, likely on his tomb.

EDCS-31400833.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sempronius Marcellinus Cassiae Placidae uxori infelicissimae quae vixit annos mecum quindecim coniugi incomparabili\n
EDCS-31400833.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Sempronius Marcellinus to Cassia Placidus, his unfortunate wife, who lived with him for fifteen years, an incomparable wife.
EDCS-31400833.summary: Sempronius Marcellinus dedicates this to his wife Cassia Placidus, who died after fifteen years of marriage.

EDCS-31400836.translation: [---] to his patron Sil[---], hospitable, set up this monument.
EDCS-31400836.summary: This inscription records that someone set up a monument to their patron, Sil[---].

EDCS-31400837.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Soteri Leonati coniugi et patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400837.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: To Soteres, husband and patron, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400837.summary: This funerary dedication is to Soteres, honored as both husband and patron.

EDCS-31400839.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentiae Callistini coniugi incompara[bili ------\n
EDCS-31400839.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: To Terentia Callistina, an incomparable wife [---]
EDCS-31400839.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription is dedicated to Terentia Callistina, described as an incomparable wife.

EDCS-31400840.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentiae Quintinae matri pient[issimae ------\n
EDCS-31400840.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: To Terentia Quintina, most dutiful mother [---]
EDCS-31400840.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription is dedicated to Terentia Quintina, praised as a most dutiful mother.

EDCS-31400843.text_edition: \n et Tutidiae Agrippinae pientissimae suae coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400843.translation: To Tutidia Agrippina, his most devoted wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400843.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from an unknown individual to his deserving wife, Tutidia Agrippina.

EDCS-31400846.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus duobus adulescentibus Val Dinenti viro egregio nnorum XXVII et Attia Valeriae coniu<g=C>i eius nnorum XVIII Octavia Quieta genero et filiae \n
EDCS-31400846.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for two youths: for Val Dinentius, an excellent man, aged 27, and for his wife Attia Valeria, aged 18. Octavia Quieta dedicated this to her son-in-law and daughter.
EDCS-31400846.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Octavia Quieta to her son-in-law, Val Dinentius, and her daughter, Attia Valeria, both deceased at young ages.

EDCS-31400850.text_edition: \n ae alumnae defunctae annorum III Lucius Valerius Secundinus bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400850.translation: To Lucius Valerius Secundinus, for his deceased foster-daughter, aged 3, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400850.summary: This inscription records a funerary dedication by Lucius Valerius Secundinus to his foster-daughter who died at the age of three.

EDCS-31400851.text_edition: \n ------ Val]eria Aiela Valerio Secundiniano filio be[ne merenti ------\n
EDCS-31400851.translation: To Valeria Aiela, for her son Valerius Secundinianus, who deserved well.
EDCS-31400851.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication from Valeria Aiela to her son, Valerius Secundinianus.

EDCS-31400854.text_edition: \n OFO defuncto annorum XXVI Veiedius Quartinus FTP \n
EDCS-31400854.translation: For the deceased Veiedius Quartinus, aged 26. FTP.
EDCS-31400854.summary: This inscription commemorates Veiedius Quartinus, who died at the age of 26, with an unknown abbreviation FTP.

EDCS-31400857.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for M. Vero.
EDCS-31400857.summary: This is a very brief funerary dedication to M. Vero.

EDCS-31400858.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Egnatiae Victorinae pientissimae infanti annorum --- h(ic) sepulta \n
EDCS-31400858.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Egnatia Victorina, a most devoted infant, aged [---], buried here.
EDCS-31400858.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Egnatia Victorina, an infant who died at an unspecified age.

EDCS-31400864.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording the name and status of a young boy named Fes.

EDCS-31400865.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a dedication to Paternius, son of Cim[---].

EDCS-31400866.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of an individual named Septimianus.

EDCS-31400871.text_edition: \n D(is) Manibus lo nce va coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-31400871.translation: To the shades of the departed, to Ionceva, my deserving wife.
EDCS-31400871.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to a wife named Ionceva, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400876.translation: To the shades of the departed, to our most dutiful daughter [---].
EDCS-31400876.summary: A funerary epitaph for a daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400882.summary: A fragment of an inscription mentioning Valerius and "Italy", possibly a dedication or a record of office, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400883.translation: [---] sinner, placed this for Radomices, of good memory.
EDCS-31400883.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a "sinner" to Radomices, of good memory, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400885.summary: A dedication to a patron named Emi, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400888.translation: To Aie, my most unhappy sister [---].
EDCS-31400888.summary: A funerary epitaph for a sister named Aie, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400895.summary: A fragment mentioning a will and a freedman, possibly related to manumission or inheritance, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400903.summary: A fragment mentioning the name Septimius and possibly a place or rank, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400908.text_edition: \n ------ nnos] XXVI menses VII dies VIII Marcus ma posuit\n
EDCS-31400908.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age at death of an individual, erected by Marcus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400926.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia, possibly including an age or a further identifier, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31400954.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Mnio Nigrianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400955.translation: To our mother Primigenia, well deserving.
EDCS-31400955.summary: This is a dedication to the mother-goddess Primigenia, possibly by her children, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400956.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to a woman named Quinta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400958.text_edition: \n Nymphis et Silvano Aurelius Annianus bucinator cohortis VIII voluntariorum Antoninianae votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-31400958.translation: Aurelius Annianus, a trumpeter of the VIIIth cohort of Antoninian volunteers, willingly set this up to the Nymphs and Silvanus.
EDCS-31400958.summary: This is a votive offering by Aurelius Annianus, a trumpeter, to the Nymphs and Silvanus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31500386.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum lio Sa vixit annos XL ADERA \n
EDCS-31500386.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lio Sa lived 40 years.
EDCS-31500386.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lio Sa, who lived 40 years, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500387.text_edition: \n Servena Marci filia Maxima annorum XXV\n
EDCS-31500387.translation: Servena Maxima, daughter of Marcus, aged 25.
EDCS-31500387.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Servena Maxima, aged 25, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500388.text_edition: \n Dianae Lauci sacrum in memoriam Luci Iuli Elpidiani \n
EDCS-31500388.translation: Sacred to Diana Lauci, in memory of Lucius Iulius Elpidianus.
EDCS-31500388.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Lauci, set up in memory of Lucius Iulius Elpidianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500389.text_edition: \n Marcus Annius Marci filius Triarius praefectus militum \n
EDCS-31500389.translation: Marcus Annius Triarius, son of Marcus, a military prefect.
EDCS-31500389.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Marcus Annius Triarius, son of Marcus, a military prefect, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500390.text_edition: \n Aquilio Tromentina Aquilino aedili IIviro iure dicundo IIviro quinquennali \n
EDCS-31500390.translation: To Aquilius Tromentinus Aquilinus, as aedile, as duovir with judicial power, and as quinquennial duovir.
EDCS-31500390.summary: This is a dedication honoring Aquilius Tromentinus Aquilinus for his various magistracies in a Roman city, likely from the 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31500392.text_edition: \n domo Arimino miles legionis XIII donatus torquibus armillis phaleris et |(centurio) cohortis I Campestris annorum LX testamento fieri iussit Posidonius et Prunicus liberti posuerunt et ali nemini hic situs est\n
EDCS-31500392.translation: A soldier from Ariminum, of the XIIIth legion, honored with torques, armillae, and phalerae, and (centurion) of the 1st cohort of Campestris, aged 60, ordered this to be made in his will. Posidonius and Prunicus, freedmen, erected it and nobody else is buried here.
EDCS-31500392.summary: This is a funerary monument for a soldier of the XIIIth legion and centurion of the I cohort Campestris, who died at 60, erected by his freedmen and specifying the plot is not for others, likely from the 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31900002.translation: Velosunus erected this monument at his own expense.
EDCS-31900002.summary: This is a dedication by Velosunus, likely erected during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900003.summary: This fragment is part of a funerary monument, likely honoring Petronia Lucilla, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900008.summary: This very fragmented inscription mentions Verantius Iunior, possibly part of a dedication or funerary record from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900017.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Iulio Verecund |(centurioni) cohortis VIII volunt(ariorum) --- Titia Spes coniux Iulius Iulianus parentes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-31900017.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titia Spes, his wife, and Iulius Iulianus, his parents, erected this for Quintus Iulius Verecundus, centurion of the 8th cohort of volunteers, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-31900017.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Quintus Iulius Verecundus, a centurion, by his wife and parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900020.text_edition: \n Titus Iulius Dianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31900020.translation: Titus Iulius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Diana.
EDCS-31900020.summary: This is a votive inscription by Titus Iulius dedicating something to the goddess Diana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900021.text_edition: \n Minucia Secunda votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31900021.translation: Minucia Secunda willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-31900021.summary: This is a votive inscription by Minucia Secunda, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900022.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Victorina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900024.text_edition: \n ice quae mecum vixit annis XX \n
EDCS-31900024.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a person who lived with the deceased for 20 years, possibly part of a funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900026.text_edition: \n Matri Magnae Marcus Calpurnius Primus et Trebonia Chreste restituerunt\n
EDCS-31900026.translation: Marcus Calpurnius Primus and Trebonia Chreste restored this to the Great Mother.
EDCS-31900026.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument dedicated to the Great Mother (Cybele) by Marcus Calpurnius Primus and Trebonia Chreste, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900028.summary: This fragmentary inscription refers to a veteran of a cohort, possibly part of a military dedication or funerary record from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900029.text_edition: \n Sextus Clodius C(ai) filius Publilia Verona miles legionis VII Claudia pia fidelis \n
EDCS-31900029.translation: Sextus Clodius, son of Gaius, of the Publilia tribe, from Verona, a soldier of the Seventh Claudia Pia Fidelis legion.
EDCS-31900029.summary: This inscription records a soldier, Sextus Clodius, serving in the Seventh Claudia Pia Fidelis legion.

EDCS-31900030.text_edition: \n Sextus Cornelius Sexti filius Camilia nonanorum veteranus I \n
EDCS-31900030.translation: Sextus Cornelius, son of Sextus, of the Camilia tribe, a veteran of the Nonani, 1 [year of service or rank].
EDCS-31900030.summary: This inscription identifies Sextus Cornelius, a veteran from the Nonani, likely indicating his service or rank.

EDCS-31900031.text_edition: \n Caius Parp-- Fabia ARECX annorum XLV stipendiorum XVI N\n
EDCS-31900031.translation: Gaius Parp[---], of the Fabia tribe, ARECX, aged 45 years, with 16 years of service.
EDCS-31900031.summary: This incomplete inscription records Gaius Parp[---], aged 45 with 16 years of service, belonging to the Fabia tribe.

EDCS-31900033.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Novellius Ursus pater filiae Flaviae Tertullinae dulcissimae defunctae annorum VIII mensum sic IIII\n
EDCS-31900033.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Novellius Ursus, father, for his daughter Flavia Tertullina, most beloved, who died aged 8 years and 4 months.
EDCS-31900033.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Marcus Novellius Ursus dedicating a monument to his daughter Flavia Tertullina, who died at the age of 8 years and 4 months.

EDCS-31900034.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori Aeneas Proclianus aedem restituit libens\n
EDCS-31900034.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Repeller. Aeneas Proclianus restored this temple willingly.
EDCS-31900034.summary: This inscription records Aeneas Proclianus willingly restoring a temple dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Depulsor.

EDCS-31900036.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Surus Pirami libens posuit\n
EDCS-31900036.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Surus Pirami willingly dedicated this.
EDCS-31900036.summary: This inscription is a dedication of a sacred space to Silvanus by Surus Pirami.

EDCS-31900037.summary: This is a short funerary inscription dedicating something to a son named Sperantius.

EDCS-31900042.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Mi veteranus legionis V Macedonicae qui vixit [annos ------\n
EDCS-31900042.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Mi, veteran of the Fifth Macedonian legion, who lived [X] years.
EDCS-31900042.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius Mi, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian legion, whose lifespan is incomplete.

EDCS-31900045.text_edition: \n AGI et Agathiali Veneria mater bene merenti\n
EDCS-31900045.translation: To AGI and Agathialis, from Veneria, his mother, well-deserving.
EDCS-31900045.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Veneria, mother of AGI and Agathialis, honoring their deserving nature.

EDCS-31900050.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Castinus Aeliae Iobinae sic uxori infelicissimae quae vixxit sic annos XXVI si quis autem voluerit super haec trea sic corpora ponere voluerit inferret decreto folles |(mille) curiae nostrae\n
EDCS-31900050.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Castinus for Aelia Iobina, his most unfortunate wife, who lived 26 years; if anyone else wishes to place bodies here beyond these three, he shall be fined 1000 [denarii or similar currency] by our curia.
EDCS-31900050.summary: This funerary inscription from Aurelius Castinus for his wife Aelia Iobina includes a penalty for additional burials, specifying a fine of 1000 units from the curia.

EDCS-31900052.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Gemella, likely erected by Aurelius, and is incomplete.

EDCS-31900055.translation: Lucius (?), son of C(aius?) and C(aius?)...
EDCS-31900055.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with a dedication, possibly to a person named Lucius, with missing information about parentage and purpose.

EDCS-31900056.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that mentions the name Celerinus, likely part of a larger text.

EDCS-31900058.text_edition: \n II Elpistus Corneliae Severae amicae bene merenti\n
EDCS-31900058.translation: To Elpistus, for Cornelia Severa, his well-deserving friend.
EDCS-31900058.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Elpistus for his deserving friend Cornelia Severa.

EDCS-31900061.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Fannia Maritima. Capriusmus...
EDCS-31900061.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Fannia Maritima by Capriusmus, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-31900062.summary: This is a severely damaged inscription mentioning someone named Felici, with considerable text missing.

EDCS-31900063.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Flavio Felici patri et Flaviae \n
EDCS-31900063.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Quintus Flavius Felix, father, and Flavia...
EDCS-31900063.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Flavius Felix, identified as a father, and Flavia, with the remainder of the dedication missing.

EDCS-31900065.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Cai filio Tromentina Admeto nnum XXII\n
EDCS-31900065.translation: To Gaius Julius Admetus, son of Gaius, of the tribe Tromentina, aged 22 years.
EDCS-31900065.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Admetus, noting his age and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-31900066.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the names Lucianus and EDOA, with the context of the inscription missing.

EDCS-31900067.text_edition: \n MAM filio dulcissimo et piissimo mihi posuit \n
EDCS-31900067.translation: Erected by me for my sweetest and most devoted son...
EDCS-31900067.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by an individual for their beloved son, with names and further details missing.

EDCS-31900070.summary: This is a fragment of a tomb inscription, likely commemorating someone named Maximus.

EDCS-31900071.translation: To the Divine Manes of Pomponia, freedwoman...
EDCS-31900071.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Divine Manes for Pomponia, identified as a freedwoman.

EDCS-31900073.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Publicio Proculo defuncto nnorum XV Titus Publicius Ursus filio dulcissimo pater \n
EDCS-31900073.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Titus Publicius Proculus, who died at 15 years of age. Titus Publicius Ursus, father, to his most beloved son.
EDCS-31900073.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young boy, Titus Publicius Proculus, and is dedicated by his father.

EDCS-31900076.summary: This is a fragment of a tomb inscription, mentioning Secundus and his piety.

EDCS-31900077.summary: This fragment of an inscription indicates a testament or will left by Publius Statius.

EDCS-31900078.text_edition: \n matri pientissimae et merenti Sullius Inventus filius bene merenti\n
EDCS-31900078.translation: To his most pious and deserving mother, Sullius Inventus, to her son who served her well.
EDCS-31900078.summary: This dedication is from Sullius Inventus to his deserving mother.

EDCS-31900081.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription fragment that may contain personal names.

EDCS-31900083.text_edition: \n IL ERSII PAXA F nus FIII ssimo\n
EDCS-31900083.translation: IL ERSII PAXA F son FIII most...
EDCS-31900083.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication with unclear names and relationships.

EDCS-31900085.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a wife named Undi for her deserving conduct.

EDCS-31900096.text_edition: \n Sexto Fundanio Quinti f(ilio) --- Ponponiae sic Sexti filiae Tertiae Caius Fundanius Clemens parentibus fecit\n
EDCS-31900096.translation: For Sextus Fundanius, son of Quintus. For Tertia, daughter of Sextus. Caius Fundanius Clemens made this for his parents.
EDCS-31900096.summary: This funerary inscription records Caius Fundanius Clemens dedicating a monument to his parents, Sextus Fundanius and Tertia.

EDCS-31900099.summary: This inscription fragment records the name of the emperor Vespasian, likely part of a larger imperial monument from the 1st century CE.

EDCS-31900100.translation: Aemilia Marcellina of the municipality of Novensium...
EDCS-31900100.summary: This fragment mentions Aemilia Marcellina and the municipality of Novensium, possibly related to a public inscription or dedication from the Roman period.

EDCS-31900211.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Urso domo A<c=Q>ruvino qui vixit annos plus minus XXXVIII Valeria Marcelli[na ------ \n
EDCS-31900211.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Flavius Urnus, from Acruvinum, who lived about 38 years. Valeria Marcellina...
EDCS-31900211.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Flavius Urnus, who died around the age of 38, and was set up by Valeria Marcellina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900216.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Flaviae Ianuariae quae vixit annos XLVIII Gratus coniugi incomparabili posuit\n
EDCS-31900216.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. For Flavia Ianuaria, who lived 48 years. Gratus placed this for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-31900216.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Flavia Ianuaria at age 48 and was erected by her husband Gratus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Pinniae quae vixit annos XXX Marcus Valerius Quintianus uxori et Flaviae Quintinae filiae quae vixit annos X\n
EDCS-31900217.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Flavia Pinna, who lived 30 years. Marcus Valerius Quintianus for his wife, and for Flavia Quintina, his daughter, who lived 10 years.
EDCS-31900217.summary: This funerary inscription honors Flavia Pinna, who died at 30, and her daughter Flavia Quintina, who died at 10, erected by Marcus Valerius Quintianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900219.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Tullius Claudianus.
EDCS-31900219.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription dedicated to Lucius Tullius Claudianus, typical of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900225.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-31900225.text_edition: \n Ausonia diaconissa pro voto suo et filiora sic suora sic fecit\n
EDCS-31900225.translation: Ausonia the deaconess, for her vow and for her daughters, her sisters, made this.
EDCS-31900225.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Ausonia, identified as a deaconess, on behalf of herself and her daughters, likely from the Late Antique or early Christian period.

EDCS-31900226.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Publius Aelius Fuscinus miles legionis I adiutricis beneficiarius consularis Imperatore Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinace Augusto et Albino Caesare consulibus votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-31900226.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Publius Aelius Fuscinus, a soldier of the I Adjutrix legion, serving as a beneficiarius consularis, willingly and deservedly placed this vow in fulfillment during the consulship of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Albinus Caesar.
EDCS-31900226.summary: This military dedication records a soldier, Publius Aelius Fuscinus, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus under the consulship of Emperors Septimius Severus and Albinus, dating to 193-197 CE.

EDCS-31900227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Aentio filio piissimo qui vixit annos XIII Herma et Martia parentes ipsi sibi in memoriam posuerunt\n
EDCS-31900227.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Aentius, a most dutiful son, who lived 13 years. Herma and Martia, his parents, set this up for themselves in memory.
EDCS-31900227.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Lucius Aentius, who died at 13, and was erected by his parents Herma and Martia as a memorial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900229.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Germano qui vixit annos XV NISV filio pater posuit\n
EDCS-31900229.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Germano, who lived 15 years. His father placed this.
EDCS-31900229.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Germano at the age of 15 and was erected by his father, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900234.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Menander Aphrodisieys sic <I=L>nvicto Au<g=C>(usto) votum fecit \n
EDCS-31900234.translation: Lucius Antonius Menander of Aphrodisias made a vow to the unconquered Augustus.
EDCS-31900234.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Lucius Antonius Menander to the emperor Augustus.

EDCS-31900235.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Booni vixit annos LVI hic situs est Titus Aurelius Laiscus patri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31900235.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Aurelius Booni lived 56 years; he is buried here. Titus Aurelius Laiscus placed this for his deserving father.
EDCS-31900235.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Aurelius Booni, set up by his son Titus Aurelius Laiscus.

EDCS-31900236.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Numeriano fratri pientissimo defuncto annorum XXVI et Aurelio Valerio avuncu<l=I>o de<f=E>(uncto) annorum XL Aurelius Dazas pro pietate \n
EDCS-31900236.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Aurelius Numerianus, dearest brother, who died at 26 years old, and for Aurelius Valerius, uncle, who died at 40 years old. Aurelius Dazas, out of piety.
EDCS-31900236.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius Numerianus and his uncle Aurelius Valerius, dedicated by Aurelius Dazas.

EDCS-31900237.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureli-- Tit Bricussa- TiSIAtae matri infelicissimae defunctae annorum LXXV\n
EDCS-31900237.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Aurelius --, son of Tit Bricussa, for his most unhappy mother, who died at 75 years old.
EDCS-31900237.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for an unnamed son and his mother, Aurelia Tit Bricussa.

EDCS-31900238.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Aurelius Saturninus Aureliae Amurcianae coniugi pientissimae bene merenti et sibi vivus memoriam posuit quae vixit annos XXVIIII menses IIII\n
EDCS-31900238.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Aurelius Saturninus placed this memorial for his most pious and deserving wife Aurelia Amurciana, who lived 29 years and 4 months, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-31900238.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelia Amurciana and was also set up for the dedicator, Titus Aurelius Saturninus.

EDCS-31900239.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Crescens.
EDCS-31900239.summary: This is a very brief funerary epitaph for an individual named Crescens.

EDCS-31900240.text_edition: \n I Flavio Aprioni Aurelia Apronia m[emoriam ------\n
EDCS-31900240.translation: For Flavius Aprionius. Aurelia Apronia [set up] a memorial [to ------
EDCS-31900240.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Apronia to Flavius Aprionius.

EDCS-31900250.text_edition: \n ------ fi]lius Tromentina IIvir iure dicundo Laurens [Lavinas ------\n
EDCS-31900250.translation: [------ son] of Tromentina, duovir with judicial power, Laurens [Lavinas ------
EDCS-31900250.summary: This is a severely fragmented inscription, possibly recording an official, Laurens Lavinas, son of Tromentina.

EDCS-31900259.text_edition: \n nta Travannorum LV millus cus uxori\n
EDCS-31900259.translation: [------ of the] Travanni people, 55 miles, with his wife
EDCS-31900259.summary: This fragment likely refers to a distance and mentions a man and his wife, possibly related to a boundary marker or itinerary.

EDCS-31900260.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunatae annorum XVIII et Rufo annorum XII Restutianus Valentini et Crescentilla filiis infelicibus\n
EDCS-31900260.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Fortunata, 18 years old, and Rufus, 12 years old. Restutianus and Valentini and Crescentilla for their unhappy children.
EDCS-31900260.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates two children, Fortunata and Rufus, dedicated by their parents Restutianus, Valentini, and Crescentilla.

EDCS-31900261.text_edition: \n A fili- sibi et libertae Iustinae et fratri Basiliano sae sic vibis sic fecit\n
EDCS-31900261.translation: To his freedwoman Iustina and his brother Bassianus, A. made this for himself and them while alive.
EDCS-31900261.summary: This is a funerary dedication by A. to his freedwoman and brother, made during his lifetime, likely on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-31900262.text_edition: \n Persiliae coniugi incomparabili Marcus Lollius Silvanus maritus aeius sic vivus fecit\n
EDCS-31900262.translation: Marcus Lollius Silvanus, her husband, made this for his incomparable wife Persilia while alive.
EDCS-31900262.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Marcus Lollius Silvanus for his wife Persilia, made during his lifetime.

EDCS-31900263.summary: A brief dedication to Sevidolius, identifying him as a freedman.

EDCS-31900265.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a name, likely part of a larger dedication or ownership marker.

EDCS-31900266.text_edition: \n L(ucii) Pomponii Valentinus Gemellus votum solverunt libentes merito pro FI restitu\n
EDCS-31900266.translation: Lucius Pomponius Valentinus and Gemellus fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly for FI.
EDCS-31900266.summary: This records the fulfillment of a vow by Lucius Pomponius Valentinus and Gemellus, likely to a deity, with the abbreviation 'FI' possibly indicating the deity or purpose.

EDCS-31900267.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Marcus Sextius Epaphroditus\n
EDCS-31900267.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater, Marcus Sextius Epaphroditus.
EDCS-31900267.summary: This is a dedication of something sacred to the god Liber Pater by Marcus Sextius Epaphroditus.

EDCS-31900268.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto Titus Aelius Firminus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit merito libens\n
EDCS-31900268.translation: Titus Aelius Firminus, a beneficiary and aide to the consular legate of the 14th Gemina legion, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to Mars Augustus.
EDCS-31900268.summary: This inscription records Titus Aelius Firminus, a soldier with specific military duties, fulfilling a vow to the god Mars Augustus.

EDCS-31900270.summary: This inscription indicates the name and place of origin of an individual, likely Lucius Aemilius from Cremona.

EDCS-31900272.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victori Scenobarbi miles cohortis I Belgarum stipendiorum X tubicen testamento fieri iussit Munnius et Gentius heredes posuerunt\n
EDCS-31900272.translation: Munnius and Gentius, his heirs, erected this in accordance with his will to Victorius Scenobarbus, a soldier of the 1st Cohort of Belgae, after 10 years of service, a trumpeter.
EDCS-31900272.summary: This funerary inscription records the posthumous erection of a monument by heirs for Victorius Scenobarbus, a trumpeter in the Roman army.

EDCS-31900273.text_edition: \n Caio Publicio Romano decurioni nnorum XXX Caio Licinio Expectato Publiciae Iluricae nnorum V infelicissima mater filiis et nepotiae viva fecit et pater infelix senex\n
EDCS-31900273.translation: To Gaius Publicius Romanus, a decurion of 30 years, and Gaius Licinius Expectatus, 5 years old, Publicia Ilurica, the most unhappy mother, and her unhappy old father made this monument for their sons and grandson while alive.
EDCS-31900273.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by grieving parents for their son and grandson, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900274.text_edition: \n Sexto Publicio Sexti liberto Secundo IIIIIIviro Sextus Publicius Hesper VIvir libertus\n
EDCS-31900274.translation: To Sextus Publicius Secundus, freedman of Sextus Publicius, a member of the college of Six, and to Sextus Publicius Hesper, a freedman and member of the college of Six.
EDCS-31900274.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring two freedmen who were members of the college of Six, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-31900275.text_edition: \n Quinto Turelio Quinti liberto Acuto IIIIIIviro Tureliae Quinti libertae Hilarae Lucio Turelio Quinti filio Feroci nnorum XXII Sexto Turelio Quinti filio Veloci DI \n
EDCS-31900275.translation: To Quintus Turelius Acutus, freedman of Quintus, a member of the college of Six; to Turelia Hilara, freedwoman of Quintus; to Lucius Turelius Ferox, son of Quintus, aged 22; and to Sextus Turelius Velox, son of Quintus.
EDCS-31900275.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates several members of the Turelius family, including freedmen and sons, one of whom was a member of the college of Six, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-31900276.text_edition: \n Quinto Veturio Secundino decurioni coloniae Naronae qui vixit nnos XLVIII Livinia Fortunata marito inconparabili sic posuit\n
EDCS-31900276.translation: Livinia Fortunata erected this for her peerless husband, Quintus Veturius Secundinus, decurion of the colony of Narona, who lived 48 years.
EDCS-31900276.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deceased husband, Quintus Veturius Secundinus, a decurion in the Roman colony of Narona, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900277.text_edition: \n Saturnino actori Saturnianus et Valeria patri rarissimo\n
EDCS-31900277.translation: To Saturninus the actor. Saturnianus and Valeria erected this for their most rare father.
EDCS-31900277.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Saturnianus and Valeria to their father, Saturninus, who was an actor, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900278.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Annaeo Quinto filio Annaeus Pudens pater miser qui filium terrae dedi annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-31900278.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Annaeus Pudens, a grieving father, erected this for his son Quintus, aged 30 years, whom he gave to the earth.
EDCS-31900278.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a father to his deceased son, Quintus, who died at the age of 30, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900279.text_edition: \n Aquilliae Luci libertae Myrsinae Exoratae Luci filiae Luculla Luci filia Tigris matri et patri et sorori et fratri fecit\n
EDCS-31900279.translation: To Aquillia Myrsina, freedwoman of Lucius, daughter of Lucius. Luculla, daughter of Lucius, erected this for her mother, father, sister, and brother.
EDCS-31900279.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Luculla for her family, including her mother Aquillia Myrsina, a freedwoman, and other relatives, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900280.text_edition: \n Atilia Tranquilla et Atilius Theseus vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-31900280.translation: Atilia Tranquilla and Atilius Theseus made this, while alive, for themselves and their kin.
EDCS-31900280.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by Atilia Tranquilla and Atilius Theseus for themselves and their family, indicating they planned for their burials while still living, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900282.text_edition: \n Cornelia Luci liberta Baebia Ha here--- eius \n
EDCS-31900282.translation: To Cornelia Baebia, freedwoman of Lucius. Ha [---] his heir...
EDCS-31900282.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a funerary dedication to Cornelia Baebia, a freedwoman, with an incomplete mention of her heir, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900284.text_edition: \n annorum Iulia Primitiva uxor marito bene merito\n
EDCS-31900284.translation: Iulia Primitiva, wife of [---], erected this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-31900284.summary: This funerary epitaph was set up by Iulia Primitiva for her deserving husband, the details of whom are lost, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900285.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a funerary dedication or memorial for Marcus Junius Ingenuus, dating possibly to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31900286.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Maximus Lupulae coniugi incomparabi<l=I>i benae sic merenti fecit et con sic <q=O>ua vixit annos plus minus XX et filiae Maximae <q=O>uae vixit annos plus minus IIIII \n
EDCS-31900286.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Maximus made this for his incomparable wife Lupula, who well deserved it, and for himself, having lived with her. She lived about 20 years and for his daughter Maxima, who lived about 5 years.
EDCS-31900286.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Maximus to his wife Lupula and daughter Maxima, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900287.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Mucio Filumeno sic marito rarissimo et Muciae Proculinae filiae Porcia Procula bene merenti viva fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-31900287.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Porcia Procula made this for her most rare husband Mucio Filumenus and for her daughter Mucia Proculina, who deserved it, and for herself and her family while alive.
EDCS-31900287.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Porcia Procula for her husband Mucio Filumenus and daughter Mucia Proculina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900288.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Philete bene meritae Publius Mescenius Severianus\n
EDCS-31900288.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Mescenius Severianus [dedicated this] for Philete, who deserved it.
EDCS-31900288.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Publius Mescenius Severianus for Philete, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900289.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Publius Publicius by Antho, the nature of their relationship being lost.

EDCS-31900290.text_edition: \n Titus Pullicus Rufi filius Romilia Caius Pullicus Titi filius Romilia annorum XXIIII Quintus Pullicus Titi filius Salvius\n
EDCS-31900290.translation: Titus Pullicus, son of Rufi (from the Romilia tribe). Caius Pullicus, son of Titus (from the Romilia tribe), aged 24. Quintus Pullicus, son of Titus, named Salvius.
EDCS-31900290.summary: This inscription records the names and tribal affiliations of three individuals, likely brothers, of the Pullicus family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900291.text_edition: \n nia Secunda i avonculo sic consubrino sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-31900291.translation: [...]nia Secunda made this with her own money for her uncle and cousin.
EDCS-31900291.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription where Secunda funded the monument for her uncle and cousin, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900292.text_edition: \n Valeriae Piae annorum XII Spedia Procula mater filiae pientissimae et Valerio Tauro coniugi et sibi et posteris suis viva fecit\n
EDCS-31900292.translation: Spedia Procula made this for her daughter Valeria Pia, aged 12, most dutiful, and for her husband Valerius Taurus, and for herself and her descendants, while alive.
EDCS-31900292.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Spedia Procula for her daughter Valeria Pia and husband Valerius Taurus, also providing for herself and posterity.

EDCS-31900293.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victori filio pientissimo vixit nnis XX diebus XVI Eutyches et Ursula parentes et sibi et suis vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-31900293.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Eutyches and Ursula, parents, made this for their most dutiful son Victor, who lived 20 years and 16 days, and for themselves and their family, while alive.
EDCS-31900293.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Eutyches and Ursula for their deceased son Victor, and for themselves and their household.

EDCS-31900294.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ger Lucullae sorori pientissimae titulum posuit defunctae annorum XXVI\n
EDCS-31900294.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. [Someone] placed this tombstone for Ger Luculla, his most dutiful sister, who died at the age of 26.
EDCS-31900294.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by an unknown person to their sister Ger Luculla, who died at 26.

EDCS-31900295.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its content or purpose.

EDCS-31900298.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Lucretiae Aurelius Surus et Aurelia Licinia matri pientissimae posuerunt titulum\n
EDCS-31900298.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Surus and Aurelia Licinia dedicated this tomb to their most dutiful mother, Aurelia Lucretia.
EDCS-31900298.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by children to their mother, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900299.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Ursina Artoriae Privatae matri pientissimae posuit quae vixit annos LX menses sex\n
EDCS-31900299.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Artoria Prívata dedicated this to her most dutiful mother, Aurelia Ursina, who lived sixty years and six months.
EDCS-31900299.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a daughter to her mother, including the mother's age at death, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900304.text_edition: \n Andamionius Andami filius eques cohortis I Lucensium nnorum XXXV stipendiorum XV hic situs est Gavillius frater eius posuit\n
EDCS-31900304.translation: Andamionius, son of Andamius, an equestrian of the I Cohort of Lucenses, aged 35 and with 15 years of service, lies here. His brother Gavillius dedicated this.
EDCS-31900304.summary: A tombstone for a soldier of the I Cohort of Lucenses, set up by his brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900305.text_edition: \n Lucius Domitius Luci filius nniensi Aquila veteranus legionis VII domo Myliada sic stipendiorum XXXIII vivus fecit sibi et Caio Domitio Aniensi Aquilino militi legionis VII stipendiorum XX honesta missione A V\n
EDCS-31900305.translation: Lucius Domitius Aquila, son of Lucius, from Niens, a veteran of the VII Legion, with 33 years of service, made this while alive for himself and for Gaius Domitius Aquilinus of Mylias, a soldier of the VII Legion, with 20 years of service, honourably discharged.
EDCS-31900305.summary: A funerary monument for a veteran and a serving soldier of the VII Legion, made by one of them while alive, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900306.text_edition: \n Rufus Angeti filius miles cohortis I Lucensium annorum XXX stipendiorum XI hic situs est heres posuit\n
EDCS-31900306.translation: Rufus, son of Angetius, a soldier of the I Cohort of Lucenses, aged 30 and with 11 years of service, lies here. His heir dedicated this.
EDCS-31900306.summary: A tombstone for a soldier of the I Cohort of Lucenses, set up by his heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900307.text_edition: \n Marcus Sosius Marci filius Fabia Sebastopoli veteranus? legionis VII \n
EDCS-31900307.translation: Marcus Sosius, son of Marcus, of the Fabia tribe, a veteran(?) of the VII Legion from Sebastopolis.
EDCS-31900307.summary: An incomplete inscription possibly recording the details of a veteran of the VII Legion, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31900309.text_edition: \n Vanaius Venionti filius domo Bodionticus? eques cohortis III Alpinorum annorum LIIII stipendiorum XXV hic situs est Valeria et Marcella posuerunt\n
EDCS-31900309.translation: Vanaius, son of Veniontius, from Bodionticus(?), an equestrian of the III Cohort of Alpinorum, aged 54 and with 25 years of service, lies here. Valeria and Marcella dedicated this.
EDCS-31900309.summary: A tombstone for an equestrian officer of the III Cohort of Alpinorum, set up by two women, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32100153.text_edition: \n I et Iacus CE frater et -A Festus coniugi pientissimae\n
EDCS-32100153.translation: To Iacchus and [---]us, his brother, and to Festus [---] for their most dutiful wife.
EDCS-32100153.summary: An incomplete dedication to a wife, likely funerary, with multiple dedicators and a possible family connection, possibly Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32100154.text_edition: \n e Ni<g=C>idius filius us VE us libertus eius maritu[s ------ \n
EDCS-32100154.translation: His freedman husband [---]us, son of Nigidius, [---] VE...
EDCS-32100154.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for a wife, mentioning her freedman husband and father, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32100155.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Marti Marcus Ulpius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis legionis X geminae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32100155.translation: Marcus Ulpius Vitalis, a beneficiary of a consular legate of the X Gemina Legion, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Mars.
EDCS-32100155.summary: A votive inscription by a soldier of the X Gemina Legion to Jupiter and Mars, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200004.text_edition: \n Trosia Prima Buccionis liberta votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32200004.translation: Trosia Prima, freedwoman of Buccius, gladly paid her vow, as deserved.
EDCS-32200004.summary: This is a votive inscription by Trosia Prima, a freedwoman, likely to a deity.

EDCS-32200008.text_edition: \n ssimo qui vixit dies XX Publius IIIIIIvir pateret sibi\n
EDCS-32200008.translation: Publius, a priest of the Six (collegium), who lived for 20 days, set this up for himself.
EDCS-32200008.summary: This is a funerary or memorial inscription for Publius, who died very young, set up by his father.

EDCS-32200011.summary: This is a dedication or tombstone inscription for Caius Oppius Clemens.

EDCS-32200014.text_edition: \n Dis sacrum Manibus Flavius Rufantius Deidmu vivus sibi posuit et Aureliae Melliti coniugi pientissimae quae vicxit sic nnos XLVII\n
EDCS-32200014.translation: Sacred to the Shades. Flavius Rufantius set this up for himself while alive, and for his most devoted wife Aurelia Mellita, who lived for 47 years.
EDCS-32200014.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Flavius Rufantius for himself and his wife Aurelia Mellita, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus s(acrum) PPFOS PII sibi et coniugi -\n
EDCS-32200016.translation: Sacred to the Shades. P. P. F. O. S. Pii set this up for himself and his wife.
EDCS-32200016.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pii and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32200026.translation: Gaius Flavius Valens, a soldier of the legion...
EDCS-32200026.summary: This is an incomplete inscription likely recording the name and military affiliation of Gaius Flavius Valens.

EDCS-32200027.text_edition: \n Bindo Neptuno sacrum ob honorem IV-sti PROG amici posuerunt\n
EDCS-32200027.translation: Bindo and PROG, friends, set this up in honor of Neptune.
EDCS-32200027.summary: This is a dedication to Neptune, set up by Bindo and PROG.

EDCS-32201011.text_edition: \n Iovi Iunoni Minervae Marcus Ulpius Canius beneficiarius consularis legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32201011.translation: Marcus Ulpius Canius, a special assistant to a consular legate of the I Adiutrix legion, gladly paid his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-32201011.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Ulpius Canius, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201014.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Naevius Maximus beneficiarius consularis legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32201014.translation: Lucius Naevius Maximus, a special assistant to a consular legate of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, gladly paid his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-32201014.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Naevius Maximus, a soldier of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201016.text_edition: \n Lucius Aurelius Lupus cum suis donum dedit\n
EDCS-32201016.translation: Lucius Aurelius Lupus gave this gift with his family.
EDCS-32201016.summary: This is a dedication of a gift by Lucius Aurelius Lupus and his family.

EDCS-32201017.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti pontifici maximo tribunicia postestate XXI consuli IIII decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-32201017.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, son of the divine Hadrian, grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, in his 21st year of tribunician power, consul for the 4th time, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-32201017.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from a public monument or building, dated to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-32201021.text_edition: \n ANI Flavii Silvanus et Severus patri filii D \n
EDCS-32201021.translation: To Flavius Silvanus and Severus, his sons, from Flavius.
EDCS-32201021.summary: This is a dedication from a father, Flavius, to his sons, Flavius Silvanus and Severus, likely on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-32201022.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seiae Flavi Silvani servae LXXV Scorpus maritus pro pietate\n
EDCS-32201022.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Seia, a slave of Flavius Silvanus, aged 75. Scorpus, her husband, set this up out of devotion.
EDCS-32201022.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Seia, a freedwoman, set up by her husband Scorpus, recording her age and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201024.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Minervae Flavius M AV--- b(ene)f(iciarius)? co(n)s(ularis) legionis X |(centurio) legionis XI votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32201024.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, and Minerva. Flavius M[---], a beneficiarius consularis of the 10th legion, a centurion of the 11th legion, has paid his vow freely and deservedly.
EDCS-32201024.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Capitoline Triad by Flavius, a military officer serving in the 10th and 11th legions, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Catiae annorum L iugius coniugi pientissimae et sibi vivo posuit\n
EDCS-32201027.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iugius placed this for his most devoted wife Ulpia Catia, aged 50, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-32201027.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ulpia Catia, set up by her husband Iugius, recording her age and the fact it was erected during their lifetime, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimia Matisa vixit annos LX Septimius Victor sibi et coniugi vivus bene merenti pro pietate\n
EDCS-32201029.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Septimia Matisa lived 60 years. Septimius Victor set this up for himself and his deserving wife, while alive, out of devotion.
EDCS-32201029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Septimia Matisa, set up by Septimius Victor for himself and his wife, recording her age and erected during their lifetime, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201030.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio loci Lucius Sicinius Macrinus beneficiarius consularis Pannoniae superioris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32201030.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the place. Lucius Sicinius Macrinus, a beneficiarius consularis of Upper Pannonia, has paid his vow freely and deservedly.
EDCS-32201030.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and the local genius by Lucius Sicinius Macrinus, a military official in Upper Pannonia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201031.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimio A vet<e=F>rano vixit annos LII Titia marito pientissimo et sibi C B \n
EDCS-32201031.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titia placed this for her most devoted husband Septimius Avetranus, who lived 52 years, and for herself.
EDCS-32201031.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Septimius Avetranus, set up by his wife Titia, recording his age and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201032.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae V<e=F>turius Lucius votum solvit \n
EDCS-32201032.translation: To Sol Invictus Mithras. Veturrius Lucius paid his vow.
EDCS-32201032.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by Veturrius Lucius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period when Mithraism was popular.

EDCS-32201033.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a funerary epitaph, mentioning the name Saturnina and likely recording ages or relationships, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32201034.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name Lucius Frontus, possibly part of a funerary or dedication text.

EDCS-32201037.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Publius Aelius C...
EDCS-32201037.summary: This is a funerary inscription beginning with the standard invocation 'Dis Manibus' for Publius Aelius C., with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-32201040.text_edition: \n LIO Iuniori decurioni coloniae Salonitanorum quaestori aedili IIviro iure dicundo designato \n
EDCS-32201040.translation: ...to Iunior, decurion of the colony of Salona, quaestor, aedile, and designated duovir for dispensing justice.
EDCS-32201040.summary: This inscription lists the career of an unnamed individual who held significant magistracies in the colony of Salona.

EDCS-32201041.text_edition: \n C MERCAISA AEV IIIIvir iure dicundo CH ordinis II IAIII C HAC VXIICIIAIIIS C ESTCCS\n
EDCS-32201041.translation: [...] C. Mercaisa AEV [died aged] IIII, duovir for dispensing justice, CH of the order, II IAIII C. Hac VIICIIAIIIS C. ESTCCS.
EDCS-32201041.summary: This is a highly abbreviated and difficult inscription, likely recording civic offices and possibly a death date or age for someone named Mercaisa.

EDCS-32201043.text_edition: \n -? Aelio Epidio Statorius Florus amico bene merenti\n
EDCS-32201043.translation: To Aelius Epidius, his friend who deserved well.
EDCS-32201043.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aelius Epidius from his friend Statorius Florus, praising him for his merits.

EDCS-32201046.text_edition: \n Flavio ? Crescenti filia pientissima patri bono bene merenti testamento iussa\n
EDCS-32201046.translation: Flavius Crescenti, his most dutiful daughter, for her good father, who deserved well, as instructed in her will.
EDCS-32201046.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from a daughter to her deserving father, fulfilling a testamentary instruction.

EDCS-32201047.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata Fortunio <f=E>ilio defuncto nnorum III mensium VII \n
EDCS-32201047.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fortunata [places this] for her son Fortunius, who died aged 3 months and 7 days.
EDCS-32201047.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a very young child, Fortunius, from his mother Fortunata.

EDCS-32201048.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Murcidio Maximo defuncto annorum X mensium VI Murcidia Maxima mater\n
EDCS-32201048.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Murcidius Maximus, who died aged 10 years and 6 months. Murcidia Maxima, his mother.
EDCS-32201048.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a mother, Murcidia Maxima, for her son Marcus Murcidius Maximus, who died in childhood.

EDCS-32201049.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Murcidiae Maximae heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-32201049.translation: To the spirits of the departed. His heirs placed [this] for Murcidia Maxima, who deserved well.
EDCS-32201049.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by the heirs for Murcidia Maxima, honoring her merits.

EDCS-32201052.text_edition: \n Viscia Paula soror et coniunx Viscia Firma uno anno obiere simul duo mensibus inter nonaginta annos vixerunt adque duobus sic quadraginta et quinque soror reliquos fuit uxor quarum pro meritis tumulis duo corpora iunxi Quintus Viscius Valens duplici pietate propinquus\n
EDCS-32201052.translation: Viscia Paula, sister and wife, and Viscia Firma, died in the same year; together they lived for two months within ninety years, and thus one was forty-five years younger than the other; Quintus Viscius Valens, a relative, joined two bodies in one tomb out of double devotion for their merits.
EDCS-32201052.summary: This complex funerary inscription records the deaths of two sisters, Viscia Paula and Viscia Firma, detailing their ages and their relative Quintus Viscius Valens's act of devotion.

EDCS-32201056.summary: This very fragmentary inscription seems to record a personal name, possibly of a deceased individual, with an uncertain attribution.

EDCS-32201057.summary: This is a very short dedication to the god Liber Pater.

EDCS-32201058.text_edition: \n Deo Magno Conservatori loci huius et omnium suorum Aurelius Terentianus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-32201058.translation: Aurelius Terentianus willingly fulfilled his vow to the Great God, Preserver of this place and of all his kin.
EDCS-32201058.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aurelius Terentianus to a "Great God" protecting his locality and family.

EDCS-32201059.text_edition: \n Iunia Rhodine cum coniuge et fili- Deum Matri Magnae aedem refecit et ampliavit votum susceptum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32201059.translation: Iunia Rhodine, with her husband and children, restored and enlarged the temple of the Great Mother Goddess, willingly fulfilling her vow as deserved.
EDCS-32201059.summary: This inscription records the restoration and enlargement of a temple to the Great Mother Goddess by Iunia Rhodine and her family, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-32201060.summary: This is a brief dedication to the god Nocturnus.

EDCS-32201064.translation: From the cohort of --- Corneli Prisci...
EDCS-32201064.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely relates to a military unit and possibly a commander named Corneli Priscus.

EDCS-32201065.text_edition: \n Flavius Serenus ex primicerio vixit nnos X\n
EDCS-32201065.translation: Flavius Serenus, formerly chief centurion, lived 10 years.
EDCS-32201065.summary: This inscription records the death of Flavius Serenus, a former primicerius (chief centurion), at the age of 10.

EDCS-32201068.text_edition: \n cohortis ex peditibus defuncto nnorum stipendiorum XIIII Amandus fili-- \n
EDCS-32201068.translation: Amandus, son of [a soldier] of the cohort, a foot soldier, who died after 14 years of service...
EDCS-32201068.summary: This fragmentary epitaph commemorates Amandus, the son of a foot soldier in a cohort who served for 14 years.

EDCS-32201070.text_edition: \n ------ im]munis cohortis a heres pro pietate fecit a R--- et \n
EDCS-32201070.translation: ... exempted from service in the cohort, his heir made this out of duty, from R...
EDCS-32201070.summary: This fragmentary inscription records an heir fulfilling an obligation concerning someone exempted from cohort service.

EDCS-32201080.text_edition: \n Africano defunto mensium VIII Aurelia Fortunata et Aurelius Saturninus filio infelicissimo\n
EDCS-32201080.translation: To Africanus, deceased at 8 months, Aurelia Fortunata and Aurelius Saturninus, to their most unhappy son.
EDCS-32201080.summary: This is a tombstone for a child named Africanus, who died at 8 months old, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-32201085.summary: This inscription appears to record the name Aurelius Epi, likely part of a funerary monument, with much of the text lost.

EDCS-32201086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Asterio defuncto nnorum XL Aurelia Octavia coniux inconparabili et Aurelius Dionysius patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-32201086.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Octavia, his incomparable wife, and Aurelius Dionysius set this up for their deserving father, Aurelius Asterius, who died at the age of 40.
EDCS-32201086.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Asterius, erected by his wife and son.

EDCS-32201087.text_edition: \n Aurelius Titianus qui vixit nnos plus minus XXXI menses II Aurelius Trophimus et Praecillia Thallussa sic nutritores piissimo de suo posuerunt\n
EDCS-32201087.translation: Aurelius Titianus, who lived about 31 years and 2 months. Aurelius Trophimus and Praecillia Thallussa, his devoted foster parents, set this up at their own expense.
EDCS-32201087.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Titianus, erected by his foster parents.

EDCS-32201088.summary: This inscription likely records the name Aurelia A, possibly on a funerary monument, with most of the text missing.

EDCS-32201089.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caerellia Succes[sa]...
EDCS-32201089.summary: This inscription begins with a dedication to the underworld deities for Caerellia Successa, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-32201092.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cania Quadratia Canioio Lucifero filio qui vicxit sic annos XXX mater i<n=M>felicissima posuit \n
EDCS-32201092.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. His mother, most unhappy, set this up for Canius Lucifero, her son, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-32201092.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Canius Lucifero, erected by his grieving mother.

EDCS-32201094.text_edition: \n ------ v]ixit annis LXVI Clara marito inco[mparabili ------\n
EDCS-32201094.translation: ...lived 66 years. Clara for her incomparable husband...
EDCS-32201094.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of a man and the devotion of his wife Clara, with significant portions missing.

EDCS-32201095.summary: This inscription records the death of a woman named Claudia, with the rest of the text lost.

EDCS-32201098.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Maximinae quae vixit annos XX Cornelius ius pater filiae infelicissimae fecit\n
EDCS-32201098.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cornelius set this up for his most unhappy daughter, Cornelia Maximina, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-32201098.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Maximina, erected by her father.

EDCS-32201099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ia Crescentilla Septimiae Secundinae matri pientissimae defunctae nnorum XL \n
EDCS-32201099.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Septimia Secundina, his most devoted mother, who died at the age of 40. ia Crescentilla...
EDCS-32201099.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Septimia Secundina by her son ia Crescentilla, mentioning her age at death.

EDCS-32201101.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Euryalini Onesimus maritus et filii Ursulianus et Ursina matri pientissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-32201101.translation: To the Divine Manes. Onesimus, husband, and his sons Ursulianus and Ursina erected this for their most devoted mother.
EDCS-32201101.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband and sons for their mother, Euryalinus.

EDCS-32201102.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felicissimae bene merenti quae vixit aannos \n
EDCS-32201102.translation: To the Divine Manes. For the well-deserving Felicissima, who lived for [some number of] years.
EDCS-32201102.summary: A funerary epitaph for Felicissima, recording her name and acknowledging her merit.

EDCS-32201104.text_edition: \n defunct-- annorum III mensium Titus Flavius Syneros alumn\n
EDCS-32201104.translation: Deceased at the age of 3 years, [and some number of] months. Titus Flavius Syneros, foster-child...
EDCS-32201104.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child named Titus Flavius Syneros, identifying him as a foster-child.

EDCS-32201105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Rufinae annorum plus minus XXVI Donatus coniu<g=C>i i<n=A>conparabili sic \n
EDCS-32201105.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Flavia Rufina, aged more or less 26 years. Donatus, for his incomparable wife, thus...
EDCS-32201105.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Donatus for his wife Flavia Rufina, noting her age and his deep affection.

EDCS-32201107.text_edition: \n Fundaniae Cai libertae Ias hic sita <e=F>st Caius Quintius Proculus heres fecit \n
EDCS-32201107.translation: For Fundania Ias, freedwoman of Caius. Gaius Quintius Proculus, the heir, made this here.
EDCS-32201107.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fundania Ias, a freedwoman, erected by her heir Gaius Quintius Proculus.

EDCS-32201108.translation: ... Galaticus, husband, for his sweetest wife...
EDCS-32201108.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment mentioning a husband, Galaticus, and his 'sweetest wife'.

EDCS-32201109.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly containing a name or a title.

EDCS-32201110.text_edition: \n M Illyrica Iulio Diadumeno marito pientissimo defuncto annorum \n
EDCS-32201110.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Julius Diadumenus, her most devoted husband, deceased at the age of...
EDCS-32201110.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julius Diadumenus, described as a devoted husband, with his age partially recorded.

EDCS-32201111.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gaius Iulius Fil...
EDCS-32201111.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment starting with the dedication to the Divine Manes and the beginning of a name, Gaius Iulius Fil.

EDCS-32201112.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording someone erecting something for their patron, likely Valerius.

EDCS-32201113.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia<e=F> Fau<s>tinae de<f=E>un<c>tae annorum XXV posuerunt parentes pientissimi? --- \n
EDCS-32201113.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Julia Faustina, who died at the age of 25, her most devoted parents placed [this monument]. [---]
EDCS-32201113.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julia Faustina, set up by her parents, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201114.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Julia Satriena Chresimis.
EDCS-32201114.summary: A funerary dedication to Julia Satriena Chresimis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201118.text_edition: \n Obultroniae Marci libertae Romanae coniugi et tu hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-32201118.translation: To Obultronia Romana, freedwoman of Marcus, [as] wife, and you shall not claim an heir for this monument.
EDCS-32201118.summary: An epitaph for Obultronia Romana, specifying her relationship as wife and stating that no heir will succeed to the monument, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201119.text_edition: \n ------ de]funct-- nnorum X occoni Fortunat co[niugi ------\n
EDCS-32201119.translation: [---] who died [---] aged X. To Occonius Fortunatus, [husband?] of [---]
EDCS-32201119.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph recording a death and mentioning Occonius Fortunatus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papiriae Eutunae Arrius Primus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-32201120.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Papiria Eutuna, Arrius Primus placed [this monument] for his deserving wife.
EDCS-32201120.summary: A funerary dedication by Arrius Primus to his wife, Papiria Eutuna, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201125.text_edition: \n s Sex tus et ia Chres[te ------\n
EDCS-32201125.summary: A very fragmented inscription mentioning names, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201126.text_edition: \n Sorto Serviliae Eleutherionis et Lucio Servilio Luci filio Frontoni annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-32201126.translation: For Sorto, freedwoman of Servilia Eleutherio, and for Lucius Servilius Fronto, son of Lucius, aged 18.
EDCS-32201126.summary: A funerary dedication for Sorto and Lucius Servilius Fronto, mentioning their names and age, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201128.text_edition: \n Aelius Tertius et CISIClia sibi vivi fecerun<t=L> et libertis suis et posterisque eorum in <f=E>ronte sunt pedes viginti in agro sunt pedes viginti quinque \n
EDCS-32201128.translation: Aelius Tertius and Clodia made this for themselves while alive, and for their freedmen and their descendants. In front there are twenty feet, in the field there are twenty-five feet.
EDCS-32201128.summary: A funerary monument deed recording the dimensions of the plot and intended beneficiaries, set up by Aelius Tertius and Clodia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201129.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tesseriae Severinae defunctae nnorum XX Tesserius Severus et Tesseria Aphrodisia filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-32201129.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Tesseria Severina, who died at the age of 20, Tesserius Severus and Tesseria Aphrodisia placed [this monument] for their most devoted daughter.
EDCS-32201129.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tesseria Severina, set up by her parents, Tesserius Severus and Tesseria Aphrodisia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201131.translation: Vera Curtia placed [this monument] for her true daughter.
EDCS-32201131.summary: A funerary dedication by Vera Curtia for her daughter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201132.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Viviae Papiriae quae fuit annorum sic I mensium III Gemella mater fili<a=H>e infelicissimae \n
EDCS-32201132.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Vivia Papiria, who lived for one year and three months. Her extremely unhappy mother Gemella [erected this].
EDCS-32201132.summary: A funerary epitaph for Vivia Papiria, recording her age and erected by her mother, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201133.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-32201133.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Celerina dulces sic habe sic Gorgonio tuo castissima senper sic cuius simplicitas et obsequentia laudatur et amatur ubique et cuius <c=O>onsiliis nulla decepta amore aeternamque domum ut fecit superstes maritus sic maeret virginia votissima mihi uno marito et vos be<n=A>e vivendo haec saltem sperare de<b=P>etis Ulpius Gorgonius piissimus Celerinae conpari pientissimae cum qua concorditer vicxit sic nnos XXIIII menses V dies XXV vi<v=B>i sibi posuerunt posterisque suis \n
EDCS-32201133.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. O Ulpia Celerina, may you have your sweet Gorgonius, always chaste, whose simplicity and obedience were praised and loved everywhere, and whose counsel never deceived, and who, surviving him, made for her eternal home. Her husband grieves, a devoted virgin to me, with only one husband. And you, live happily and at least hope for these things. Ulpius Gorgonius, most devoted, to his most devoted wife Celerina, with whom he lived in harmony for 24 years, 5 months, and 25 days; they erected this for themselves while alive and for their descendants.
EDCS-32201133.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpia Celerina, set up by her devoted husband Ulpius Gorgonius, detailing their long and harmonious marriage of 24 years, 5 months, and 25 days, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32201134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus lio piano ---lius Ve filius Flac[cilla ------\n
EDCS-32201134.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lio Piano, son of ---lius Ve, son of Flacc[illa] ---
EDCS-32201134.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Lio Piano, identifying his father and maternal lineage, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-32300017.text_edition: \n Sextus Oppius Rufus testamento fieri iussit sibi et Sexto Oppio Dinae patri et Oppiae Sextae libertae Surae matri\n
EDCS-32300017.translation: Sextus Oppius Rufus ordered this to be made by his will for himself, and for his father Sextus Oppius Dina, and for his mother Oppia Sura, his freedwoman.
EDCS-32300017.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Sextus Oppius Rufus in his will for himself, his father, and his freedwoman mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300019.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly a funerary marker, identifying an individual as the son of Lucius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300020.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that likely records the name and perhaps age or death date of an individual identified as the son of Augustus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300021.text_edition: \n Dasas annorum IX Pantus Tit Panes filio\n
EDCS-32300021.translation: Dasas, aged nine, [erected by] Pantus Tit Panes for his son.
EDCS-32300021.summary: A funerary epitaph for Dasas, aged nine, erected by his father Pantus Tit Panes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300022.summary: A funerary epitaph identifying Tritus Druanus as the son of Plaheninus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Suttis IOM posuit sibi annorum LXI\n
EDCS-32300023.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Suttis erected this for herself, aged sixty-one.
EDCS-32300023.summary: A funerary epitaph for Suttis, who erected the monument for herself at the age of sixty-one, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300025.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that begins with the name Ulpius followed by a partial age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300026.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus AEQIOM annorum et Dasio \n
EDCS-32300026.translation: To the spirits of Aeqius, aged [---], and of Dasia.
EDCS-32300026.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aeqius and Dasia, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-32300027.text_edition: \n Armato Augusto sacrum Mattonia Tertia libens posuit\n
EDCS-32300027.translation: Mattonia Tertia willingly dedicated this to Armatus Augustus.
EDCS-32300027.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Armatus Augustus, made by Mattonia Tertia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300028.translation: Aelius Maximinus dedicated this to Diana.
EDCS-32300028.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana by Aelius Maximinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300029.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Titus Flavius Secundus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300029.translation: Titus Flavius Secundus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Diana Augusta.
EDCS-32300029.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana Augusta by Titus Flavius Secundus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300031.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a personal name, possibly from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300033.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Ursio Lavoni coniugi bene merenti defunctae annorum XXX posuit\n
EDCS-32300033.translation: Titus Flavius Ursio dedicated this to his deserving wife Lavonia, who died at the age of 30.
EDCS-32300033.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Titus Flavius Ursio to his wife Lavonia, who died at the age of 30, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300034.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name and age of Victor, likely funerary and from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300036.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly containing the name Victorinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.
