EDCS-32300038.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto sacrum Caius Terentius Celsus evocatus Augusti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300038.translation: Caius Terentius Celsus, an evocatus Augusti, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars Augustus.
EDCS-32300038.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mars Augustus by Caius Terentius Celsus, a special soldier, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300039.text_edition: \n Quintus Gavius Quinti filius Arnensis Optatus domo Brixello miles legionis XI annorum XXXIII stipendiorum XIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-32300039.translation: Quintus Gavius Optatus, son of Quintus, of the Arnensis tribe, from Brixellum, a soldier of the 11th legion, aged 33, with 13 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-32300039.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Gavius Optatus, a soldier of the 11th legion, detailing his age and service length, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32300040.text_edition: \n Iano p(atri) Publius Ae<l=I>(ius) Castus votum solvit libens merito somnio admonitus \n
EDCS-32300040.translation: To Janus the Father, Publius Aelius Castus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, having been warned by a dream.
EDCS-32300040.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Janus by Publius Aelius Castus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300043.text_edition: \n D(is)? M(anibus) Valeri Valeriani militis legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis nnorum XXXVII \n
EDCS-32300043.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Valerianus, soldier of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, aged 37.
EDCS-32300043.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerianus, a soldier of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32300045.translation: [---] soldier of the [---] cohort, pia fidelis, aged [---]
EDCS-32300045.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording a soldier of a cohort, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32300049.text_edition: \n ------ Lo]llio mo filio t(estamento) fieri iussit arbitratu \n
EDCS-32300049.translation: [---] his son Lollius ordered to be made by his will, under the supervision of [---]
EDCS-32300049.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating a son, Lollius, commissioned a monument by his will, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32300050.text_edition: \n Situs est crudelem funus puaero Gaio Paetronio Valenti annorum XIIII parentes desola[ti ------\n
EDCS-32300050.translation: He lies here, a cruel death, our son Gaius Paetronius Valens, aged 14, his desolate parents [---]
EDCS-32300050.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Paetronius Valens, a boy of 14, commemorated by his parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32300055.text_edition: \n Quintus Publicius et Flavia Primula Augustioni delicato suo Dis Manibus\n
EDCS-32300055.translation: Quintus Publicius and Flavia Primula to Augustio, their darling, to the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-32300055.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Quintus Publicius and Flavia Primula for their beloved pet, Augustio, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300056.text_edition: \n Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius Augustus imperator pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXVI dedicavit Lucio Volusio Saturnino legato pro praetore\n
EDCS-32300056.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 36th time, dedicated [this] under Lucius Volusius Saturninus, legate pro praetore.
EDCS-32300056.summary: This is a dedication inscription by Emperor Tiberius, dated to 36 AD, commemorating a public work overseen by the legate Lucius Volusius Saturninus.

EDCS-32300058.text_edition: \n Publius M S Silvano votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300058.translation: Publius M. S. willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-32300058.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus by Publius M. S., likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300059.text_edition: \n Curticus Sergia votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300059.translation: Curticus Sergia willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-32300059.summary: This is a votive inscription by Curticus Sergia, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300060.summary: This is a simple funerary or commemorative inscription for Aeta, daughter of Roscius Turius, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300063.summary: This inscription appears to be the beginning of a personal name, possibly from a funerary monument, with incomplete text.

EDCS-32300064.translation: Here lie the bones for you, [---]
EDCS-32300064.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription, beginning with 'Here lie the bones', with the rest of the text lost.

EDCS-32300065.text_edition: \n Tito Iulio Titi filio Nicephoro Tito Iulio Hilaro Tito Iulio T(iti) filio Sergia Caro Petillia Volsoni filia Maxima viva fecit\n
EDCS-32300065.translation: To Titus Iulius Nicephorus, son of Titus, and Titus Iulius Hilarus, son of Titus, and Titus Iulius Charus, son of Titus Iulius Sergius, his son, Maxima, daughter of Petillia Volsoni, made this while alive.
EDCS-32300065.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Maxima, likely a wife or mother, to her son Titus Iulius Nicephorus and other male relatives, suggesting a family burial plot.

EDCS-32300066.summary: This inscription seems to be the beginning of a dedication to Gaius Julius, possibly on a tomb or public monument, with the text incomplete.

EDCS-32300068.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Rutilius Narcissus Rutilio Narcisso <p=R>atri b<e=G>n<e=G> m<e=G>r<e=G>nti pos<ui=IV>t plus minus qui bixxit sic annos LXV \n
EDCS-32300068.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Rutilius Narcissus placed this for his well-deserving father, Rutilius Narcissus, with whom he lived for more or less 65 years.
EDCS-32300068.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Rutilius Narcissus commemorates his deceased father, also named Rutilius Narcissus, and notes the father's lifespan of 65 years.

EDCS-32300069.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Rutiliae Victoriae annorum XXVII mensium V coniugi carissimaesius Rus maritus cum qua vixi \n
EDCS-32300069.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Rutilia Victoria, aged 27 years and 5 months, his dearest wife, Rus, her husband, with whom he lived [---]
EDCS-32300069.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a husband named Rus to his wife Rutilia Victoria, who died at the age of 27 years and 5 months.

EDCS-32300070.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Rutili- F Rutili- T Rutilia F infelicissimis filiis bene merentibus qui vixerunt Rutili- F annos XXIII Rutili- T annos XXV mater infelicissima posuit filiis suis tituluam\n
EDCS-32300070.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Rutilius, son of Rutilius, and Rutilius, son of Titus, his most unfortunate sons, who lived 23 years and 25 years respectively. Their most unfortunate mother placed this monument for her sons.
EDCS-32300070.summary: This funerary inscription is a monument set up by a mother for her two sons, identified as Rutilius (son of Rutilius) and Rutilius (son of Titus), who died at ages 23 and 25.

EDCS-32300074.text_edition: \n Titus Loantius Rufus praepositus Iapodum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300074.translation: Titus Loantius Rufus, prefect of the Iapodes, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-32300074.summary: This inscription records Titus Loantius Rufus, who held the position of prefect of the Iapodes, fulfilling a vow, likely to a deity.

EDCS-32300076.text_edition: \n Martinus qui Felicissimus s vixit menses VIII memoriam Lochu(s) fecit\n
EDCS-32300076.translation: Martinus, who lived 8 months [---], Lochus made this memorial.
EDCS-32300076.summary: This is a memorial inscription commemorating Martinus, who lived only 8 months, and was set up by someone named Lochus.

EDCS-32300080.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple epitaph or marker for Gaius Julius Mustela, with no further details.

EDCS-32300082.translation: To Jupiter, Quintus Papinius Proculus (dedicated this).
EDCS-32300082.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Quintus Papinius Proculus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-32300083.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a dedication to someone named Iulia, with the full context missing.

EDCS-32300084.text_edition: \n Ut decet parenti filius fecit suo Lucius Statius Luci filius Venetus hic situs est\n
EDCS-32300084.translation: As befits a parent, the son made this for himself: Lucius Statius Venetus, son of Lucius, is buried here.
EDCS-32300084.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of Lucius Statius Venetus, set up by his son.

EDCS-32300085.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus Publio Licinio Valeriano pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate \n
EDCS-32300085.translation: To the Emperors Caesars, to Publius Licinius Valerianus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power...
EDCS-32300085.summary: This is the beginning of an imperial dedication, likely to Emperor Valerian, mentioning his titles.

EDCS-32300087.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Valerio Diocletiano Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-32300087.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-32300087.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-32400035.translation: To Volusia Tertia, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-32400035.summary: This inscription records Volusia Tertia, daughter of Lucius, likely part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-32400038.summary: This fragmented inscription contains parts of a name and possibly a number, its purpose is unclear.

EDCS-32400312.text_edition: \n Lucius Artorius Castus primus pilus legionis V Macedonicae praefectus legionis VI victricis \n
EDCS-32400312.translation: Lucius Artorius Castus, chief centurion of the Legio V Macedonica, prefect of the Legio VI Victrix.
EDCS-32400312.summary: This inscription records the career of Lucius Artorius Castus, detailing his military promotions.

EDCS-32500102.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sextus Aurelius Arg sibi et Ae\n
EDCS-32500102.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sextus Aurelius Arg(o) for himself and Ae(lia)...
EDCS-32500102.summary: This is the beginning of a funerary dedication by Sextus Aurelius Arg(o) for himself and another person.

EDCS-32500105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Marcellina vixit annos XLV pia mater et Bonus et Urbanus Marcellinae infelicissimae et sibi ipsorum superviventibus titulum posuerunt \n
EDCS-32500105.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aurelia Marcellina, who lived 45 years, a dutiful mother, and Bonus and Urbanus erected this monument for the most unhappy Marcellina and for themselves, the survivors.
EDCS-32500105.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Marcellina, erected by her family, also commemorating the survivors.

EDCS-32500106.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ipuienis vixit annos LXX IX\n
EDCS-32500106.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; she lived 79 years.
EDCS-32500106.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed woman who lived to be 79 years old.

EDCS-32500107.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Marcus Ulpius Marcianus cum suis libens posuit\n
EDCS-32500107.translation: Marcus Ulpius Marcianus with his family gladly dedicated this to Juno Regina.
EDCS-32500107.summary: A dedication to Juno Regina by Marcus Ulpius Marcianus and his household.

EDCS-32500108.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo <L=I>(ucii) Petronii Maximinus et Severus \n
EDCS-32500108.translation: Lucius Petronius Maximinus and Severus dedicated this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-32500108.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Petronius Maximinus and Severus.

EDCS-32500109.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Probo defuncto nnorum IV MAAMAo filio fecit\n
EDCS-32500109.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publius Aelius Probus, dead at 4 years of age, made this for his son.
EDCS-32500109.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Aelius Probus, who died at the age of 4, made by his father.

EDCS-32500110.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeli<o=S> Rufo et Tattuiae defunctae nnorum LX Publius Aelius Verus parentibus posuit \n
EDCS-32500110.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publius Aelius Verus dedicated this to his parents, Aelius Rufus and Tattuia, dead at 60 years of age.
EDCS-32500110.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Aelius Verus for his parents, Aelius Rufus and Tattuia, who died at ages unknown and 60 respectively.

EDCS-32500111.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Maximus et Mandeta vivi fecerunt sibi et Aureliae Macae defunctae annorum et Maximino defuncto annorum II carissimis\n
EDCS-32500111.translation: Aurelius Maximus and Mandeta, while alive, made this for themselves and for Aurelia Maca, deceased, and for Maximinus, deceased at 2 years of age, their dearest.
EDCS-32500111.summary: A funerary dedication made by Aurelius Maximus and Mandeta for themselves and for their deceased loved ones, Aurelia Maca and Maximinus (aged 2).

EDCS-32500114.text_edition: \n Aelia Zorada hic sita annorum LX Zanatis et Tatta matri bene merent(i)\n
EDCS-32500114.translation: Aelia Zorada lies here, 60 years of age; for Zanatis and Tatta her mother, deserving of goodwill.
EDCS-32500114.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Zorada, aged 60, and her mother Tatta, also possibly commemorated by Zanatis.

EDCS-32500117.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum Maximus IV- votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32500117.translation: Maximus, aged 4, fulfilled his vow to Fortuna Augusta, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-32500117.summary: A votive inscription by Maximus, aged 4, dedicated to Fortuna Augusta.

EDCS-32500120.text_edition: \n Caius Safius Severus aedilis IIIIvir quinquennalis patronus coloniae simulacrum ex sua pecunia posuit\n
EDCS-32500120.translation: Gaius Safius Severus, twice aedile, quinquennial magistrate, patron of the colony, dedicated this statue at his own expense.
EDCS-32500120.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of a statue by Gaius Safius Severus, who held various civic offices including patron of the colony.

EDCS-32500121.summary: A simple funerary epitaph stating that Bolana, a mother, is buried here.

EDCS-32500123.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Epictesi coniugi bene merenti Haermes sic datus locus a Caio Cli Censorino\n
EDCS-32500123.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Epictesis, my deserving wife. Haermes dedicated this plot of land by Gaius Cli Censorinus.
EDCS-32500123.summary: A funerary dedication for Epictesis by her husband Haermes, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500126.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Octavius Sabinus veteranus cohortis octavae voluntariorum et Aelia Titia vivi sibi fecerunt et Haemilio! Rufo filio et libertis suis\n
EDCS-32500126.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Octavius Sabinus, a veteran of the Eighth Cohort of Volunteers, and Aelia Titia, while alive, made this for themselves, and for Haemilius Rufus, their son, and for their freedmen.
EDCS-32500126.summary: A self-funded funerary monument for Lucius Octavius Sabinus, a veteran, and Aelia Titia, intended for themselves, their son, and freedmen, probably from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32500128.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Herculanus beneficiarius consularis legionis I Italicae\n
EDCS-32500128.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Julius Herculanus, a beneficiary official of the consular legate of the First Italic Legion.
EDCS-32500128.summary: An inscription recording Julius Herculanus's official position within the First Italic Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500129.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo iuxt<a=X> quod diivina - ] Ava Batoniana votum solvit \n
EDCS-32500129.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. [---] Ava Batoniana fulfilled a vow near [---] by divine [will].
EDCS-32500129.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter, mentioning Ava Batoniana and likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500130.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii N(ovensium) Publius Satrius \n
EDCS-32500130.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of the municipality of the Novensians. Publius Satrius [dedicated this].
EDCS-32500130.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and the Genius of Novensium by Publius Satrius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500132.text_edition: \n miles legionis beneficiarius consularis votum libens merito solvit d(ominis)? n(ostris)? Deciis Messiis consulibus \n
EDCS-32500132.translation: A soldier, a beneficiary official of the consular legate, willingly and deservedly fulfilled a vow to our lords, under the consulship of Decius Messius.
EDCS-32500132.summary: A votive inscription by a soldier, identified as a beneficiary official, mentioning the consuls Decius Messius, likely dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500133.text_edition: \n us beneficiarius consularis legionis X geminae \n
EDCS-32500133.translation: [---] a beneficiary official of the consular legate of the Tenth Gemina Legion.
EDCS-32500133.summary: An inscription fragment recording the official position of a beneficiary official within the Tenth Gemina Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500134.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo L NIENA beneficiarius consularis legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis\n
EDCS-32500134.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. L NIENA, a beneficiary official of the consular legate of the Eleventh Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion.
EDCS-32500134.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by L. NIENA, a beneficiary official serving in the Eleventh Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500137.text_edition: \n anus ex collegio saccariorum qui vixit annus sic XL menses IIII dies novem infelicissimus iubenis sic\n
EDCS-32500137.translation: [---] from the guild of saccarii, who lived 40 years, 4 months, and nine days. A most unhappy youth.
EDCS-32500137.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young member of the saccarii guild, recording his age and lamenting his early death, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500138.text_edition: \n Anchario defuncto o suo Ancharius Abellio et Iulius Augustalis filio et Iulia Macrina coniu<g=C>i posuerunt \n
EDCS-32500138.translation: Upon the death of Ancharius, his [father?]. Ancharius Abellio and Julius Augustalis, for their son, and Iulia Macrina, for her husband, placed this.
EDCS-32500138.summary: A funerary monument erected by Ancharius Abellio and Iulia Macrina for their deceased son and husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32500139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus t Apolloni vixit annos XXVII infelicissimo \n
EDCS-32500139.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Apollonius lived 27 years, most unhappily.
EDCS-32500139.summary: A funerary dedication to Apollonius, recording his age at death and a lament, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-32500140.text_edition: \n Aurelius Titus filius patri defuncto nnorum XXV\n
EDCS-32500140.translation: Aurelius Titus, son, his father having passed away, aged 25 years.
EDCS-32500140.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Titus, recording his age at death and mentioning his deceased father, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-32500141.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Ianuaria Aurelius P VPsimi III \n
EDCS-32500141.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Ianuaria, and Aurelius P., his father, [or similar relationship], for his most beloved [daughter/wife?], [age unspecified].
EDCS-32500141.summary: A fragmented funerary dedication likely mentioning Aurelia Ianuaria and Aurelius P. as dedicants for an unnamed individual, possibly a daughter or wife.

EDCS-32500142.text_edition: \n annorum mens<iu=VI>m I Ianuaria mater posuit \n
EDCS-32500142.translation: Ianuaria, mother, placed this, aged 6 months.
EDCS-32500142.summary: A funerary inscription recording a mother, Ianuaria, as the dedicator for a child who died at 6 months of age.

EDCS-32500143.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Hermadio Iuliae coniugi pientissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32500143.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Hermadius placed this for his wife Julia, most devoted, well-deserving.
EDCS-32500143.summary: A funerary dedication by Gaius Julius Hermadius for his devoted wife, Julia, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-32500145.text_edition: \n Laetae Laetillae Iulius Zoticus coniugi bene merenti defunctae annorum XV\n
EDCS-32500145.translation: To Laeta Laetilla, Julius Zoticus placed this for his well-deserving wife, who died aged 15 years.
EDCS-32500145.summary: A funerary dedication by Julius Zoticus for his wife, Laeta Laetilla, who died at age 15.

EDCS-32500146.text_edition: \n ]] []E [[]] [[]] merenti posu--- Larcia Ursil<l=I>a Aurelio Amantio liberto posuit qui vixit annos plus minus LX \n
EDCS-32500146.translation: ... well-deserving ... Larcia Ursila placed this for her freedman Aurelius Amantius, who lived more or less 60 years.
EDCS-32500146.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription by Larcia Ursila for her freedman Aurelius Amantius, recording his age at death.

EDCS-32500147.translation: For Maxima, aged 5 years, she placed this.
EDCS-32500147.summary: A funerary inscription for a child named Maxima, who died at age 5, with the dedicator unspecified but likely a parent.

EDCS-32500149.text_edition: \n IV coniugi infelicissimo qui vixit annos plus minus scripsit Naevius Cassius fratri misero XXXVIII\n
EDCS-32500149.translation: To [his wife], most unhappy, who lived more or less 38 years, wrote Naevius Cassius, for his miserable brother.
EDCS-32500149.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription where Naevius Cassius commemorates his brother, who died at approximately 38 years old, and also mentions an unhappy wife.

EDCS-32500150.text_edition: \n IVL LXX SECV Aelia Crescentilla matri M \n
EDCS-32500150.translation: Aelia Crescentilla placed this for her mother M[...], aged 70 years.
EDCS-32500150.summary: A funerary inscription by Aelia Crescentilla dedicating a monument to her mother, who died at age 70.

EDCS-32500151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titio Quintiliano infanti infelicissimo qui vixit annos V pater infelicissimus posuit\n
EDCS-32500151.translation: To the Divine Spirits. His most unfortunate father placed this for Titius Quintilianus, an unfortunate infant, who lived 5 years.
EDCS-32500151.summary: A funerary epitaph for an infant named Titius Quintilianus, set up by his grieving father, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32500152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vendimiae sic defunctae annorum VIII eques Romanus filiae posuit\n
EDCS-32500152.translation: To the Divine Spirits. An equestrian Roman dedicated this for his daughter Vendimia, who died at the age of 8 years.
EDCS-32500152.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl named Vendimia, erected by her father, a Roman equestrian, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32500153.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Viscio Victori Dis Manibus C \n
EDCS-32500153.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Viscius Victor. To the Divine Spirits. C...
EDCS-32500153.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription mentioning Viscius Victor and dedicated to the Divine Spirits, its full context and date are unclear.

EDCS-32500154.text_edition: \n vixit annos l Ursu coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-32500154.translation: She lived 50 years. Ursa for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-32500154.summary: A funerary epitaph for a wife who lived 50 years, set up by her husband Ursa, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32500156.text_edition: \n mus tribunus cohortis eiusdem praefectus cohortis I Flaviae Hispanorum\n
EDCS-32500156.translation: ... tribune of the same cohort, prefect of the I Flavian cohort of Spaniards.
EDCS-32500156.summary: A military inscription fragment identifying an officer's rank and command, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700227.text_edition: \n ius Publi libertus Phileros sevir libertus Himeros sevir ---? aedem deae Barbaricae sua pecunia faciendum coeraverunt idemque dedicaverunt probaveruntque\n
EDCS-32700227.translation: Publius' freedman Phileros, a freedman sevir, Himeros, a freedman sevir, and ... supervised the construction of the temple of the barbaric goddess with their own money, and they dedicated and approved it.
EDCS-32700227.summary: An inscription recording the construction and dedication of a temple to a 'barbaric goddess' by freedmen who held the position of sevir, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700229.translation: Sempronius Marcellinus in fulfillment of a vow to Diana.
EDCS-32700229.summary: An inscription recording a votive offering made to the goddess Diana by Sempronius Marcellinus, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700230.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum Papinius Maximianus Fortunae ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-32700230.translation: Sacred to Imperial Fortune. Papinius Maximianus placed this in fulfillment of a vision.
EDCS-32700230.summary: An inscription dedicating a shrine or offering to Imperial Fortune, made by Papinius Maximianus in response to a vision, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700237.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Septumius sic Secundus\n
EDCS-32700237.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Lucius Septimius, known as Secundus.
EDCS-32700237.summary: An inscription dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Septimius Secundus, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700238.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Augusto sacrum Iulia Auli liberta Maxima ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-32700238.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater Augustus. Iulia Maxima, freedwoman of Aulus, placed this in fulfillment of a vision.
EDCS-32700238.summary: An inscription dedicating a sacred object or place to Liber Pater Augustus, made by Iulia Maxima in fulfillment of a vision, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700242.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Quintus Cloelius Helius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32700242.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus, Quintus Cloelius Helius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32700242.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Augustus by Quintus Cloelius Helius, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700243.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Lupus Marciani ex visu sic posuit\n
EDCS-32700243.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus, Lupus, son of Marcianus, set this up as he saw in a vision.
EDCS-32700243.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Augustus by Lupus, son of Marcianus, who claims divine inspiration, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700248.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to mention a person named Lucius and possibly a consul, with incomplete details.

EDCS-32700253.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Acci Zosimi nnorum XIII mensium et Aeli Hermaisci annorum XXXV mensium Aelius Augusti libertus I provincia Dalmatia Accia Amabilis si\n
EDCS-32700253.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Lucius Accius Zosimus, 13 years old, and Aelius Hermaiscus, 35 years and 5 months old. Aelius Augusti libertus, from the province of Dalmatia, Accia Amabilis...
EDCS-32700253.summary: This is a tombstone inscription commemorating Lucius Accius Zosimus and Aelius Hermaiscus, set up by Accia Amabilis, a freedwoman of Aelius, from Dalmatia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700255.text_edition: \n Aurelius s duplicarius legionis VIII piae fidelis \n
EDCS-32700255.translation: Aurelius, duplicarius of the 8th Legion Pia Fidelis.
EDCS-32700255.summary: This is an inscription identifying Aurelius as a duplicarius (a soldier receiving double pay) in the 8th Legion Pia Fidelis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700262.text_edition: \n Pompeius Titullus eques ? domo Dertona testamento fieri iussit ae Secundae uxoris Cordiae Cae uxori Secun[d------\n
EDCS-32700262.translation: Pompeius Titullus, an equestrian from Dertona, ordered by his will that this be made for his wife Secunda, for his wife Cordia, for Secunda...
EDCS-32700262.summary: This is a funerary inscription initiated by Pompeius Titullus, an equestrian from Dertona, to honor his wives Secunda and Cordia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700264.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Surus Victoris miles cohortis I miliariae Aureliae Terentiae hospitae nnorum XXXVII bene merenti posuit titulum\n
EDCS-32700264.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Surus, son of Victor, a soldier of the 1st Cohort Miliaria Aurelia, and for Terentia, his hospita (a woman with whom he had a formal relationship of hospitality), aged 37. He placed this monument...
EDCS-32700264.summary: This tombstone records Surus Victoris, a soldier of the 1st Cohort Miliaria Aurelia, and Terentia, his hospita, who died at age 37, likely erected during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700266.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Vettio Probo beneficiario consularis legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis Lucius Vettius Gratus et Vettia Ianuaria patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-32700266.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed, for Lucius Vettius Probus, beneficiarius (adjutant) of the consular legate of the 11th Legion Claudia Pia Fidelis. Lucius Vettius Gratus and Vettia Ianuaria set this up for their deserving father.
EDCS-32700266.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Lucius Vettius Gratus and Vettia Ianuaria to their father, Lucius Vettius Probus, an adjutant in the 11th Legion Claudia Pia Fidelis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700268.summary: This is a heavily fragmented inscription, possibly containing personal names or titles related to imperial veneration.

EDCS-32700273.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly related to a soldier's rank or unit.

EDCS-32700275.text_edition: \n Lucio Anicio Cai filio Paetinati IIIIviro iure dicundo praefecto fabrum IIIIviro iure dicundo quinquennali decurionum decreto publice bene merito\n
EDCS-32700275.translation: To Lucius Anicius Paetinatus, son of Caius, for his distinguished services as a four-man official with judicial authority, prefect of engineers, and quinquennial four-man official with judicial authority, honored by a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-32700275.summary: This is a public honorary inscription for Lucius Anicius Paetinatus, acknowledging his civic and military roles in a Roman colony, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-32700277.text_edition: \n Fabiano viro egregio decurioni coloniae Salonitanorum sacerdoti qui vixit anni[s ------\n
EDCS-32700277.translation: To Fabius, a distinguished man, councilor of the colony of Salona, a priest, who lived [for...].
EDCS-32700277.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Fabius, a councilor and priest of Salona, with a significant portion of his lifespan missing.

EDCS-32700278.text_edition: \n Quinto Maesio Ursulo VIviro Augustali defuncto nnorum XXX die natalis Aurelia Quinta coniugi inconparabili sic posuit\n
EDCS-32700278.translation: Aurelia Quinta erected this for her incomparable husband, Quintus Maesius Ursulus, a six-man official, Augustalis, who died at the age of thirty years and [number] days.
EDCS-32700278.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife for her husband, Quintus Maesius Ursulus, who held religious and civic office, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700280.translation: As a six-man official and grandson...
EDCS-32700280.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording a civic official ('IIIIIIvir') and a familial relationship ('nepot'), with the specific context lost.

EDCS-32700281.text_edition: \n ------ menses s]ex dies XV us decurio coloniae filiae dulciss[imae ------\n
EDCS-32700281.translation: ...six months and fifteen days, for his most sweet daughter, a councilor of the colony.
EDCS-32700281.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a deceased daughter, noting her age at death and her father's status as a councilor of the colony, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700282.translation: To the gods, the Manes. [Inscription fragment]
EDCS-32700282.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription beginning with the standard invocation to the gods of the underworld, with no further identifiable text.

EDCS-32700283.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus AN pontifex I N \n
EDCS-32700283.translation: To the gods, the Manes. [Inscription fragment]
EDCS-32700283.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription invoking the 'Dis Manibus', with only the beginning of a name or title preserved.

EDCS-32700284.translation: To the gods, the Manes. For Aurelius.
EDCS-32700284.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aurelius, beginning with the standard invocation to the gods of the underworld.

EDCS-32700288.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Silvestro homini probissimo peregrino huius loci Sextinus conserv<u=O>s posuit \n
EDCS-32700288.translation: To the gods, the Manes. Sextinus set this up for Silvester, a most worthy man, a peregrinus of this place.
EDCS-32700288.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Sextinus for Silvester, a respected non-citizen resident of the location, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700289.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus magister Threptioni discenti suo bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700289.translation: To the gods, the Manes. For Threption, his master, a deserving student.
EDCS-32700289.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Threption, identified as a master and a deserving student, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700291.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Fortunatus Flaviae Tertiae fecit bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700291.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Fortunatus made this for Flavia Tertia, deserving of his devotion.
EDCS-32700291.summary: A funerary dedication by Aurelius Fortunatus to his wife Flavia Tertia.

EDCS-32700292.text_edition: \n Gaio A T Caius P S filius patri suo merenti\n
EDCS-32700292.translation: To Gaius. His son, Caius, from the tribe Publicia, to his deserving father.
EDCS-32700292.summary: A funerary dedication by a son, Gaius, to his father.

EDCS-32700295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus A<e=F>liae Florae alumnae Titus Ae<l=I>(ius) Florus bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-32700295.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Aelius Florus set up this monument for Aelia Flora, his foster-daughter, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-32700295.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Aelius Florus for his foster-daughter Aelia Flora.

EDCS-32700296.translation: To my wife, by Aelia Maxima.
EDCS-32700296.summary: A dedication by Aelia Maxima to her wife.

EDCS-32700298.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication mentioning Aphrodisia and her husband.

EDCS-32700299.text_edition: \n ------ Flor]iniae Aphrodite Florinia Florentina patronae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700299.translation: To Florinia Aphrodite, patroness. Florinia Florentina set this up for her, deserving of her favor.
EDCS-32700299.summary: A dedication by Florinia Florentina to her patroness, Florinia Aphrodite.

EDCS-32700300.summary: A fragmentary dedication to someone named Apronius.

EDCS-32700301.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Armenius Cresc<e=I>ns Aureliae Marcianini conugi suae bene merenti posuit defuncta est annorum XVI mensium VIII \n
EDCS-32700301.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Armenius Crescens set this up for his deserving wife, Aurelia Marcianina, who died at the age of 16 years and 8 months.
EDCS-32700301.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Armenius Crescens for his wife Aurelia Marcianina, including her age at death.

EDCS-32700303.translation: To the Divine Shades. Attia Pardalis, to Herma.
EDCS-32700303.summary: A funerary dedication by Attia Pardalis for Herma.

EDCS-32700306.text_edition: \n ------] Aureli Apolloni--- Cai libert-- OP SC\n
EDCS-32700306.summary: A fragmentary inscription potentially dedicating a monument to Aurelius Apollonius, a freedman.

EDCS-32700307.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Communi defuncto annorum LX Cassia GAMIO et coniugi\n
EDCS-32700307.translation: To the divine shades of Marcus Aurelius Communis, who lived 60 years. Cassia Gamio set this up for her husband.
EDCS-32700307.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Communis, set up by his wife Cassia Gamio, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700308.text_edition: \n Aurelius Lucius delicato suo defuncto dier(um) XXV infanti? --- posuit\n
EDCS-32700308.translation: Aurelius Lucius set this up for his beloved [son?], an infant who lived 25 days.
EDCS-32700308.summary: A funerary epitaph for a very young child, dedicated by Aurelius Lucius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700310.text_edition: \n Aurelia Athenodora Clementiano marito suo et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-32700310.translation: Aurelia Athenodora set this up for her husband Clementianus and for herself.
EDCS-32700310.summary: A funerary epitaph for Clementianus and Aurelia Athenodora, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700314.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Birriae Primae quae et \n
EDCS-32700314.translation: To the divine shades of Birria Prima, who was also known as...
EDCS-32700314.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment for Birria Prima, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700316.text_edition: \n annos XXI Baebius Caecus coniugi inconparabili sic\n
EDCS-32700316.translation: [She lived] 21 years. Baebius Caecus [set this up] for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-32700316.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment for an unnamed woman, dedicated by her husband Baebius Caecus, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700317.summary: An inscription fragment mentioning Gaius Caesius, purpose and date uncertain.

EDCS-32700320.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio nnio Maximo Caetennia Aphrodisia mater\n
EDCS-32700320.translation: To the divine shades of Publius Annius Maximus. Caetennia Aphrodisia, [his] mother [set this up].
EDCS-32700320.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Annius Maximus, dedicated by his mother Caetennia Aphrodisia, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700321.text_edition: \n defuncto annorum Publius Caetennius Onesimus patronus alumno posuit\n
EDCS-32700321.translation: [Who lived X] years. Publius Caetennius Onesimus set this up for his freedman pupil.
EDCS-32700321.summary: A funerary epitaph for a freedman pupil, dedicated by his patron Publius Caetennius Onesimus, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700322.translation: To the divine shades of Caltilia Hilarilla, [his] wife.
EDCS-32700322.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caltilia Hilarilla, dedicated by her husband, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700323.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassiano nnos XVII Cassia Gemina \n
EDCS-32700323.translation: To the divine shades of Cassianus, [who lived] 17 years. Cassia Gemina [set this up].
EDCS-32700323.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cassianus, set up by Cassia Gemina, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700324.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassius Aelius Marciae Aureliae def(unctae) coniugi annorum XXVI bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700324.translation: To the divine shades, for Cassius Aelius, to his deserving wife Marcia Aurelia, deceased at twenty-six years of age.
EDCS-32700324.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcia Aurelia, dedicated by her husband, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700326.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudio o defuncto Lucius Taro[nius ------\n
EDCS-32700326.translation: To the divine shades, for Claudius, deceased. Lucius Taronius [---
EDCS-32700326.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning Claudius and a dedicator Lucius Taronius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700328.text_edition: \n Coeliae Kasiae libertae quondam Coeliae Romulae quae vixit nnos XLV Iulius Papianus maritus coniugi rarissimae fecit\n
EDCS-32700328.translation: For Coelia Kasia, freedwoman of Coelia Romula, who lived 45 years. Iulius Papianus, her husband, made this for his most rare wife.
EDCS-32700328.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Coelia Kasia, erected by her husband Iulius Papianus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700330.text_edition: \n RIS Chresimus defunctus nnorum super et N\n
EDCS-32700330.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary epitaph for Chresimus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700331.text_edition: \n ------ C]rispinus? --- in fronte pedes XII in [agro pedes ------\n
EDCS-32700331.translation: [---] Crispinus? --- twelve feet in front, in the field [---
EDCS-32700331.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely related to land division or property boundaries, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700332.translation: To the divine shades, for Curtius Nepos.
EDCS-32700332.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Curtius Nepos, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700333.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cusiae Sperantiae infanti defunctae annorum mensium II Rufi[nus ------\n
EDCS-32700333.translation: To the divine shades, for Cusia Sperantia, an infant deceased, two months old. Rufinus [---
EDCS-32700333.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an infant named Cusia Sperantia, dedicated by Rufinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700334.text_edition: \n ------] Dasantillae quae vixit nnos plus minus XXX quae <b=V>ene laboravit posui \n
EDCS-32700334.translation: [---] for Dasantilla, who lived more or less thirty years, who labored well. I placed.
EDCS-32700334.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Dasantilla, erected by an unnamed dedicator, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700336.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Democrito Fa VMETVM IN\n
EDCS-32700336.translation: To the divine shades, for Democritus [---] VMETVM IN
EDCS-32700336.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning Democritus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700337.text_edition: \n Dionysiae Avidi Spendontis Lucius Atatienus Secundus\n
EDCS-32700337.translation: For Dionysia, daughter of Avidus Spendon. Lucius Atatienus Secundus [erected this].
EDCS-32700337.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Dionysia, daughter of Avidus Spendon, dedicated by Lucius Atatienus Secundus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700338.text_edition: \n IT Marco Domitio Saturnino in fronte pedes XV in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-32700338.translation: For Marcus Domitius Saturninus, 15 feet in front and 20 feet in the field.
EDCS-32700338.summary: This inscription marks the dimensions of a property belonging to Marcus Domitius Saturninus, likely related to land or a tomb.

EDCS-32700339.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Domitia Fortunata matri piissimae inconparabili sic quae vixit annos plus minus \n
EDCS-32700339.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Domitia Fortunata, most devoted and incomparable mother, who lived approximately...
EDCS-32700339.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Domitia Fortunata, praising her as a mother and mentioning her lifespan, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700340.text_edition: \n filius Lo Tertullam Lucius Dr SI ICD\n
EDCS-32700340.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary funerary dedication, possibly to a woman named Tertulla, with a patronymic provided.

EDCS-32700341.text_edition: \n Lucius Egnatius Luci libertus Maximus vivus fecit sibi et Egnatis Amyco patri eidem patrono Myrinae matri Liberali sorori Lucensi fratri et Iuliae Proculae uxori suis in fronte pedes XXXX in agro pedes XXX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-32700341.translation: Lucius Egnatius Maximus, freedman of Lucius, made this monument for himself while alive, and for his father Egnatius Amyc(u)s, the same patron, and his mother Myrina, and his sister Liberalis, and his brother Lucens, and his wife Julia Procula. 40 feet in front and 30 feet in the field. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-32700341.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument inscription by Lucius Egnatius Maximus, detailing the family members included and specifying property dimensions and restrictions on inheritance, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700343.text_edition: \n Eutychia Victorina uxor coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700343.translation: To her deserving husband, Victorina, his wife.
EDCS-32700343.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory epitaph from Victorina to her well-deserving husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700344.translation: To her husband and to Faberia, his sister.
EDCS-32700344.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription or dedication for a husband and his sister Faberia.

EDCS-32700345.text_edition: \n Lucio Fabio Surai sic Fausta liberta \n
EDCS-32700345.translation: For Lucius Fabio Surus, Fausta his freedwoman.
EDCS-32700345.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Fausta, a freedwoman, for her former master Lucius Fabio Surus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700346.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ae defunctae mensium dierum XII A Nymfae sic Felici filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700346.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Nymfa[e] Felici, his daughter, who died after twelve months and days, well-deserving.
EDCS-32700346.summary: This is a funerary inscription to a daughter named Nymfae Felici, mentioning her young age (twelve months and days), indicating a Roman Imperial date.

EDCS-32700347.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Agathoni filio piissimo Sextus pater\n
EDCS-32700347.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Flavius Agathon, his most devoted son. Sextus, his father.
EDCS-32700347.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a father, Sextus, to his devoted son, Flavius Agathon, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700348.text_edition: \n ------ F]lavia Flavio Alexandro bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700348.translation: [...] Flavia [erected this] for her well-deserving Flavius Alexander.
EDCS-32700348.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Flavia dedicates a monument to Flavius Alexander, describing him as well-deserving.

EDCS-32700356.text_edition: \n nae coniugi incomparabili Iulius Aper et sibi\n
EDCS-32700356.translation: To Nae, his incomparable wife, Julius Aper and himself.
EDCS-32700356.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Julius Aper to his wife Nae, with a self-dedication. It is likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-32700357.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Crescenti qui et Asifa Servilia Prima coniugi bene merenti bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700357.translation: To the Divine Manes of Julius Crescentius, also called Asifa. Servilia Prima set this up for her deserving wife.
EDCS-32700357.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Servilia Prima for her wife, Julius Crescentius (also known as Asifa), who is addressed with the title 'Divine Manes'.

EDCS-32700358.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Epicadi filius vivi fecerunt coniugi et Saturnino Caio Iulio Plotino hoc monumentum in fronte pedes \n
EDCS-32700358.translation: Gaius Julius, son of Epicadus, while alive, made this monument for his wife and for Saturninus, Gaius Julius Plotinus, [measuring] twenty feet in front.
EDCS-32700358.summary: This inscription records a tomb monument constructed by Gaius Julius for his wife and for Plotinus, with specified dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Felici Quintius Sabianus fratri bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700359.translation: To the Divine Manes of Gaius Julius Felix. Quintius Sabianus set this up for his deserving brother.
EDCS-32700359.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Quintius Sabianus for his brother, Gaius Julius Felix, invoking the 'Divine Manes'.

EDCS-32700360.text_edition: \n Iulio Ursino infanti infelicissimo qui vixit \n
EDCS-32700360.translation: To Julius Ursinus, a most unlucky infant, who lived...
EDCS-32700360.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to an infant named Julius Ursinus, lamenting his short life.

EDCS-32700361.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or personal name, mentioning 'Iulia'.

EDCS-32700364.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Felicitati defunctae nnorum plus minus XX Vitalio uxori inf<e=F>licissimae quae vixit mecum annis IIII quo<d=T> vivae melius destinavi mortuae tamen bene merenti posui \n
EDCS-32700364.translation: To the Divine Manes of Iulia Felicitas, deceased, about 20 years old. Vitalio set this up for his most unlucky wife, who lived with me for 4 years. What I better intended while she was alive, I nevertheless set up for her, deserving, in death.
EDCS-32700364.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Vitalio for his wife, Iulia Felicitas, who died young, expressing his sorrow and love.

EDCS-32700365.text_edition: \n Iuliae Palaestrice nnum V Eleutheris mater \n
EDCS-32700365.translation: To Iulia Palaestrica, 5 years old. Eleutheris, mother.
EDCS-32700365.summary: This inscription records the dedication for Iulia Palaestrica, a five-year-old child, with her mother Eleutheris mentioned.

EDCS-32700367.text_edition: \n ilae Iulia Zosime patronae bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700367.translation: ...ilae. Iulia Zosime for her deserving patroness.
EDCS-32700367.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Iulia Zosime to her deserving patroness.

EDCS-32700368.text_edition: \n Iulia Ursa viva fecit sibi et Lucio Iulio <E=F>uaristo patrono bene merenti Ceriali filio annorum XXIII et suis in fronte pedes XV in a<g=C>ro pedes XX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur \n
EDCS-32700368.translation: Iulia Ursa, while alive, made this for herself and for Lucius Julius Evanthus, her deserving patron, Cerialis' son, 23 years old, and for her own [family]. Fifteen feet in front, twenty feet in depth, this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-32700368.summary: This is a self-dedicated tomb monument by Iulia Ursa for herself, her patron Lucius Julius Evanthus, and her family, specifying dimensions and a condition about inheritance.

EDCS-32700371.translation: BR and C. Licinius and friends.
EDCS-32700371.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial by BR and C. Licinius and their friends, likely of Roman Republican or early Imperial date.

EDCS-32700374.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name of Longinus, possibly as a dedication or a person commemorated, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700375.text_edition: \n Quinto Lucio Democrito defuncto annorum LXXIIII Quintus Lucius Democritus patri optimo et Ravonia Marronia coniugi incomparabili\n
EDCS-32700375.translation: To Quintus Lucius Democritus, deceased at seventy-four years. Quintus Lucius Democritus, to his most excellent father and to his incomparable wife Ravonia Marronia.
EDCS-32700375.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Quintus Lucius Democritus, who died at 74, and is dedicated by his son to his father and wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700376.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luria Bictoria sic quae nata XXVI item filius Marinus qui natus IIII posibit sic coniug(i) Aurelius Dioclis sic\n
EDCS-32700376.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Luria Victoria, who lived 26 years, and her son Marinus, who lived 4 years. Aurelius Dioclis set this up for his wife.
EDCS-32700376.summary: This funerary inscription dedicates a monument to Luria Victoria (26) and her son Marinus (4), set up by Aurelius Dioclis for his wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700378.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name Marcellinus, possibly for a dedication or memorial, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700380.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naevius Tertullinus Larcia Ursilla filio suo Publio Domit[io ------\n
EDCS-32700380.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Naevius Tertullinus and Larcia Ursilla for their son Publius Domitius [...]
EDCS-32700380.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Naevius Tertullinus and Larcia Ursilla to their son Publius Domitius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700385.summary: This very fragmentary inscription likely contains the name Papinius and an initial, possibly part of a dedication or funerary text from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700386.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papinio Ursino homini verecundo \n
EDCS-32700386.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Papinius Ursus, a modest man.
EDCS-32700386.summary: This funerary inscription honors Papinius Ursus as a modest man, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700387.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Papirio Suro defuncto nnorum LXV Primitiva uxor et Prima filia bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700387.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Publius Papirius Surus, deceased at 65 years. His wife Primitiva and daughter Prima, to one who deserved well.
EDCS-32700387.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Publius Papirius Surus, who died at 65, and is dedicated by his wife Primitiva and daughter Prima, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700388.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name Lucius Papius, possibly as part of a dedication or funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700389.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pardalae nnorum XIII mensium V Flavius verna et Domitia Primitiva parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-32700389.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavius Verna and Domitia Primitiva, parents, set this up for Pardala, aged thirteen years and five months.
EDCS-32700389.summary: This is a tombstone set up by parents for their daughter Pardala, who died at the age of 13 years and 5 months.

EDCS-32700390.text_edition: \n innocus Peregrinus Quintus Vedius Restitutus Dasius ria iuveni<s=L> ANA R F<r=P>ont Severa a Blaste Primigenia CV Aurelio Agatho<r=P>o \n
EDCS-32700390.translation: Innocuus Peregrinus, Quintus Vedius Restitutus, Dasius, Ria, iuvenes; Severa and Blastus, Primigenia; Aurelius Agathopus.
EDCS-32700390.summary: This inscription lists names, possibly of donors or individuals associated with a funerary monument, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-32700392.text_edition: \n ------ Petro]nio Polycarpo Petronia Cervia bene m(erenti) p(osuit)\n
EDCS-32700392.translation: To [Petronius] Polycarpus, whom Petronia Cervia set up as deserving well.
EDCS-32700392.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Petronia Cervia for the well-deserving Petronius Polycarpus, with the beginning of the name lost.

EDCS-32700393.translation: To Polytimus, a friend, as deserving well.
EDCS-32700393.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a friend for Polytimus.

EDCS-32700394.text_edition: \n Pomponia Vera testamento fieri iussit sibi et libertis libertabusque suis ex HS XXviginti milibus curantibus heredibus in quo opere adiecerunt heredes HS IIIIquattuor milia\n
EDCS-32700394.translation: Pomponia Vera ordered this to be made in her will for herself and for her freedmen and freedwomen, from 20,000 sesterces, with the heirs overseeing it; to which work the heirs added 4,000 sesterces.
EDCS-32700394.summary: This funerary inscription details a will by Pomponia Vera, establishing a fund for her tomb and that of her freedmen, with additional contributions from her heirs.

EDCS-32700395.text_edition: \n Vi Prima Montano fratri defuncto annorum XVI bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700395.translation: Vi Prima set this up for her brother Montanus, deceased at sixteen years of age, as deserving well.
EDCS-32700395.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Vi Prima for her brother Montanus, who died at age 16.

EDCS-32700397.translation: Primitivus set this up for his deserving wife.
EDCS-32700397.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Primitivus for his wife.

EDCS-32700399.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Priva defunct- annorum VI HE T F \n
EDCS-32700399.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Priva, deceased, aged 6 years...
EDCS-32700399.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone for Priva, who died at the age of 6.

EDCS-32700400.text_edition: \n RIIO Publio Publ Aemilia be[ne merenti ------\n
EDCS-32700400.translation: To Publius Publilius [---], whom Aemilia [---] as deserving well.
EDCS-32700400.summary: This is a damaged funerary inscription with missing parts, likely a dedication to Publius Publilius by Aemilia.

EDCS-32700401.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Publicius.
EDCS-32700401.summary: This is a simple funerary dedication to Publicius.

EDCS-32700402.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publiciae Secundinae quae vixit nnos XXXX Aemilius Secundinus filius et Coylius Severus maritus Apius posuerunt\n
EDCS-32700402.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publicia Secundina, who lived 40 years. Aemilius Secundinus, her son, and Coylius Severus, her husband, Apius, erected [this].
EDCS-32700402.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publicia Secundina, erected by her son and husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32700403.translation: AF, aged 25 years. For his brother.
EDCS-32700403.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for a man named AF, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700405.text_edition: \n ------] Quinto iu<v=B>eni i<n=M>felicissimo defuncto annorum XXI Titus Aelius Philus \n
EDCS-32700405.translation: [...]. To Quintus Juvenis, most unhappy, who died aged 21 years. Titus Aelius Philus [erected this].
EDCS-32700405.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Juvenis, erected by Titus Aelius Philus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700407.text_edition: \n Rufio Saturnini vernae hic sito VO\n
EDCS-32700407.translation: Rufio Saturnini, the slave, buried here.
EDCS-32700407.summary: A simple funerary marker for Rufio Saturnini, a slave, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700408.text_edition: \n ------ Satur]nino annorum mensium VI dierum XV Titus Valerius Iulianus ITSAI\n
EDCS-32700408.translation: [...]. To Saturninus, aged 6 months and 15 days. Titus Valerius Iulianus [erected this].
EDCS-32700408.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturninus, a child, erected by Titus Valerius Iulianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700410.summary: A very fragmentary inscription mentioning a father named Saufeius, the original context and purpose are unclear.

EDCS-32700413.text_edition: \n EP IOS Secundi to marito um \n
EDCS-32700413.summary: A fragmentary epitaph dedicated to a husband named Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700416.text_edition: \n Quinto Septimio Antonio defuncto nnorum XVIII mensium V dierum II et Quinto Septimio Antonino defuncto nnorum XXII mensium XI dierum XXIII ego Antoninus umbra tenus have tibi dico meator quamvis festinanti gradu carpas iter resiste paulum et lege casum sic ego VAMMAFIretinere cupisco spiritum MCMreliquit malignusque casus MA corpori toto post cuius exitiumque funeris terrae datas decubui prope POI noctesque diesque iam trahor in tenebras ducorque amplectere fratrem hoc fatali casu datum est pergam nunc Tartara divo reliquiasque meas curentC orbati parentes iam casum cognosti meum feliciter perge viator decepti senes testemur funera natuum et pietas titulo deplorat fata parentum\n
EDCS-32700416.translation: To Quintus Septimius Antoninus, who died aged 18 years, 5 months, and 2 days, and to Quintus Septimius Antoninus, who died aged 22 years, 11 months, and 23 days. I, Antoninus, barely holding onto life, speak to you. Though you hasten your steps, traveler, pause a while and read my fate. Thus I too wished to retain my spirit, but cruel fate snatched away my body. After his death and funeral, I lay in the earth, and day and night I am dragged into darkness and embraced by my brother. This was given by fatal chance; I shall now go to the underworld, divinely favored. May my orphaned parents care for my remains. You have now learned my fate; happily proceed, traveler. Deceived elders bear witness to the funeral of their children, and piety laments the fate of parents.
EDCS-32700416.summary: A complex funerary epitaph composed in verse for two brothers, Quintus Septimius Antoninus, erected by another family member, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimiae Pusinnae defunctae nnorum XXX et Septimiae Hilarae filiae nnorum X Aurelia Marcia soror bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700418.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Septimia Pusinna, deceased, aged 30 years, and for her daughter Septimia Hilara, aged 10 years. Aurelia Marcia, sister, erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-32700418.summary: A funerary epitaph for Septimia Pusinna and her daughter Septimia Hilara, erected by Aurelia Marcia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700420.text_edition: \n Severus et A Severina ae Severae matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700420.translation: Severus and A Severina, to their mother Severa, well-deserving.
EDCS-32700420.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Severa by her children Severus and A Severina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700421.text_edition: \n Statia Donata enio oto filio defuncto anorum XVI\n
EDCS-32700421.translation: To Statia Donata, for her son who died at sixteen years of age.
EDCS-32700421.summary: This epitaph records the death of a sixteen-year-old boy named Statia Donata, likely set up by a parent or relative.

EDCS-32700422.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Stefano infelicissimo nnorum XIII \n
EDCS-32700422.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Stefano, most unfortunate, aged thirteen years.
EDCS-32700422.summary: This epitaph records the death of a thirteen-year-old named Stefano, intended for the underworld deities.

EDCS-32700426.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentiae Syntrophe defunctae nnorum XIII Apuleia Primitiva mater filiae carissimae\n
EDCS-32700426.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Terentia Syntrophe, who died at thirteen years of age. Apuleia Primitiva, her mother, [erected this] for her dearest daughter.
EDCS-32700426.summary: This epitaph records the death of Terentia Syntrophe at thirteen years old, set up by her mother.

EDCS-32700427.text_edition: \n Lucius Titius Eutychus Viriae Aphrodisiae coniugi bene merenti vivus fecit\n
EDCS-32700427.translation: Lucius Titius Eutychus made this for his wife Viria Aphrodisia, who served him well, during his lifetime.
EDCS-32700427.summary: This is a dedication made by Lucius Titius Eutychus for his deserving wife Viria Aphrodisia, erected while he was alive.

EDCS-32700428.text_edition: \n Titiae Firminae coniugi Statius Faustinus maritus\n
EDCS-32700428.translation: Statius Faustinus, husband, [erected this] for his wife Titia Firmina.
EDCS-32700428.summary: This is a dedication by Statius Faustinus for his wife Titia Firmina.

EDCS-32700430.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tulliae Veneriae coniugi incomparabili quae vicxit sic nnos mecum XXIII menses VIII dies XXI Titus Aurelius Ursinu<s=Y> et sibi posuit \n
EDCS-32700430.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Tullia Veneria, an incomparable wife, who lived with me for 23 years, 8 months, and 21 days. Titus Aurelius Ursinus placed this for her and himself.
EDCS-32700430.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Tullia Veneria as 23 years, 8 months, and 21 days, set up by her husband Titus Aurelius Ursinus for both of them.

EDCS-32700431.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Tulli Veneriani vixit annos V menses VIIII dies XXV So\n
EDCS-32700431.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Lucius Titius Venerianus, who lived 5 years, 9 months, and 25 days.
EDCS-32700431.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Lucius Titius Venerianus as 5 years, 9 months, and 25 days.

EDCS-32700434.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record a lifespan of 20 years for Lucio Calo Valerio.

EDCS-32700435.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Mariniano Valeria Firmina fratri\n
EDCS-32700435.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Valerius Marinianus. Valeria Firmina [set this up] for her brother.
EDCS-32700435.summary: This epitaph records the death of Valerius Marinianus, set up by his sister Valeria Firmina.

EDCS-32700436.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Valerio Marci filio Phocati Aurelia Fortunata coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700436.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Marcus Valerius Phocatus, son of Marcus. Aurelia Fortunata, his wife, who served him well, placed this.
EDCS-32700436.summary: This epitaph records the death of Marcus Valerius Phocatus, son of Marcus, set up by his deserving wife Aurelia Fortunata.

EDCS-32700438.text_edition: \n ------ One]sima Valeria Onesimini filiae defunctae annorum V\n
EDCS-32700438.translation: To Onesima, daughter of Valeria Onesimus, aged 5 years.
EDCS-32700438.summary: This epitaph records the death of a young girl named Onesima, daughter of Valeria Onesimus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700439.text_edition: \n Valeriae Quintianae de<f=E>unctae nnorum III m<e=F>nsium X dierum XX nepoti Rutilius Marinianus MA Valeria Felicula avia infanti infelicissima<e=F> \n
EDCS-32700439.translation: To Valeria Quintiana, who died aged 3 years, 10 months, and 20 days. Rutilius Marinianus, with his wife Valeria Felicula, grandmother to the most unhappy infant.
EDCS-32700439.summary: This epitaph commemorates the death of the infant Valeria Quintiana, erected by her paternal relatives, Rutilius Marinianus and Valeria Felicula, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700440.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Sever<i=E>nae defunct<a=V>e annorum XXIIII filiae infelicissimae Calpurnia Marcellina mater cum suis \n
EDCS-32700440.translation: To the departed spirits, for Valeria Severina, who died aged 24 years, the most unhappy daughter. Calpurnia Marcellina, her mother, with her family.
EDCS-32700440.summary: This epitaph is dedicated to the memory of Valeria Severina, erected by her grieving mother, Calpurnia Marcellina, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700441.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Victorinae Lucius Baebilius Epaphroditus coniugi de se bene merenti\n
EDCS-32700441.translation: To the departed spirits, for Valeria Victorina, a deserving wife. Lucius Baebilius Epaphroditus erected this for her.
EDCS-32700441.summary: This epitaph commemorates Valeria Victorina, erected by her husband Lucius Baebilius Epaphroditus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700443.text_edition: \n ano defuncto annorum XI mensium II dierum X Vetidia Secunda mater\n
EDCS-32700443.translation: To [her] son who died aged 11 years, 2 months, and 10 days. Vetidia Secunda, mother.
EDCS-32700443.summary: This epitaph records the death of a young boy, erected by his mother Vetidia Secunda, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700444.summary: This fragment likely refers to the dedication of a monument to Vibius Syntyche, possibly in conjunction with another inscription, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700445.text_edition: \n Vibiano Tertio Auli filio Gelasio VLV \n
EDCS-32700445.translation: To Vibianus Tertius, son of Aulus, Gelasus VLV.
EDCS-32700445.summary: This epitaph commemorates Vibianus Tertius, son of Aulus, erected by Gelasus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700446.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victoriae coniugi bene merenti fecit Ialysus\n
EDCS-32700446.translation: To the departed spirits, for Victoria, a deserving wife. Ialysus made this.
EDCS-32700446.summary: This epitaph honors Victoria, a deserving wife, erected by Ialysus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700449.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Volusio Respecto Vo\n
EDCS-32700449.translation: To the departed spirits, for Quintus Volusius Respectus.
EDCS-32700449.summary: This epitaph is dedicated to Quintus Volusius Respectus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700451.summary: This inscription likely records a funerary dedication made by Ursa for herself, possibly indicating a self-purchased burial plot, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700453.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording the maker of an object, possibly funerary, from an unknown date.

EDCS-32700455.text_edition: \n AN lia cundi a Nomedito bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700455.translation: Anlia Cunda erected this for Nomeditus, deserving well.
EDCS-32700455.summary: This funerary inscription records Anlia Cunda dedicating a monument to Nomeditus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700457.text_edition: \n ------ I]usta TILEO vixit annos L P piis CV \n
EDCS-32700457.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Justa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700459.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly mentioning names like Valenus and Velde, with an unknown purpose and date.

EDCS-32700460.text_edition: \n CII AXV amici C miramini ec qui habet \n
EDCS-32700460.translation: Look, friends, and marvel at me, who has [...]
EDCS-32700460.summary: This inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, is a short, personal message, possibly on a tomb, inviting passersby to observe the deceased.

EDCS-32700463.text_edition: \n ------ tu]mulum est inan I rogitantis con m laus est non fac tres EIIV Aninia coniugi FVIGINI IIII\n
EDCS-32700463.translation: [...] it is an empty tomb. Of those asking, praise is not made. EIIV Aninia for her wife FVIGINI, 4 [...]
EDCS-32700463.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records Aninia dedicating a monument to her wife FVIGINI and comments on the emptiness of the tomb.

EDCS-32700481.text_edition: \n ------ Z]osimi ? emeriti per Deum omnipotente<m=N> <eos=IIS> adiuro \n
EDCS-32700481.translation: [...] Zosimus, an ex-soldier, by almighty God I adjure you [...]
EDCS-32700481.summary: This is a fragment of a magical or religious inscription, possibly funerary, invoking God and mentioning Zosimus, an ex-soldier, dating likely to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-32700488.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Plares miles cohortis VIII voluntariorum beneficiarius praefecti defunctus nnorum XXXX stipendiorum XX Lucius Valerius Surus heres eius genero bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700488.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Valerius Surus, his heir and son-in-law, erected this for Flavius Plares, soldier of the VIII Cohort of Volunteers, a beneficiarius of the prefect, who died aged 40 years and 20 years of service, deserving well.
EDCS-32700488.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Valerius Surus dedicating a monument to his deceased son-in-law, Flavius Plares, a soldier, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32700491.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning an imaginifer (standard-bearer) and years of service, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700494.text_edition: \n Claudiae Quintinae defunctae annorum II mensium III Iulia Maxima avia et Tiberius Claudius Tiberinus pater fecerunt\n
EDCS-32700494.translation: Iulia Maxima, grandmother, and Tiberius Claudius Tiberinus, father, made this for Claudia Quintina, deceased, aged 2 years and 3 months.
EDCS-32700494.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records Iulia Maxima and Tiberius Claudius Tiberinus dedicating a monument to their deceased daughter and granddaughter, Claudia Quintina.

EDCS-32700497.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sacronius Ursinus Anniae Quintae defunctae annorum LX bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-32700497.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacronius Ursinus set this up for his deserving Annia Quīnta, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-32700497.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Sacronius Ursinus for his deceased wife Annia Quīnta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700500.text_edition: \n Caius Octavius Cai filius Fabia Vitalis domo Brixiae sic veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis hic situs est testamento fieri iussit arbitratu Octaviae Secundaes sic libertae suae\n
EDCS-32700500.translation: Caius Octavius Vitalis, son of Caius, of the Fabian tribe, from Brixia, a veteran of Legio VII Claudia Pia Fidelis, lies here; he ordered this to be made by his will, under the supervision of Octavia Secunda, his freedwoman.
EDCS-32700500.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Octavius Vitalis, a veteran of Legio VII Claudia Pia Fidelis, detailing his place of origin and the arrangements for his burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700501.text_edition: \n Alfia Luci filia Valentina viva fecit sibi et Lucio Alfio Valenti patri et Raiae Marcellae matri optimae et Lucio Alfio Valentino fratri posterisque suis\n
EDCS-32700501.translation: Alfia Valentina, daughter of Lucius, made this while alive for herself, and for her father Lucius Alfius Valens, her excellent mother Raia Marcella, her brother Lucius Alfius Valentinus, and her descendants.
EDCS-32700501.summary: A funerary dedication made by Alfia Valentina during her lifetime for herself and her family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700502.summary: A brief funerary dedication to a deceased fellow freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700503.text_edition: \n D--- Paulina filiae dulcissimae anni unius mensium XI\n
EDCS-32700503.translation: To [---], Paulina, to her sweetest daughter, aged one year and eleven months.
EDCS-32700503.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child, Paulina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700504.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cassius Dasantis cohortis III Alpinorum tesserarius centuria Nunnii Isiani votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32700504.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Cassius Dasantis, a signaller (tesserarius) of the 3rd Cohort of Alpiners, in the century of Nunnīus Isīanus, willingly fulfilled his vow for a just cause.
EDCS-32700504.summary: A votive inscription dedicated by Cassius Dasantis, a soldier from the 3rd Cohort of Alpiners, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700509.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Nigrinianus \n
EDCS-32700509.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Titus Flavius Nigrinianus.
EDCS-32700509.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Flavius Nigrinianus, the context and exact nature of which are unclear, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700511.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio municipii Valerius Severus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis votum solvit\n
EDCS-32700511.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the municipality. Valerius Severus, a staff officer (beneficiarius consularis) of Legio XIIII Gemina Martia Victrix, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32700511.summary: A votive inscription by Valerius Severus, a soldier of Legio XIIII Gemina Martia Victrix, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the municipality, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700522.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Aurelius Maximinus quinquennalis suo votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-32700522.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Titus Aurelius Maximinus, quinquennalis, willingly fulfilled his vow for himself.
EDCS-32700522.summary: A votive inscription by Titus Aurelius Maximinus, who held the office of quinquennalis, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700523.summary: A fragmented inscription possibly dedicated to Apollo or another deity, with an unclear name, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700524.text_edition: \n Septimius Panes et Septimius Titianus Apuri patri suo pientissimo et matri posuerunt\n
EDCS-32700524.translation: Septimius Panes and Septimius Titianus erected this for their most devoted father and mother.
EDCS-32700524.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from a tombstone, records a dedication by two sons to their parents, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700530.text_edition: \n ni suorumque omnium Philocyrius tibicen ex voto posuit et tempulum sic restituit Idibus Iuliis Imperatore domino nostro Caesare Gordiano Augusto II et Pompeiano consulibus\n
EDCS-32700530.translation: Philocyrius, the piper, erected this temple and restored it in accordance with his vow, along with all his family, on the Ides of July, during the consulship of our Emperor Lord Caesar Gordianus Augustus II and Pompeianus.
EDCS-32700530.summary: This dedication records the restoration of a temple and its setting up in fulfillment of a vow by a piper named Philocyrius, dated to 244 AD.

EDCS-32700531.text_edition: \n defuncto annorum LX et filio Marcus Ulpius Rusticus SIT REL annorum X plus minus titulum pecunia sua posuit VEM \n
EDCS-32700531.translation: Marcus Ulpius Rusticus erected this monument at his own expense for his deceased son, who was about 10 years old, and for [another child, his] son, who was about 60 years old.
EDCS-32700531.summary: This funerary inscription records a father's erection of a monument for his deceased son and another individual, likely a child, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700546.translation: To their most unfortunate mother Aurelia.
EDCS-32700546.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records a dedication to a mother named Aurelia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700547.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dastoni matri et Paiioni sorori Publius Aelius Varro pro pietate\n
EDCS-32700547.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Dasto, his mother, and Paiio, his sister, Publius Aelius Varro, out of devotion.
EDCS-32700547.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Publius Aelius Varro to his mother Dasto and sister Paiio, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700550.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Geminus ex voto suscepto\n
EDCS-32700550.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Julius Geminus, in fulfillment of his vow.
EDCS-32700550.summary: This dedication records an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Julius Geminus in fulfillment of a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700552.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus cTae Severinae filiae dulcissimae posuit\n
EDCS-32700552.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, [erected by X] for his sweetest daughter Severina.
EDCS-32700552.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication to a deceased daughter named Severina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700556.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum Claudius Peregrinus decurio cohortis I Belgarum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32700556.translation: Sacred to Hercules. Claudius Peregrinus, decurion of the I Cohort of Belgae, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-32700556.summary: This dedication records a soldier named Claudius Peregrinus fulfilling a vow to Hercules, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700558.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titulenius Respectus TIM \n
EDCS-32700558.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Titulenius Respectus [erected this].
EDCS-32700558.summary: This fragmentary dedication records an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titulenius Respectus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700560.summary: This dedication records Caius Valerius Euhodus dedicating something to the god Silvanus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32700561.translation: Sicilius Secundus gladly paid his vow.
